And yet you do not use them. Some of you do not even recognize what you have received or who gave it. Others see it clearly and still resort to self-pity and blame.

I can show you that you have the powers for courage, endurance, and greatness of soul. But show me your powers for finding fault and making accusations — because that is all you seem to exercise.
D: Elegant: God gave you faculties for endurance, courage, and greatness — free, unhindered, entirely your own. He did not even reserve the power to interfere with them. Yet you use none of this, then blame him. I can show you the powers for greatness. Show me your powers for complaint.
D: Distilled: You have been given every faculty needed for greatness of soul — and you use none of them, then blame God for your suffering.
Notes: The closing argument inverts the charge of impiety: it is the person who refuses to use their faculties, not the critic of providence, who dishonors the divine. 'Like a good king and a true father' — Epictetus bridges Stoic theology and Roman political metaphor. The chapter's arc moves from design (1.29) to purpose (1.30) to application (1.31-1.32) to accountability (1.33). This connects to the framework's Right pillar: we are responsible for what we do with the capacities we have, regardless of the conditions we face.

D: Original: Chapter 7: Of the use of sophistical arguments, and hypothetical, and the like

The handling of sophistical and hypothetical arguments, and of those which derive their conclusions from questioning, and in a word the handling of all such arguments, relates to the duties of life, though the many do not know this truth. For in every matter we inquire how the wise and good man shall discover the proper path and the proper method of dealing with the matter. Let, then, people either say that the grave man will not descend into the contest of question and answer, or that, if he does descend into the contest, he will take no care about not conducting himself rashly or carelessly in questioning and answering.
D: Refashioned: Most people assume that logic — the formal handling of arguments, hypotheses, and deductions — has nothing to do with real life. They are wrong. In every situation we ask how a wise person should navigate the problem. Either the serious person refuses to engage in argument at all, or they engage carefully and deliberately. There is no third option — and the first is not an option at all.
D: Elegant: Logic is not an academic exercise. It is the discipline of thinking clearly when it matters. The serious person either refuses to argue altogether or argues with care. There is no middle ground.
D: Distilled: Logic is not separate from life — it is the discipline of thinking clearly when it matters.
Notes: Epictetus's defense of logic is pragmatic: formal reasoning skills prevent you from being misled by sophists and from misleading yourself. The 'grave man' (spoudaios) is the Stoic technical term for the person of moral seriousness. This connects to the framework's Reason pillar: sound reasoning is not a specialized skill but a basic requirement for navigating reality.

D: Original: But if they do not allow either the one or the other of these things, they must admit that some inquiry ought to be made into those topics on which particularly questioning and answering are employed. For what is the end proposed in reasoning? To establish true propositions, to remove the false, to withhold assent from those which are not plain. Is it enough then to have learned only this? "It is enough," a man may reply. Is it, then, also enough for a man, who would not make a mistake in the use of coined money, to have heard this precept, that he should receive the genuine drachmae and reject the spurious? "It is not enough." What, then, ought to be added to this precept? What else than the faculty which proves and distinguishes the genuine and the spurious drachmae? Consequently also in reasoning what has been said is not enough; but is it necessary that a man should acquire the faculty of examining and distinguishing the true and the false, and that which is not plain? "It is necessary." Besides this, what is proposed in reasoning? "That you should accept what follows from that which you have properly granted." Well, is it then enough in this case also to know this? It is not enough; but a man must learn how one thing is a consequence of other things, and when one thing follows from one thing, and when it follows from several collectively. Consider, then if it be not necessary that this power should also be acquired by him who purposes to conduct himself skillfully in reasoning, the power of demonstrating himself the several things which he has proposed, and the power of understanding the demonstrations of others, including of not being deceived by sophists, as if they were demonstrating.
D: Refashioned:
Knowing that you should accept true propositions and reject false ones is not enough — just as knowing you should accept real coins and reject counterfeits is not enough. You need the trained faculty of telling them apart.

This faculty includes: the ability to test whether something is true, false, or unclear; the understanding of how one thing follows from another; and the capacity to construct your own demonstrations while seeing through false ones. Without this training, you are defenseless against anyone who reasons badly — including yourself.
D: Elegant: Knowing to accept real coins and reject counterfeits is not enough — you need the faculty of telling them apart. The same applies to propositions. Without trained judgment, you are defenseless against bad reasoning, including your own.
D: Distilled: Knowing that truth matters is not enough — you need the trained faculty of distinguishing truth from falsehood.
Notes: The coin-testing metaphor (dokimazein) runs through Greek philosophy. Diogenes the Cynic was said to have been exiled for 'defacing the currency,' later reinterpreted as 'revaluing the currency of convention.' Epictetus applies the metaphor to logic: a money-changer needs practical skill, not just theory.

D: Original:
Therefore there has arisen among us the practice and exercise of conclusive arguments and figures, and it has been shown to be necessary.

But in fact in some cases we have properly granted the premisses or assumptions, and there results from them something; and though it is not true, yet none the less it does result. What then ought I to do? Ought I to admit the falsehood? And how is that possible? Well, should I say that I did not properly grant that which we agreed upon? "But you are not allowed to do even this." Shall I then say that the consequence does not arise through what has been conceded? "But neither is it allowed." What then must be done in this case? Consider if it is not this: as to have borrowed is not enough to make a man still a debtor, but to this must be added the fact that he continues to owe the money and that the debt is not paid, so it is not enough to compel you to admit the inference that you have granted the premisses, but you must abide by what you have granted. Indeed, if the premisses continue to the end such as they were when they were granted, it is absolutely necessary for us to abide by what we have granted, and we must accept their consequences: but if the premisses do not remain such as they were when they were granted, it is absolutely necessary for us also to withdraw from what we granted, and from accepting what does not follow from the words in which our concessions were made. For the inference is now not our inference, nor does it result with our assent, since we have withdrawn from the premisses which we granted. We ought then both to examine such kind of premisses, and such change and variation of them, by which in the course of questioning or answering, or in making the syllogistic conclusion, or in any other such way, the premisses undergo variations, and give occasion to the foolish to be confounded, if they do not see what conclusions are. For what reason ought we to examine? In order that we may not in this matter be employed in an improper manner nor in a confused way.
D: Refashioned:
The study of formal argument is necessary because premises can shift. Sometimes you grant premises that produce valid but false conclusions — not because the logic failed, but because the premises changed during the argument.

The analogy is debt: having once borrowed money does not make you permanently a debtor — the debt must still be outstanding. Similarly, having once granted premises does not mean you must accept every consequence derived from them, if those premises have since been altered or undermined. You must track when and how the ground shifts beneath an argument, or you will be trapped by conclusions that no longer follow from what you actually believe.
D: Elegant: Having borrowed once does not make you a permanent debtor — the debt must still be outstanding. Having granted a premise does not mean you must accept every consequence — the premise must still hold. Track the ground beneath an argument, or be trapped by conclusions that no longer follow.
D: Distilled: A granted premise is like a debt: binding only as long as it still holds — track when the ground shifts.
Notes: The debt analogy is Epictetus's own: just as a debt is dissolved by repayment, a logical obligation dissolves when the premises that created it are withdrawn. This connects to the framework's emphasis on intellectual honesty: willingness to withdraw from a position when its foundations change is a mark of rationality, not weakness.

D: Original: And the same in hypotheses and hypothetical arguments; for it is necessary sometimes to demand the granting of some hypothesis as a kind of passage to the argument which follows. Must we then allow every hypothesis that is proposed, or not allow every one? And if not every one, which should we allow? And if a man has allowed an hypothesis, must he in every case abide by allowing it? or must he sometimes withdraw from it, but admit the consequences and not admit contradictions? Yes; but suppose that a man says, "If you admit the hypothesis of a possibility, I will draw you to an impossibility." With such a person shall a man of sense refuse to enter into a contest, and avoid discussion and conversation with him? But what other man than the man of sense can use argumentation and is skillful in questioning and answering, and incapable of being cheated and deceived by false reasoning? And shall he enter into the contest, and yet not take care whether he shall engage in argument not rashly and not carelessly? And if he does not take care, how can he be such a man as we conceive him to be? But without some such exercise and preparation, can he maintain a continuous and consistent argument? Let them show this; and all these speculations become superfluous, and are absurd and inconsistent with our notion of a good and serious man.
D: Refashioned:
The same applies to hypothetical arguments. Must you accept every hypothesis offered? If not, which ones? Once you have accepted one, must you hold it forever, or can you withdraw when it leads somewhere impossible?

The person of sense must enter these contests, because only the trained mind can argue skillfully and detect deception. But they must enter prepared. Without exercise in these disciplines, you cannot maintain a coherent, consistent argument — and a person who cannot do that does not deserve the title of philosopher.
D: Elegant: Must you accept every hypothesis? Can you withdraw when it leads to impossibility? Only the trained mind can navigate these questions. Without preparation, consistent argument is impossible — and without consistent argument, the title of philosopher is empty.
D: Distilled: Without disciplined training in reasoning, you cannot maintain a coherent argument — and that undermines everything else.
Notes: Hypothetical arguments (hypothesis) were central to Stoic and Academic debate: you grant a premise provisionally and track consequences. Epictetus's concern is practical — poorly trained thinkers either accept everything uncritically or refuse to engage. Both are failures of the rational faculty.

D: Original: Why are we still indolent and negligent and sluggish, and why do we seek pretences for not labouring and not being watchful in cultivating our reason? "If then I shall make a mistake in these matters may I not have killed my father?" Slave, where was there a father in this matter that you could kill him? What, then, have you done? The only fault that was possible here is the fault which you have committed. This is the very remark which I made to Rufus when he blamed me for not having discovered the one thing omitted in a certain syllogism: "I suppose," I said, "that I have burnt the Capitol." "Slave," he replied, "was the thing omitted here the Capitol?" Or are these the only crimes, to burn the Capitol and to kill your father? But for a man to use the appearances resented to him rashly and foolishly and carelessly, not to understand argument, nor demonstration, nor sophism, nor, in a word, to see in questioning and answering what is consistent with that which we have granted or is not consistent; is there no error in this?
D: Refashioned:
Why are we so lazy about this? When someone makes a logical mistake, we shrug: 'It's not as if I burned down the Capitol.' Rufus had the perfect reply: 'Was the Capitol involved? The only error possible here is the one you committed.'

Logical carelessness — using appearances rashly, failing to distinguish valid argument from sophistry, not understanding what follows from what — is a real failure. It may not be dramatic, but it is a failure of the one faculty that makes you human. Treating it as trivial is exactly the kind of indolence that keeps us from genuine progress.
D: Elegant: 'It's not as if I burned the Capitol.' No — but the only error possible was the one you committed. Logical carelessness is not dramatic, but it is a failure of the one faculty that defines you. Treating it as trivial is itself the problem.
D: Distilled: A mistake in reasoning may not be dramatic, but it is a failure of the one faculty that defines you as human.
Notes: The Capitol (Capitolium) — Temple of Jupiter on the Capitoline Hill, Rome's most sacred religious site, burned in 69 CE during civil war. Rufus (Musonius Rufus) again appears as demanding mentor. The passage insists that logical discipline is not separate from moral discipline: sloppy thinking is a moral failing because the rational faculty is the seat of human dignity. This connects to the framework's argument that reason is the meta-capacity on which all others depend.

D: Original: Chapter 8: That the faculties are not safe to the uninstructed

In as many ways as we can change things which are equivalent to one another, in just so many ways we can change the forms of arguments and enthymemes in argumentation. This is an instance: "If you have borrowed and not repaid, you owe me the money: you have not borrowed and you have not repaid; then you do not owe me the money." To do this skillfully is suitable to no man more than to the philosopher; for if the enthymeme is all imperfect syllogism. it is plain that he who has been exercised in the perfect syllogism must be equally expert in the imperfect also.
D: Refashioned: The philosopher who has mastered the complete syllogism is naturally equipped to handle incomplete ones — enthymemes, the abbreviated arguments that dominate ordinary speech. This is like recognizing that equivalent quantities can be expressed in different forms. The skill transfers.
D: Elegant: Master the complete syllogism and you can handle the abbreviated arguments of ordinary speech. The skill transfers from the formal to the practical.
D: Distilled: Mastery of formal reasoning equips you to navigate the informal reasoning that dominates everyday life.
Notes: An enthymeme is a syllogism with one premise implied rather than stated — the standard form of persuasion in rhetoric (Aristotle's Rhetoric treats it as the rhetorical counterpart of the syllogism). Epictetus's point is that formal logic training is practically useful precisely because real-world arguments are almost always incomplete.

D: Original: "Why then do we not exercise ourselves and one another in this manner?" Because, I reply, at present, though we are not exercised in these things and not distracted from the study of morality, by me at least, still we make no progress in virtue. What then must we expect if we should add this occupation? and particularly as this would not only be an occupation which would withdraw us from more necessary things, but would also be a cause of self conceit and arrogance, and no small cause. For great is the power of arguing and the faculty of persuasion, and particularly if it should be much exercised, and also receive additional ornament from language: and so universally, every faculty acquired by the uninstructed and weak brings with it the danger of these persons being elated and inflated by it. For by what means could one persuade a young man who excels in these matters that he ought not to become an appendage to them, but to make them an appendage to himself? Does he not trample on all such reasons, and strut before us elated and inflated, not enduring that any man should reprove him and remind him of what he has neglected and to what he has turned aside?
D: Refashioned:
Then why don't we drill ourselves in these skills constantly? Because even without adding logic training to our routine, we barely make progress in virtue as it is. If we added another preoccupation — especially one that breeds self-congratulation — we would be worse off.

The power of argument and persuasion is considerable. Give it to someone who has not yet mastered themselves, and it becomes a weapon of vanity. The young man who excels at debate begins to see everything else as beneath him. He struts, refuses correction, and forgets what truly matters. Every powerful faculty, in the hands of the uninstructed, becomes a source of arrogance rather than wisdom.
D: Elegant: We barely make progress in virtue as it is. Adding a preoccupation that breeds vanity would make us worse. Every powerful faculty, in the hands of the uninstructed, becomes a source of arrogance rather than wisdom.
D: Distilled: Every powerful faculty, given to someone who has not mastered themselves, becomes a weapon of vanity.
Notes: This passage provides the chapter title: 'faculties are not safe to the uninstructed.' Epictetus is not against logic — he just taught five thoughts in its defense — but he warns against prioritizing rhetorical skill over moral development. The pattern is visible in modern education: analytical brilliance without ethical grounding produces sophistication, not wisdom. This connects to the framework's critique of institutions that develop intellectual capacity without moral formation.

D: Original: "What, then, was not Plato a philosopher?" I reply, "And was not Hippocrates a physician? but you see how Hippocrates speaks." Does Hippocrates, then, speak thus in respect of being a physician? Why do you mingle things which have been accidentally united in the same men? And if Plato was handsome and strong, ought I also to set to work and endeavor to become handsome or strong, as if this was necessary for philosophy, because a certain philosopher was at the same time handsome and a philosopher? Will you not choose to see and to distinguish in respect to what men become philosophers, and what things belong to belong to them in other respects? And if I were a philosopher, ought you also to be made lame? What then? Do I take away these faculties which you possess? By no means; for neither do I take away the faculty of seeing. But if you ask me what is the good of man, I cannot mention to you anything else than that it is a certain disposition of the will with respect to appearances.
D: Refashioned:
'But Plato was a philosopher who also wrote beautifully.' Yes, and Hippocrates was a physician who also spoke eloquently. Does that mean eloquence is medicine? If Plato was handsome and strong, should I train for beauty and strength because a philosopher happened to possess them? These are accidental qualities, not essential ones.

I am not taking away any faculty you possess. But if you ask me what the good of a human being is, I can only give you this answer: a certain disposition of the will with respect to appearances. That is where the work lies.
D: Elegant: Plato wrote beautifully and was also a philosopher. Does that make beautiful writing philosophy? If Plato was handsome, must philosophers be handsome? Separate accidental qualities from essential ones. The good of a human being is one thing: the right disposition of the will.
D: Distilled: Do not confuse what a philosopher happened to be with what philosophy actually is — the right disposition of the will.
Notes: Hippocrates (c. 460-370 BCE): father of Western medicine. Plato (c. 428-348 BCE): whose literary style was unmatched among Greek philosophers. Epictetus's point is about category errors: admiring Plato's prose is not the same as doing philosophy. The 'lame' reference is autobiographical — Epictetus was reportedly lame, possibly from his time as a slave. The good of man as 'disposition of the will with respect to appearances' (prohairesis peri phantasias) is Epictetus's core formula.

D: Original: Chapter 9: How from the fact that we are akin to God a man may proceed to the consequences

If the things are true which are said by the philosophers about the kinship between God and man, what else remains for men to do then what Socrates did? Never in reply to the question, to what country you belong, say that you are an Athenian or a Corinthian, but that you are a citizen of the world. For why do you say that you are an Athenian, and why do you not say that you belong to the small nook only into which your poor body was cast at birth? Is it not plain that you call yourself an Athenian or Corinthian from the place which has a greater authority and comprises not only that small nook itself and all your family, but even the whole country from which the stock of your progenitors is derived down to you? He then who has observed with intelligence the administration of the world, and has learned that the greatest and supreme and the most comprehensive community is that which is composed of men and God, and that from God have descended the seeds not only to my father and grandfather, but to all beings which are generated on the earth and are produced, and particularly to rational beings- for these only are by their nature formed to have communion with God, being by means of reason conjoined with Him- why should not such a man call himself a citizen of the world, why not a son of God, and why should he be afraid of anything which happens among men? Is kinship with Caesar or with any other of the powerful in Rome sufficient to enable us to live in safety, and above contempt and without any fear at all? and to have God for your maker and father and guardian, shall not this release us from sorrows and fears?
D: Refashioned:
If what the philosophers say about our kinship with God is true, the conclusion is Socratic: you are not merely an Athenian or a Corinthian but a citizen of the world. Why identify with the small corner where your body happened to be born when you belong to the largest community of all — the one composed of human beings and God?

If kinship with Caesar is enough to live without fear, how much more should kinship with God be? He who sees the administration of the universe and understands that the seeds of reason descend from the divine into every rational being — this person has no cause for fear. The greatest community is the one connected by reason, and you are a member of it.
D: Elegant: You are not merely a citizen of one city but of the world — the community composed of human beings and God, connected by reason. If kinship with Caesar protects you from fear, kinship with God should do immeasurably more.
D: Distilled: You are not a citizen of one city but of the world — the community of rational beings connected to the divine.
Notes: The cosmopolitan idea — 'citizen of the world' (kosmopolites) — originated with the Cynics (attributed to Diogenes) and was adopted by the Stoics as a core ethical commitment. Socrates in Plato's Apology (28e) speaks of his duty to a post assigned by God. Epictetus extends this: if God assigns your post in the cosmos, local identity is secondary. This maps to the framework's universalist ethics: wisdom is not parochial.

D: Original:
But a man may say, "Whence shall I get bread to eat when I have nothing?"

And how do slaves, and runaways, on what do they rely when they leave their masters? Do they rely on their lands or slaves, or their vessels of silver? They rely on nothing but themselves, and food does not fail them. And shall it be necessary for one among us who is a philosopher to travel into foreign parts, and trust to and rely on others, and not to take care of himself, and shall he be inferior to irrational animals and more cowardly, each of which, being self-sufficient, neither fails to get its proper food, nor to find a suitable way of living, and one conformable to nature?
D: Refashioned: 'But where will I get food if I have nothing?' Even runaway slaves manage to feed themselves — they rely on nothing but their own resourcefulness, and food does not fail them. Shall a philosopher, who claims kinship with God, be less self-sufficient than a fugitive? Less capable than an irrational animal, each of which finds its own food and its own way of living without external help?
D: Elegant: Even runaway slaves manage to feed themselves. Shall a philosopher be less self-sufficient than a fugitive — or less capable than an animal that finds its food without help?
D: Distilled: If even fugitives and animals are self-sufficient, a philosopher who fears hunger has not understood what philosophy teaches.
Notes: Epictetus was himself a former slave — this reference carries personal weight. The comparison to animals echoes Cynic philosophy (Diogenes observed animals to derive ethical principles). The passage challenges the common excuse that philosophical detachment is a luxury for the comfortable: the Stoic claim is that it works precisely when material conditions are worst.

D: Original: I indeed think that the old man ought to be sitting here, not to contrive how you may have no mean thoughts nor mean and ignoble talk about yourselves, but to take care that there be not among us any young men of such a mind that, when they have recognized their kinship to God, and that we are fettered by these bonds, the body, I mean, and its possessions, and whatever else on account of them is necessary to us for the economy and commerce of life, they should intend to throw off these things as if they were burdens painful and intolerable, and to depart to their kinsmen. But this is the labour that your teacher and instructor ought to be employed upon, if he really were what he should be. You should come to him and say, "Epictetus, we can no longer endure being bound to this poor body, and feeding it and giving it drink, and rest, and cleaning it, and for the sake of the body complying with the wishes of these and of those. Are not these things indifferent and nothing to us, and is not death no evil? And are we not in a manner kinsmen of God, and did we not come from Him? Allow us to depart to the place from which we came; allow us to be released at last from these bonds by which we are bound and weighed down. Here there are robbers and thieves and courts of justice, and those who are named tyrants, and think that they have some power over us by means of the body and its possessions. Permit us to show them that they have no power over any man." And I on my part would say, "Friends, wait for God; when He shall give the signal and release you from this service, then go to Him; but for the present endure to dwell in this place where He has put you: short indeed is this time of your dwelling here, and easy to bear for those who are so disposed: for what tyrant or what thief, or what courts of justice, are formidable to those who have thus considered as things of no value the body and the possessions of the body? Wait then, do not depart without a reason."
D: Refashioned:
The teacher's real job is not to teach contempt for the body but to prevent students from taking Stoic doctrine as a license for suicide. When young men recognize their kinship with God and then declare: 'We cannot endure being chained to this body — let us return to our divine origin' — the teacher must answer: 'Wait for God. When he gives the signal, then go. For now, endure your posting.'

This life, however burdensome, is short and bearable for those who are properly trained. What tyrant, what thief, what court has power over someone who has already decided that the body and its possessions do not matter? Wait. Do not depart without a reason.
D: Elegant: The teacher's real job is not to preach contempt for the body but to prevent students from using Stoic doctrine as a license for escape. Wait for God's signal. Your posting is short and bearable. Do not depart without a reason.
D: Distilled: Stoic detachment is not a license for escape — wait for the signal, and endure your posting.
Notes: The anti-suicide passage is crucial for understanding Stoic ethics: despite teaching that death is 'indifferent,' the Stoics did not endorse voluntary death except in extreme circumstances (Cato, Seneca). Epictetus uses military language — 'posting' (taxis) — to frame life as a duty station assigned by God. Departure without orders is desertion, not liberation. This connects to the framework's concept of Right: commitment to purpose, even when the purpose is difficult.

D: Original: Something like this ought to be said by the teacher to ingenuous youths. But now what happens? The teacher is a lifeless body, and you are lifeless bodies. When you have been well filled to-day, you sit down and lament about the morrow, how you shall get something to eat. Wretch, if you have it, you will have it; if you have it not, you will depart from life. The door is open. Why do you grieve? where does there remain any room for tears? and where is there occasion for flattery? why shall one man envy another? why should a man admire the rich or the powerful, even if they be both very strong and of violent temper? for what will they do to us? We shall not care for that which they can do; and what we do care for, that they cannot do. How did Socrates behave with respect to these matters? Why, in what other way than a man ought to do who was convinced that he was a kinsman of the gods? "If you say to me now," said Socrates to his judges, "'We will acquit you on the condition that you no longer discourse in the way in which you have hitherto discoursed, nor trouble either our young or our old men,' I shall answer, 'you make yourselves ridiculous by thinking that, if one of our commanders has appointed me to a certain post, it is my duty to keep and maintain it, and to resolve to die a thousand times rather than desert it; but if God has put us in any place and way of life, we ought to desert it.'" Socrates speaks like a man who is really a kinsman of the gods. But we think about ourselves as if we were only stomachs, and intestines, and shameful parts; we fear, we desire; we flatter those who are able to help us in these matters, and we fear them also.
D: Refashioned:
What should be happening here is this: the teacher should be alive, the students should be alive, and the teaching should produce something. Instead, the teacher is a lifeless body lecturing lifeless bodies. You eat your fill today and then worry about tomorrow. If you have food tomorrow, you will have it. If not — the door is open. Why grieve? Why flatter? Why envy the rich and powerful when they can do nothing to what truly matters?

Socrates told his judges: if you think a man should abandon the post God placed him in because you threaten him with death, you are ridiculous. We agree with Socrates in theory, then live as though we are nothing but stomachs and appetites — fearing, desiring, flattering anyone who has power over our bodies.
D: Elegant: The teacher is a corpse, the students are corpses, and the teaching produces nothing. You eat today and fret about tomorrow. The door is open — why grieve? Socrates told his judges: you are ridiculous if you think I will abandon the post God placed me in. We agree in theory, then live as stomachs.
D: Distilled: We agree with Socrates in theory and then live as though we are nothing but stomachs and appetites.
Notes: Socrates's defense at his trial (Plato's Apology, 28d-29a): he compares his philosophical mission to a military posting assigned by God, which he cannot abandon under threat of death. 'The door is open' — a recurring Stoic metaphor for death as an exit always available, meaning that continued life is always a choice. Epictetus's critique of corpse-like teaching connects to the framework's argument about educational institutions: transmission without transformation is mere performance.

D: Original: A man asked me to write to Rome about him, a man who, as most people thought, had been unfortunate, for formerly he was a man of rank and rich, but had been stripped of all, and was living here. I wrote on his behalf in a submissive manner; but when he had read the letter, he gave it back to me and said, "I wished for your help, not your pity: no evil has happened to me."
D: Refashioned: A man who had lost his rank and wealth asked Epictetus to write a letter on his behalf to Rome. Epictetus wrote it in a submissive, pitying tone. The man read it and handed it back: 'I wanted your help, not your pity. No evil has happened to me.'
D: Elegant: Asked to write a letter for a man who had lost everything, Epictetus wrote with pity. The man returned it: 'I wanted help, not pity. No evil has happened to me.'
D: Distilled: The person who has truly internalized Stoic teaching rejects pity — because they do not experience loss of externals as evil.
Notes: This brief anecdote demonstrates the teaching in action: the man's response shows he has absorbed the Stoic distinction between external loss and genuine harm. His correction of Epictetus is itself a philosophical act. The passage illustrates the framework's concept of integrated wisdom — the difference between understanding a principle and living from it.

D: Original: Thus also Musonius Rufus, in order to try me, used to say: "This and this will befall you from your master"; and I replied that these were things which happen in the ordinary course of human affairs. "Why, then," said he, "should I ask him for anything when I can obtain it from you?" For, in fact, what a man has from himself, it is superfluous and foolish to receive from another? Shall I, then, who am able to receive from myself greatness of soul and a generous spirit, receive from you land and money or a magisterial office? I hope not: I will not be so ignorant about my own possessions. But when a man is cowardly and mean, what else must be done for him than to write letters as you would about a corpse. "Please to grant us the body of a certain person and a sextarius of poor blood." For such a person is, in fact, a carcass and a sextarius of blood, and nothing more. But if he were anything more, he would know that one man is not miserable through the means of another.
D: Refashioned:
Musonius Rufus once tested Epictetus by warning: 'This and that will happen to you from your master.' Epictetus replied: these are ordinary things. 'Then why should I petition your master for anything,' Rufus said, 'when I can get it from you directly?'

The point is this: what a person can provide for themselves — greatness of soul, generosity of spirit — it is foolish and superfluous to seek from another. If you can give yourself these things, why beg for land, money, or office? But the person who cannot provide for themselves is, in practical terms, a corpse. You would write letters on their behalf the way you would request the return of a dead body: 'Please grant us the remains of this person and a pint of blood.'
D: Elegant: What you can give yourself — greatness of soul, generous spirit — it is foolish to beg from another. Rufus tested Epictetus: 'Why petition your master when I can get it from you?' The person who cannot provide for themselves is a corpse. You petition for them the way you request a body's return.
D: Distilled: What you can give yourself — greatness of soul — it is foolish and degrading to seek from anyone else.
Notes: Musonius Rufus (c. 30-101 CE) testing his student Epictetus — both were subject to others' power (Rufus was exiled, Epictetus was enslaved). The 'sextarius of blood' is deliberately degrading: reducing a person to their material components. This passage shows the Stoic hierarchy: internal goods are the only real goods; external goods received from others are mere supplements. The framework parallel: wisdom is self-sourced, not institutional.

D: Original: Chapter 10: Against those who eagerly seek preferment at Rome

If we applied ourselves as busily to our own work as the old men at Rome do to those matters about which they are employed, perhaps we also might accomplish something. I am acquainted with a man older than myself who is now superintendent of corn at Rome, and remember the time when he came here on his way back from exile, and what he said as he related the events of his former life, and how he declared that with respect to the future after his return he would look after nothing else than passing the rest of his life in quiet and tranquillity. "For how little of life," he said, remains for me." I replied, "You will not do it, but as soon as you smell Rome, you will forget all that you have said; and if admission is allowed even into the imperial palace, you will gladly thrust yourself in and thank God." "If you find me, Epictetus," he answered, "setting even one foot within the palace, think what you please." Well, what then did he do? Before he entered the city he was met by letters from Caesar, and as soon as he received them he forgot all, and ever after has added one piece of business to another. I wish that I were now by his side to remind him of what he said when he was passing this way and to tell him how much better a seer I am than he is.
D: Refashioned:
If we applied ourselves to philosophy with the same energy that Roman politicians apply to their careers, we might accomplish something.

A man returning from exile passed through Nicopolis and told Epictetus that once he returned to Rome, he would live in quiet and tranquility. 'How little of life remains for me,' he said. Epictetus replied: 'You won't do it. The moment you smell Rome and the palace admits you, you will thrust yourself in and thank God for the opportunity.' The man denied it. But before he even reached the city, letters arrived from Caesar — and from that moment on, he piled one appointment upon another. Epictetus wished he could be there to remind the man of his resolution, and to note what a better prophet he had been.
D: Elegant: A man returning from exile swore to live in quiet. Epictetus predicted: the moment you smell Rome, you will forget. Before the man reached the city, letters from Caesar arrived — and he never stopped accumulating.
D: Distilled: We all promise ourselves tranquility until ambition calls — and then we forget everything we resolved.
Notes: The unnamed man is likely a historical figure known to Epictetus's audience. The 'superintendent of corn' (praefectus annonae) was one of the most powerful positions in Rome, controlling the grain supply. Nicopolis — the city in Epirus where Epictetus established his school after being expelled from Rome by Domitian's edict against philosophers (c. 89-93 CE). The passage illustrates a recurrent Stoic warning: insight without discipline dissolves on contact with opportunity.

D: Original: Well, then, do I say that man is an animal made for doing nothing? Certainly not. But why are we not active? For example, as to myself, as soon as day comes, in a few words I remind myself of what I must read over to my pupils; then forthwith I say to myself, "But what is it to me how a certain person shall read? the first thing for me is to sleep." And indeed what resemblance is there between what other persons do and what we do? If you observe what they do, you will understand. And what else do they do all day long than make up accounts, inquire among themselves, give and take advice about some small quantity of grain, a bit of land, and such kind of profits? Is it then the same thing to receive a petition and to read in it: "I entreat you to permit me to export a small quantity of corn"; and one to this effect: "I entreat you to learn from Chrysippus what is the administration of the world, and what place in it the rational animal holds; consider also who you are, and what is the nature of your good and bad." Are these things like the other, do they require equal care, and is it equally base to neglect these and those? Well, then, are we the only persons who are lazy and love sleep? No; but much rather you young men are. For we old men, when we see young men amusing themselves, are eager to play with them; and if I saw you active and zealous, much more should I be eager myself to join you in your serious pursuits.
D: Refashioned:
Am I saying that humans are made for idleness? Not at all. As soon as day comes, I review what I must teach my students. But the difference between our work and theirs is this: they spend their days on grain exports, land deals, and petty profits. The petition that crosses their desk reads: 'Permit me to export a small quantity of corn.' The petition that should concern us reads: 'Learn from Chrysippus what the administration of the world is, and what place the rational animal holds in it. Consider who you are.'

These are not equivalent tasks, and they do not require equal care in the same sense. But if anyone thinks that we who pursue the second kind of work are lazy — I only wish they could see how eager I would be if my students were truly on fire for the things that matter.
D: Elegant: Their petition reads: 'Permit me to export corn.' Ours reads: 'Learn who you are and what place you hold in the order of things.' We are not idle — we are occupied with different work. And I would burn with eagerness if my students burned for the things that matter.
D: Distilled: We are not idle — we are occupied with the only work that matters: understanding who we are and what we should do.
Notes: The contrast between bureaucratic and philosophical work reflects Epictetus's audience: many students came from families involved in Roman administration. The grain petition is not trivial — the annona (grain supply) was essential to Rome's stability — but Epictetus insists it is secondary to self-knowledge. This connects to the framework's hierarchy of concerns: material infrastructure serves human flourishing, not the reverse.

D: Original: Chapter 11: Of natural affection

When he was visited by one of the magistrates, Epictetus inquired of him about several particulars, and asked if he had children and a wife. The man replied that he had; and Epictetus inquired further, how he felt under the circumstances. "Miserable," the man said. Then Epictetus asked, "In what respect," for men do not marry and beget children in order to be wretched, but rather to be happy. "But I," the man replied, "am so wretched about my children that lately, when my little daughter was sick and was supposed to be in danger, I could not endure to stay with her, but I left home till a person sent me news that she had recovered." Well then, said Epictetus, do you think that you acted right? "I acted naturally," the man replied. But convince me of this that you acted naturally, and I will convince you that everything which takes place according to nature takes place rightly. "This is the case," said the man, "with all or at least most fathers." I do not deny that: but the matter about which we are inquiring is whether such behavior is right; for in respect to this matter we must say that tumours also come for the good of the body, because they do come; and generally we must say that to do wrong is natural, because nearly all or at least most of us do wrong. Do you show me then how your behavior is natural. "I cannot," he said; "but do you rather show me how it is not according to nature and is not rightly done.
D: Refashioned:
A magistrate told Epictetus he had a wife and children, and that family life made him miserable. When his young daughter fell ill and seemed in danger, he could not bear to stay — he fled the house and waited elsewhere for news of her recovery.

Epictetus asked: do you think you acted rightly? 'I acted naturally,' the man said. 'Most fathers would do the same.' Epictetus replied: I am not asking whether it is common — tumors are common too, but we do not call them good for the body. The fact that most people do something does not make it right. Show me that fleeing your sick child is natural in the sense of being rational and good — not merely in the sense of being widespread.
D: Elegant: A father fled his sick daughter's bedside and called it natural. Epictetus replied: tumors are natural too. Common is not the same as right. Show me that your flight was rational, not merely widespread.
D: Distilled: The fact that most people do something does not make it right — common is not the same as natural.
Notes: This chapter demonstrates Epictetus's Socratic method in action: he does not lecture but leads the man through his own contradictions. The distinction between 'natural' (kata phusin) in the sense of 'common' versus 'natural' in the sense of 'aligned with rational nature' is fundamental to Stoic ethics. This connects to the framework's emphasis on Reality: understanding what is true about human nature requires distinguishing between what humans commonly do and what human nature is designed for.

D: Original: Well, said Epictetus, if we were inquiring about white and black, what criterion should we employ for distinguishing between them? "The sight," he said. And if about hot and cold, and hard and soft, what criterion? "The touch." Well then, since we are inquiring about things which are according to nature, and those which are done rightly or not rightly, what kind of criterion do you think that we should employ? "I do not know," he said. And yet not to know the criterion of colors and smells, and also of tastes, is perhaps no great harm; but if a man do not know the criterion of good and bad, and of things according to nature and contrary to nature, does this seem to you a small harm? "The greatest harm." Come tell me, do all things which seem to some persons to be good and becoming rightly appear such; and at present as to Jews and Syrians and Egyptians and Romans, is it possible that the opinions of all of them in respect to food are right? "How is it possible?" he said. Well, I suppose it is absolutely necessary that, if the opinions of the Egyptians are right, the opinions of the rest must be wrong: if the opinions of the Jews are right, those of the rest cannot be right. "Certainly." But where there is ignorance, there also there is want of learning and training in things which are necessary. He assented to this. You then, said Epictetus, since you know this, for the future will employ yourself seriously about nothing else, and will apply your mind to nothing else than to learn the criterion of things which are according to nature, and by using it also to determine each several thing.
D: Refashioned:
If we were debating colors, we would use sight as our criterion. For temperature, touch. But what criterion do we use for distinguishing what is natural and right from what is merely common?

'I don't know,' the man admitted. And not knowing the criterion for good and bad is not a minor gap like not knowing how to distinguish smells — it is the greatest possible deficiency.

Consider: Jews, Syrians, Egyptians, and Romans all disagree about food. They cannot all be right. Where there is disagreement, there is ignorance; where there is ignorance, there is need for training. Once you see this, you will devote yourself to nothing else but learning the criterion by which to judge what is truly according to nature.
D: Elegant: What criterion distinguishes right from merely common? If you don't know, that is not a minor gap — it is the greatest deficiency. Jews, Syrians, Egyptians, Romans all disagree about food. They cannot all be right. Where there is disagreement, there is need for training.
D: Distilled: Not knowing the criterion for distinguishing right from wrong is not a minor gap — it is the greatest possible deficiency.
Notes: The criterion (kanon) problem is central to Hellenistic philosophy — the Stoics, Epicureans, and Skeptics all proposed different answers. Epictetus uses cultural disagreement about dietary laws to show that 'what feels natural' varies and therefore cannot be the standard. The mention of Jews, Syrians, Egyptians, and Romans reflects the multicultural character of the Roman Empire. This connects to the framework's universalist approach: true ethical standards must transcend cultural convention.

D: Original: But in the present matter I have so much as this to aid you toward what you wish. Does affection to those of your family appear to you to be according to nature and to be good? "Certainly." Well, is such affection natural and good, and is a thing consistent with reason not good? "By no means." Is then that which is consistent with reason in contradiction with affection? "I think not." You are right, for if it is otherwise, it is necessary that one of the contradictions being according to nature, the other must be contrary to nature. Is it not so? "It is," he said. Whatever, then, we shall discover to be at the same time affectionate and also consistent with reason, this we confidently declare to be right and good. "Agreed." Well then to leave your sick child and to go away is not reasonable, and I suppose that you will not say that it is; but it remains for us to inquire if it is consistent with affection. "Yes, let us consider." Did you, then, since you had an affectionate disposition to your child, do right when you ran off and left her; and has the mother no affection for the child? "Certainly, she has." Ought, then, the mother also to have left her, or ought she not? "She ought not." And the nurse, does she love her? "She does." Ought, then, she also to have left her? "By no means." And the pedagogue, does he not love her? "He does love her." Ought, then, he also to have deserted her? and so should the child have been left alone and without help on account of the great affection of you, the parents, and of those about her, or should she have died in the hands of those who neither loved her nor cared for her? "Certainly not." Now this is unfair and unreasonable, not to allow those who have equal affection with yourself to do what you think to be proper for yourself to do because you have affection. It is absurd. Come then, if you were sick, would you wish your relations to be so affectionate, and all the rest, children and wife, as to leave you alone and deserted? "By no means." And would you wish to be so loved by your own that through their excessive affection you would always be left alone in sickness? or for this reason would you rather pray, if it were possible, to be loved by your enemies and deserted by them? But if this is so, it results that your behavior was not at all an affectionate act.
D: Refashioned:
You say your affection for your daughter is natural and good. Agreed. You also agree that what is consistent with reason is good, and that reason and affection cannot truly contradict each other.

Then consider: was leaving your sick child reasonable? If running away was the right expression of affection, then the mother should have run too, and the nurse, and the tutor — and the child would have died surrounded by no one. That is absurd. You would not want your own family to express their love for you by abandoning you when you were sick. If the logical consequence of your 'affection' is that the person you love dies alone, it was not affection at all.
D: Elegant: If fleeing your sick child was the right expression of love, then her mother, nurse, and tutor should have fled too — and she would have died alone. You would not want to be loved that way. What you called affection was something else.
D: Distilled: If the logical consequence of your love is that the person you love dies alone, it was not love — it was something else.
Notes: Epictetus uses logical extension (reductio ad absurdum) to expose the father's self-deception: if his behavior were universalized, the child would be abandoned. This is structurally similar to Kant's universalizability test, though Epictetus arrives at it through Stoic reasoning rather than deontological ethics. The passage connects to the framework's insistence that emotional claims must survive rational examination.

D: Original: Well then, was it nothing which moved you and induced you to desert your child? and how is that possible? But it might be something of the kind which moved a man at Rome to wrap up his head while a horse was running which he favoured; and when contrary to expectation the horse won, he required sponges to recover from his fainting fit. What then is the thing which moved? The exact discussion of this does not belong to the present occasion perhaps; but it is enough to be convinced of this, if what the philosophers say is true, that we must not look for it anywhere without, but in all cases it is one and the same thing which is the cause of our doing or not doing something, of saying or not saying something, of being elated or depressed, of avoiding anything or pursuing: the very thing which is now the cause to me and to you, to you of coming to me and sitting and hearing, and to me of saying what I do say. And what is this? Is it any other than our will to do so? "No other." But if we had willed otherwise, what else should we have been doing than that which we willed to do? This, then, was the cause of Achilles' lamentation, not the death of Patroclus; for another man does not behave thus on the death of his companion; but it was because he chose to do so. And to you this was the very cause of your then running away, that you chose to do so; and on the other side, if you should stay with her, the reason will be the same. And now you are going to Rome because you choose; and if you should change your mind, you will not go thither. And in a word, neither death nor exile nor pain nor anything of the kind is the cause of our doing anything or not doing; but our own opinions and our wills.
D: Refashioned:
What actually caused you to run? Not your daughter's illness — another father facing the same situation would have stayed. The cause was your own judgment, your own will. This is always the case. Achilles did not lament because Patroclus died — others lost companions without lamenting. He lamented because he chose to.

In every situation, the cause of what we do or fail to do is not the external event but our own opinion about it. The same event produces opposite responses in different people, which proves the event itself is not the cause. Your will brought you here today; your will could take you to Rome tomorrow. Nothing external forces anything — it is always the will.
D: Elegant: Achilles did not lament because Patroclus died — others lost companions and did not lament. He lamented because he chose to. The cause is never the event. It is always the will — your opinion about the event, which you have the power to change.
D: Distilled: The cause of what you do is never the external event — it is always your own judgment about it.
Notes: Achilles and Patroclus — Iliad, Books 18-19. Achilles's grief is excessive even by Homeric standards, producing his devastating re-entry into battle. Epictetus reads this as a case study in prohairesis (will/choice): the grief was not caused by the death but by Achilles's judgment about the death. This is the Stoic psychological model in miniature: events are indifferent; our responses to them are the only things under our control.

D: Original: Do I convince you of this or not? "You do convince me." Such, then, as the causes are in each case, such also are the effects. When, then, we are doing anything not rightly, from this day we shall impute it to nothing else than to the will from which we have done it: and it is that which we shall endeavour to take away and to extirpate more than the tumours and abscesses out of the body. And in like manner we shall give the same account of the cause of the things which we do right; and we shall no longer allege as causes of any evil to us, either slave or neighbour, or wife or children, being persuaded that, if we do not think things to he what we do think them to be, we do not the acts which follow from such opinions; and as to thinking or not thinking, that is in our power and not in externals. "It is so," he said. From this day then we shall inquire into and examine nothing else, what its quality is, or its state, neither land nor slaves nor horses nor dogs, nothing else than opinions. "I hope so." You see, then, that you must become a Scholasticus, an animal whom all ridicule, if you really intend to make an examination of your own opinions: and that this is not the work of one hour or day, you know yourself.
D: Refashioned:
If this convinces you, then from today attribute everything you do — right or wrong — to your own will and nothing else. Your will is what we must work on, the way a surgeon works on tumors: identify it, examine it, and where it is malformed, cut it out.

We will no longer blame slaves, neighbors, wives, or children. We will examine opinions — and nothing else. But understand: this makes you a scholasticus, and everyone will mock you for it. The work of examining your own judgments is not glamorous, and it is not the work of one hour or one day. You know this yourself.
D: Elegant: From today, blame nothing external. Examine only opinions — your own. This makes you a scholasticus, and everyone will mock you. The work is not glamorous, not quick, and not easy. You know this.
D: Distilled: From today, attribute everything to your own will and examine nothing but your own opinions.
Notes: Scholasticus — originally a student or devotee of a school, by Epictetus's time the term carried overtones of being impractical or ridiculous. Epictetus deliberately embraces the mockery: the pursuit of self-knowledge will always look foolish to those who have not begun it. The chapter closes the dialogue: from the father's initial claim of 'natural' behavior through logical examination to the commitment to examine one's own judgments. This connects to the framework's concept of paideia: genuine education always involves discomfort and social cost.

D: Original: Chapter 12: Of contentment

With respect to gods, there are some who say that a divine being does not exist: others say that it exists, but is inactive and careless, and takes no forethought about anything; a third class say that such a being exists and exercises forethought, but only about great things and heavenly things, and about nothing on the earth; a fourth class say that a divine being exercises forethought both about things on the earth and heavenly things, but in a general way only, and not about things severally. There is a fifth class to whom Ulysses and Socrates belong, who say: "I move not without thy knowledge."
D: Refashioned:
There are five positions on the divine. First: God does not exist. Second: God exists but is inactive and indifferent. Third: God exists and cares about cosmic matters — stars, planets — but not about anything on earth. Fourth: God cares about earthly things in general but not about individuals. Fifth: God oversees everything, including the particular life of each person.

Socrates and Odysseus belong to the fifth category. Odysseus said: 'I move not without your knowledge.' That is the starting point for contentment.
D: Elegant: Five positions on God: nonexistent, indifferent, cosmic only, generally attentive, or fully present in each life. Socrates and Odysseus belong to the last. Start there.
D: Distilled: Contentment begins with the conviction that the divine oversees not just the cosmos but your particular life.
Notes: The five-position taxonomy is Epictetus's own systematic overview of ancient theology. The Odysseus quotation is from the Iliad (10.278-279), spoken before a night raid — Odysseus asking Athena to watch over him. Socrates's claim of divine oversight runs throughout Plato's dialogues (Apology, Phaedo). The framework does not require theological commitment but uses a structurally similar insight: meaning requires the conviction that your particular choices matter, not just the grand pattern.

D: Original: Before all other things, then, it is necessary to inquire about each of these opinions, whether it is affirmed truly or not truly. For if there are no gods, how is it our proper end to follow them? And if they exist, but take no care of anything, in this case also how will it be right to follow them? But if indeed they do exist and look after things, still if there is nothing communicated from them to men, nor in fact to myself, how even so is it right? The wise and good man, then, after considering all these things, submits his own mind to him who administers the whole, as good citizens do to the law of the state. He who is receiving instruction ought to come to the instructed with this intention: How shall I follow the gods in all things, how shall I be contented with the divine administration, and how can I become free?" For he is free to whom everything happens according, to his will, and whom no man can hinder. "What then, is freedom madness?" Certainly not: for madness and freedom do not consist. "But," you say, "I would have everything result just as I like, and in whatever way I like." You are mad, you are beside yourself.
D: Refashioned:
Each of these positions has consequences. If there are no gods, or if they do not care, following them makes no sense. If they exist and care but communicate nothing to us, there is still no basis for guidance. Only if the divine exists, cares, and communicates — at least through the structure of nature and reason — does the philosophical life make sense.

The wise person, having considered all this, submits their mind to the administration of the whole, the way a good citizen submits to the law. The student should come with one question: how can I follow the gods, accept what happens, and become free? Freedom means that everything happens according to your will. But that does not mean bending reality to your wishes — that is madness, not freedom.
D: Elegant: The wise person submits to the whole as a citizen submits to law. Freedom means that everything happens according to your will — but that does not mean bending reality to your wishes. That is madness. Freedom means willing what actually happens.
D: Distilled: Freedom is not getting what you want — it is wanting what you get, because you have aligned your will with reality.
Notes: The 'good citizen' analogy connects Stoic theology to political philosophy: submitting to divine administration is not passive resignation but active participation in the rational order. The distinction between 'everything happening as I wish' (freedom) and 'wishing everything to happen as I please' (madness) is one of Epictetus's sharpest formulations. This connects to the framework's Reality pillar: alignment with what is actually true is the prerequisite for effective action.

D: Original: Do you not know that freedom is a noble and valuable thing? But for me inconsiderately to wish for things to happen as I inconsiderately like, this appears to be not only not noble, but even most base. For how do we proceed in the matter of writing? Do I wish to write the name of Dion as I choose? No, but I am taught to choose to write it as it ought to be written. And how with respect to music? In the same manner. And what universally in every art or science? Just the same. If it were not so, it would be of no value to know anything, if knowledge were adapted to every man's whim. Is it, then, in this alone, in this which is the greatest and the chief thing, I mean freedom, that I am permitted to will inconsiderately? By no means; but to be instructed is this, to learn to wish that everything may happen as it does. And how do things happen? As the disposer has disposed them? And he has appointed summer and winter, and abundance and scarcity, and virtue and vice, and all such opposites for the harmony of the whole; and to each of us he has given a body, and parts of the body, and possessions, and companions.
D: Refashioned:
Freedom is not wanting things to go your way. That is the most base form of desire. When learning to write, you do not write however you please — you learn the correct forms. When learning music, the same. In every art, freedom comes through mastering the discipline, not through ignoring it.

Why should the greatest art — the art of living — be the one exception where untrained impulse counts as freedom? To be instructed in life means learning to will that things happen as they do. The disposer has arranged summer and winter, abundance and scarcity, virtue and vice, all for the harmony of the whole. Each of us has been given a body, circumstances, and companions. Learning to work with what is given — that is freedom.
D: Elegant: In writing, you learn correct forms. In music, you learn the discipline. Why should the art of living be the one exception where raw impulse counts as freedom? True freedom is learning to will what actually happens — working with what is given, not demanding otherwise.
D: Distilled: In every art, freedom comes through mastering the discipline — the art of living is no exception.
Notes: The writing/music analogy echoes Epictetus's consistent strategy of comparing philosophy to technical skills: both require training, both involve submission to standards beyond personal preference. The cosmological claim — that the disposer has arranged opposites 'for the harmony of the whole' — reflects the Stoic doctrine of cosmic sympathy (sympatheia), where apparent evils serve the rational order of the cosmos.

D: Original: Remembering, then, this disposition of things we ought to go to be instructed, not that we may change the constitution of things- for we have not the power to do it, nor is it better that we should have the power-but in order that, as the things around us are what they are and by nature exist, we may maintain our minds in harmony with them things which happen. For can we escape from men? and how is it possible? And if we associate with them, can we chance them? Who gives us the power? What then remains, or what method is discovered of holding commerce with them? Is there such a method by which they shall do what seems fit to them, and we not the less shall be in a mood which is conformable to nature? But you are unwilling to endure and are discontented: and if you are alone, you call it solitude; and of you are with men, you call them knaves and robbers; and you find fault with your own parents and children, and brothers and neighbours. But you ought when you are alone to call this condition by the name of tranquillity and freedom, and to think yourself like to the gods; and when you are with many, you ought not to call it crowd, nor trouble, nor uneasiness, but festival and assembly, and so accept all contentedly.
D: Refashioned:
We go to be instructed not to change the constitution of things — we do not have that power, and it would not be better if we did — but to maintain our minds in harmony with things as they are.

Can we escape from other people? No. Can we change them? No. What remains is finding a way to live among them while staying true to our own nature. When you are alone, do not call it loneliness — call it tranquility and freedom. When you are with others, do not call it a crowd — call it a festival. Accept everything with contentment, instead of being miserable in solitude and resentful in company.
D: Elegant: We cannot change the world or escape from people. What remains is this: when alone, call it tranquility. When with others, call it a festival. Accept what is given — instead of being miserable alone and resentful in company.
D: Distilled: When alone, call it tranquility; when with others, call it a festival — the difference is in your judgment, not your circumstances.
Notes: The reframing technique — solitude as tranquility, company as festival — is a practical application of the Stoic doctrine that our experience is determined by judgment (dogma), not by events. This is not denial but deliberate cognitive reframing. The passage connects to the framework's emphasis on the power of interpretation: how we name our circumstances shapes how we experience them.

D: Original: What, then, is the punishment of those who do not accept? It is to be what they are. Is any person dissatisfied with being alone, let him be alone. Is a man dissatisfied with his parents? let him be a bad son, and lament. Is he dissatisfied with his children? let him be a bad father. "Cast him into prison." What prison? Where he is already, for he is there against his will; and where a man is against his will, there he is in prison. So Socrates was not in prison, for he was there willingly. "Must my leg then be lamed?" Wretch, do you then on account of one poor leg find fault with the world? Will you not willingly surrender it for the whole? Will you not withdraw from it? Will you not gladly part with it to him who gave it? And will you be vexed and discontented with the things established by Zeus, which he with the Moirae who were present and spinning the thread of your generation, defined and put in order? Know you not how small a part you are compared with the whole. I mean with respect to the body, for as to intelligence you are not inferior to the gods nor less; for the magnitude of intelligence is not measured by length nor yet by height, but by thoughts.
D: Refashioned:
What is the punishment for those who refuse to accept? Simply this: they remain as they are. Dissatisfied with being alone? Then be alone and miserable. Dissatisfied with your parents? Then be a bad child and lament. Dissatisfied with your children? Then be a bad parent. The punishment for discontent is the discontent itself.

'Cast him into prison.' He is already in prison — anyone who is somewhere against their will is in prison. Socrates was not imprisoned, because he was there willingly.

'My leg is lame.' Will you fault the entire cosmos for one leg? Will you not surrender it gladly in exchange for the whole? You are a small thing compared to the universe in body — but in intelligence, you are not less than the gods. The magnitude of mind is not measured by height or length but by the quality of its thoughts.
D: Elegant: The punishment for refusing to accept is simply remaining as you are — miserable by your own judgment. Socrates was not in prison because he was there willingly. Your body is small compared to the cosmos, but your mind is not less than the gods. Intelligence is measured by thoughts, not dimensions.
D: Distilled: The punishment for refusing to accept your circumstances is the discontent itself — you imprison yourself.
Notes: Socrates's willingness to remain in prison (Plato's Crito, 44b-54e) despite opportunities to escape is Epictetus's archetypal example of freedom as inner disposition rather than external condition. The lame leg reference is likely autobiographical — Epictetus was reportedly lame. The Moirae (Fates) who spin the thread of life are the three goddesses Clotho, Lachesis, and Atropos. The claim that intelligence is measured by thoughts, not physical dimensions, connects to the framework's concept of the ideational domain as the highest form of power.

D: Original: Will you not, then, choose to place your good in that in which you are equal to the gods? "Wretch that I am to have such a father and mother." What, then, was it permitted to you to come forth, and to select, and to say: "Let such a man at this moment unite with such a woman that I may be produced?" It was not permitted, but it was a necessity for your parents to exist first, and then for you to be begotten. Of what kind of parents? Of such as they were. Well then, since they are such as they are, is there no remedy given to you? Now if you did not know for what purpose you possess the faculty of vision, you would be unfortunate and wretched if you closed your eyes when colors were brought before them; but in that you possess greatness of soul and nobility of spirit for every event that may happen, and you know not that you possess them, are you not more unfortunate and wretched? Things are brought close to you which are proportionate to the power which you possess, but you turn away this power most particularly at the very time when you ought to maintain it open and discerning. Do you not rather thank the gods that they have allowed you to be above these things which they have not placed in your power; and have made you accountable only for those which are in your power? As to your parents, the gods have left you free from responsibility; and so with respect to your brothers, and your body, and possessions, and death and life. For what, then, have they made you responsible? For that which alone is in your power, the proper use of appearances. Why then do you draw on yourself the things for which you are not responsible? It is, indeed, a giving of trouble to yourself.
D: Refashioned:
Will you not place your good where you are equal to the gods? 'But I did not choose my parents.' No — you did not get to select your birth any more than you chose your body. But these things were never under your control, and you were never made accountable for them.

You possess faculties proportionate to every difficulty — greatness of soul, nobility of spirit — and you do not even know you have them. Things come at you and you close the very powers that could meet them. Thank the gods: they placed you above the things not in your power and made you accountable only for the one thing that is — the proper use of appearances. Why do you drag in everything else?
D: Elegant: You did not choose your parents, your body, or your circumstances. But you were never made accountable for those. You are accountable for one thing: the proper use of appearances. You have faculties equal to every difficulty — and you do not even know you possess them.
D: Distilled: You are accountable for one thing only — the proper use of appearances — and you have been given every faculty needed to fulfill it.
Notes: The chapter's closing argument returns to the core Stoic formula: we are responsible only for prohairesis (the proper use of appearances). The 'faculty of vision' metaphor — possessing eyes but closing them when colors appear — captures the tragedy of unused capacity. This connects to the framework's concept of wisdom as the active exercise of faculties we already possess, not the acquisition of new ones.

D: Original: Chapter 13: How everything may he done acceptably to the gods

When some one asked, how may a man eat acceptably to the gods, he answered: If he can eat justly and contentedly, and with equanimity, and temperately and orderly, will it not be also acceptably to the gods? But when you have asked for warm water and the slave has not heard, or if he did hear has brought only tepid water, or he is not even found to be in the house, then not to be vexed or to burst with passion, is not this acceptable to the gods? "How then shall a man endure such persons as this slave?" Slave yourself, will you not bear with your own brother, who has Zeus for his progenitor, and is like a son from the same seeds and of the same descent from above? But if you have been put in any such higher place, will you immediately make yourself a tyrant? Will you not remember who you are, and whom you rule? that they are kinsmen, that they are brethren by nature, that they are the offspring of Zeus? "But I have purchased them, and they have not purchased me." Do you see in what direction you are looking, that it is toward the earth, toward the pit, that it is toward these wretched laws of dead men? but toward the laws of the gods you are not looking.
D: Refashioned:
Someone asked how to eat in a way acceptable to the gods. Epictetus answered: justly, contentedly, temperately, and with composure. When your slave brings tepid water instead of hot, or fails to appear at all — not to erupt in anger, that is what is acceptable to the gods.

'How should I endure such people?' You are a slave yourself — will you not bear with your own brother, who shares your descent from Zeus? If you have been placed in a position of authority, will you become a tyrant? Remember who you rule: they are your kin by nature, offspring of the same divine source. When you treat them as property rather than persons, you are looking toward the earth and the laws of dead men, not toward the laws of the gods.
D: Elegant: Your slave brings tepid water. Do not erupt. He is your brother by nature, offspring of Zeus. If your position makes you a tyrant, you are following the laws of dead men, not the gods.
D: Distilled: How you treat the person who serves you badly reveals whether you follow the laws of the gods or the laws of dead men.
Notes: Epictetus was himself a former slave of Epaphroditus (Nero's secretary). His insistence on the divine kinship between master and slave is radical in a Roman context where slaves had no legal personhood. 'Laws of dead men' — Roman civil law, which treated slaves as property (res). Epictetus contrasts this with the 'laws of the gods' — the Stoic natural law that recognizes universal rational kinship. This connects to the framework's universalist ethics and its critique of power structures that deny shared humanity.

D: Original: Chapter 14: That the deity oversees all things

When a person asked him how a man could be convinced that all his actions are under the inspection of God, he answered, Do you not think that all things are united in one? "I do," the person replied. Well, do you not think that earthly things have a natural agreement and union with heavenly things "I do." And how else so regularly as if by God's command, when He bids the plants to flower, do they flower? when He bids them to send forth shoots, do they shoot? when He bids them to produce fruit, how else do they produce fruit? when He bids the fruit to ripen, does it ripen? when again He bids them to cast down the fruits, how else do they cast them down? and when to shed the leaves, do they shed the leaves? and when He bids them to fold themselves up and to remain quiet and rest, how else do they remain quiet and rest? And how else at the growth and the wane of the moon, and at the approach and recession of the sun, are so great an alteration and change to the contrary seen in earthly things?
