But are plants and our bodies so bound up and united with the whole, and are not our souls much more? and our souls so bound up and in contact with God as parts of Him and portions of Him; and does not God perceive every motion of these parts as being His own motion connate with Himself? Now are you able to think of the divine administration, and about all things divine, and at the same time also about human affairs, and to be moved by ten thousand things at the same time in your senses and in your understanding, and to assent to some, and to dissent from others, and again as to some things to suspend your judgment; and do you retain in your soul so many impressions from so many and various things, and being moved by them, do you fall upon notions similar to those first impressed, and do you retain numerous arts and the memories of ten thousand things; and is not God able to oversee all things, and to be present with all, and to receive from all a certain communication? And is the sun able to illuminate so large a part of the All, and to leave so little not illuminated, that part only which is occupied by the earth's shadow; and He who made the sun itself and makes it go round, being a small part of Himself compared with the whole, cannot He perceive all things?
D: Refashioned:
How can someone be convinced that God oversees all their actions? Consider the unity of things. Plants flower, fruit, and shed their leaves at the bidding of the seasons. The moon waxes and wanes. Everything on earth responds to the order above. If our bodies are bound to this system, are our souls — which are parts of God, portions of the divine intelligence — not bound even more intimately?

You yourself can simultaneously process ten thousand impressions, select among them, combine them, remember them. If your finite mind can do this, cannot the infinite intelligence that made it oversee all things? If the sun illuminates most of the world despite being a small part of the whole, cannot the maker of the sun perceive everything?
D: Elegant: Plants flower and shed on schedule. The moon obeys. If your finite mind can hold ten thousand impressions at once, cannot the intelligence that made it oversee all things? If the sun — a small part of the whole — illuminates most of the world, cannot its maker perceive everything?
D: Distilled: If your finite mind can hold ten thousand impressions at once, the intelligence that made it can certainly oversee everything.
Notes: Epictetus's argument from analogy — scaling from human cognitive capacity to divine omniscience — is a standard Stoic move. The claim that souls are 'parts of God' (mere tou theou) reflects Stoic pantheism: the individual rational faculty is literally a fragment of the cosmic logos. The argument from the sun is also found in Cleanthes (SVF 1.499). This connects to the framework's observation that the capacity for understanding is itself evidence of an intelligible order.

D: Original: "But I cannot," the man may reply, "comprehend all these things at once." But who tells you that you have equal power with Zeus? Nevertheless he has placed by every man a guardian, every man's Demon, to whom he has committed the care of the man, a guardian who never sleeps, is never deceived. For to what better and more careful guardian could He have entrusted each of us? When, then, you have shut the doors and made darkness within, remember never to say that you are alone, for you are not; but God is within, and your Demon is within, and what need have they of light to see what you are doing? To this God you ought to swear an oath just as the soldiers do to Caesar. But they who are hired for pay swear to regard the safety of Caesar before all things; and you who have received so many and such great favours, will you not swear, or when you have sworn, will you not abide by your oath? And what shall you swear? Never to be disobedient, never to make any charges, never to find fault with anything that he has given, and never unwillingly to do or to suffer anything, that is necessary. Is this oath like the soldier's oath? The soldiers swear not to prefer any man to Caesar: in this oath men swear to honour themselves before all.
D: Refashioned:
'But I cannot comprehend all these things at once.' No one said you could match God's power. But God has placed beside every person a guardian — your daemon, your inner witness — who never sleeps and is never deceived.

When you shut the door and make darkness within, never say you are alone. You are not alone. God is within you, and your daemon is within you. They do not need light to see what you are doing.

Swear an oath to this God — not the way soldiers swear to Caesar, pledging to put his safety above all else, but a deeper oath: to honor yourself above all. Never to be disobedient to what you know is right, never to blame the universe for what it has given you, never to do or suffer anything necessary with resentment. The soldier swears to prefer Caesar. You swear to prefer your own integrity.
D: Elegant: When you shut the door and make darkness, never say you are alone. God is within you, and your daemon — your inner witness — never sleeps. Swear an oath: not like a soldier to Caesar, but to yourself. Never to be disobedient, never to blame, never to resent what is necessary. The soldier prefers Caesar. You prefer your own integrity.
D: Distilled: When you are alone in the dark, you are not alone — your inner witness never sleeps, and the oath you owe is to your own integrity.
Notes: The daemon (daimon) — the Stoic personal guardian, related to Socrates's famous daimonion (the inner voice that warned him against wrong actions). The military oath (sacramentum) was the most binding commitment in Roman society — soldiers swore to die rather than desert. Epictetus raises the philosophical commitment above the military one: the soldier serves Caesar; the philosopher serves truth. This connects to the framework's concept of Right: the ultimate authority is internal, not institutional.

D: Original: Chapter 15: What philosophy promises

When a man was consulting him how he should persuade his brother to cease being angry with him, Epictetus replied: Philosophy does not propose to secure for a man any external thing. If it did philosophy would be allowing something which is not within its province. For as the carpenter's material is wood, and that of the statuary is copper, so the matter of the art of living is each man's life. "What then is my brother's?" That again belongs to his own art; but with respect to yours, it is one of the external things, like a piece of land, like health, like reputation. But Philosophy promises none of these. "In every circumstance I will maintain," she says, "the governing part conformable to nature." Whose governing part? "His in whom I am," she says.
D: Refashioned: A man asked how to make his brother stop being angry with him. Epictetus replied: philosophy does not promise to secure anything external. A carpenter works with wood, a sculptor with bronze — a philosopher's material is their own life. Your brother's anger belongs to his art, not yours. Philosophy promises one thing: 'I will maintain the governing faculty in accordance with nature.' Whose governing faculty? The one in the person who practices it — yours, not his.
D: Elegant: Philosophy does not promise to fix your brother. A carpenter works with wood; you work with your own life. Philosophy promises one thing: to keep your governing faculty aligned with nature. Yours — not anyone else's.
D: Distilled: Philosophy does not promise to change others — only to maintain your own governing faculty in accordance with nature.
Notes: The material analogy (hyle) — carpenter/wood, sculptor/bronze, philosopher/life — is Epictetus's clearest statement of philosophy's scope and limits. The governing faculty (hegemonikon) aligned with nature (kata phusin) is the Stoic definition of virtue. This connects to the framework's emphasis on the limits of influence: you cannot directly change another person's mind, only govern your own response.

D: Original:
"How then shall my brother cease to be angry with me?" Bring him to me and I will tell him. But I have nothing to say to you about his anger.

When the man, who was consulting him, said, "I seek to know this- how, even if my brother is not reconciled to me, shall I maintain myself in a state conformable to nature?" Nothing great, said Epictetus, is produced suddenly, since not even the grape or the fig is. If you say to me now that you want a fig, I will answer to you that it requires time: let it flower first, then put forth fruit, and then ripen. Is, then, the fruit of a fig-tree not perfected suddenly and in one hour, and would you possess the fruit of a man's mind in so short a time and so easily? Do not expect it, even if I tell you.
D: Refashioned:
'Then how do I get my brother to stop being angry?' Bring him to me and I will talk to him. But I have nothing to say to you about his anger.

When the man rephrased his question — 'How can I maintain my own composure even if my brother never reconciles?' — Epictetus answered: nothing great is produced suddenly. If you tell me you want a fig, I will tell you it takes time. Let it flower first, then fruit, then ripen. If the fruit of a fig tree cannot be rushed, why would you expect the fruit of a human mind to appear in an hour? Do not expect it, even if I show you the way.
D: Elegant: You want a fig? Let it flower, fruit, and ripen. The fruit of a human mind requires no less patience. Do not expect composure to appear in an hour. Growth takes what growth takes.
D: Distilled: Nothing great is produced suddenly — do not expect the fruit of a mind to ripen faster than the fruit of a tree.
Notes: The fig metaphor is one of Epictetus's most memorable images for the pace of moral development. It recurs in Marcus Aurelius (Meditations 11.33), who may have encountered it through Epictetus's Discourses. The passage distinguishes between two questions: 'how do I change him?' (outside your scope) and 'how do I maintain myself?' (your actual work). This connects to the framework's patience with the process of intellectual formation.

D: Original: Chapter 16: Of providence

Do not wonder if for other animals than man all things are provided for the body, not only food and drink, but beds also, and they have no need of shoes nor bed materials, nor clothing; but we require all these additional things. For, animals not being made for themselves, but for service, it was not fit for them to he made so as to need other things. For consider what it would be for us to take care not only of ourselves, but also about cattle and asses, how they should be clothed, and how shod, and how they should eat and drink. Now as soldiers are ready for their commander, shod, clothed and armed: but it would be a hard thing, for the chiliarch to go round and shoe or clothe his thousand men; so also nature has formed the animals which are made for service, all ready, prepared, and requiring no further care. So one little boy with only a stick drives the cattle.
D: Refashioned:
Do not be surprised that animals come equipped with everything their bodies need — food, shelter, warmth — while humans require additional provisions. Animals were not made for themselves but for service. If cattle needed shoes and clothing, the whole system would collapse under the burden of caring for them. Nature made them self-sufficient so that a single child with a stick can drive a herd.

Humans require more because we serve a higher purpose. The additional needs are not a design flaw — they are the price of being made for something beyond mere survival.
D: Elegant: Animals come self-equipped because they were made for service. A child with a stick drives the herd. Humans require more because we serve a higher purpose. The additional burden is the price of being made for something beyond survival.
D: Distilled: Humans require more than animals because we were made for more than animals — the extra burden is the price of the higher purpose.
Notes: The chiliarch (commander of a thousand) who would be overwhelmed if he had to shoe and clothe every soldier — Epictetus uses military logistics to illustrate the efficiency of natural design. The passage is a minor argument within the providence theme, but it establishes that human neediness is not evidence against divine design but a consequence of human complexity. This connects to the framework's recognition that higher-order capacities (reason, moral judgment) require supporting infrastructure that simpler organisms do not need.

D: Original:
But now we, instead of being thankful that we need not take the same care of animals as of ourselves, complain of God on our own account; and yet, in the name of Zeus and the gods, any one thing of those which exist would be enough to make a man perceive the providence of God, at least a man who is modest and grateful. And speak not to me now of the great thins, but only of this, that milk is produced from grass, and cheese from milk, and wool from skins. Who made these things or devised them? "No one," you say. Oh, amazing shamelessness and stupidity!

Well, let us omit the works of nature and contemplate her smaller acts. Is there anything less useful than
D: Refashioned:
Instead of being grateful that we do not have to care for animals the way we care for ourselves, we complain about our own condition. Yet any single thing in nature — milk from grass, cheese from milk, wool from skin — should be enough to make a modest person see providence at work. Who designed these transformations? 'No one,' some say. The shamelessness and stupidity of that answer speaks for itself.

We need not even consider the grand works of nature. The smallest processes — the ones we walk past every day without noticing — already demonstrate more design than we have the honesty to acknowledge.
D: Elegant: Milk from grass, cheese from milk, wool from skin — who designed these? 'No one,' some say. The shamelessness of that answer speaks for itself. We need not look at the grand works. The smallest processes already demonstrate more design than we have the honesty to acknowledge.
D: Distilled: Milk from grass, cheese from milk, wool from skin — the smallest transformations in nature already demonstrate more design than we are willing to admit.
Notes: This brief chapter closes Book 1's exploration of providence with an argument from everyday observation rather than cosmic scale. The text appears truncated in the Long translation ('Is there anything less useful than' — mid-sentence), suggesting the original may have continued. Epictetus's rhetorical move — that even the modest person should see design in simple processes — connects to the framework's emphasis on attention: wisdom begins with noticing what is already in front of you.

D: Original: Chapter 1: That confidence is not inconsistent with caution

The opinion of the philosophers, perhaps, seems to some to be a paradox; but still let us examine as well as we can, if it is true that it is possible to do everything both with caution and with confidence. For caution seems to be in a manner contrary to confidence, and contraries are in no way consistent. That which seems to many to be a paradox in the matter under consideration in my opinion is of this kind: if we asserted that we ought to employ caution and in the same things, men might justly accuse us of bringing together things which cannot be united. But now where is the difficulty in what is said? for if these things are true, which have been often said and often proved, that the nature of good is in the use of appearances, and the nature of evil likewise, and that things independent of our will do not admit either the nature of evil nor of good, what paradox do the philosophers assert if they say that where things are not dependent on the will, there you should employ confidence, but where they are dependent on the will, there you should employ caution? For if the bad consists in a bad exercise of the will, caution ought only to be used where things are dependent on the will. But if things independent of the will and not in our power are nothing to us, with respect to these we must employ confidence; and thus we shall both be cautious and confident, and indeed confident because of our caution. For by employing caution toward things which are really bad, it will result that we shall have confidence with respect to things which are not so.
D: Refashioned:
It sounds paradoxical to say you should be both cautious and confident at the same time. But the paradox dissolves once you apply the basic distinction: the nature of good and evil lies in how we use our will, not in external events. Be cautious where the will is involved — where you can actually go wrong. Be confident about everything else — the things outside your control.

By being cautious about the only things that are genuinely in your power, you earn the right to be confident about everything that is not. Caution toward real dangers produces confidence toward apparent ones.
D: Elegant: Be cautious where your will is involved — that is where you can go wrong. Be confident about everything else. Caution toward what you can control produces confidence toward what you cannot.
D: Distilled: Caution about the things in your power produces confidence about everything outside it.
Notes: The 'paradox' (paradoxon) that opens Book 2 is a deliberate provocation: Stoics were famous for their paradoxes (Cicero wrote a treatise called Paradoxa Stoicorum). Epictetus resolves it by applying the eph' hemin / ouk eph' hemin distinction from Book 1. This connects to the framework's emphasis on directed attention: knowing where to focus your concern is itself a form of wisdom.

D: Original: We are then in the condition of deer; when they flee from the huntsmen's feathers in fright, whither do they turn and in what do they seek refuge as safe? They turn to the nets, and thus they perish by confounding things which are objects of fear with things that they ought not to fear. Thus we also act: in what cases do we fear? In things which are independent of the will. In what cases, on the contrary, do we behave with confidence, as if there were no danger? In things dependent on the will. To be deceived then, or to act rashly, or shamelessly or with base desire to seek something, does not concern us at all, if we only hit the mark in things which are independent of our will. But where there is death, or exile or pain or infamy, there we attempt or examine to run away, there we are struck with terror. Therefore, as we may expect it to happen with those who err in the greatest matters, we convert natural confidence into audacity, desperation, rashness, shamelessness; and we convert natural caution and modesty into cowardice and meanness, which are full of fear and confusion. For if a man should transfer caution to those things in which the will may be exercised and the acts of the will, he will immediately, by willing to be cautious, have also the power of avoiding what he chooses: but if he transfer it to the things which are not in his power and will, and attempt to avoid the things which are in the power of others, he will of necessity fear, he will be unstable, he will be disturbed. For death or pain is not formidable, but the fear of pain or death. For this reason we commend the poet who said
D: Refashioned:
We are like deer driven by hunters' flags toward the nets. Frightened by what cannot harm us, we run straight into what can. We fear the wrong things and are confident about the wrong things — fearful about external events we cannot control, careless about our own judgments which we can.

We convert natural confidence into recklessness and natural caution into cowardice. A person who directs caution where the will operates gains the power to avoid what they choose. But a person who directs caution toward things outside their control will be unstable, fearful, and at the mercy of circumstances. Death and pain are not formidable. The fear of death and pain — that is what destroys us.
D: Elegant: We are deer driven by flags toward the nets — fearful of what cannot harm us, careless about what can. Death is not formidable. The fear of death is. We convert natural confidence into recklessness and natural caution into cowardice by directing each toward the wrong objects.
D: Distilled: Death is not formidable — the fear of death is what destroys us.
Notes: The deer metaphor (elaphoi) — driven by beaters' feathers toward the nets — is a hunting image from Greek and Roman life. The inversion of confidence and caution maps to Stoic psychology: pathē (passions) are misdirected judgments, not irrational impulses. The framework parallel: most suffering comes not from events but from anticipatory dread, which is a failure of the rational faculty.

D: Original: Not death is evil, but a shameful death. Confidence then ought to be employed against death, and caution against the fear of death. But now we do the contrary, and employ against death the attempt to escape; and to our opinion about it we employ carelessness, rashness and indifference. These things Socrates properly used to call "tragic masks"; for as to children masks appear terrible and fearful from inexperience, we also are affected in like manner by events for no other reason than children are by masks. For what is a child? Ignorance. What is a child? Want of knowledge. For when a child knows these things, he is in no way inferior to us. What is death? A "tragic mask." Turn it and examine it. See, it does not bite. The poor body must be separated from the spirit either now or later, as it was separated from it before. Why, then, are you troubled, if it be separated now? for if it is not separated now, it will be separated afterward. Why? That the period of the universe may be completed, for it has need of the present, and of the future, and of the past. What is pain? A mask. Turn it and examine it. The poor flesh is moved roughly, then, on the contrary, smoothly. If this does not satisfy you, the door is open: if it does, bear. For the door ought to be open for all occasions; and so we have no trouble.
D: Refashioned:
Not death is evil, but a shameful death. We should deploy confidence against death and caution against the fear of death. Instead we do the opposite — fleeing from death with panic while approaching our own judgments with carelessness and indifference.

Socrates called these things 'tragic masks.' Children are terrified by masks because they don't know what they are. We are terrified by death and pain for the same reason — ignorance. Turn the mask around and examine it. Death does not bite. The body must separate from the spirit eventually; if not now, then later. Pain is another mask — rough sensation, then smooth. If you cannot bear it, the door is open. But first, look at the thing clearly.
D: Elegant: Socrates called death a tragic mask. Children fear masks from ignorance. So do we. Turn it around and look — it does not bite. The body will separate from the spirit sooner or later. Pain is another mask. If you cannot bear it, the door is open. But first, look clearly.
D: Distilled: Death and pain are tragic masks — terrifying only to those who have not turned them around and looked.
Notes: 'Tragic masks' (prosōpeia tragika) — Socrates's metaphor, possibly from a lost dialogue or from oral tradition transmitted through the Stoic school. The 'door is open' (thyra anoiktai) is a recurring Stoic formula meaning death is always an available exit, which transforms continued life into a voluntary commitment. The poet who said 'not death is evil, but a shameful death' is uncertain — possibly Euripides.

D: Original: What then is the fruit of these opinions? It is that which ought to he the most noble and the most becoming to those who are really educated, release from perturbation, release from fear, freedom. For in these matters we must not believe the many, who say that free persons only ought to be educated, but we should rather believe the philosophers, who say that the educated only are free. "How is this?" In this manner. Is freedom anything else than the power of living as we choose? "Nothing else." Tell me then, ye men, do you wish to live in error? "We do not." No one then who lives in error is free. Do you wish to live in fear? Do you wish to live in sorrow? Do you wish to live in perturbation? "By no means." No one, then, who is in a state of fear or sorrow or perturbation is free; but whoever is delivered from sorrows and fears and perturbations, he is at the same time also delivered from servitude. How then can we continue to believe you, most dear legislators, when you say, "We only allow free persons to be educated?" For philosophers say we allow none to be free except the educated; that is, God does not allow it. "When then a man has turned round before the praetor his own slave, has he done nothing?" He has done something. "What?" He has turned round his own slave before the praetor. "Has he done nothing, more?" Yes: he is also bound to pay for him the tax called the twentieth. "Well then, is not the man who has gone through this ceremony become free?" No more than he is become free from perturbations. Have you who are able to turn round others no master? is not money your master, or a girl or a boy, or some tyrant, or some friend of the tyrant? why do you tremble then when you are going off to any trial of this kind? It is for this reason that I often say: Study and hold in readiness these principles by which you may determine what those things are with reference to which you ought to have confidence, and those things with reference to which you ought to be cautious: courageous in that which does not depend on your will; cautious in that which does depend on it.
D: Refashioned:
The fruit of these convictions is what education should most produce: freedom from perturbation, freedom from fear. The many say only free persons should be educated. The philosophers say only the educated are free.

Is freedom anything other than the power to live as we choose? Then no one who lives in error, fear, sorrow, or perturbation is free — regardless of their legal status. When a master frees a slave before the praetor, has he made the man truly free? He has performed a legal ceremony. But if the freed man is still enslaved to money, to desire, to a lover, to fear of a tyrant — in what sense is he free? True freedom requires education of the will, not a change of legal category.

Hold these principles ready: be confident about what does not depend on your will, cautious about what does.
D: Elegant: The many say only free people should be educated. Philosophers say only the educated are free. A slave freed before the praetor gains a legal status. But if he remains enslaved to fear, desire, and money — in what sense is he free? True freedom is education of the will, not a change of legal category.
D: Distilled: Only the educated are free — because freedom is not a legal status but the power to live without perturbation.
Notes: The praetor ceremony — manumissio vindicta — was the formal process of freeing a slave in Roman law; the master turned the slave around before the magistrate. The 'twentieth' (vicesima libertatis) was a 5% tax on the value of freed slaves. Epictetus, himself a former slave freed by Epaphroditus, speaks from direct experience. The reversal — 'not free persons should be educated, but the educated are free' — is one of the most politically radical claims in ancient philosophy. This connects to the framework's argument that genuine freedom is intellectual, not merely political.

D: Original: "Well have I not read to you, and do you not know what I was doing?" In what? "In my little dissertations." Show me how you are with respect to desire and aversion; and show if you do not fail in getting what you wish, me and if you do not fall into the things which you would avoid: but as to these long and laboured sentences, you will take them and blot them out.
D: Refashioned: 'But I have studied your dissertations.' Show me how you handle desire and aversion. Show me that you do not fail to get what you wish and do not stumble into what you would avoid. As for those labored sentences — take them and blot them out.
D: Elegant: You have studied the dissertations? Blot them out. Show me instead how you handle desire and aversion — whether you get what you wish and avoid what you fear.
D: Distilled: The test of philosophy is not what you have studied but how you handle desire and aversion.
Notes: A brief, sharp exchange that reprises the Book 1 theme of practice over theory. 'Blot them out' (exaleipson) — literally erase them from the wax tablet. Epictetus's impatience with students who confuse reading with transformation is one of his most consistent rhetorical positions.

D: Original: "What then did not Socrates write?" And who wrote so much? But how? As he could not always have at hand one to argue against his principles or to be argued against in turn, he used to argue with and examine himself, and he was always treating at least some one subject in a practical way. These are the things which a philosopher writes. But little dissertations and that method, which I speak of, he leaves to others, to the stupid, or to those happy men who being free from perturbations have leisure, or to such as are too foolish to reckon consequences.
D: Refashioned: Socrates did not write dissertations — and yet who wrote more, in the sense that matters? He could not always find someone to argue with, so he argued with himself, testing his own principles against real situations. This is what a philosopher writes: not words on a page, but principles into a life. The formal dissertations he left to others — to the comfortable, the leisured, or the foolish.
D: Elegant: Socrates wrote no dissertations — he wrote principles into a life. He argued with himself when no opponent was available, testing his convictions against real situations. Formal writings he left to the comfortable and the foolish.
D: Distilled: Socrates did not write dissertations — he wrote principles into a life, which is the only writing that matters.
Notes: The paradox of Socrates as 'the greatest writer who never wrote' runs through ancient philosophy. Plato dramatized it; Epictetus makes it a practical principle. The distinction between writing for display and writing-into-life connects to the framework's critique of academic philosophy: the point is not to produce texts but to produce transformed persons.

D: Original: And will you now, when the opportunity invites, go and display those things which you possess, and recite them, and make an idle show, and say, "See how I make dialogues?" Do not so, my man: but rather say: "See how I am not disappointed of that which I desire. See how I do not fall into that which I would avoid. Set death before me, and you will see. Set before me pain, prison, disgrace and condemnation." This is the proper display of a young man who is come out of the schools. But leave the rest to others, and let no one ever hear you say a word about these things; and if any man commends you for them, do not allow it; but think that you are nobody and know nothing. Only show that you know this, how never to be disappointed in your desire and how never to fall into that which you would avoid. Let others labour at forensic causes, problems and syllogisms: do you labour at thinking about death, chains, the rack, exile; and do all this with confidence and reliance on him who has called you to these sufferings, who has judged you worthy of the place in which, being stationed, you will show what things the rational governing power can do when it takes its stand against the forces which are not within the power of our will. And thus this paradox will no longer appear either impossible or a paradox, that a man ought to be at the same time cautious and courageous: courageous toward the things which do not depend on the will, and cautious in things which are within the power of the will.
D: Refashioned:
Do not go and display your knowledge, reciting dialogues and showing off technique. Instead say: 'See how I am not disappointed in my desires. See how I do not fall into what I wish to avoid. Set death before me and you will see. Set pain, prison, disgrace before me.' This is the proper demonstration of someone who has actually learned something.

Let others labor at forensic rhetoric, problems, and syllogisms. You labor at thinking about death, chains, exile — and face them with confidence, relying on the one who placed you at this post. Show what the rational governing power can do when it stands against forces outside the will. Then the paradox resolves itself: a person can be both cautious and courageous — courageous toward what does not depend on the will, cautious about what does.
D: Elegant: Do not display your knowledge — display your life. Set death before me, and I will show you what I have learned. Set prison, disgrace, condemnation. Let others labor at syllogisms. I labor at facing what comes with confidence. Courageous toward what I cannot control. Cautious about what I can.
D: Distilled: The proper display of philosophy is not what you can recite but how you face death, prison, and disgrace.
Notes: The chapter closes by restating the paradox from 2.1, now resolved: confidence and caution are not contradictory when directed at their proper objects. 'The one who placed you at this post' — the military metaphor from Book 1 (life as a duty station assigned by God). The distinction between displaying knowledge and displaying character is central to Epictetus's pedagogy. This connects to the framework's emphasis on verification: wisdom that has not been tested under pressure is not yet wisdom.

D: Original: Chapter 2: Of Tranquillity

Consider, you who are going into court, what you wish to maintain and what you wish to succeed in. For if you wish to maintain a will conformable to nature, you have every security, every facility, you have no troubles. For if you wish to maintain what is in your own power and is naturally free, and if you are content with these, what else do you care for? For who is the master of such things? Who can take them away? If you choose to be modest and faithful, who shall not allow you to be so? If you choose not to be restrained or compelled, who shall compel you to desire what you think that you ought not to desire? who shall compel you to avoid what you do not think fit to avoid? But what do you say? The judge will determine against you something that appears formidable; but that you should also suffer in trying to avoid it, how can he do that? When then the pursuit of objects and the avoiding of them are in your power, what else do you care for? Let this be your preface, this your narrative, this your confirmation, this your victory, this your peroration, this your applause.
D: Refashioned:
You are going into court. What do you wish to maintain? If you wish to maintain a will aligned with nature, you have every security. No one can compel you to desire what you should not, or to fear what you need not. The judge may rule against you — but that you should suffer internally from the ruling, he has no power to enforce.

When your pursuit and avoidance are under your own control, what else matters? Let this be your opening argument, your evidence, your closing statement, your victory, and your applause.
D: Elegant: The judge may rule against you. But that you should suffer internally from the ruling — that he cannot enforce. When pursuit and avoidance are under your control, what else matters? Let this be your entire case.
D: Distilled: The judge controls the verdict; you control whether the verdict disturbs you — and that is the only case that matters.
Notes: The courtroom setting reflects the social reality of Epictetus's students: Roman elites frequently faced legal proceedings. The rhetorical terms — preface, narrative, confirmation, peroration — are from Roman forensic oratory (Cicero, Quintilian). Epictetus repurposes them: the only 'case' worth winning is internal composure. This connects to the framework's distinction between external outcomes and the quality of one's response.

D: Original: Therefore Socrates said to one who was reminding him to prepare for his trial, "Do you not think then that I have been preparing for it all my life?" By what kind of preparation? "I have maintained that which was in my own power." How then? "I have never done anything unjust either in my private or in my public life."
D: Refashioned: When someone reminded Socrates to prepare for his trial, he answered: 'Do you think I have not been preparing my entire life?' His preparation was this: 'I have never done anything unjust, in private or in public.'
D: Elegant: 'Prepare for your trial.' Socrates replied: I have been preparing my entire life. I have never done anything unjust — in private or in public.
D: Distilled: The best preparation for any trial is a life in which you have never done anything unjust.
Notes: Socrates's response (from Xenophon's Apology, not Plato's) reduces trial preparation to its essence: if your life has been just, you are always prepared. Epictetus uses this to illustrate that Stoic 'readiness' is not situational but dispositional. This connects to the framework's concept of integrated character: readiness is not preparation for a specific event but the accumulated product of daily practice.

D: Original: But if you wish to maintain externals also, your poor body, your little property and your little estimation, I advise you to make from this moment all possible preparation, and then consider both the nature of your judge and your adversary. If it is necessary to embrace his knees, embrace his knees; if to weep, weep; if to groan, groan. For when you have subjected to externals what is your own, then be a slave and do not resist, and do not sometimes choose to be a slave, and sometimes not choose, but with all your mind be one or the other, either free or a slave, either instructed or uninstructed, either a well-bred cock or a mean one, either endure to be beaten until you die or yield at once; and let it not happen to you to receive many stripes and then to yield. But if these things are base, determine immediately: "Where is the nature of evil and good? It is where truth is: where truth is and where nature is, there is caution: where truth is, there is courage where nature is."
D: Refashioned:
But if you insist on maintaining externals — your body, your property, your reputation — then prepare thoroughly, because you have chosen to play a different game. Embrace knees, weep, groan — do whatever the game demands. But do not play both games at once. Be either free or a slave, either instructed or uninstructed, either a fighting cock or a tame one. Endure the beating until you die, or yield at once. Do not take many blows and then yield.

If this seems degrading, then decide now: where is the nature of good and evil? Where truth is. Where truth is, there is caution. Where nature is, there is courage.
D: Elegant: If you insist on maintaining externals, prepare to grovel — weep, embrace knees, do what the game demands. But do not play both games. Be free or slave, instructed or not. If this seems degrading, decide now: where is good and evil? Where truth is.
D: Distilled: Choose: be entirely free or entirely a slave — but do not take the blows and then yield anyway.
Notes: The fighting cock metaphor (alektor) — either fight to the death or surrender immediately, but do not suffer pointlessly and then surrender — is Epictetus at his most demanding. The passage forces an absolute choice: you cannot partially commit to Stoic freedom. This connects to the framework's observation that half-measures in matters of principle are worse than either full commitment or honest surrender.

D: Original: For what do you think? do you think that, if Socrates had wished to preserve externals, he would have come forward and said: "Anytus and Meletus can certainly kill me, but to harm me they are not able?" Was he so foolish as not to see that this way leads not to the preservation of life and fortune, but to another end? What is the reason then that he takes no account of his adversaries, and even irritates them? Just in the same way my friend Heraclitus, who had a little suit in Rhodes about a bit of land, and had proved to the judges that his case was just, said, when he had come to the peroration of his speech, "I will neither entreat you nor do I care what wi judgment you will give, and it is you rather than I who are on your trial." And thus he ended the business. What need was there of this? Only do not entreat; but do not also say, "I. do not entreat"; unless there is a fit occasion to irritate purposely the judges, as was the case with Socrates. And you, if you are preparing such a peroration, why do you wait, why do you obey the order to submit to trial? For if you wish to be crucified, wait and the cross will come: but if you choose to submit and to plead your cause as well as you can, you must do what is consistent with this object, provided you maintain what is your own.
D: Refashioned:
Do you think Socrates would have said 'Anytus and Meletus can kill me but cannot harm me' if he had wished to preserve his life and property? That statement leads not to survival but to something else entirely. He irritated his accusers on purpose. Similarly, Epictetus's friend Heraclitus, in a trivial lawsuit in Rhodes, told the judges: 'I will neither beg you nor care what verdict you give. It is you, not I, who are on trial.'

But this approach only makes sense if you have already decided that externals do not matter. If you want to survive, then plead your case skillfully — but maintain what is yours. Do not provoke your accusers and then complain about the consequences.
D: Elegant: Socrates irritated his judges on purpose. Heraclitus told the court: it is you on trial, not I. This only works if you have already decided externals do not matter. If you want to survive, plead skillfully — but maintain what is yours.
D: Distilled: Socrates provoked his accusers because he had already decided that survival was not the point — and that choice must be made before the trial begins.
Notes: Anytus and Meletus — Socrates's accusers at his trial in 399 BCE. The quote is from Plato's Apology (30c-d). Heraclitus (not the pre-Socratic philosopher) — likely a contemporary of Epictetus. The key point: defiance is not the same as recklessness. Socrates and Heraclitus could afford to provoke because they had already settled what mattered. This connects to the framework's distinction between principled defiance and mere provocation.

D: Original: For this reason also it is ridiculous to say, "Suggest something to me." What should I suggest to you? "Well, form my mind so as to accommodate itself to any event." Why that is just the same as if a man who is ignorant of letters should say, "Tell me what to write when any name is proposed to me." For if I should tell him to write Dion, and then another should come and propose to him not the name of Dion but that of Theon, what will be done? what will he write? But if you behave practiced writing, you are also prepared to write anything that is required. If you are not, what. can I now suggest? For if circumstances require something else, what will you say or what will you do? Remember, then, this general precept and you will need no suggestion. But if you gape after externals, you must of necessity ramble up and down in obedience to the will of your master. And who is the master? He who has the power over the things which you seek to gain or try to avoid.
D: Refashioned:
It is absurd to say, 'Tell me what to do.' That is like an illiterate person asking, 'Tell me what to write when someone gives me a name.' If I tell you to write 'Dion' and then someone asks you to write 'Theon,' you are stuck again — because you never learned to write.

If you have practiced the discipline of the will, you are prepared for anything. If you have not, no advice will help. Remember the general principle and you need no specific suggestions. But if you gape after externals, you will ramble in obedience to whoever has power over the things you desire or fear. That person is your master.
D: Elegant: Asking 'tell me what to do' is like an illiterate person asking 'tell me what to write.' Learn to write, and you can handle any name. Learn the discipline of the will, and you can handle any situation. Otherwise, whoever controls what you desire is your master.
D: Distilled: If you have disciplined your will, you are prepared for anything — if not, no advice will help.
Notes: The writing analogy recurs throughout the Discourses: literacy as a metaphor for philosophical competence. 'Gape after externals' (kechēnotes pros ta ektos) — literally 'standing open-mouthed toward external things.' The passage connects to the framework's concept of wisdom as capacity rather than content: the wise person does not need instructions for every situation because they have internalized the governing principle.

D: Original: Chapter 3: To those who recommend persons to philosophers

Diogenes said well to one who asked from him letters of recommendation, "That you are a man he said, "he will know as soon as he sees you; and he will know whether you are good or bad, if he is by experience skillful to distinguish the good and the bad; but if he is without experience, he will never know, if I write to him ten thousand times." For it is just the same as if a drachma asked to be recommended to a person to be tested. If he is skillful in testing silver, he will know what you are, for you will recommend yourself. We ought then in life also to have some skill as in the case of silver coin that a man may be able to say, like the judge of silver, "Bring me any drachma and I will test it." But in the case of syllogisms I would say, "Bring any man that you please, and I will distinguish for you the man who knows how to resolve syllogisms and the man who does not." Why? Because I know how to resolve syllogisms. I have the power, which a man must have who is able to discover those who have the power of resolving syllogisms. But in life how do I act? At one time I call a thing good, and at another time bad. What is the reason? The contrary to that which is in the case of syllogisms, ignorance and inexperience.
D: Refashioned:
Diogenes was right: when someone asked him for a letter of recommendation, he said, 'That you are a man, he will see the moment he meets you. Whether you are a good or bad man, he will know if he has experience in judging character. If he doesn't, ten thousand letters won't help.'

It is like a coin asking to be recommended to a money-tester. If the tester is skilled, the coin recommends itself. I can test syllogisms — bring me any, and I will tell you whether the person understands them. But in life, I call the same thing good one day and bad the next. Why? The same reason: ignorance and inexperience.
D: Elegant: A coin does not need a letter of recommendation — it recommends itself to any skilled tester. So should you. If the judge of character is skilled, he will know you on sight. If not, ten thousand letters will not help.
D: Distilled: A good coin needs no letter of recommendation — it recommends itself to anyone skilled enough to test it.
Notes: Diogenes of Sinope (c. 412-323 BCE) — founder of Cynicism, famous for radical self-sufficiency and contempt for social conventions. The drachma/coin-testing metaphor echoes 1.35. The self-deprecating admission at the end — 'in life I call the same thing good one day and bad the next' — shows Epictetus's honesty about the gap between theoretical knowledge and practical wisdom.

D: Original: Chapter 4: Against a person who had once been detected in adultery

As Epictetus was saying that man is formed for fidelity, and that he who subverts fidelity subverts the peculiar characteristic of men, there entered one of those who are considered to be men of letters, who had once been detected in adultery in the city. Then Epictetus continued: But if we lay aside this fidelity for which we are formed and make designs against our neighbor's wife, what are we are we doing? What else but destroying and overthrowing? Whom? The man of fidelity, the man of modesty, the man of sanctity. Is this all? And are we not overthrowing neighbourhood, and friendship, and the community; and in what place are we putting ourselves? How shall I consider you, man? As a neighbour, as a friend? What kind of one? As a citizen? Wherein shall I trust you? So if you were an utensil so worthless that a man could not use you, you would be pitched out on the dung heaps, and no man would pick you up. But if, being a man, you are unable to fill any place which befits a man, what shall we do with you? For suppose that you cannot hold the place of a friend, can you hold the place of a slave? And who will trust you? Are you not then content that you also should be pitched somewhere on a dung heap, as a useless utensil, and a bit of dung? Then will you say, "No man, cares for me, a man of letters"? They do not, because you are bad and useless. It is just as if the wasps complained because no man cares for them, but all fly from them, and if a man can, he strikes them and knocks them down. You have such a sting that you throw into trouble and pain any man that you wound with it. What would you have us do with you? You have no place where you can be put.
D: Refashioned:
Humans were formed for fidelity. The man who betrays that — who schemes against his neighbor's wife — is not merely breaking a rule. He is destroying himself. He is dismantling the man of trust, modesty, and sanctity. He is undermining neighborhood, friendship, and civic life.

What shall we do with such a person? If an implement were so broken that no one could use it, we would throw it on the dung heap. But you are not an implement — you are a human being who has made yourself useless for every human function. Can you hold the place of a friend? Of a neighbor? Of a citizen? No one will trust you. And then you complain: 'No one cares about me, a man of letters.' They do not care because you are a wasp — you sting everyone you touch. What would you have us do with you?
D: Elegant: The adulterer does not merely break a rule — he destroys the man of trust, modesty, and sanctity. He makes himself useless for every human function: friend, neighbor, citizen. Then he complains that no one cares about him. You are a wasp. You sting everything you touch. What would you have us do with you?
D: Distilled: The adulterer does not merely break a rule — he dismantles the very faculties that make him useful as a human being.
Notes: The 'man of letters' (philologos) who was caught in adultery — Epictetus attacks the assumption that intellectual accomplishment excuses moral failure. The wasp metaphor is characteristically blunt. The passage connects to the framework's concept of Right: character is not separate from competence; moral failure undermines every other capacity.

D: Original: "What then, are not women common by nature?" So I say also; for a little pig is common to all the invited guests, but when the portions have been distributed, go, if you think it right, and snatch up the portion of him who reclines next to you, or slyly steal it, or place your hand down by it and lay hold of it, and if you cannot tear away a bit of the meat, grease your fingers and lick them. A fine companion over cups, and Socratic guest indeed! "Well, is not the theatre common to the citizens?" When then they have taken their seats, come, if you think proper, and eject one of them. In this way women also are common by nature. When, then, the legislator, like the master of a feast, has distributed them, will you not also look for your own portion and not filch and handle what belongs to another. "But I am a man of letters and understand Archedemus." Understand Archedemus then, and be an adulterer, and faithless, and instead of a man, be a wolf or an ape: for what is the difference?
D: Refashioned:
You say women are 'common by nature.' Fine — a pig at a banquet is common too, but once the portions are distributed, you do not snatch meat from your neighbor's plate and lick the grease off your fingers. Seats at the theatre are common, but you do not eject someone who is already seated.

Women have been distributed by the order of things. Your job is to find your own portion, not steal someone else's. You say, 'But I am a man of letters who understands Archedemus.' Then understand Archedemus and be an adulterer and a traitor — instead of a man, be a wolf or a fox. What is the difference?
D: Elegant: A pig at a banquet is common too — but once portions are served, you do not snatch your neighbor's plate. Women have been distributed by the order of things. Find your own portion. You understand Archedemus? Then understand him and be a wolf. What is the difference?
D: Distilled: Knowing philosophy while practicing betrayal makes you not a philosopher but a wolf with a vocabulary.
Notes: Archedemus of Tarsus — Stoic philosopher of the 2nd century BCE, known for his work on logic. Epictetus's point: philosophical learning that does not produce faithful behavior is worse than useless. The 'Socratic guest' reference is ironic — Socrates at banquets was famous for his restraint, the opposite of the adulterer's behavior. This connects to the framework's insistence that knowledge without character is not wisdom but a more sophisticated form of corruption.

D: Original: Chapter 5: How magnanimity is consistent with care

Things themselves are indifferent; but the use of them is not indifferent. How then shall a man preserve firmness and tranquillity, and at the same time be careful and neither rash nor negligent? If he imitates those who play at dice. The counters are indifferent; the dice are indifferent. How do I know what the cast will be? But to use carefully and dexterously the cast of the dice, this is my business. Thus in life also the chief business is this: distinguish and separate things, and say, "Externals are not in my power: will is in my power. Where shall I seek the good and the bad? Within, in the things which are my own." But in what does not belong to you call nothing either good or bad, or profit or damage or anything of the kind.
D: Refashioned: External things are indifferent. The use of them is not. This is the same principle as playing dice: the throw is beyond your control, but how you play the result is entirely your business. Separate the two: externals are not in your power, the will is. Seek good and bad only within — in what belongs to you. In what does not belong to you, call nothing good or bad.
D: Elegant: The dice throw is indifferent. How you play the result is your business. Externals are not in your power; the will is. Seek good and bad only within.
D: Distilled: External things are indifferent; the use of them is not — and the use is entirely your business.
Notes: The dice metaphor (pessoi) is one of Epictetus's most effective illustrations: you cannot choose the roll, but you choose how to play it. This parallels the Stoic concept of 'preferred indifferents' (proēgmena) — things like health and wealth that have natural value but are not the good itself. The framework uses a similar structure: circumstances are given; response is chosen.

D: Original: "What then? Should we use such things carelessly?" In no way: for this on the other hand is bad for the faculty of the will, and consequently against nature; but we should act carefully because the use is not indifferent and we should also act with firmness and freedom from perturbations because the material is indifferent. For where the material is not indifferent, there no man can hinder me nor compel me. Where I can be hindered and compelled the obtaining of those things is not in my power, nor is it good or bad; but the use is either bad or good, and the use is in my power. But it is difficult to mingle and to bring together these two things, the carefulness of him who is affected by the matter and the firmness of him who has no regard for it; but it is not impossible; and if it is, happiness is impossible. But we should act as we do in the case of a voyage. What can I do? I can choose the master of the ship, the sailors, the day, the opportunity. Then comes a storm. What more have I to care for? for my part is done. The business belongs to another- the master. But the ship is sinking- what then have I to do? I do the only things that I can, not to be drowned full of fear, nor screaming, nor blaming God, but knowing that what has been produced must also perish: for I am not an immortal being, but a man, a part of the whole, as an hour is a part of the day: I must be present like the hour, and past like the hour. What difference, then, does it make to me how I pass away, whether by being suffocated or by a fever, for I must pass through some such means?
D: Refashioned:
Should we then be careless about externals? Not at all — carelessness is itself a failure of the will. Be careful about how you use things, because the use matters. But be firm about your composure, because the material is indifferent. The hard part is combining the two: the care of someone invested in the outcome with the steadiness of someone who is not. Difficult, but not impossible — and without it, happiness is impossible.

Think of a voyage. You choose the ship, the captain, the day. Then a storm comes. What is left for you? Not to drown screaming and blaming God, but to know that what was produced must perish. You are not immortal. You are a part of the whole, the way an hour is part of a day — present for a time, then past. What difference does it make whether you exit by drowning or by fever?
D: Elegant: Be careful about use, because it matters. Be firm about composure, because the material is indifferent. Combining the two is the hard part. You chose the ship and captain. Then the storm came. What remains? Not to drown screaming, but to know that what was born must die. You are an hour in a day — present, then past.
D: Distilled: Be careful about how you use things but steady about what happens to them — and know that what was born must eventually perish.
Notes: The voyage metaphor extends through several Stoic writers (Seneca, Marcus Aurelius). The image of the hour as part of a day captures the Stoic view of individual life within the cosmic order. 'What has been produced must also perish' — a fundamental Stoic physical principle connecting to the framework's emphasis on accepting the conditions of existence rather than protesting them.

D: Original: This is just what you will see those doing who play at ball skillfully. No one cares about the ball being good or bad, but about throwing and catching it. In this therefore is the skill, this the art, the quickness, the judgement, so that if I spread out my lap I may not be able to catch it, and another, if I throw, may catch the ball. But if with perturbation and fear we receive or throw the ball, what kind of play is it then, and wherein shall a man be steady, and how shall a man see the order in the game? But one will say, "Throw"; or, "Do not throw"; and another will say, "You have thrown once." This is quarreling, not play.
D: Refashioned:
Watch skilled ball players. No one cares about the ball itself — what matters is the throwing and catching. The skill, the art, the judgment are all in the play, not the object. But if you play with anxiety and fear, it stops being a game and becomes a quarrel.

Life works the same way. The materials are indifferent. The art is in how you handle them.
D: Elegant: Skilled ball players do not care about the ball — they care about the throw and the catch. Play life the same way: the materials are indifferent, the art is in the handling. But if you play with anxiety, it stops being a game and becomes a quarrel.
D: Distilled: The ball is indifferent — the skill is in the throwing and catching, and the same is true of life.
Notes: Ball games (sphairomachia) were popular in Greek and Roman culture; Galen wrote a treatise praising ball play as the ideal exercise. Epictetus's analogy captures the Stoic ideal of engaged detachment: full involvement in the activity without attachment to the specific object. This connects to the framework's concept of wisdom as a mode of engagement, not a withdrawal from life.

D: Original: Socrates, then, knew how to play at ball. How?" By using pleasantry in the court where he was tried. "Tell me," he says, "Anytus, how do you say that I do not believe in God. The Demons, who are they, think you? Are they not sons of Gods, or compounded of gods and men?" When Anytus admitted this, Socrates said, "Who then, think you, can believe that there are mules, but not asses"; and this he said as if he were playing at ball. And what was the ball in that case? Life, chains, banishment, a draught of poison, separation from wife and leaving children orphans. These were the things with which he was playing; but still he did play and threw the ball skillfully. So we should do: we must employ all the care of the players, but show the same indifference about the ball. For we ought by all means to apply our art to some external material, not as valuing the material, but, whatever it may be, showing our art in it. Thus too the weaver does not make wool, but exercises his art upon such as he receives. Another gives you food and property and is able to take them away and your poor body also. When then you have received the material, work on it. If then you come out without having suffered anything, all who meet you will congratulate you on your escape; but he who knows how to look at such things, if he shall see that you have behaved properly in the matter, will commend you and be pleased with you; and if he shall find that you owe your escape to any want of proper behavior, he will do the contrary. For where rejoicing is reasonable, there also is congratulation reasonable.
D: Refashioned:
Socrates knew how to play ball. In court, facing death, he used playful reasoning with Anytus: 'You say I don't believe in gods. But I believe in daemons. And daemons are children of gods. Who believes in mules but denies the existence of donkeys?'

What was the ball in that game? Life, chains, exile, poison, the loss of his wife and children. He played with the highest stakes — and he played skillfully. We must do the same: apply full art to whatever material we are given, without valuing the material itself. The weaver does not make the wool — he works with what he receives. If you emerge from your trial with your composure intact, the person who truly understands will congratulate you not on your survival but on your conduct.
D: Elegant: Socrates played ball with life, chains, and poison — and played skillfully. The weaver does not choose the wool; he works with what is given. Apply your full art to whatever material arrives. The wise will judge you not by whether you survived but by how you played.
D: Distilled: Apply your full art to whatever material life gives you — the wise will judge you by your conduct, not your survival.
Notes: The Socrates–Anytus exchange is from Plato's Apology (27a-e): Socrates's logical trap about daemons being children of gods. The weaver metaphor — working with given material — reinforces the Stoic position that circumstances are raw material, not destiny. 'The person who knows how to look' (ho eidōs theōrein) — the philosophical observer who evaluates character rather than outcomes. This connects to the framework's distinction between success (external) and excellence (internal).

D: Original: How then is it said that some external things are according to nature and others contrary to nature? It is said as it might be said if we were separated from union: for to the foot I shall say that it is according to nature for it to be clean; but if you take it as a foot and as a thing not detached, it will befit it both to step into the mud and tread on thorns, and sometimes to be cut off for the benefit of the whole body; otherwise it is no longer a foot. We should think in some way about ourselves also. What are you? A man. If you consider yourself as detached from other men, it is according to nature to live to old age, to be rich, to be healthy. But if you consider yourself as a man and a part of a certain whole, it is for the sake of that whole that at one time you should be sick, at another time take a voyage and run into danger, and at another time be in want, and, in some cases, die prematurely. Why then are you troubled? Do you not know, that as a foot is no longer a foot if it is detached from the body, so you are no longer a man if you are separated from other men. For what is a man? A part of a state, of that first which consists of Gods and of men; then of that which is called next to it, which is a small image of the universal state. "What then must I be brought to trial; must another have a fever, another sail on the sea, another die, and another be condemned?" Yes, for it is impossible in such a body, in such a universe of things, among so many living together, that such things should not happen, some to one and others to others. It is your duty then, since you are come here, to say what you ought, to arrange these things as it is fit. Then some one says, "I shall charge you with doing me wrong." Much good may it do you: I have done my part; but whether you also have done yours, you must look to that; for there is some danger of this too, that it may escape your notice.
D: Refashioned:
Are some external things more 'natural' than others? Yes — in the way that a clean foot is more natural than a muddy one. But if you consider the foot as part of the body, it must sometimes step in mud and thorns, and sometimes be amputated for the good of the whole.

The same applies to you. Considered alone, health and long life are natural preferences. But you are not alone — you are part of a community, a state, the cosmos. Sometimes the whole requires you to be sick, to take a dangerous voyage, to suffer want, even to die early. Why are you troubled? A foot detached from the body is no longer a foot. A person separated from humanity is no longer a person.

Someone will say, 'But I am being tried, another has a fever, another is exiled.' Of course — in a universe this complex, such things must happen to someone. Your duty is to play your part properly. If someone charges you with wrongdoing, that is their business. Whether they have played their part well is something they should examine.
D: Elegant: A clean foot is natural — but a foot must sometimes step in mud or be cut off for the body. You are a part, not the whole. Sometimes the whole requires you to be sick, endangered, or cut short. A foot detached from the body is no longer a foot. A person separated from humanity is no longer a person.
D: Distilled: You are a part, not the whole — and sometimes the whole requires you to suffer, just as the body sometimes requires the foot to be cut off.
Notes: The foot/body analogy is one of Stoicism's most important political metaphors, used extensively by Marcus Aurelius (Meditations 2.1, 7.13). It grounds Stoic cosmopolitanism in a physical metaphor: just as organs serve the body, individuals serve the community. The 'small image of the universal state' (mikra eikōn tēs megalēs poleōs) connects to the framework's conception of nested communities: individual, local, national, civilizational, cosmic.

D: Original: Chapter 6: Of indifference

The hypothetical proposition is indifferent: the judgment about it is not indifferent, but it is either knowledge or opinion or error. Thus life is indifferent: the use is not indifferent. When any man then tells you that these things also are indifferent, do not become negligent; and when a man invites you to be careful, do not become abject and struck with admiration of material things. And it is good for you to know your own preparation and power, that in those matters where you have not been prepared, you may keep quiet, and not be vexed, if others have the advantage over you. For you, too, in syllogisms will claim to have the advantage over them; and if others should be vexed at this, you will console them by saying, "I have learned them, and you have not." Thus also where there is need of any practice, seek not that which is required from the need, but yield in that matter to those who have had practice, and be yourself content with firmness of mind.
D: Refashioned:
A logical proposition is indifferent; your judgment about it is not — it is either knowledge, opinion, or error. The same applies to life: life is indifferent, but the use of life is not.

When someone tells you everything is indifferent, do not become negligent. When someone urges caution, do not become servile toward material things. Know your own preparation: where you have trained, be confident; where you have not, keep quiet and do not resent others who have the advantage. You would not be upset if someone outperformed you at syllogisms you had not studied. Apply the same composure to every area where you lack practice.
D: Elegant: Life is indifferent; the use of life is not. When told everything is indifferent, do not become careless. When urged to be cautious, do not become servile. Know where you have trained and where you have not — and be content with firmness of mind.
D: Distilled: Life is indifferent but the use of life is not — knowing the difference prevents both negligence and servility.
Notes: This chapter refines the indifference doctrine: 'indifferent' does not mean 'unimportant' — it means the material is not where good and evil reside. The distinction between the thing and the use of the thing is one of Stoicism's most practically important formulations. This connects to the framework's concept of Reality: understanding what things actually are prevents both overvaluation and undervaluation.

D: Original: Go and salute a certain person. "How?" Not meanly. "But I have been shut out, for I have not learned to make my way through the window; and when I have found the door shut, I must either come back or enter through the window." But still speak to him. "In what way?" Not meanly. But suppose that you have not got what you wanted. Was this your business, and not his? Why then do you claim that which belongs to another? Always remember what is your own, and what belongs to another; and you will not be disturbed. Chrysippus therefore said well, "So long as future things are uncertain, I always cling to those which are more adapted to the conservation of that which is according to nature; for God himself has given me the faculty of such choice." But if I knew that it was fated for me to be sick, I would even move toward it; for the foot also, if it had intelligence, would move to go into the mud.
D: Refashioned:
You go to petition a powerful person and the door is shut. Do not crawl through the window — and do not be crushed. You have not learned to enter through windows, only through doors. If the door is closed, come back or go home. Was getting what you wanted your business, or his? Always remember what is yours and what belongs to another.

Chrysippus said: 'When the future is uncertain, I cling to what is more naturally suitable for choice. But if I knew it was fated for me to be sick, I would move toward the sickness.' Just as a foot, if it had intelligence, would step willingly into the mud when the body required it.
D: Elegant: The door is shut. Do not crawl through the window. Was getting what you wanted your business, or his? Remember what is yours. Chrysippus said: when the future is uncertain, choose what seems naturally best. But if sickness is fated, walk toward it — as a foot would willingly step into mud when the body requires.
D: Distilled: When the door is shut, do not crawl through the window — remember what is yours and what belongs to another.
Notes: Chrysippus of Soli (c. 279-206 BCE) — the passage articulates the Stoic doctrine of 'preferred indifferents' combined with acceptance of fate. The foot-in-mud metaphor extends from 2.20. The 'door shut' scenario reflects Roman patronage culture, where clients waited at their patron's door each morning (salutatio). This connects to the framework's practical ethics: maintaining dignity while operating within systems you cannot control.

D: Original: For why are ears of corn produced? Is it not that they may become dry? And do they not become dry that they may be reaped? for they are not separated from communion with other things. If then they had perception, ought they to wish never to be reaped? But this is a curse upon ears of corn, never to be reaped. So we must know that in the case of men too it is a curse not to die, just the same as not to be ripened and not to be reaped. But since we must be reaped, and we also know that we are reaped, we are vexed at it; for we neither know what we are nor have we studied what belongs to man, as those who have studied horses know what belongs to horses.
D: Refashioned:
Ears of corn grow, dry, and are reaped. If they could think, should they wish never to be harvested? That would be a curse — to grow and never ripen, to ripen and never be gathered. The same applies to human beings. Not to die is as much a curse as not to ripen.

We are vexed by death not because death is terrible but because we do not know what we are. A person who understood their own nature — the way a horse trainer understands horses — would not be disturbed by the natural course of that nature.
D: Elegant: If ears of corn could think, should they wish never to be reaped? That would be a curse — to ripen and never be gathered. Not to die is the same curse. We are vexed because we do not know what we are.
D: Distilled: Not to die is as much a curse as not to ripen — we are vexed by death only because we do not understand our own nature.
Notes: The harvest metaphor is one of Epictetus's most lyrical: human life as a natural cycle of growth, ripening, and gathering. Marcus Aurelius uses similar agricultural imagery (Meditations 4.48). The comparison to horse-trainers emphasizes practical knowledge of nature over theoretical speculation. This connects to the framework's Reality pillar: understanding what you are is the prerequisite for understanding what happens to you.

D: Original: But Chrysantas, when he was going to strike the enemy, checked himself when he heard the trumpet sounding a retreat: so it seemed better to him to obey the general's command than to follow his own inclination. But not one of us chooses, even when necessity summons, readily to obey it, but weeping and groaning we suffer what we do suffer, and we call them "circumstances." What kind of circumstances, man? If you give the name of circumstances to the things which are around you, all things are circumstances; but if you call hardships by this name, what hardship is there in the dying of that which has been produced? But that which destroys is either a sword, or a wheel, or the sea, or a tile, or a tyrant. Why do you care about the way of going down to Hades? All ways are equal.
D: Refashioned:
Chrysantas was about to strike the enemy when the trumpet sounded retreat. He checked his blow and obeyed the command — he valued the general's order above his own impulse. But when death calls us, we weep, groan, and call it 'circumstances' — as if circumstances were something imposed on us rather than simply the things around us.

What hardship is there in the destruction of something that was produced? A sword, a wheel, the sea, a tile, a tyrant — does it matter which one? All roads to Hades are equal.
D: Elegant: Chrysantas checked his sword when the trumpet sounded retreat — he valued the order above his impulse. When death calls us, we weep and call it circumstances. But what hardship is there in the destruction of what was produced? All roads to Hades are equal.
D: Distilled: All roads to Hades are equal — what matters is not how you leave but whether you obeyed the command when it came.
Notes: Chrysantas — a Persian cavalry officer in Xenophon's Cyropaedia (4.1.3), famous for his discipline. Epictetus uses a non-Greek, non-Stoic military example to universalize the point: discipline under authority is admirable regardless of the tradition. 'All roads to Hades are equal' — a formulation that strips death of its drama. This connects to the framework's observation that the mode of death is less important than the quality of the response to mortality.

D: Original:
But if you will listen to the truth, the way which the tyrant sends you is shorter. A tyrant never killed a man in six months: but a fever is often a year about it. All these things are only sound and the noise of empty names.

"I am in danger of my life from Caesar." And am not I in danger who dwell in Nicopolis, where there are so many earthquakes: and when you are crossing the Hadriatic, what hazard do you run? Is it not the hazard of your life? "But I am in danger also as to opinion." Do you mean your own? how? For who can compel you to have any opinion which you do not choose? But is it as to another man's opinion? and what kind of danger is yours, if others have false opinions? "But I am in danger of being banished." What is it to be banished? To be somewhere else than at Rome? "Yes: what then if I should be sent to Gyara?" If that suits you, you will go there; but if it does not, you can go to another place instead of Gyara, whither he also will go, who sends you to Gyara, whether he choose or not. Why then do you go up to Rome as if it were something great? It is not worth all this preparation, that an ingenuous youth should say, "It was not worth while to have heard so much and to have written so much and to have sat so long by the side of an old man who is not worth much." Only remember that division by which your own and not your own are distinguished: never claim anything which belongs to others. A tribunal and a prison are each a place, one high and the other low; but the will can be maintained equal, if you choose to maintain it equal in each. And we shall then be imitators of Socrates, when we are able to write paeans in prison. But in our present disposition, consider if we could endure in prison another person saying to us. "Would you like me to read Paeans to you?" "Why do you trouble me? do you not know the evils which hold me? Can I in such circumstances?" What circumstances? "I am going to die." And will other men be immortal?
D: Refashioned:
If you listen carefully, the tyrant's method is actually the fastest. No tyrant ever took six months to kill a man. A fever can take a year. All the things we fear — death, exile, confiscation — are just noise, empty names.

'I am in danger from Caesar.' And I am in danger from earthquakes in Nicopolis. When you cross the Adriatic, you risk your life. We are always in danger. 'But my reputation is at risk.' That is someone else's opinion — how is their false judgment your problem? 'I may be banished.' To where? Somewhere other than Rome? If you end up in Gyara, and Gyara does not suit you, there are other places — and the man who sent you to Gyara will himself end up somewhere he did not choose.
