Remember the division: what is yours and what is not yours. A tribunal and a prison are each just a place — one high, the other low. The will can be equal in both. We would be imitators of Socrates if we could write hymns in prison. But as things stand, most of us cannot even hear someone else recite poetry in prison without lamenting.
D: Elegant: A tyrant's method is the fastest — no tyrant ever took six months. A fever can take a year. Death, exile, confiscation — empty names, all of them. A tribunal and a prison are each a place. The will can be equal in both. Socrates wrote hymns in prison. Can you?
D: Distilled: A tribunal and a prison are each just a place — one high, the other low — and the will can be maintained equal in both.
Notes: Gyara (modern Gyaros) — a barren island in the Cyclades used by the Romans for political exile. 'Write paeans in prison' — Socrates composed a hymn to Apollo while awaiting execution (Plato, Phaedo 60d-61b). Nicopolis, where Epictetus taught, was in earthquake-prone Epirus. The passage progressively strips away the drama from every feared outcome: death, exile, reputation, location. This connects to the framework's emphasis on deflating the appearance of catastrophe through rational examination.

D: Original: Chapter 7: How we ought to use divination

Through an unreasonable regard to divination many of us omit many duties. For what more can the diviner see than death or danger or disease, generally things of that kind? If then I must expose myself to danger for a friend, and if it is my duty even to die for him, what need have I then for divination? Have I not within me a diviner who has told me the nature of good and of evil, and has explained to me the signs of both? What need have I then to consult the viscera of victims or the flight of birds, and why do I submit when he says, "It is for your interest"? For does he know what is for my interest, does he know what is good; and as he has learned the signs of the viscera, has he also learned the signs of good and evil? For if he knows the signs of these, he knows the signs both of the beautiful and of the ugly, and of the just and of the unjust. Do you tell me, man, what is the thing which is signified for me: is it life or death, poverty or wealth? But whether these things are for my interest or whether they are not, I do not intend to ask you. Why don't you give your opinion on matters of grammar, and why do you give it here about things on which we are all in error and disputing with one another? The woman, therefore, who intended to send by a vessel a month's provisions to Gratilla in her banishment, made a good answer to him who said that Domitian would seize what she sent. "I would rather," she replied, "that Domitian should seize all than that I should not send it."
D: Refashioned:
Many people neglect their duties because of divination — consulting prophets about whether to act when duty already tells them what to do. If I must risk my life for a friend, or die for what is right, what use is a diviner? I already have a diviner within me — the rational faculty that knows good from evil.

The diviner reads entrails and tells you what will happen: death, danger, disease. But whether these things are good or bad for you — that he does not know. He has learned to read signs in viscera, not signs of virtue and vice.

A woman was told that Domitian might seize the provisions she was sending to her exiled friend Gratilla. She replied: 'I would rather Domitian seize them than that I not send them.' That is the proper use of judgment — not divination.
D: Elegant: If duty tells you to risk your life for a friend, what use is a diviner? You already have one within you — the rational faculty that knows good from evil. The diviner reads entrails. He does not read virtue. A woman told that Domitian might seize her gift replied: 'I would rather he seize it than that I not send it.'
D: Distilled: If duty already tells you what to do, consulting a diviner is not caution — it is cowardice.
Notes: Gratilla — Verulana Gratilla, wife of the Stoic Arulenus Rusticus, exiled under Domitian (c. 93 CE). Domitian's persecution of Stoic philosophers and their families was the immediate context for Epictetus's own expulsion from Rome. The woman's response embodies the Stoic principle: act rightly regardless of consequences. Divination (mantikē) was deeply embedded in Roman public and private life. This connects to the framework's emphasis on internal authority over external validation.

D: Original: What then leads us to frequent use of divination? Cowardice, the dread of what will happen. This is the reason why we flatter the diviners. "Pray, master, shall I succeed to the property of my father?" "Let us see: let us sacrifice on the occasion." "Yes, master, as fortune chooses." When he has said, "You shall succeed to the inheritance," we thank him as if we received the inheritance from him. The consequence is that they play upon us.
D: Refashioned: What drives us to diviners? Cowardice — the dread of what might happen. So we flatter the prophet: 'Will I inherit my father's property?' He performs the sacrifice, reads the signs, says yes — and we thank him as though he gave us the inheritance. This is how they exploit us.
D: Elegant: What drives us to diviners? Cowardice. We flatter the prophet, he tells us what we want to hear, and we thank him as though he gave us the inheritance. This is how they exploit us.
D: Distilled: We consult diviners not for wisdom but for reassurance — and they exploit our cowardice accordingly.
Notes: The transactional nature of Roman divination — sacrifice, reading of signs, payment — made it vulnerable to exactly the kind of manipulation Epictetus describes. The 'thanking him as if he gave us the inheritance' captures the psychological mechanism: we attribute to the prophet the power that belongs to fate. This connects to the framework's critique of epistemic outsourcing: seeking certainty from authority rather than developing internal judgment.

D: Original: What then should we do? We ought to come without desire or aversion, as the wayfarer asks of the man whom he meets which of two roads leads (to his journey's end), without any desire for that which leads to the right rather than to the left, for he has no wish to go by any road except the road which leads (to his end). In the same way ought we to come to God also as a guide; as we use our eyes, not asking them to show us rather such things as we wish, but receiving the appearances of things such as the eyes present them to us. But now we trembling take the augur by the hand, and, while we invoke God, we entreat the augur, and say, "Master have mercy on me; suffer me to come safe out of this difficulty." Wretch would you have, then, anything other than what is best? Is there then anything better than what pleases God? Why do you, so far as in your power, corrupt your judge and lead astray your adviser?
D: Refashioned:
How should we use divination? The way a traveler asks directions — without preference for the road that leads right over the road that leads left, wanting only the road that leads to the destination. Come to God as a guide, not as a servant to be flattered. Do not ask the oracle to show you what you want to see. Receive the appearances as your eyes present them.

But instead we grab the augur's hand, trembling, and beg: 'Let me escape this difficulty.' You want something other than what is best? Is there anything better than what pleases God? Why do you corrupt your judge and mislead your advisor?
D: Elegant: Come to God as a traveler asks directions — without preference for right or left, wanting only the road to the destination. Do not ask the oracle to show you what you want to see. Is there anything better than what pleases God? Why corrupt your judge and mislead your advisor?
D: Distilled: Come to divination without preference — wanting only the road to the destination, not the road that avoids difficulty.
Notes: The traveler analogy is one of Epictetus's most practical formulations of Stoic acceptance: you want to get somewhere, not to choose the easiest path. The accusation of 'corrupting the judge' inverts the language of Socrates's trial: just as Socrates was accused of corrupting the young, the fearful person corrupts the divine oracle by approaching it with a predetermined agenda. This connects to the framework's emphasis on intellectual honesty: seeking truth rather than confirmation.

D: Original: Chapter 8: What is the nature of the good

God is beneficial. But the Good also is beneficial. It is consistent then that where the nature of God is, there also the nature of the good should be. What then is the nature of God? Flesh? Certainly not. An estate in land? By no means. Fame? No. Is it intelligence, knowledge, right reason? Yes. Herein then simply seek the nature of the good; for I suppose that you do not seek it in a plant. No. Do you seek it in an irrational animal? No. If then you seek it in a rational animal, why do you still seek it anywhere except in the superiority of rational over irrational animals? Now plants have not even the power of using appearances, and for this reason you do not apply the term good to them. The good then requires the use of appearances. Does it require this use only? For if you say that it requires this use only, say that the good, and that happiness and unhappiness are in irrational animals also. But you do not say this, and you do right; for if they possess even in the highest degree the use of appearances, yet they have not the faculty of understanding the use of appearances; and there is good reason for this, for they exist for the purpose of serving others, and they exercise no superiority. For the ass, I suppose, does not exist for any superiority over others. No; but because we had need of a back which is able to bear something; and in truth we had need also of his being able to walk, and for this reason he received also the faculty of making use of appearances, for otherwise he would not have been able to walk. And here then the matter stopped. For if he had also received the faculty of comprehending the use of appearances, it is plain that consistently with reason he would not then have been subjected to us, nor would he have done us these services, but he would have been equal to us and like to us.
D: Refashioned:
God is beneficial. The good is beneficial. Therefore the nature of the good must be found where the nature of God is found. Is God flesh? Property? Fame? No — God is intelligence, knowledge, right reason. So seek the good there.

Plants have no power to use appearances — we do not call them good or bad. Animals use appearances but do not understand them — they have enough for their purpose but no surplus of comprehension. The good requires not just using appearances but understanding the use. If the ass could understand its appearances, it would not serve us — it would be our equal. The good belongs to the rational animal, and only to the rational animal, precisely because it involves the comprehension that other beings lack.
D: Elegant: God is intelligence, knowledge, right reason — so seek the good there. Plants cannot use appearances. Animals use them but cannot understand them. The good belongs to the being that both uses and understands — and that is the rational animal alone.
D: Distilled: The good is found where God is found: not in flesh, property, or fame, but in intelligence, knowledge, and right reason.
Notes: This chapter builds a careful hierarchy: plants (no use of appearances) → animals (use without understanding) → humans (use with understanding). The ass example — if it could understand, it would be our equal and refuse to serve — is a thought experiment about the relationship between rationality and moral status. This connects to the framework's Reason pillar and the hierarchy of being: higher capacities confer both greater dignity and greater responsibility.

D: Original: Will you not then seek the nature of good in the rational animal? for if it is not there, you not choose to say that it exists in any other thing. "What then? are not plants and animals also the works of God?" They are; but they are not superior things, nor yet parts of the Gods. But you are a superior thing; you are a portion separated from the deity; you have in yourself a certain portion of him. Why then are you ignorant of your own noble descent? Why do you not know whence you came? will you not remember when you are eating, who you are who eat and whom you feed? When you are in conjunction with a woman, will you not remember who you are who do this thing? When you are in social intercourse, when you are exercising yourself, when you are engaged in discussion, know you not that you are nourishing a god, that you are exercising a god? Wretch, you are carrying about a god with you, and you know it not. Do you think that I mean some God of silver or of gold, and external? You carry him within yourself, and you perceive not that you are polluting him by impure thoughts and dirty deeds. And if an image of God were present, you would not dare to do any of the things which you are doing: but when God himself is present within and sees all and hears all, you are not ashamed of thinking such things and doing such things, ignorant as you are of your own nature and subject to the anger of God. Then why do we fear when we are sending a young man from the school into active life, lest he should do anything improperly, eat improperly, have improper intercourse with women; and lest the rags in which he is wrapped should debase him, lest fine garments should make him proud? This youth does not know his own God: he knows not with whom he sets out.
D: Refashioned:
You are not merely a work of God — you are a superior work, a portion separated from the deity itself. You carry a piece of the divine within you. Will you not remember this when you eat, when you interact with others, when you exercise? You are nourishing a god. You are exercising a god.

You would not dare to think impure thoughts in front of a golden statue of God. But God himself is present within you, seeing and hearing everything — and you are not ashamed? We fear sending a young person into the world because they might eat badly or have improper relationships. But the real failure is that they do not know their own God — they do not know what they carry within them.
D: Elegant: You carry a portion of the divine within you. You would not think impure thoughts before a golden statue of God — yet God himself is within you, seeing everything, and you are not ashamed. The real failure is not bad behavior but not knowing what you carry.
D: Distilled: You carry a god within you — and you do not even know it, which is why you dishonor yourself.
Notes: The 'carrying a god' (theon periphereis) passage is one of Epictetus's most powerful: the divine is not external but internal, present in the rational faculty. The golden statue comparison shames the student: you show more reverence to a piece of metal than to the living divinity within. This connects to the framework's concept of dignity as inherent rather than conferred: the source of human worth is not social status but rational nature.

D: Original: But can we endure when he says, "I wish I had you with me." Have you not God with you? and do you seek for any other, when you have him? or will God tell you anything else than this? If you were a statue of Phidias, either Athena or Zeus you would think broth of yourself and of the artist, and if you had any understanding you would try to do nothing unworthy of him who made you or of yourself, and try not to appear in an unbecoming dress to those who look on you. But now because Zeus has made you, for this reason do you care not how you shall appear? And yet is the artist like the artist in the other? or the work in the one case like the other? And what work of an artist, for instance, has in itself the faculties, which the artist shows in making it? Is it not marble or bronze, or gold or ivory? and the Athena of Phidias when she has once extended the hand and received in it the figure of Victory stands in that attitude forever.
D: Refashioned:
You say, 'I wish I had you with me.' But you do have God with you — what more do you need? If you were a statue made by Phidias — an Athena or a Zeus — you would think well of yourself and try to be worthy of your maker. You would not appear in an unbecoming way. But Zeus made you, not Phidias — and you show less concern for your maker's work?

The difference is this: Phidias's Athena, once she extends her hand and holds the figure of Victory, holds that pose forever. But you are not marble. You are alive, moving, breathing — capable of using and understanding every appearance that comes to you. That is infinitely more than any statue.
D: Elegant: If Phidias made you, you would try to be worthy of the artist. Zeus made you — and you show less concern? The difference: Phidias's Athena holds one pose forever. You are alive, moving, capable of understanding. That is infinitely more.
D: Distilled: If you would try to honor the work of Phidias, how much more should you honor the work of God — which is alive and capable of understanding?
Notes: The Athena Parthenos and Zeus at Olympia — Phidias's two most famous chryselephantine (gold and ivory) statues. The Athena held a figure of Nike (Victory) in her extended right hand. The comparison between static art and living rational beings drives home the Stoic point: human beings are God's masterwork precisely because they are dynamic, not fixed. This connects to the framework's concept of human potential as active capacity rather than static endowment.

D: Original: But the works of God have power of motion, they breathe, they have the faculty of using the appearances of things, and the power of examining them. Being the work of such an artist, do you dishonor him? And what shall I say, not only that he made you, but also intrusted you to yourself and made you a deposit to yourself? Will you not think of this too, but do you also dishonor your guardianship? But if God had intrusted an orphan to you, would you thus neglect him? He has delivered yourself to your care, and says, "I had no one fitter to intrust him to than yourself: keep him for me such as he is by nature, modest, faithful, erect, unterrified, free from passion and perturbation." And then you do not keep him such.
D: Refashioned: God's works, unlike Phidias's, have the power of motion, breath, and understanding. Being the work of such an artist, do you dishonor him? He did not merely make you — he entrusted you to yourself, as a guardian entrusts an orphan. Would you neglect an orphan placed in your care? God says: 'Keep this person as nature made them — modest, faithful, upright, fearless, free from passion.' And you do not keep them so.
D: Elegant: God entrusted you to yourself as a guardian entrusts an orphan. 'Keep this person as nature made them — modest, faithful, fearless, free.' And you do not.
D: Distilled: God entrusted you to yourself — and the charge was to keep yourself modest, faithful, and free from perturbation.
Notes: The guardianship (epitropē) metaphor transforms self-care from selfishness into sacred duty: you are not your own possession but a trust placed in your own keeping. The orphan analogy (orphanos) would resonate powerfully in a Roman context where guardianship of orphans was a serious legal and moral obligation. This connects to the framework's concept of self-governance as the foundation of all other virtues.

D: Original: But some will say, "Whence has this fellow got the arrogance which he displays and these supercilious looks?" I have not yet so much gravity as befits a philosopher; for I do not yet feel confidence in what I have learned and what I have assented to: I still fear my own weakness. Let me get confidence and the, you shall see a countenance such as I ought to have and an attitude such as I ought to have: then I will show to you the statue, when it is perfected, when it is polished. What do you expect? a supercilious countenance? Does the Zeus at Olympia lift up his brow? No, his look is fixed as becomes him who is ready to say
D: Refashioned:
People accuse me of arrogance. But I have not yet achieved the gravity a philosopher should have. I still fear my own weakness. I have not yet reached full confidence in what I have learned. Let me achieve that confidence, and then you will see the proper countenance — not a supercilious look, but the settled gaze of someone who is ready.

Does the Zeus at Olympia raise his brow in arrogance? No. His expression is fixed and calm, as befits someone prepared to say what cannot be taken back.
D: Elegant: I have not yet achieved the gravity I should have — I still fear my own weakness. When I do, you will see not arrogance but the settled gaze of the Zeus at Olympia: calm, fixed, ready to say what cannot be taken back.
D: Distilled: The philosopher's proper countenance is not arrogance but the settled calm of someone ready to say what cannot be unsaid.
Notes: Epictetus's admission of ongoing struggle — 'I still fear my own weakness' — is a rare moment of vulnerability that authenticates his teaching: even the teacher is still in progress. The Zeus at Olympia's expression becomes a model for the philosophical ideal: not pride but readiness. The passage connects to the framework's insistence that wisdom is a direction of travel, not a destination reached.

D: Original: Irrevocable is my word and shall not fail. Such will I show myself to you, faithful, modest, noble, free from perturbation. "What, and immortal too, exempt from old age, and from sickness?" No, but dying as becomes a god, sickening as becomes a god. This power I possess; this I can do. But the rest I do not possess, nor can I do. I will show the nerves of a philosopher. "What nerves are these?" A desire never disappointed, an aversion which never falls on that which it would avoid, a proper pursuit, a diligent purpose, an assent which is not rash. These you shall see.
D: Refashioned:
'My word is irrevocable and shall not fail.' This is what I will show: a person who is faithful, modest, noble, and free from perturbation. Will I also be immortal, exempt from age and sickness? No. But I will die as becomes a god, and be sick as becomes a god. This power I possess.

What are the nerves of a philosopher? A desire that is never disappointed. An aversion that never falls on what it would avoid. A proper pursuit. A diligent purpose. An assent that is not rash. These you shall see.
D: Elegant: 'My word shall not fail.' Immortal? No. But I will die and sicken as becomes a god. The nerves of a philosopher: desire never disappointed, aversion never misplaced, purpose never rash. These you shall see.
D: Distilled: The philosopher's power is not immortality but this: desire never disappointed, aversion never misplaced, and assent never rash.
Notes: The opening quotation — 'irrevocable is my word' — is from Homer (Iliad 1.526), spoken by Zeus himself. Epictetus claims for the philosopher the same reliability: not divine power but divine consistency. The 'nerves of a philosopher' (neura philosophou) — desire, aversion, pursuit, purpose, assent — maps to the three Stoic disciplines (topoi). 'Dying as becomes a god' — not avoiding death but meeting it with the composure that the divine nature within us demands. This connects to the framework's vision of wisdom: not superhuman capacity but fully human excellence.

D: Original: Chapter 9: That when we cannot fulfill that which the character of a man promises, we assume the character of a philosopher

It is no common thing to do this only, to fulfill the promise of a man's nature. For what is a man? The answer is: "A rational and mortal being." Then, by the rational faculty, from whom are we separated? From wild beasts. And from what others? From sheep and like animals. Take care then to do nothing like a wild beast; but if you do, you have lost the character of a man; you have not fulfilled your promise. See that you do nothing like a sheep; but if you do, in this case the man is lost. What then do we do as sheep? When we act gluttonously, when we act lewdly, when we act rashly, filthily, inconsiderately, to what have we declined? To sheep. What have we lost? The rational faculty. When we act contentiously and harmfully and passionately, and violently, to what have we declined? To wild beasts. Consequently some of us are great wild beasts, and others little beasts, of a bad disposition and small, whence we may say, "Let me be eaten by a lion." But in all these ways the promise of a man acting as a man is destroyed. For when is a conjunctive proposition maintained? When it fulfills what its nature promises; so that the preservation of a complex proposition is when it is a conjunction of truths. When is a disjunctive maintained? When it fulfills what it promises. When are flutes, a lyre, a horse, a dog, preserved? What is the wonder then if man also in like manner is preserved, and in like manner is lost? Each man is improved and preserved by corresponding acts, the carpenter by acts of carpentry, the grammarian by acts of grammar. But if a man accustoms himself to write ungrammatically, of necessity his art will be corrupted and destroyed. Thus modest actions preserve the modest man, and immodest actions destroy him: and actions of fidelity preserve the faithful man, and the contrary actions destroy him. And on the other hand contrary actions strengthen contrary characters: shamelessness strengthens the shameless man, faithlessness the faithless man, abusive words the abusive man, anger the man of an angry temper, and unequal receiving and giving make the avaricious man more avaricious.
D: Refashioned:
It is no small thing simply to fulfill the promise of being human: a rational, mortal being, separated from wild beasts by reason. When we act with gluttony, lewdness, or thoughtlessness, we have declined to sheep. When we act with violence, cruelty, and aggression, we have declined to wild beasts. In either case, the man is lost.

Each person is preserved by the actions that correspond to their nature, the way a carpenter is preserved by carpentry and a grammarian by grammar. Write ungrammatically and the art is destroyed. Act immodestly and the modest person is destroyed. Shamelessness strengthens the shameless person; faithlessness strengthens the faithless. Every action either builds or erodes the character that performs it.
D: Elegant: Act as a glutton and you decline to sheep. Act with cruelty and you decline to a wild beast. Every action either builds the character that performs it or erodes it — there is no neutral ground.
D: Distilled: Every action either builds the character that performs it or erodes it — there is no neutral ground.
Notes: The preservation metaphor — 'a conjunctive proposition is maintained when it fulfills what its nature promises' — is characteristically Epictetan: logical and ethical terminology are interchangeable because rationality underlies both. The Ajax reference ('the stone which Ajax lifted') appears in the Iliad (7.268) — Ajax hurled a stone too large for ordinary men. The framework parallel: moral character is not a fixed attribute but a skill that must be actively maintained through practice.

D: Original: For this reason philosophers admonish us not to be satisfied with learning only, but also to add study, and then practice. For we have long been accustomed to do contrary things, and we put in practice opinions which are contrary to true opinions. If then we shall not also put in practice right opinions, we shall be nothing more than the expositors of the opinions of others. For now who among us is not able to discuss according to the rules of art about good and evil things? "That of things some are good, and some are bad, and some are indifferent: the good then are virtues, and the things which participate in virtues; and the are the contrary; and the indifferent are wealth, health, reputation." Then, if in the midst of our talk there should happen some greater noise than usual, or some of those who are present should laugh at us, we are disturbed. Philosopher, where are the things which you were talking about? Whence did you produce and utter them? From the lips, and thence only. Why then do you corrupt the aids provided by others? Why do you treat the weightiest matters as if you were playing a game of dice? For it is one thing to lay up bread and wine as in a storehouse, and another thing to eat. That which has been eaten, is digested, distributed, and is become sinews, flesh, bones, blood, healthy colour, healthy breath. Whatever is stored up, when you choose you can readily take and show it; but you have no other advantage from it except so far as to appear to possess it.
D: Refashioned:
Philosophers warn that learning is not enough — you must add practice, and then habit. We have spent so long doing the wrong things that right opinions, merely held in the mind, cannot overcome wrong actions embedded in the body.

We can all recite the theory: 'Some things are good, some bad, some indifferent. Virtue is good, vice is bad, wealth and health are indifferent.' Then a loud noise comes, or someone laughs at us, and we crumble. Where are the principles now? They were on our lips, not in our sinews. It is one thing to store bread in a pantry and another to eat, digest, and convert it into muscle, bone, and blood. Our philosophical knowledge is stored, not digested — and undigested knowledge is useless.
D: Elegant: We can all recite the theory. Then a loud noise comes and we crumble. The principles were on our lips, not in our sinews. It is one thing to store bread and another to digest it into bone and blood. Undigested philosophy is useless.
D: Distilled: Principles stored on the lips but not digested into the sinews are useless — the first loud noise will scatter them.
Notes: The digestion metaphor — bread stored versus bread converted to bone and blood — is one of Epictetus's most vivid images for the gap between intellectual and practical wisdom. The Stoic three-stage learning process (mathesis → meletē → askesis: learning → study → practice) parallels modern skill acquisition theory. This connects to the framework's emphasis on paideia as transformation, not transmission.

D: Original: For what is the difference between explaining these doctrines and those of men who have different opinions? Sit down now and explain according to the rules of art the opinions of Epicurus, and perhaps you will explain his opinions in a more useful manner than Epicurus himself. Why then do you call yourself a Stoic? Why do you deceive the many? Why do you deceive the many? Why do you act the part of a Jew, when you are a Greek? Do you not see how each is called a Jew, or a Syrian or an Egyptian? and when we see a man inclining to two sides, we are accustomed to say, "This man is not a Jew, but he acts as one." But when he has assumed the affects of one who has been imbued with Jewish doctrine and has adopted that sect, then he is in fact and he is named a Jew. Thus we too being falsely imbued, are in name Jews, but in fact we are something else. Our affects are inconsistent with our words; we are far from practicing what we say, and that of which we are proud, as if we knew it. Thus being, unable to fulfill even what the character of a man promises, we even add to it the profession of a philosopher, which is as heavy a burden, as if a man who is unable to bear ten pounds should attempt to raise the stone which Ajax lifted.
D: Refashioned:
What is the difference between you and a skilled expositor of Epicurus? You can explain Stoic doctrine beautifully. He can explain Epicurean doctrine beautifully. Neither of you lives by what you teach. You call yourself a Stoic the way someone 'acts the part of a Jew' without actually being one — when the affects and the actions match the doctrine, then you are truly what you claim to be.

As it stands, we cannot even fulfill the basic promise of being human, and then we add the burden of calling ourselves philosophers. It is as if a person who cannot carry ten pounds tried to lift the stone that Ajax threw.
D: Elegant: You explain Stoic doctrine as well as an Epicurean explains his. Neither of you lives it. Call yourself a Stoic when your actions match — not before. As it stands, you cannot carry ten pounds and claim to lift the stone of Ajax.
D: Distilled: Do not call yourself a philosopher until your actions match the doctrine — anything else is costume, not identity.
Notes: The comparison between 'acting the part of a Jew' and actually being one reflects the multicultural world of the early Roman Empire; conversion to Judaism involved not just belief but comprehensive behavioral change. Ajax's stone (Iliad 7.268) — a stone so large that later generations could barely lift it — symbolizes the weight of genuine philosophical commitment versus the pretense of it. This connects to the framework's concept of integrity: identity is constituted by action, not declaration.

D: Original: Chapter 10: How we may discover the duties of life from names

Consider who you are. In the first place, you are a man; and this is one who has nothing superior to the faculty of the will, but all other things subjected to it; and the faculty itself he possesses unenslaved and free from subjection. Consider then from what things you have been separated by reason. You have been separated from wild beasts: you have been separated from domestic animals. Further, you are a citizen of the world, and a part of it, not one of the subservient, but one of the principal parts, for you are capable of comprehending the divine administration and of considering the connection of things. What then does the character of a citizen promise? To hold nothing as profitable to himself; to deliberate about nothing as if he were detached from the community, but to act as the hand or foot would do, if they had reason and understood the constitution of nature, for they would never put themselves in motion nor desire anything, otherwise than with reference to the whole. Therefore the philosophers say well, that if the good man had foreknowledge of what would happen, he would cooperate toward his own sickness and death and mutilation, since he knows that these things are assigned to him according to the universal arrangement, and that the whole is superior to the part and the state to the citizen. But now, because we do not know the future, it is our duty to stick to the things which are in their nature more suitable for our choice, for we were made among other things for this.
D: Refashioned:
Consider who you are. First: a human being — one who has nothing superior to the faculty of the will, with all other things subject to it. Second: a citizen of the world, not a subordinate part but a principal one, capable of comprehending the divine administration.

What does citizenship promise? To hold nothing as profitable to yourself alone. To deliberate about nothing as if detached from the community. To act as a hand or foot would act if it understood the constitution of the body — never moving except with reference to the whole. The philosophers say that if a good person foreknew what would happen, they would cooperate even with their own sickness and death, since the whole is superior to the part. But since we do not know the future, we must cling to what is naturally preferable while remaining prepared for anything.
D: Elegant: You are a citizen of the world — a principal part, not a subordinate one. Act as a hand would if it understood the body: never for itself alone, always with reference to the whole. The philosophers say the good person would cooperate even with their own death if they could see the whole pattern.
D: Distilled: A citizen of the world holds nothing as profitable to themselves alone and acts always with reference to the whole.
Notes: The hand/foot analogy reprises 2.20 but now with positive content: the part that understands the whole cooperates willingly. 'Cooperate with one's own sickness and death' — amor fati avant la lettre, the Stoic doctrine of willing acceptance that Nietzsche would later reformulate. This connects to the framework's cosmopolitan ethics: genuine citizenship requires seeing yourself as part of a larger order.

D: Original: After this, remember that you are a son. What does this character promise? To consider that everything which is the son's belongs to the father, to obey him in all things, never to blame him to another, nor to say or do anything which does him injury, to yield to him in all things and give way, cooperating with him as far as you can. After this know that you are a brother also, and that to this character it is due to make concessions; to be easily persuaded, to speak good of your brother, never to claim in opposition to him any of the things which are independent of the will, but readily to give them up, that you may have the larger share in what is dependent on the will. For see what a thing it is, in place of a lettuce, if it should so happen, or a seat, to gain for yourself goodness of disposition. How great is the advantage.
D: Refashioned:
You are also a son. What does this role require? To consider everything of yours as belonging to your father. To obey, never to blame him to others, never to harm him, to yield in all things and cooperate as far as you can.

You are also a brother. This role requires concession, generosity, good speech — never claiming against your brother any of the external things that are not under your will, but yielding them readily. For the trade is excellent: give up a lettuce or a chair, and gain goodness of disposition. The advantage is immense.
D: Elegant: You are a son: obey, yield, cooperate. You are a brother: concede, speak well, never claim externals against him. Give up a lettuce and gain goodness of disposition. The trade is immense.
D: Distilled: Every role — son, brother, citizen — specifies duties, and fulfilling them is always a better trade than claiming externals.
Notes: Epictetus's 'role ethics' (prosōpon theory) — each social identity carries specific obligations that are discovered by examining the role itself. The lettuce/seat example grounds abstract ethics in everyday domestic life: the opportunity cost of generosity is always trivial compared to the character gained. This connects to the framework's practical ethics: virtue is exercised in small daily decisions, not grand dramatic moments.

D: Original: Next to this, if you are senator of any state, remember that you are a senator: if a youth, that you are a youth: if an old man, that you are an old man; for each of such names, if it comes to be examined, marks out the proper duties. But if you go and blame your brother, I say to you, "You have forgotten who you are and what is your name." In the next place, if you were a smith and made a wrong use of the hammer, you would have forgotten the smith; and if you have forgotten the brother and instead of a brother have become an enemy, would you appear not to have changed one thing for another in that case? And if instead of a man, who is a tame animal and social, you are become a mischievous wild beast, treacherous, and biting, have you lost nothing? But, you must lose a bit of money that you may suffer damage? And does the loss of nothing else do a man damage? If you had lost the art of grammar or music, would you think the loss of it a damage? and if you shall lose modesty, moderation and gentleness, do you think the loss nothing? And yet the things first mentioned are lost by some cause external and independent of the will, and the second by our own fault; and as to the first neither to have them nor to lose them is shameful; but as to the second, not to have them and to lose them is shameful and matter of reproach and a misfortune. What does the pathic lose? He loses the man. What does he lose who makes the pathic what he is? Many other things; and he also loses the man no less than the other. What does he lose who commits adultery? He loses the modest, the temperate, the decent, the citizen, the neighbour. What does he lose who is angry? Something else. What does the coward lose? Something else. No man is bad without suffering some loss and damage. If then you look for the damage in the loss of money only, all these men receive no harm or damage; it may be, they have even profit and gain, when they acquire a bit of money by any of these deeds.
D: Refashioned:
Every name you hold — senator, youth, elder — defines a set of duties. If you blame your brother, you have forgotten the name 'brother.' If you misuse your tools, you have forgotten the name 'smith.' But these are relatively minor lapses. To forget 'man' — to lose modesty, gentleness, and self-control — is a far greater catastrophe, and one we barely notice.

We track damage only in terms of money. But the adulterer loses the modest person within him. The angry person loses composure. The coward loses courage. These are real losses — far greater than financial ones — yet we do not even count them. The things lost involuntarily (money, property) carry no shame. The things lost voluntarily (character, integrity) carry all of it.
D: Elegant: Every name defines duties. Forget 'brother' and you become an enemy. Forget 'man' and you become something worse. We count losses only in money — but the adulterer loses modesty, the coward loses courage, and we do not even notice. The real losses are the ones we choose.
D: Distilled: We count losses only in money, but the real losses — modesty, courage, integrity — are the ones we choose to surrender.
Notes: The pathic (kinaidos) reference — used here as in 1.28 to illustrate the loss of the 'man' within — reflects ancient social categories. Epictetus's accounting of moral losses is deliberately structured to contrast with financial accounting: we track coins but not character. This connects to the framework's critique of materialist value systems: a civilization that measures only economic damage is blind to its most catastrophic losses.

D: Original: But consider that if you refer everything to a small coin, not even he who loses his nose is in your opinion damaged. "Yes," you say, "for he is mutilated in his body." Well; but does he who has lost his smell only lose nothing? Is there, then, no energy of the soul which is an advantage to him who possesses it, and a damage to him who has lost it? "Tell me what sort you mean." Have we not a natural modesty? "We have." Does he who loses this sustain no damage? is he deprived of nothing, does he part with nothing of the things which belong to him? Have we not naturally fidelity? natural affection, a natural disposition to help others, a natural disposition to forbearance? The man then who allows himself to be damaged in these matters, can he be free from harm and uninjured? "What then? shall I not hurt him, who has hurt me?" In the first place consider what hurt is, and remember what you have heard from the philosophers.
D: Refashioned:
If the only damage you recognize is financial, then even losing your sense of smell seems trivial. But does the person who has lost modesty, fidelity, natural affection, and self-restraint suffer no damage? Are these not real possessions — and is losing them not a real injury?

'Should I not then hurt the person who has hurt me?' Consider what hurt means. If good and evil reside only in the will, then what you are really asking is: 'Since that man harmed himself by acting unjustly toward me, shall I harm myself by acting unjustly toward him?' Once you see it that way, revenge loses its appeal.
D: Elegant: 'Should I not hurt the one who hurt me?' Rephrase: since he harmed himself by acting unjustly, shall I harm myself by doing the same? Once you see it that way, revenge loses its appeal.
D: Distilled: Revenge is harming yourself in response to someone else's self-harm — once you see it clearly, it loses all appeal.
Notes: The retribution argument — 'should I not hurt him who hurt me?' — is one of the oldest questions in ethics (Plato, Crito 49a-d). Epictetus resolves it with strict Stoic logic: if evil is in the will, then the unjust person has already harmed themselves. Your retaliation adds a second injury — to yourself. This connects to the framework's concept of Right: ethical action is not calculated by external outcomes but by the disposition of the will.

D: Original:
For if the good consists in the will, and the evil also in the will, see if what you say is not this: "What then, since that man has hurt himself by doing an unjust act to me, shall I not hurt myself by doing some unjust act to him?" Why do we not imagine to something of this kind? But where there is any detriment to the body or to our possession, there is harm there; and where the same thing happens to the faculty of the will, there is no harm; for he who has been deceived or he who has done an unjust act neither suffers in the head nor in the eye nor in the hip, nor does he lose his estate; and we wish for nothing else than these things.

But whether we shall have the will modest and faithful or shameless and faithless, we care not the least, except only in the school so far as a few words are concerned. Therefore our proficiency is limited to these few words; but beyond them it does not exist even in the slightest degree.
D: Refashioned:
We care about whether our will is modest and faithful only in the classroom — 'so far as a few words are concerned.' In actual life, we care about our heads, our eyes, our property. When someone is deceived or acts unjustly, they do not suffer in the head or hip. But they have lost something far more important: the right disposition of the will. And we treat this as nothing.

Our philosophical proficiency extends exactly as far as our classroom discussions, and not one step further.
D: Elegant: We care about the will's quality only in the classroom. Outside, we care about heads and hips. Our philosophy extends as far as our discussions — and not one step further.
D: Distilled: Our philosophical proficiency extends exactly as far as our classroom discussions — and not one step beyond.
Notes: This closing passage is one of Epictetus's most damning assessments of his own students: they master the theory but do not practice it. 'So far as a few words are concerned' (mechri logariōn tinōn) — the diminutive is deliberately contemptuous. This connects to the framework's critique of educational institutions that produce verbal fluency without moral transformation.

D: Original: Chapter 11: What the beginning of philosophy is

The beginning of philosophy to him at least who enters on it in the right way and by the door, is a consciousness of his own weakness and inability about necessary things. For we come into the world with no natural notion of a right-angled triangle, or of a diesis, or of a half tone; but we learn each of these things by a certain transmission according to art; and for this reason those who do not know them, do not think that they know them. But as to good and evil, and beautiful and ugly, and becoming and unbecoming, and happiness and misfortune, and proper and improper, and what we ought to do and what we ought not to do, whoever came into the world without having an innate idea of them? Wherefore we all use these names, and we endeavor to fit the preconceptions to the several cases thus: "He has done well, he has not done well; he has done as he ought, not as he ought; he has been unfortunate, he has been fortunate; he is unjust, he is just": who does not use these names? who among us defers the use of them till he has learned them, as he defers the use of the words about lines or sounds? And the cause of this is that we come into the world already taught as it were by nature some things on this matter, and proceeding from these we have added to them self-conceit. "For why," a man says, "do I not know the beautiful and the ugly? Have I not the notion of it?" You have. "Do I not adapt it to particulars?" You do. "Do I not then adapt it properly?" In that lies the whole question; and conceit is added here. For, beginning from these things which are admitted, men proceed to that which is matter of dispute by means of unsuitable adaptation; for if they possessed this power of adaptation in addition to those things, what would hinder them from being perfect? But now since you think that you properly adapt the preconceptions to the particulars, tell me whence you derive this. Because I think so.
D: Refashioned:
Philosophy begins with the recognition that you do not know what you think you know. We come into the world without innate knowledge of triangles or musical intervals — and we know it, so we learn. But we come into the world with innate notions of good and evil, justice and injustice, beauty and ugliness — and because we have these rough preconceptions, we assume we already know how to apply them correctly.

This is where all the trouble starts. Everyone uses the words — 'he acted rightly,' 'she was unjust,' 'that is beautiful' — but when we try to apply these preconceptions to particular cases, we contradict each other and ourselves. The problem is not that we lack moral concepts but that we do not know how to apply them. And the reason we do not seek training is that we think we already have what we need.
D: Elegant: We are born with rough notions of good, evil, and justice — and because we have them, we assume we apply them correctly. This is where all trouble starts. We do not lack moral concepts. We lack the skill to apply them. And we do not seek training because we think we already know.
D: Distilled: Philosophy begins with realizing that having moral concepts is not the same as knowing how to apply them correctly.
Notes: Epictetus's theory of prolepsis (preconception) — innate, pre-theoretical moral notions that all humans share but apply differently. The task of philosophy is not to provide new concepts but to train the correct application of concepts everyone already possesses. This is a fundamentally different pedagogical model from 'teaching new content' and connects to the framework's concept of wisdom as skill (knowing how) rather than knowledge (knowing that).

D: Original: But it does not seem so to another, and he thinks that he also makes a proper adaptation; or does he not think so? He does think so. Is it possible then that both of you can properly apply the preconceptions to things about which you have contrary opinions? It is not possible. Can you then show us anything better toward adapting the preconceptions beyond your thinking that you do? Does the madman do any other things than the things as in which seem to him right? Is then this criterion for him also? It is not sufficient. Come then to something which is superior to seeming. What is this?
D: Refashioned: Two people disagree about what is right. Each thinks their application of the concept is correct. Both cannot be right. So 'it seems right to me' cannot be the criterion — the madman also does what seems right to him. We need something superior to mere seeming.
D: Elegant: You think your judgment is correct. So does your opponent. So does the madman. 'It seems right to me' is not a criterion. We need something superior to seeming.
D: Distilled: The madman also acts on what seems right to him — 'it seems so to me' is not a criterion for truth.
Notes: The madman argument is a standard Stoic challenge to subjectivism: if 'seeming' were the criterion, the insane person's judgments would be as valid as anyone's. Epictetus uses this to motivate the search for an objective standard. This connects to the framework's argument against epistemic relativism: disagreement is evidence of the need for training, not proof that no standard exists.

D: Original: Observe, this is the beginning of philosophy, a perception of the disagreement of men with one another, and an inquiry into the cause of the disagreement, and a condemnation and distrust of that which only "seems," and a certain investigation of that which "seems" whether it "seems" rightly, and a discovery of some rule, as we have discovered a balance in the determination of weights, and a carpenter's rule in the case of straight and crooked things. This is the beginning of philosophy. "Must we say that all thins are right which seem so to all?" And how is it possible that contradictions can be right? "Not all then, but all which seem to us to be right." How more to you than those which seem right to the Syrians? why more than what seem right to the Egyptians? why more than what seems right to me or to any other man? "Not at all more." What then "seems" to every man is not sufficient for determining what "is"; for neither in the case of weights or measures are we satisfied with the bare appearance, but in each case we have discovered a certain rule. In this matter then is there no rule certain to what "seems?" And how is it possible that the most necessary things among men should have no sign, and be incapable of being discovered? There is then some rule. And why then do we not seek the rule and discover it, and afterward use it without varying from it, not even stretching out the finger without it? For this, I think, is that which when it is discovered cures of their madness those who use mere "seeming" as a measure, and misuse it; so that for the future proceeding from certain things known and made clear we may use in the case of particular things the preconceptions which are distinctly fixed.
D: Refashioned:
This, then, is the true beginning of philosophy: perceiving that people disagree with one another, investigating the cause of the disagreement, condemning mere 'seeming,' and searching for a standard that goes beyond it — the way we developed scales for weight and rulers for straightness.

'What seems right to everyone is enough.' But contradictory claims cannot both be right. What seems right to you is no more valid than what seems right to the Syrian, the Egyptian, or anyone else. In weights and measures, we do not trust bare appearance — we use instruments. In moral matters, there must also be a rule. Our task is to find it and then apply it without deviation. This discovery is what cures the madness of those who use 'seeming' as their measure.
D: Elegant: Philosophy begins by noticing disagreement, distrusting mere 'seeming,' and searching for a rule — the way we found scales for weight and rulers for straightness. In moral matters, 'it seems right' is no more valid than the appearance of weight. Find the rule. Apply it without deviation.
D: Distilled: Philosophy begins with noticing disagreement, distrusting 'seeming,' and searching for a rule that goes beyond personal conviction.
Notes: The balance (zygon) and carpenter's rule (kanon) as metaphors for philosophical method — tools that correct subjective impressions with objective standards. 'Cures of their madness' — philosophy as therapy for cognitive disorder, a model shared by all Hellenistic schools. This connects to the framework's Reason pillar: the pursuit of objective standards is not optional but the defining activity of the rational mind.

D: Original: What is the matter presented to us about which we are inquiring? "Pleasure." Subject it to the rule, throw it into the balance. Ought the good to be such a thing that it is fit that we have confidence in it? "Yes." And in which we ought to confide? "It ought to be." Is it fit to trust to anything which is insecure? "No." Is then pleasure anything secure? "No." Take it then and throw it out of the scale, and drive it far away from the place of good things. But if you are not sharp-sighted, and one balance is not enough for you, bring another. Is it fit to be elated over what is good? "Yes." Is it proper then to be elated over present pleasure? See that you do not say that it is proper; but if you do, I shall then not think you are worthy even of the balance. Thus things are tested and weighed when the rules are ready. And to philosophize is this, to examine and confirm the rules; and then to use them when they are known is the act of a wise and good man.
D: Refashioned:
Apply the method: take pleasure, put it on the scale. Is the good something we should have confidence in? Yes. Is pleasure something we can have confidence in? No — it is insecure. Then pleasure is not the good.

Should we be elated over what is genuinely good? Yes. Should we be elated over pleasure? If you say yes, I will not think you worthy of even using the scale. This is how things are tested and weighed when the rules are established. Philosophy is first examining and confirming the rules, then applying them. The first task is the philosopher's. The second is the task of the wise and good person.
D: Elegant: Put pleasure on the scale. Is the good something we can rely on? Yes. Can we rely on pleasure? No. Remove it. Philosophy is first confirming the rules, then applying them. The first is theory. The second is life.
D: Distilled: Put pleasure on the scale: if it is insecure, it is not the good — and philosophy is the discipline of making this test and living by the result.
Notes: The 'pleasure test' is directed at Epicurean ethics, which identified the good with pleasure (hēdonē). Epictetus's method — test by necessary conditions (is it reliable? does it merit confidence?) — shows Stoic ethical reasoning in action. The distinction between 'examining rules' (philosopher's task) and 'using rules' (wise person's task) suggests that philosophy is preparation for wisdom, not wisdom itself. This connects to the framework's view that philosophy is the methodology that enables wisdom.

D: Original: Chapter 12: Of disputation or discussion

What things a man must learn in order to be able to apply the art of disputation, has been accurately shown by our philosophers; but with respect to the proper use of the things, we are entirely without practice. Only give to any of us, whom you please, an illiterate man to discuss with,, and he cannot discover how to deal with the man. But when he has moved the man a little, if he answers beside the purpose, he does not know how to treat him, but he then either abuses or ridicules him, and says, "He is an illiterate man; it is not possible to do anything with him." Now a guide, when he has found a man out of the road leads him into the right way: he does not ridicule or abuse him and then leave him. Do you also show this illiterate man the truth, and you will see that he follows. But so long as you do not show him the truth, do not ridicule him, but rather feel your own incapacity.
D: Refashioned:
We know the theory of argument well enough. But when faced with an actual illiterate person who needs convincing, we cannot manage it. The man answers beside the point — and instead of guiding him, we ridicule him and walk away.

A guide who finds a traveler lost does not mock him and leave — he shows him the road. Do the same. Show the man the truth, and he will follow. As long as you cannot do that, recognize your own incapacity rather than blaming his ignorance.
D: Elegant: A guide who finds a lost traveler does not mock him and leave — he shows the road. If you cannot show the truth to an uneducated person, the failure is yours, not his.
D: Distilled: If you cannot guide the ignorant toward truth, the failure is yours — a guide does not mock the lost.
Notes: The guide/traveler analogy frames philosophical teaching as a service, not a display. Epictetus's critique targets students who confuse intellectual superiority with philosophical skill: true skill is the ability to lead others to understanding, not to demonstrate your own. This connects to the framework's concept of education as guided formation rather than mere content delivery.

D: Original: How then did Socrates act? He used to compel his adversary in disputation to bear testimony to him, and he wanted no other witness. Therefore he could say, "I care not for other witnesses, but I am always satisfied with the evidence of my adversary, and I do not ask the opinion of others, but only the opinion of him who is disputing with me." For he used to make the conclusions drawn from natural notions so plain that every man saw the contradiction and withdrew from it: "Does the envious man rejoice?" "By no means, but he is rather pained." Well, "Do you think that envy is pain over evils? and what envy is there of evils?" Therefore he made his adversary say that envy is pain over good things. "Well then, would any man envy those who are nothing to him?" "By no means." Thus having completed the notion and distinctly fixed it he would go away without saying to his adversary, "Define to me envy"; and if the adversary had defined envy, he did not say, "You have defined it badly, for the terms of the definition do not correspond to the thing defined." These are technical terms, and for this reason disagreeable and hardly intelligible to illiterate men, which terms we cannot lay aside. But that the illiterate man himself, who follows the appearances presented to him, should be able to concede anything or reject it, we can never by the use of these terms move him to do. Accordingly, being conscious of our own inability, we do not attempt the thing; at least such of us as have any caution do not. But the greater part and the rash, when they enter into such disputations, confuse themselves and confuse others; and finally abusing their adversaries and abused by them, they walk away.
D: Refashioned:
Socrates's method was to compel his interlocutor to become his own witness. He never needed external evidence — only the testimony of the person he was arguing with. He drew conclusions from natural notions so clearly that the contradiction became visible and the person withdrew from it on their own.

He would ask: 'Does the envious person rejoice?' 'No — they are pained.' 'Pained by what? By evils? But who envies evils? Then envy is pain over another person's good.' Through questions alone, he assembled the definition without ever demanding that the man define the term in technical language.

Technical terminology is what drives ordinary people away. We cannot set it aside, and so we fail to move them. The cautious among us recognize this incapacity. The rest plunge in, confuse everyone including themselves, and walk away trading insults.
D: Elegant: Socrates never needed external evidence — only the testimony of the person he argued with. He drew conclusions from natural notions until the contradiction became visible and the person withdrew from it on their own. We use technical terms they cannot follow, then blame them for not following.
D: Distilled: Socrates made the contradiction visible through questions alone — and the person withdrew from their error on their own.
Notes: The elenctic method (Socratic cross-examination) — drawing out contradictions from the interlocutor's own premises — is Epictetus's model for philosophical dialogue. The envy example (phthonos as pain over another's good) shows the method in miniature. The critique of technical terminology (technikai phonai) reflects Epictetus's preference for plain speech. This connects to the framework's emphasis on accessibility: philosophy that cannot reach ordinary people has failed at its most important task.

D: Original: Now this was the first and chief peculiarity of Socrates, never to be irritated in argument, never to utter anything abusive, anything insulting, but to bear with abusive persons and to put an end to the quarrel. If you would know what great power he had in this way, read the Symposium of Xenophon, and you will see how many quarrels he put an end to. Hence with good reason in the poets also this power is most highly praised,
D: Refashioned: The first and chief quality of Socrates was this: he was never irritated in argument, never abusive, never insulting. He bore insults from others and brought quarrels to an end. This power is praised even by the poets.
D: Elegant: Socrates's chief quality: never irritated, never abusive, never insulting — he bore insults and ended quarrels. Even the poets praised this power.
D: Distilled: The chief quality of Socrates was not his intellect but his refusal to be irritated, insulted, or provoked in argument.
Notes: Xenophon's Symposium — Epictetus references this rather than Plato's more famous work, likely because Xenophon's version better illustrates Socrates's social skills and ability to manage group dynamics. 'Even the poets praised this' — possibly a reference to a line from Euripides or another tragedian used in Stoic anthologies. This connects to the framework's concept of Reason as temperament, not just intellect.

D: Original:
Quickly with the skill he settles great disputes.

Well then; the matter is not now very safe, and particularly at Rome; for he who attempts to do it, must not do it in a corner, you may be sure, but must go to a man of consular rank, if it so happen, or to a rich man, and ask him, "Can you tell me, Sir, to whose care you have entrusted your horses?" "I can tell you." Here you entrusted them to a person indifferently and to one who has no experience of horses? "By no means." Well then; can you tell me to whom you entrust your gold or silver things or your vestments? "I don't entrust even these to anyone indifferently." Well; your own body, have you already considered about entrusting the care of it to any person? "Certainly." To a man of experience, I suppose, and one acquainted with the aliptic, or with the healing art? "Without a doubt." Are these the best things that you have, or do you also possess something else which is better than all these? "What kind of thing do you mean?" That I mean which makes use of these things, and tests each of these things and deliberates. "Is it the soul that you mean?" You think right, for it is the soul that I mean. "In truth I do think the soul is a much better thing than all the others which I possess." Can you then show us in what way you have taken care of the soul? for it is not likely that you, who are so wise a man and have a reputation in the city, inconsiderately and carelessly allow the most valuable thing that you possess to be neglected and to perish? "Certainly not." But have you taken care of the soul yourself; and have you learned from another to do this, or have you discovered the means yourself? Here comes the danger that in the first place he may say, "What is this to you, my good man, who are you?" Next, if you persist in troubling him, there is a danger that he may raise his hands and give you blows. I was once myself also an admirer of this mode of instruction until I fell into these dangers.
D: Refashioned:
'Quickly with skill he settles great disputes.' But this is not safe work — especially in Rome. You cannot do it in a corner. You must approach the powerful directly: 'To whom have you entrusted your horses? Your silver? Your body? And your soul — have you entrusted that to anyone with skill?'

But here comes the danger. The man you question may not appreciate the inquiry. He is rich, respected, connected — and you are asking whether he has neglected the most important thing he possesses. He may raise his fist. Epictetus himself once admired this Socratic method — until he tried it and encountered the consequences.
D: Elegant: You must approach the powerful: 'You have entrusted your horses, your silver, your body to skilled keepers. To whom have you entrusted your soul?' But here comes the danger — the powerful do not appreciate the question. I once admired this method, until I tried it.
D: Distilled: Asking the powerful whether they have neglected their souls is the most important question — and the most dangerous to ask.
Notes: The passage adapts Socrates's method (Plato, Apology 29d-30a) to Roman social reality: philosophical questioning of elites risks physical violence, not just intellectual embarrassment. Epictetus's confession — 'I fell into these dangers' — humanizes the teaching: even the master had to learn the limits of confrontational philosophy. The 'aliptic art' (aliptikē) refers to the art of the trainer who anoints and massages athletes. This connects to the framework's realism about institutional power: speaking truth to power is necessary but costly.

D: Original: Chapter 13: On anxiety

When I see a man anxious, I say, "What does this man want? If he did not want something which is not in his power, how could he be anxious?" For this reason a lute player when he is singing by himself has no anxiety, but when he enters the theatre, he is anxious even if he has a good voice and plays well on the lute; for he not only wishes to sing well, but also to obtain applause: but this is not in his power. Accordingly, where he has skill, there he has confidence. Bring any single person who knows nothing of music, and the musician does not care for him.
D: Refashioned:
When I see an anxious person, I know exactly what is wrong: they want something not in their power. A musician who sings alone at home is confident. The moment they enter the theatre, they become anxious — not because their skill has changed but because they now want applause, and applause is not in their power.

Where you have skill and knowledge, you have confidence. Where you want something beyond your control, you have anxiety.
D: Elegant: The musician sings confidently at home. In the theatre, anxiety arrives — not because the skill changed but because the desire did. Anxiety always means wanting something not in your power.
D: Distilled: Anxiety is the infallible sign that you want something not in your power.
Notes: The musician analogy is psychologically precise: the addition of an audience introduces a variable outside the performer's control (applause), which transforms confidence into anxiety. Epictetus's diagnostic method — 'when I see an anxious person' — treats anxiety as a symptom to be read, not a feeling to be managed. This connects to the framework's approach to emotional disturbance: anxiety is not a problem to be treated but a signal to be understood.

D: Original: But in the matter where a man knows nothing and has not been practiced, there he is anxious. What matter is this? He knows not what a crowd is or what the praise of a crowd is. However he has learned to strike the lowest chord and the highest; but what the praise of the many is, and what power it has in life he neither knows nor has he thought about it. Hence he must of necessity tremble and grow pale. I cannot then say that a man is not a lute player when I see him afraid, but I can say something else, and not one thing, but many. And first of all I call him a stranger and say, "This man does not know in what part of the world he is, but though he has been here so long, he is ignorant of the laws of the State and the customs, and what is permitted and what is not; and he has never employed any lawyer to tell him and to explain the laws." But a man does not write a will, if he does not does not know how it ought to be written, or he employs a person who does know; nor does he rashly seal a bond or write a security.
D: Refashioned: The musician knows music but does not know what a crowd is or what the power of praise is. He has never studied these things. He is a stranger in his own city — ignorant of the laws that govern reputation and its value. A person would not write a will without legal advice or seal a document without knowing the form. Yet they deploy their desires and aversions — the most consequential things in life — without any training at all.
D: Elegant: The musician knows music but not what a crowd is or what praise is worth. He is a stranger in his own city. We would not write a will without legal advice — yet we deploy our desires and aversions without any training at all.
D: Distilled: We would not write a legal document without training, yet we deploy our desires and aversions — the most consequential forces in life — with no training at all.
Notes: The 'stranger in his own city' metaphor — not knowing the laws of the place where you live — extends the political analogy: ignorance of ethical principles is a form of alienation from one's own nature. The legal document comparison grounds the abstract point: we recognize the need for expertise in trivial matters but not in the one that matters most.

D: Original: But he uses his desire without a lawyer's advice, and aversion, and pursuit, and attempt and purpose. "How do you mean without a lawyer?" He does not know that he wills what is not allowed, and does not will that which is of necessity; and he does not know either what is his own or what is or what is another man's; but if he did know, he could never be impeded, he would never be hindered, he would not be anxious. "How so?" Is any man then afraid about things which are not evil? "No." Is he afraid about things which are evils, but still so far within his power that they may not happen? "Certainly he is not." If, then, the things which are independent of the will are neither good nor bad, and all things which do depend on the will are within our power, and no man can either take them from us or give them to us, if we do not choose, where is room left for anxiety? But we are anxious about our poor body, our little property, about the will of Caesar; but not anxious about things internal. Are we anxious about not forming a false opinion? No, for this is in my power. About not exerting our movements contrary to nature? No, not even about this. When then you see a man pale, as the physician says, judging from the complexion, this man's spleen is disordered, that man's liver; so also say, this man's desire and aversion are disordered, he is not in the right way, he is in a fever.
D: Refashioned:
He uses desire and aversion without counsel. He does not know that he wills what is not permitted and fails to will what is necessary. If he understood what was his and what was another's, he would never be impeded or anxious.

Is anyone afraid of things that are not evil? No. Is anyone afraid of evils they can prevent? No. Then if things outside the will are neither good nor bad, and everything within the will is in our power — where is room for anxiety?

But we are anxious about our bodies, our property, the opinions of those in power — never about what is internal. We are never anxious about forming a false opinion. When you see a pale, trembling person, diagnose them: their desire and aversion are disordered, as surely as a physician diagnoses a disordered spleen from a pale complexion.
D: Elegant: Anxiety about externals. Never about forming a false opinion. When you see a pale, trembling person, the diagnosis is clear: desire and aversion are disordered. Treat anxiety as a physician treats a symptom — it tells you exactly what is wrong.
D: Distilled: When you see an anxious person, the diagnosis is simple: their desire and aversion are aimed at things outside their control.
Notes: The medical diagnostic model — reading anxiety as a physician reads complexion — is characteristic of the Stoic-as-physician tradition (Galen explicitly connected Stoic philosophy with medical practice). The list of things we worry about (body, property, Caesar's opinion) versus things we never worry about (forming false opinions) exposes the inversion of priorities. This connects to the framework's emphasis on directing concern toward what can actually be improved.

D: Original:
For nothing else changes the color, or causes trembling or chattering of the teeth, or causes a man to

Sink in his knees and shift from foot to foot.
D: Refashioned: Nothing else causes the pallor, the trembling, the weak knees — nothing except the disorder of desire and aversion directed at things outside our power.
D: Elegant: Nothing else causes the trembling — nothing except desire and aversion aimed at what you cannot control.
D: Distilled: Every symptom of anxiety traces to one cause: desire or aversion directed at what is not in your power.
Notes: The quoted poetry ('sink in his knees and shift from foot to foot') is from Homer (Iliad 13.281 or similar), describing a warrior's fear. Epictetus reads physical symptoms of fear as diagnostic evidence of philosophical disorder. This brief passage serves as a transitional summary.

D: Original: For this reason when Zeno was going to meet Antigonus, he was not anxious, for Antigonus had no power over any of the things which Zeno admired; and Zeno did not care for those things over which Antigonus had power. But Antigonus was anxious when he was going to meet Zeno, for he wished to please Zeno; but this was a thing external. But Zeno did not want to please Antigonus; for no man who is skilled in any art wishes to please one who has no such skill.
D: Refashioned:
When Zeno went to meet King Antigonus, he felt no anxiety — because Antigonus had no power over anything Zeno valued. But Antigonus was anxious about meeting Zeno — because he wanted to please Zeno, and pleasing a philosopher was not in his power.

The king feared the philosopher. The philosopher did not fear the king. Whoever has power over the things you care about is your master — and Zeno's cares were beyond any king's reach.
D: Elegant: Zeno felt no anxiety meeting the king — the king had no power over what Zeno valued. But the king was anxious meeting the philosopher — because he wanted to please, and pleasing was not in his power. The king feared the philosopher.
D: Distilled: Zeno did not fear the king because the king had no power over anything Zeno valued — and the king feared the philosopher for the same reason in reverse.
Notes: Zeno of Citium (c. 334-262 BCE) — founder of Stoicism. Antigonus II Gonatas — King of Macedon, known admirer of Stoic philosophy who reportedly invited Zeno to his court. The power inversion — the philosopher is secure, the king is anxious — illustrates the Stoic claim that true power belongs to those whose desires are aligned with what they control. This connects to the framework's concept of the ideational domain: intellectual sovereignty is more fundamental than political power.

D: Original: Should I try to please you? Why? I suppose, you know the measure by which one man is estimated by another. Have you taken pains to learn what is a good man and what is a bad man, and how a man becomes one or the other? Why, then, are you not good yourself? "How," he replies, "am I not good?" Because no good man laments or roans or weeps, no good man is pale and trembles, or says, "How will he receive me, how will he listen to me?" Slave, just as it pleases him. Why do you care about what belongs to others? Is it now his fault if he receives badly what proceeds from you? "Certainly." And is it possible that a fault should be one man's, and the evil in another? "No." Why then are you anxious about that which belongs to others? "Your question is reasonable; but I am anxious how I shall speak to him." Cannot you then speak to him as you choose? "But I fear that I may be disconcerted?" If you are going to write the name of Dion, are you afraid that you would be disconcerted? "By no means." Why? is it not because you have practiced writing the name? "Certainly." Well, if you were going to read the name, would you not feel the same? and why? Because every art has a certain strength and confidence in the things which belong to it.
