Have you then not practiced speaking? and what else did you learn in the school? Syllogisms and sophistical propositions? For what purpose? was it not for the purpose of discoursing skillfully? and is not discoursing skillfully the same as discoursing seasonably and cautiously and with intelligence, and also without making mistakes and without hindrance, and besides all this with confidence? "Yes." When, then, you are mounted on a horse and go into a plain, are you anxious at being matched against a man who is on foot, and anxious in a matter in which you are practiced, and he is not? "Yes, but that person has power to kill me." Speak the truth then, unhappy man, and do not brag, nor claim to be a philosopher, nor refuse to acknowledge your masters, but so long as you present this handle in your body, follow every man who is stronger than yourself. Socrates used to practice speaking, he who talked as he did to the tyrants, to the dicasts, he who talked in his prison. Diogenes had practiced speaking, he who spoke as he did to Alexander, to the pirates, to the person who bought him. These men were confident in the things which they practiced.
D: Refashioned:
'Should I try to please you?' Why? Do you know the standard by which one person judges another? Have you studied what makes a person good or bad? No good person laments, groans, trembles, or says, 'How will he receive me?' If you are doing that, you are not good — you are a slave, regardless of your title.

You are afraid of how you will speak to someone important. But have you not been trained in speaking? You studied syllogisms and arguments — for what purpose, if not for this? When mounted on a horse facing a man on foot, are you anxious? You have the advantage. The trouble is that you present a 'handle' — your body, your property — to anyone stronger, and as long as that handle exists, you are vulnerable. Socrates practiced speaking — that is why he spoke as he did to tyrants, judges, and jailers. Diogenes practiced — that is why he spoke as he did to Alexander and to pirates. Confidence comes from practice, not from talent.
D: Elegant: No good person trembles and asks, 'How will he receive me?' If you do, you are a slave. Socrates practiced speaking — that is why he spoke to tyrants without fear. Diogenes practiced — that is why he spoke to Alexander. Confidence comes from practice, not talent.
D: Distilled: Confidence before the powerful comes from practice, not talent — Socrates and Diogenes spoke fearlessly because they had trained for it.
Notes: Diogenes confronting Alexander the Great ('Stand aside, you're blocking my sun') is one of philosophy's most famous encounters. The 'handle' (labē) metaphor — your body and property as a grip by which others control you — is a key Stoic image. Socrates speaking to the Thirty Tyrants, to his judges, and in prison exemplifies the three contexts where philosophical training is tested. This connects to the framework's emphasis on preparation: courage in the moment is the product of discipline in advance.

D: Original: But do you walk off to your own affairs and never leave them: go and sit in a corner, and weave syllogisms, and propose them to another. There is not in you the man who can rule a state.
D: Refashioned: As for you — go sit in your corner and weave syllogisms. The person who can rule a state is not in you.
D: Elegant: Go sit in your corner and weave syllogisms. There is not in you the person who can rule a state.
D: Distilled: If your philosophy does not produce the capacity to face power without trembling, it is not philosophy — it is decoration.
Notes: One of Epictetus's most brutal dismissals, closing the chapter on anxiety. The contrast between 'weaving syllogisms in a corner' and 'ruling a state' encapsulates his critique: philosophy that does not produce practical courage is merely academic. This connects to the framework's insistence that wisdom must be tested in action, not preserved in isolation.

D: Original: Chapter 14: To Naso

When a certain Roman entered with his son and listened to one reading, Epictetus said, "This is the method of instruction"; and he stopped. When the Roman asked him to go on, Epictetus said: Every art, when it is taught, causes labour to him who is unacquainted with it and is unskilled in it, and indeed the things which proceed from the arts immediately show their use in the purpose for which they were made; and most of them contain something attractive and pleasing. For indeed to be present and to observe how a shoemaker learns is not a pleasant thing; but the shoe is useful and also not disagreeable to look at. And the discipline of a smith when he is learning is very disagreeable to one who chances to be present and is a stranger to the art: but the work shows the use of the art. But you will see this much more in music; for if you are present while a person is learning, the discipline will appear most disagreeable; and yet the results of music are pleasing and delightful to those who know nothing of music. And here we conceive the work of a philosopher to be something of this kind: he must adapt his wish to what is going on, so that neither any of the things which are taking place shall take place contrary to our wish, nor any of the things which do not take place shall not take place when we wish that they should. From this the result is to those who have so arranged the work of philosophy, not to fall in the desire, nor to fall in with that which they would avoid; without uneasiness, without fear, without perturbation to pass through life themselves, together with their associates maintaining the relations both natural and acquired, as the relation of son, of father, of brother, of citizen, of man, of wife, of neighbour, of fellow-traveler, of ruler, of ruled. The work of a philosopher we conceive to be something like this. It remains next to inquire how this must be accomplished.
D: Refashioned:
When a Roman entered with his son and listened to a reading, Epictetus paused to explain: every art involves disagreeable training. Learning shoemaking is tedious, but the shoe is useful. Learning metalwork is unpleasant, but the product is valuable. Music instruction sounds terrible to the uninitiated, but the results delight everyone.

The work of philosophy is similar but higher: to arrange our desires so that nothing happens against our wish, and nothing fails to happen that we wish for. The result is a life without anxiety, fear, or perturbation — maintaining all relationships naturally: parent, child, citizen, spouse, neighbor, ruler, ruled. This is what philosophy produces. How to achieve it is what we must inquire.
D: Elegant: Learning any art is disagreeable — but the product justifies the labor. Philosophy's product is the highest: a life without anxiety, maintaining every relationship naturally. The training is hard. The result is worth everything.
D: Distilled: The work of philosophy is to arrange desire so that nothing happens against your will and nothing you wish for fails — and the training is worth the result.
Notes: Naso — a Roman visitor, possibly the father of a prospective student. Epictetus's list of relationships (son, father, brother, citizen, man, wife, neighbor, traveler, ruler, ruled) maps to his role ethics (prosōpon theory). The art/training analogy grounds the philosophical life in the familiar experience of skill acquisition. This connects to the framework's concept of philosophy as practical discipline: disagreeable in the learning, transformative in the application.

D: Original: We see then that the carpenter when he has learned certain things becomes a carpenter; the pilot by learning certain things becomes a pilot. May it not, then, in philosophy also not be sufficient to wish to be wise and good, and that there is also a necessity to learn certain things? We inquire then what these things are. The philosophers say that we ought first to learn that there is a God and that he provides for all things; also that it is not possible to conceal from him our acts, or even our intentions and thoughts. The next thing, is to learn what is the nature of the Gods; for such as they are discovered to be, he, who would please and obey them, must try with all his power to be like them. If the divine is faithful, man also must be faithful; if it is free, man also must be free; if beneficent, man also must be beneficent; if magnanimous, man also must be magnanimous; as being, then an imitator of God, he must do and say everything consistently with this fact.
D: Refashioned:
A carpenter learns carpentry to become a carpenter. A pilot learns navigation to become a pilot. Is it not the same with philosophy — that wishing to be wise is not enough, and certain things must be learned?

The philosophers say: first, learn that God exists and provides for all things, and that our actions and even our thoughts cannot be hidden from him. Second, learn the nature of the gods — for we must try to resemble what we discover them to be. If the divine is faithful, we must be faithful. If free, we must be free. If beneficent, we must be beneficent. As imitators of God, we must make every word and action consistent with this fact.
D: Elegant: Wishing to be wise is not enough — certain things must be learned. First: God exists and sees everything. Second: the divine nature is faithful, free, and beneficent — and we must become the same. Every action must be consistent with this imitation.
D: Distilled: Wisdom requires learning two things: that God exists and oversees everything, and that our task is to imitate the divine nature.
Notes: The imitatio dei (imitation of God) doctrine is one of the most positive formulations of Stoic ethics: rather than merely avoiding vice, the goal is to approximate divine qualities. The two-step curriculum — (1) divine existence and providence, (2) divine nature as the model for human behavior — provides a complete ethical framework. This connects to the framework's aspiration: not merely to avoid error but to cultivate the highest human capacities.

D: Original: "With what then must we begin?" If you will enter on the discussion, I will tell you that you must first understand names. "So, then, you say that I do not now understand names?" You do not understand them. "How, then, do I use them?" Just as the illiterate use written language, as cattle use appearances: for use is one thing, understanding is another. But if you think that you understand them, produce whatever word you please, and let us try whether we understand it. But it is a disagreeable thing for a man to be confuted who is now old and, it may be, has now served his three campaigns. I too know this: for now you are come to me as if you were in want of nothing: and what could you even imagine to be wanting to you? You are rich, you have children, and a wife, perhaps and many slaves: Caesar knows you, in Rome you have many friends, you render their dues to all, you know how to requite him who does you a favour, and to repay in the same kind him who does a wrong. What do you lack? If, then, I shall show you that you lack the things most necessary and the chief things for happiness, and that hitherto you have looked after everything rather than what you ought, and, to crown all, that you neither know what God is nor what man is, nor what is good nor what is bad; and as to what I have said about your ignorance of other matters, that may perhaps be endured, but if I say that you know nothing about yourself, how is it possible that you should endure me and bear the proof and stay here? It is not possible; but you immediately go off in bad humour. And yet what harm have I done you? unless the mirror also injures the ugly man because it shows him to himself such as he is; unless the physician also is supposed to insult the sick man, when he says to him, "Man, do you think that you ail nothing? But you have a fever: go without food to-day; drink water." And no one says, "What an insult!" But if you say to a man, "Your desires are inflamed, your aversions are low, your intentions are inconsistent, your pursuits are not comfortable to nature, your opinions are rash and false," the man immediately goes away and says, "he has insulted me."
D: Refashioned:
Where must we begin? With understanding terms. You think you already understand them — good, bad, just, unjust — but you use them the way illiterate people use written words: mechanically, without comprehension.

This is uncomfortable to hear. A man who is old, wealthy, respected, who has served three military campaigns and knows Caesar personally — he does not expect to be told he lacks self-knowledge. But if I show you that your desires are inflamed, your aversions misplaced, your judgments rash — you will be insulted. A doctor who says 'you have a fever' is not insulting you. A philosopher who says 'your opinions are disordered' should not be insulting you either. But the first you accept and the second you reject — and that rejection is itself the disease.
D: Elegant: You think you understand the terms — good, bad, just. You use them the way the illiterate use writing: mechanically, without comprehension. A doctor who says 'you have a fever' is not insulting you. Neither is a philosopher who says 'your opinions are disordered.' That you reject the second diagnosis is itself the disease.
D: Distilled: Rejecting the diagnosis that your opinions are disordered is itself the most telling symptom of the disorder.
Notes: The mirror metaphor — 'the mirror injures the ugly man because it shows him to himself' — captures the paradox of philosophical education: the person who needs it most is the person who resists it most. The Roman details (three military campaigns, knowing Caesar) sketch the typical student: accomplished by worldly standards, philosophically untrained. This connects to the framework's observation about the relationship between status and self-knowledge: the more successful the person, the harder it is to show them what they lack.

D: Original: Our way of dealing is like that of a crowded assembly. Beasts are brought to be sold and oxen; and the greater part of the men come to buy and sell, and there are some few who come to look at the market and to inquire how it is carried on, and why, and who fixes the meeting and for what purpose. So it is here also in this assembly: some like cattle trouble themselves about nothing except their fodder. For to all of you who are busy about possessions and lands and slaves and magisterial offices, these are nothing except fodder. But there are a few who attend the assembly, men who love to look on and consider what is the world, who governs it. Has it no governor? And how is it possible that a city or a family cannot continue to exist, not even the shortest time without an administrator and guardian, and that so great and beautiful a system should be administered with such order and yet without a purpose and by chance? There is then an administrator. What kind of administrator and how does he govern? And who are we, who were produced by him, and for what purpose? Have we some connection with him and some relation toward him, or none? This is the way in which these few are affected, and then they apply themselves only to this one thing, to examine the meeting and then to go away. What then? They are ridiculed by the many, as the spectators at the fair are by the traders; and if the beasts had any understanding, they would ridicule those who admired anything else than fodder.
D: Refashioned:
Life is like a crowded market. Most people come to buy and sell — to acquire possessions, property, offices. They are cattle attending to their fodder.

But a few come to look at the assembly itself and ask: what is this world? Who governs it? Does it have a governor at all? What are we, and what were we made for? These people devote themselves to this one inquiry, then leave. The traders mock them. If the cattle had understanding, they would mock anyone who admired anything other than grass.

But the question remains: are we here to trade, or are we here to understand?
D: Elegant: Most people come to the market to buy and sell — cattle attending to fodder. A few come to ask: what is this world? Who governs it? Why are we here? The traders mock them. If the cattle could think, they would mock anyone who looked up from the grass.
D: Distilled: Most people come to the market of life to trade — a few come to understand what the market is and why it exists.
Notes: The market/assembly metaphor (panēgyris) draws from the Greek festival tradition where philosophy, commerce, and spectacle coexisted. The 'cattle mocking the spectators' inverts the expected hierarchy. This connects to the framework's central argument: the pursuit of understanding is civilization's highest purpose, though it is routinely dismissed by those focused on material acquisition.

D: Original: Chapter 15: To or against those who obstinately persist in what they have determined

When some persons have heard these words, that a man ought to be constant, and that the will is naturally free and not subject to compulsion, but that all other things are subject to hindrance, to slavery, and are in the power of others, they suppose that they ought without deviation to abide by everything which they have determined. But in the first place that which has been determined ought to be sound. I require tone in the body, but such as exists in a healthy body, in an athletic body; but if it is plain to me that you have the tone of a frenzied man and you boast of it, I shall say to you, "Man, seek the physician": this is not tone, but atony. In a different way something of the same kind is felt by those who listen to these discourses in a wrong manner; which was the case with one of my companions who for no reason resolved to starve himself to death. I heard of it when it was the third day of his abstinence from food and I went to inquire what had happened. "I have resolved," he said. But still tell me what it was which induced you to resolve; for if you have resolved rightly, we shall sit with you and assist you to depart; but if you have made an unreasonable resolution, change your mind. "We ought to keep to
D: Refashioned:
Some people hear that the will is naturally free and that a person should be constant, and they conclude they should rigidly stick to whatever they have decided. But the first requirement is that the decision be sound. Rigidity without soundness is not strength — it is the tone of a sick body.

A companion of Epictetus once resolved to starve himself to death for no good reason. Epictetus went to him on the third day: 'Tell me what moved you.' The man could not give a satisfactory answer. Epictetus said: if you have resolved rightly, we will sit with you and assist your departure. But if the resolution is unreasonable, change your mind. We must keep our resolutions — but only those that deserve to be kept.
D: Elegant: Constancy without soundness is not strength — it is the tone of a sick body. If your resolution is right, I will sit with you. If it is unreasonable, change your mind. We must keep our resolutions — but only those that deserve to be kept.
D: Distilled: Constancy is a virtue only when the resolution is sound — rigidity without reason is not strength but sickness.
Notes: The chapter's title — 'when we cannot fulfill the character of a man, we assume the character of a philosopher' — is ironic: adopting philosophical constancy as a pose rather than a practice. The starving companion illustrates misapplied Stoic teaching: treating indifference to death as permission for arbitrary self-destruction. Epictetus's response is nuanced: he does not condemn voluntary death absolutely but demands that the reasoning be examined. This connects to the framework's emphasis on examined conviction: commitment must be grounded in reason, not mere stubbornness.

D: Original: Chapter 1: Of finery in dress

A certain young man a rhetorician came to see Epictetus, with his hair dressed more carefully than was usual and his attire in an ornamental style; whereupon Epictetus said: Tell me you do not think that some dogs are beautiful and some horses, and so of all other animals. "I do think so," the youth replied. Are not then some men also beautiful and others ugly? "Certainly." Do we, then, for the same reason call each of them in the same kind beautiful, or each beautiful for something peculiar? And you will judge of this matter thus. Since we see a dog naturally formed for one thing, and a horse for another, and for another still, as an example, a nightingale, we may generally and not improperly declare each of them to be beautiful then when it is most excellent according to its nature; but since the nature of each is different, each of them seems to me to be beautiful in a different way. Is it not so? He admitted that it was. That then which makes a dog beautiful, makes a horse ugly; and that which makes a horse beautiful, makes a dog ugly, if it is true that their natures are different. "It seems to be so." For I think that what makes a pancratiast beautiful, makes a wrestler to be not good, and a runner to be most ridiculous; and he who is beautiful for the Pentathlon, is very ugly for wrestling. "It is so," said he. What, then, makes a man beautiful? Is that which in its kind makes both a dog and a horse beautiful? "It is," he said.
D: Refashioned:
A young man walked into Epictetus's school with elaborate hair and flashy clothes. Epictetus began simply: some dogs are beautiful and some are ugly, yes? Same with horses? The young man agreed. Then what makes each beautiful? Excellence at being what it is. A dog is beautiful when it excels as a dog. A horse when it excels as a horse. An athlete built for the pentathlon looks ridiculous as a wrestler.

So what makes a human being beautiful? The same principle: excellence at being human. Not appearance — function.
D: Elegant: A beautiful dog excels as a dog. A beautiful horse excels as a horse. A pentathlon champion looks ridiculous as a wrestler. What makes a human being beautiful? The same principle: excellence at being what you are.
D: Distilled: Beauty in any creature is excellence at being what it is — and what makes a human beautiful is excellence at being human.
Notes: The pancratium (pankration) was an ancient Greek combat sport combining wrestling and boxing. The pentathlon combined five events (discus, javelin, long jump, stadion race, wrestling). Epictetus's argument from analogy — each species has its own form of excellence (arete) — is directly Aristotelian. This maps to the framework's concept of Right: beauty is not decoration but alignment with your nature.

D: Original: What then makes a dog beautiful? The possession of the excellence of a dog. And what makes a horse beautiful? The possession of the excellence of a horse. What then makes a man beautiful? Is it not the possession of the excellence of a man? And do you, then, if you wish to be beautiful, young man, labour at this, the acquisition of human excellence. But what is this? Observe whom you yourself praise, when you praise many persons without partiality: do you praise the just or the unjust? "The just." Whether do you praise the moderate or the immoderate? "The moderate." And the temperate or the intemperate? "The temperate." If, then, you make yourself such a person, you will know that you will make yourself beautiful: but so long as you neglect these things, you must be ugly, even though you contrive all you can to appear beautiful.
D: Refashioned:
What is human excellence, then? When you praise people without bias, who do you praise? The just over the unjust. The moderate over the excessive. The disciplined over the undisciplined.

Make yourself just, moderate, and disciplined, and you will be beautiful — regardless of how you look. Neglect these and obsess over your appearance, and you will be ugly no matter what you contrive.
D: Elegant: When you praise people honestly, you praise the just, the moderate, the disciplined. Become those things and you are beautiful. Neglect them and you are ugly — no matter what you contrive.
D: Distilled: Human beauty is justice, moderation, and discipline — everything else is costume.
Notes: The terms Epictetus uses — dikaios (just), sophron (moderate), enkrates (self-controlled) — are the classical cardinal virtues. The argument is that aesthetic judgments about people, when honest, are actually moral judgments. This connects to the framework's emphasis on character as the substance beneath all surface presentation.

D: Original: Further I do not know what to say to you: for if I say to you what I think, I shall offend you, and you will perhaps leave the school and not return to it: and if I do not say what I think, see how I shall be acting, if you come to me to be improved, and I shall not improve you at all, and if you come to me as to a philosopher, and I shall say nothing to you as a philosopher. And how cruel it is to you to leave you uncorrected. If at any time afterward you shall acquire sense, you will with good reason blame me and say, "What did Epictetus observe in me that, when he saw me in such a plight coming to him in such a scandalous condition, he neglected me and never said a word? did he so much despair of me? was I not young? was I not able to listen to reason? and how many other young men at this age commit many like errors? I hear that a certain Polemon from being a most dissolute youth underwent such a great change. Well, suppose that he did not think that I should be a Polemon; yet he might have set my hair right, he might have stripped off my decorations, he might have stopped me from plucking the hair out of my body; but when he saw me dressed like- what shall I say?- he kept silent." I do not say like what; but you will say, when you come to your senses and shall know what it is and what persons use such a dress.
D: Refashioned:
Epictetus hesitates — speaking the truth might drive the student away. But staying silent would be worse. A teacher who sees a student heading for disaster and says nothing has failed at his one job.

He imagines the student's future self looking back: 'Epictetus saw me in that condition and said nothing? Did he think I was beyond help? I was young — I could have listened. I hear that Polemon was just as dissolute and turned his life around.' The risk of saying too much is that the student leaves offended. The risk of saying nothing is that the student never changes.
D: Elegant: A teacher who sees disaster coming and stays silent has failed at his one job. The risk of speaking is that you offend. The risk of silence is that nothing changes. Polemon was just as dissolute — and he turned his whole life around.
D: Distilled: A teacher who stays silent to avoid giving offense has chosen comfort over duty.
Notes: Polemon of Athens (d. c. 270 BCE) — head of Plato's Academy, famously converted from dissolute youth to serious philosopher after wandering drunk into Xenocrates' lecture. Epictetus uses him as evidence that even extreme cases can change. The passage reveals Epictetus's pedagogical philosophy: the teacher's duty is to truth, not to the student's comfort.

D: Original: If you bring this charge against me hereafter, what defense shall I make? Why, shall I say that the man will not be persuaded by me? Was Laius persuaded by Apollo? Did he and get drunk and show no care for the oracle? Well then, for this reason did Apollo refuse to tell him the truth? I indeed do not know, whether you will be persuaded by me or not; but Apollo knew most certainly that Laius would not be persuaded and yet he spoke. But why did he speak? I say in reply: But why is he Apollo, and why does he deliver oracles, and why has he fixed himself in this place as a prophet and source of truth and for the inhabitants of the world to resort to him? and why are the words "Know yourself" written in front of the temple, though no person takes any notice of them?
D: Refashioned:
And if the student does not listen? That is not a reason to stay silent. Apollo warned Laius about the prophecy, knowing Laius would ignore it and get drunk instead. Why did Apollo speak? Because that is what Apollo does. He is a source of truth — not a source of results. The words 'Know Yourself' are carved at Delphi even though hardly anyone obeys them.

A teacher speaks the truth because that is what a teacher does. Whether it lands is not the teacher's department.
D: Elegant: Apollo warned Laius, knowing he would ignore it. The words 'Know Yourself' are carved at Delphi though hardly anyone obeys. A source of truth does not guarantee results — it guarantees the truth.
D: Distilled: Speak the truth because that is what truth-tellers do — whether anyone listens is not your department.
Notes: Laius — King of Thebes, father of Oedipus. Apollo's oracle at Delphi warned him that his son would kill him; he ignored the warning. 'Know Yourself' (gnothi seauton) — the most famous of the Delphic maxims, inscribed at the pronaos of the Temple of Apollo. Epictetus uses both to argue that the obligation to speak truth is independent of its reception. This connects to the framework's Right pillar: duty is about the action, not the outcome.

D: Original: Did Socrates persuade all his hearers to take care of themselves? Not the thousandth part. But, however, after he had been placed in this position by the deity, as he himself says, he never left it. But what does he say even to his judges? "If you acquit me on these conditions that I no longer do that which I do now, I will not consent and I will not desist; but I will go up both to young and to old, and, to speak plainly, to every man whom I meet, and I will ask the questions which I ask now; and most particularly will I do this to you my fellow-citizens, because you are more nearly related to me." Are you so curious, Socrates, and such a busybody? and how does it concern you how we act? and what is it that you say? "Being of the same community and of the same kin, you neglect yourself, and show yourself a bad citizen to the state, and a bad kinsman to your kinsmen, and a bad neighbor to your neighbors." "Who, then are you?" Here it is a great thing to say, "I am he whose duty it is to take care of men; for it is not every little heifer which dares to resist a lion; but if the bull comes up and resists him, say to the bull, if you choose, 'And who are you, and what business have you here?'" Man, in every kind there is produced something which excels; in oxen, in dogs, in bees, in horses. Do not then say to that which excels, "Who, then, are you?" If you do, it will find a voice in some way and say, "I am such a thing as the purple in a garment: do not expect me to be like the others, or blame my nature that it has made me different from the rest of men."
D: Refashioned:
Socrates did not convert everyone who heard him — not even close. But he never left his post. When his judges offered him freedom on the condition he stop questioning people, he refused: 'I will keep doing what I do, especially to you, my fellow citizens, because you are closest to me.'

When they challenged him — 'Who do you think you are?' — he had an answer. In every species, there is something that excels. The bull does not apologize to the lion for being a bull. Socrates was the purple thread in the garment — the bright stripe that gives the whole thing its distinction. 'Do not expect me to be like the others, or blame my nature for making me different.'
D: Elegant: Socrates never converted even a thousandth of his hearers. He never left his post. When challenged — 'Who are you?' — he answered like the purple thread in a garment: I am the stripe that gives the whole thing its distinction. Do not expect me to be like the rest.
D: Distilled: The purple thread does not apologize for being different from the fabric — it is what gives the garment its distinction.
Notes: The Socrates passage closely follows Plato's Apology (29c-d, 30a-31a). The 'purple thread' (porphyra) metaphor recurs across the Discourses (see also 1.12 on Agrippinus). The Tyrian purple dye, extracted from murex sea snails, was the most expensive colorant in the ancient world. Epictetus's point: excellence is by nature conspicuous and singular. This maps to the framework's concept of the individual who embodies wisdom as a standard-setter for the community.

D: Original: What then? am I such a man? Certainly not. And are you such a man as can listen to the truth? I wish you were. But however since in a manner I have been condemned to wear a white beard and a cloak, and you come to me as to a philosopher, I will not treat you in a cruel way nor yet as if I despaired of you, but I will say: Young man, whom do you wish to make beautiful? In the first place, know who you are and then adorn yourself appropriately. You are a human being; and this is a mortal animal which has the power of using appearances rationally.
D: Refashioned:
Epictetus does not claim to be a Socrates — but he has a philosopher's beard and a philosopher's cloak, and the student came to him as a philosopher. So he will speak plainly.

Young man, whom do you wish to make beautiful? First know what you are. You are a human being — a mortal creature with one distinctive power: the ability to use appearances rationally. Adorn that.
D: Elegant: First know what you are. You are a mortal creature with one distinctive power: the rational use of appearances. Adorn that — and leave the rest alone.
D: Distilled: Before you can adorn yourself properly, you must first know what you are.
Notes: The 'white beard and cloak' (tribon) mark Epictetus as a philosopher in the Socratic-Cynic tradition. 'Using appearances rationally' (chresis phantasion) is the core Stoic definition of human function — the capacity to evaluate and respond to impressions rather than merely reacting. This maps to the framework's concept of Reason as the distinctively human faculty.

D: Original: But what is meant by "rationally?" Conformably to nature and completely. What, then, do you possess which is peculiar? Is it the animal part? No. Is it the condition of mortality? No. Is it the power of using appearances? No. You possess the rational faculty as a peculiar thing: adorn and beautify this; but leave your hair to him who made it as he chose. Come, what other appellations have you? Are you man or woman? "Man." Adorn yourself then as man, not as woman. Woman is naturally smooth and delicate; and if she has much hair (on her body), she is a monster and is exhibited at Rome among monsters. And in a man it is monstrous not to have hair; and if he has no hair, he is a monster; but if he cuts off his hairs and plucks them out, what shall we do with him? where shall we exhibit him? and under what name shall we show him? "I will exhibit to you a man who chooses to be a woman rather than a man." What a terrible sight! There is no man who will not wonder at such a notice. Indeed I think that the men who pluck out their hairs do what they do without knowing what they do. Man what fault have you to find with your nature? That it made you a man? What then? was it fit that nature should make all human creatures women? and what advantage in that case would you have had in being adorned? for whom would you have adorned yourself, if all human creatures were women? But you are not pleased with the matter: set to work then upon the whole business. Take away- what is its name?- that which is the cause of the hairs: make yourself a woman in all respects, that we may not be mistaken: do not make one half man, and the other half woman. Whom do you wish to please? The women?, Please them as a man. "Well; but they like smooth men." Will you not hang yourself? and if women took delight in catamites, would you become one? Is this your business? were you born for this purpose, that dissolute women should delight in you? Shall we make such a one as you a citizen of Corinth and perchance a prefect of the city, or chief of the youth, or general or superintendent of the games? Well, and when you have taken a wife, do you intend to have your hairs plucked out? To please whom and for what purpose? And when you have begotten children, will you introduce them also into the state with the habit of plucking their hairs? A beautiful citizen, and senator and rhetorician. We ought to pray that such young men be born among us and brought up.
D: Refashioned:
What does 'rationally' mean? In accordance with nature and completely. Your animal nature is not unique — animals desire and avoid. Your mortality is not unique — everything dies. Your ability to receive impressions is not unique — animals do that too. What is unique is the rational faculty. That is what you should cultivate.

Epictetus then confronts the student's vanity with characteristic bluntness. You remove your body hair to look smooth. But smoothness is natural to women, not men. If you want to impress women by looking feminine, you have confused what you are. 'Are women delighted by smooth men? Then should you become something you are not to please them? Is that your purpose?'

The broader point: do not reshape your nature to match other people's preferences. Adorn what is actually yours — your capacity for rational choice.
D: Elegant: Animals desire and avoid. Everything dies. Only humans reason. That is what is yours to cultivate. Do not reshape your nature to match other people's preferences. A lion does not shave its mane to please the crowd.
D: Distilled: Your rational faculty is the only thing uniquely yours — adorn that, and stop reshaping your nature for applause.
Notes: The extended passage on depilation reflects Roman cultural anxieties about gender performance in the 1st-2nd century CE. Epictetus's argument is not about gender norms per se but about the principle of living according to your nature rather than distorting it for approval. The pancratium reference and the lion metaphor both serve the same point: every creature has a natural form, and beauty consists in fulfilling it, not disguising it.

D: Original: Do not so, I entreat you by the Gods, young man: but when you have once heard these words, go away and say to yourself, "Epictetus has not said this to me; for how could he? but some propitious good through him: for it would never have come into his thoughts to say this, since he is not accustomed to talk thus with any person. Come then let us obey God, that we may not be subject to his anger." You say, "No." But, if a crow by his croaking signifies anything to you, it is not the crow which signifies, but God through the crow; and if he signifies anything through a human voice, will he not cause the man to say this to you, that you may know the power of the divinity, that he signifies to some in this way, and to others in that way, and concerning the greatest things and the chief he signifies through the noblest messenger? What else is it which the poet says:
D: Refashioned:
Epictetus tells the student: do not credit me with this advice. If it reaches you, it comes through me, not from me — the way a message might come through a crow or a messenger. If God communicates through signs and omens, why not also through a cranky old philosopher?

The poet said the same thing: the gods sent Hermes down to warn Laius directly.
D: Elegant: Do not credit me. The truth comes through me, not from me — the way a message comes through a crow, or a prophet, or a cranky old teacher with a white beard.
D: Distilled: Truth comes through the messenger, not from the messenger — hear it regardless of the source.
Notes: The 'crow' (korax) as divine messenger reflects Greek augury — interpreting bird behavior as divine communication. The quotation is from Homer's Odyssey (1.37-38), where Zeus sends Hermes to warn Aegisthus. Epictetus's humility here is genuine: he positions himself as an instrument, not an authority.

D: Original:
For we ourselves have warned him, and have sent

Hermes the careful watcher, Argus' slayer,
D: Refashioned: 'We ourselves have warned him, and have sent Hermes, the careful watcher, slayer of Argus.'
D: Elegant: The gods sent Hermes himself to deliver the warning — and still it was ignored.
D: Distilled: Even direct divine warning cannot compel a person who has decided not to listen.
Notes: Homer, Odyssey 1.38 — Zeus recounting how he sent Hermes to warn Aegisthus not to murder Agamemnon or pursue Clytemnestra. Argus-slayer (Argeiphontes) is a standard Homeric epithet for Hermes. Epictetus quotes this to reinforce his point: even the gods cannot force compliance.

D: Original: The husband not to kill nor wed the wife. Was Hermes going to descend from heaven to say this to him? And now the Gods say this to you and send the messenger, the slayer of Argus, to warn you not to pervert that which is well arranged, nor to busy yourself about it, but to allow a man to be a man, and a woman to be a woman, a beautiful man to be as a beautiful man, and an ugly man as an ugly man, for you are not flesh and hair, but you are will; and if your will beautiful, then you will be beautiful. But up the present time I dare not tell you that you are ugly, for I think that you are readier to hear anything than this. But see what Socrates says to the most beautiful and blooming of men Alcibiades: "Try, then, to be beautiful." What does he say to him? "Dress your hair and pluck the hairs from your legs." Nothing of that kind. But "Adorn your will, take away bad opinions." "How with the body?" Leave it as it is by nature. Another has looked after these things: intrust them to him. "What then, must a man be uncleaned?" Certainly not; but what you are and are made by nature, cleanse this. A man should be cleanly as a man, a woman as a woman, a child as a child. You say no: but let us also pluck out the lion's mane, that he may not be uncleaned, and the cock's comb for he also ought to he cleaned. Granted, but as a cock, and the lion as a lion, and the hunting dog as a hunting dog.
D: Refashioned:
Even Hermes descending from heaven was not enough to stop Laius. The gods now send their warning through Epictetus: do not pervert what nature has arranged. Let a man be a man, a woman a woman. You are not flesh and hair — you are will. If your will is beautiful, you will be beautiful.

Socrates said the same thing to the most attractive young man in Athens — Alcibiades. He did not say 'fix your hair.' He said: 'Adorn your will. Remove bad opinions.' As for the body? Leave it as nature made it. A man should be clean as a man, not reshaped into something he is not. We do not strip the lion's mane and call it 'grooming.'
D: Elegant: You are not flesh and hair. You are will. Socrates told Alcibiades — the most beautiful man in Athens — not to fix his hair but to adorn his will and remove bad opinions. A lion does not strip its mane and call it grooming.
D: Distilled: You are not your body — you are your will, and that is the only thing worth making beautiful.
Notes: Alcibiades (c. 450-404 BCE) — Athenian statesman, general, and famously beautiful young man. His relationship with Socrates is dramatized in Plato's Symposium. The reference to Plato's First Alcibiades (or a similar Socratic exchange) is the source of Socrates's advice. The chapter's conclusion — 'you are will' (prohairesis ei) — is one of Epictetus's most radical claims: personal identity is located in the faculty of choice, not in the body. This maps directly to the framework's central thesis that wisdom is about orientation, not circumstance.

D: Original: Chapter 2: In what a man ought to be exercised who has made proficiency; and that we neglect the chief things

There are three things in which a man ought to exercise himself who would be wise and good. The first concerns the desires and the aversions, that a man may not fail to get what he desires, and that he may not fall into that which he does not desire. The second concerns the movements (toward) and the movements from an object, and generally in doing what a man ought to do, that he may act according to order, to reason, and not carelessly. The third thing concerns freedom from deception and rashness in judgement, and generally it concerns the assents. Of these topics the chief and the most urgent is that which relates to the affects; for an affect is produced in no other way than by a failing to obtain that which a man desires or a falling into that which a man would wish to avoid. This is that which brings in perturbations, disorders, bad fortune, misfortunes, sorrows, lamentations and envy; that which makes men envious and jealous; and by these causes we are unable even to listen to the precepts of reason. The second topic concerns the duties of a man; for I ought not to be free from affects like a statue, but I ought to maintain the relations natural and acquired, as a pious man, as a son, as a father, as a citizen.
D: Refashioned:
Three disciplines define the philosophical life. First: mastering desire and aversion — wanting what you should want, avoiding what you should avoid. Second: mastering action — fulfilling your duties as a parent, child, citizen, friend. Third: mastering judgment — giving assent only to what is true.

The first discipline is the most urgent. Every disturbance — envy, grief, anxiety, rage — traces back to either wanting what you cannot get or stumbling into what you tried to avoid. Get this wrong and you cannot even hear the lessons about the other two.

The second matters because philosophy is not emotional numbness. You are not a statue. You have roles and relationships that demand engagement.
D: Elegant: Three disciplines: desire, action, judgment. The first is most urgent — every disturbance traces to wanting what you cannot get or stumbling into what you tried to avoid. The second matters because you are not a statue: you have roles that demand engagement. The third refines everything else.
D: Distilled: Master desire first, duty second, judgment third — because you cannot hear the lessons if your wanting is in disorder.
Notes: The three topoi (fields of study) — desire/aversion, impulse/action, assent/judgment — form the backbone of Epictetus's entire pedagogical system. They correspond roughly to the three parts of Stoic philosophy: physics (understanding nature), ethics (right action), and logic (correct reasoning). Chrysippus and other early Stoics discussed these categories, but Epictetus's specific three-discipline formulation is his own contribution. This maps to the framework's three pillars: Reality (understanding what is), Right (acting well), Reason (thinking clearly).

D: Original: The third topic is that which immediately concerns those who are making proficiency, that which concerns the security of the other two, so that not even in sleep any appearance unexamined may surprise us, nor in intoxication, nor in melancholy. "This," it may be said, "is above our power." But the present philosophers neglecting the first topic and the second, employ themselves on the third, using sophistical arguments, making conclusions from questioning, employing hypotheses, lying. "For a man must," as it is said, "when employed on these matters, take care that he is not deceived." Who must? The wise and good man. This then is all that is wanting to you. Have you successfully worked out the rest? Are you free from deception in the matter of money? If you see a beautiful girl, do you resist the appearance? If your neighbor obtains an estate by will, are you not vexed? Now is there nothing else wanting to you except unchangeable firmness of mind? Wretch, you hear these very things with fear and anxiety that some person may despise you, and with inquiries about what any person may say about you. And if a man come and tell you that in a certain conversation in which the question was, "Who is the best philosopher," a man who was present said that a certain person was the chief philosopher, your little soul which was only a finger's length stretches out to two cubits. But if another who is present "You are mistaken; it is not worth while to listen to a certain person, for what does he know? he has only the first principles, and no more?" then you are confounded, you grow pale, you cry out immediately, "I will show him who I am, that I am a great philosopher." It is seen by these very things: why do you wish to show it by others? Do you not know that Diogenes pointed out one of the sophists in this way by stretching out his middle finger? And then when the man was wild with rage, "This," he said, "is the certain person: I pointed him out to you." For a man is not shown by the finger, as a stone or a piece of wood: but when any person shows the man s principles, then he shows him as a man.
D: Refashioned:
The third discipline — judgment, logic, assent — belongs to those who have already mastered the first two. But modern philosophers skip straight to it. They spend all their time on syllogisms and clever arguments while their desires are chaotic, their duties neglected, and their characters a mess.

Ask them: are you free from anger? From grief? From envy? They cannot answer yes. But they can dismantle a hypothetical. A student whose soul crumbles at a casual insult but who can parse Chrysippus with precision has the proportions exactly backward.

Diogenes made the point with a single gesture: he pointed his middle finger at a sophist and said, 'There he is — I have shown you the man.' You do not show a person by pointing at them. You show them by revealing their principles.
D: Elegant: Modern philosophers skip desire and duty and go straight to logic. They parse syllogisms while their characters collapse at a casual insult. Diogenes pointed his middle finger at a sophist: 'There — I have shown you the man.' You reveal a person not by pointing at them but by exposing their principles.
D: Distilled: A person who can dismantle any argument but crumbles at a casual insult has the proportions of philosophy exactly backward.
Notes: Chrysippus (c. 279-206 BCE) — third head of the Stoic school, prolific logician. Antipater of Tarsus (d. c. 130 BCE) — Stoic philosopher, successor of Chrysippus. Archedemus of Tarsus — Stoic logician. Crinis — Stoic philosopher reportedly so fragile he died of fright at a mouse. Diogenes of Sinope (c. 412-323 BCE) — the Cynic, famous for his middle finger. Epictetus's critique of prioritizing logic over character connects to the framework's observation that intellectual sophistication without moral foundation is worse than useless.

D: Original: Let us look at your principles also. For is it not plain that you value not at all your own will, but you look externally to things which are independent of your will? For instance, what will a certain person say? and what will people think of you? will you be considered a man of learning; have you read Chrysippus or Antipater? for if you have read Archedemus also, you have everything. Why are you still uneasy lest you should not show us who you are? Would you let me tell you what manner of man you have shown us that you are? You have exhibited yourself to us as a mean fellow, querulous, passionate, cowardly, finding fault with everything, blaming everybody, never quiet, vain: this is what you have exhibited to us. Go away now and read Archedemus; then, if a mouse should leap down and make a noise, you are a dead man. For such a death awaits you as it did- what was the man's name?- Crinis; and he too was proud, because he understood Archedemus.
D: Refashioned:
Look at your actual principles. You obsess over what others think. You crave the reputation of being learned. When someone praises you, your soul inflates; when someone dismisses you, you turn pale and defensive.

You have shown us exactly who you are through these reactions — not through your reading list. You have exhibited yourself as vain, anxious, irritable, and cowardly. Now go read more Archedemus — and when a mouse drops from the ceiling and makes a noise, you will die of fright. Just like Crinis, who was so proud of understanding logic and so helpless at being a human being.
D: Elegant: Your reactions have already shown us who you are — not your reading list. Vain, anxious, irritable, cowardly. Go read more logic. When a mouse drops from the ceiling, you will die of fright — just like Crinis, who understood every syllogism and could not handle being a man.
D: Distilled: Your character is revealed not by what you have read but by how you react when no one is watching.
Notes: Archedemus of Tarsus — Stoic logician whose works on logic were studied in Epictetus's time. The anecdote about Crinis dying of fright at a mouse (or similar trivial event) illustrates the central paradox: mastery of abstract reasoning without mastery of one's own responses is not philosophy. The mouse story may be apocryphal but serves Epictetus's rhetorical purpose perfectly.

D: Original: Wretch, will you not dismiss these things that do not concern you at all? These things are suitable to those who are able to learn them without perturbation, to those who can say: "I am not subject to anger, to grief, to envy: I am not hindered, I am not restrained. What remains for me? I have leisure, I am tranquil: let us see how we must deal with sophistical arguments; let us see how when a man has accepted an hypothesis he shall not be led away to anything absurd." To them such things belong. To those who are happy it is appropriate to light a fire, to dine; if they choose, both to sing and to dance. But when the vessel is sinking, you come to me and hoist the sails.
D: Refashioned:
These advanced studies belong to people who can honestly say: 'I am free from anger, grief, and envy. I am unhindered and unrestrained. I have leisure and tranquility — now let me see how to handle sophisticated arguments without being deceived.'

But you? You are at sea in a sinking ship, and you want to hoist the sails. The fundamentals come first. Master desire and aversion. Master your duties. Then — and only then — does logic become appropriate. Until then, it is like throwing a dinner party while the house is on fire.
D: Elegant: Advanced logic belongs to the person who can honestly say: I am free from anger, grief, and envy. For everyone else, it is hoisting sails on a sinking ship.
D: Distilled: Do not hoist the sails when the ship is sinking — master your desires before you refine your arguments.
Notes: The sinking ship metaphor is one of Epictetus's most memorable images. The progression — first desire/aversion, then duty, then logic — maps to a developmental model: you cannot build higher-order capacities on a disordered foundation. This connects to the framework's concept of paideia (education): the sequence matters, and skipping foundational work produces sophisticated incompetence.

D: Original: Chapter 3: What is the matter on which a good man should he employed, and in what we ought chiefly to practice ourselves

The material for the wise and good man is his own ruling faculty: and the body is the material for the physician and the aliptes; the land is the matter for the husbandman. The business of the wise and good man is to use appearances conformably to nature: and as it is the nature of every soul to assent to the truth, to dissent from the false, and to remain in suspense as to that which is uncertain; so it is its nature to be moved toward the desire of the good, and to aversion from the evil; and with respect to that which is neither good nor bad it feels indifferent. For as the money-changer is not allowed to reject Caesar's coin, nor the seller of herbs, but if you show the coin, whether he chooses or not, he must give up what is sold for the coin; so it is also in the matter of the soul. When the good appears, it immediately attracts to itself; the evil repels from itself. But the soul will never reject the manifest appearance of the good, any more than persons will reject Caesar's coin. On this principle depends every movement both of man and God.
D: Refashioned:
A physician works on the body. A farmer works on land. A philosopher works on the ruling faculty — the part of the mind that evaluates appearances and decides how to respond.

Every soul is drawn toward what appears good and repelled by what appears evil. You cannot help this any more than a shopkeeper can refuse Caesar's coin. The soul accepts what it recognizes as good automatically. The entire question, then, is whether your judgment of good and evil is accurate. Get the judgment right and the soul moves correctly on its own.
D: Elegant: A physician works on the body. A farmer works on the land. A philosopher works on the ruling faculty. The soul accepts what it recognizes as good the way a shopkeeper accepts Caesar's coin — automatically. The only question is whether your recognition is accurate.
D: Distilled: The soul moves toward what it recognizes as good automatically — the philosopher's work is making sure the recognition is accurate.
Notes: The 'ruling faculty' (hegemonikon) is the Stoic term for the commanding part of the soul — the capacity for judgment, assent, and rational evaluation. The coin metaphor (nomisma Kaisaros) reflects the economic reality of Roman provincial life: Caesar's coinage was legal tender everywhere. Epictetus's point is that the soul's attraction to perceived good is as automatic and unavoidable as commercial exchange — which makes the accuracy of perception the only variable that matters.

D: Original: For this reason the good is preferred to every intimate relationship. There is no intimate relationship between me and my father, but there is between me and the good. "Are you so hard-hearted?" Yes, for such is my nature; and this is the coin which God has given me. For this reason, if the good is something different from the beautiful and the just, both father is gone, and brother and country, and everything. But shall I overlook my own good, in order that you may have it, and shall I give it up to you? Why? "I am your father." But you are not my good. "I am your brother." But you are not my good. But if we place the good in a right determination of the will, the very observance of the relations of life is good, and accordingly he who gives up any external things obtains that which is good. Your father takes away your property. But he does not injure you. Your brother will have the greater part of the estate in land. Let him have as much as he chooses. Will he then have a greater share of modesty, of fidelity, of brotherly affection? For who will eject you from this possession? Not even Zeus, for neither has he chosen to do so; but he has made this in my own power, and he has given it to me just as he possessed it himself, free from hindrance, compulsion, and impediment. When then the coin which another uses is a different coin, if a man presents this coin, he receives that which is sold for it. Suppose that there comes into the province a thievish proconsul, what coin does he use? Silver coin. Show it to him, and carry off what you please. Suppose one comes who is an adulterer: what coin does he use? Little girls. "Take," a man says, "the coin, and sell me the small thing." "Give," says the seller, "and buy." Another is eager to possess boys. Give him the coin, and receive what you wish. Another is fond of hunting: give him a fine nag or a dog. Though he groans and laments, he will sell for it that which you want. For another compels him from within, he who has fixed this coin.
D: Refashioned:
This is why the good takes precedence over every relationship. Not because relationships do not matter — but because 'good' is the currency that makes every relationship work.

If your father demands something that conflicts with the good, you do not abandon your father — you recognize that honoring the good is what makes you capable of being a good son in the first place. Your father can take your property, your brother can take the larger inheritance. Neither can take your modesty, your integrity, or your capacity for loyalty. Those belong to you the way fire belongs to fire.

Everyone is governed by what they value most. A corrupt official values silver — show him silver and he gives you whatever you want. An adulterer values pleasure — offer it and he sells his integrity. Everyone has a price. The question is whether yours is set by things inside your will or outside it.
D: Elegant: Your father can take your property. Your brother can take the inheritance. Neither can take your integrity, your modesty, or your loyalty — Zeus himself did not claim power over those. Everyone has a currency. The corrupt official sells for silver. The adulterer sells for pleasure. The question is what you sell for, and whether it belongs to you.
D: Distilled: Everyone is governed by what they value most — and the person whose price is set by externals is always for sale.
Notes: The 'coin' metaphor extends from 3.15 into a comprehensive moral economy. The proconsul, the adulterer, the hunter — each is 'purchased' by their dominant desire. Epictetus's claim that 'the good is preferred to every intimate relationship' sounds harsh but is actually a defense of relationships: only a person with settled values can be a reliable father, brother, or citizen. This maps to the framework's concept of Right: integrity is not opposed to duty but is its foundation.

D: Original: Against this kind of thing chiefly a man should exercise himself. As soon as you go out in the morning, examine every man whom you see, every man whom you hear; answer as to a question, "What have you seen?" A handsome man or woman? Apply the rule: Is this independent of the will, or dependent? Independent. Take it away. What have you seen? A man lamenting over the death of a child. Apply the rule. Death is a thing independent of the will. Take it away. Has the proconsul met you? Apply the rule. What kind of thing is a proconsul's office? Independent of the will, or dependent on it? Independent. Take this away also: it does not stand examination: cast it away: it is nothing to you.
D: Refashioned:
Practice this daily. Every morning, examine what you encounter. A beautiful person walks by — apply the rule: is beauty within my control? No. Set it aside. Someone is grieving a death — apply the rule: is death within my control? No. Set it aside. A powerful official approaches — is rank within my control? No. Set it aside.

This is not indifference. It is triage. You are separating what you can work with from what you cannot, so that your attention goes where it can actually do something.
D: Elegant: Every morning, apply the rule. A beautiful face — in my control? No. Set it aside. A grieving parent — death in my control? No. Set it aside. A powerful official — rank in my control? No. Set it aside. This is not indifference. It is triage.
D: Distilled: Apply the rule to every appearance: is this within my power? If not, set it aside — not from indifference but from clarity.
Notes: The daily examination practice (prosoche) is central to Epictetus's practical method. The formula — 'Is this within the power of the will or not?' — functions as a cognitive filter applied to every impression. This connects to the framework's emphasis on daily practice as the mechanism of philosophical growth: wisdom is not achieved through insight but through repeated application.

D: Original: If we practiced this and exercised ourselves in it daily from morning to night, something indeed would be done. But now we are forthwith caught half-asleep by every appearance, and it is only, if ever, that in the school we are roused a little. Then when we go out, if we see a man lamenting, we say, "He is undone." If we see a consul, we say, "He is happy." If we see an exiled man, we say, "He is miserable." If we see a poor man, we say, "He is wretched: he has nothing to eat."
D: Refashioned:
If we actually practiced this — morning to night, every day — we would transform. Instead, we sleepwalk through our impressions. We wake up briefly in the lecture hall, then walk outside and immediately fall back into the old patterns: 'He lost everything — how terrible.' 'She was made consul — how lucky.' 'He has nothing to eat — how wretched.'

We evaluate every situation by externals, as if we had never heard a single word about the will.
D: Elegant: We wake up briefly in the lecture hall. Then we walk outside and fall asleep again — measuring everyone by their fortune, their rank, their losses. As if we had never heard a single word about the will.
D: Distilled: We understand the principle for an hour in the classroom and forget it for the rest of the day.
Notes: The contrast between school (schole) and daily life is one of Epictetus's recurring frustrations. The examples — the grieving man, the consul, the exile, the poor man — show how deeply embedded external valuation is. This maps to the framework's observation about the erosion pattern: knowing better is not the same as doing better, and the gap between theory and practice is where civilizations fail.

D: Original: We ought then to eradicate these bad opinions, and to this end we should direct all our efforts. For what is weeping and lamenting? Opinion. What is bad fortune? Opinion. What is civil sedition, what is divided opinion, what is blame, what is accusation, what is impiety, what is trifling? All these things are opinions, and nothing more, and opinions about things independent of the will, as if they were good and bad. Let a man transfer these opinions to things dependent on the will, and I engage for him that he will be firm and constant, whatever may be the state of things around him. Such as is a dish of water, such is the soul. Such as is the ray of light which falls on the water, such are the appearances. When the water is moved, the ray also seems to be moved, yet it is not moved. And when, then, a man is seized with giddiness, it is not the arts and the virtues which are confounded, but the spirit on which they are impressed; but if the spirit be restored to its settled state, those things also are restored.
D: Refashioned:
Weeping, lamenting, feeling cursed by fortune — these are all opinions, not facts. Civil unrest, blame, accusation, impiety — opinions about things outside the will, mistaken for goods and evils.

Transfer your opinions to what is actually within the will, and you become steady regardless of circumstances. Think of a dish of water and a ray of light falling on it. When the water is agitated, the light appears to shake. But the light itself has not moved — only the surface it falls on. When your spirit is disturbed, your virtues and skills appear to scatter. But they have not gone anywhere. Settle the spirit and everything returns.
D: Elegant: A ray of light on water appears to shake when the surface is disturbed. But the light has not moved — only the water. Your virtues do not scatter when you are agitated. Your spirit does. Settle the spirit and everything returns.
D: Distilled: When you are disturbed, it is not your virtues that have scattered — only the surface they rest on.
Notes: The water-and-light metaphor is one of Epictetus's most elegant images. The scientific observation — that the ray itself is stable while the reflecting medium moves — serves as an analogy for the relationship between character (stable) and emotional state (variable). This connects to the framework's distinction between the rational faculty itself (which endures) and the conditions under which it operates (which fluctuate).

D: Original: Chapter 4: Against a person who showed his partisanship in an unseemly way in a theatre

The governor of Epirus having shown his favor to an actor in an unseemly way and being publicly blamed on this account, and afterward having reported to Epictetus that he was blamed and that he was vexed at those who blamed him, Epictetus said: What harm have they been doing? These men also were acting, as partisans, as you were doing. The governor replied, "Does, then, any person show his partisanship in this way?" When they see you, said Epictetus, who are their governor, a friend of Caesar and his deputy, showing partisanship in this way, was it not to be expected that they also should show their partisanship in the same way? for if it is not right to show partisanship in this way, do not do so yourself; and if it is right, why are you angry if they followed your example? For whom have the many to imitate except you, who are their superiors, to whose example should they look when they go to the theatre except yours? "See how the deputy of Caesar looks on: he has cried out, and I too, then, will cry out. He springs up from his seat, and I will spring up. His slaves sit in various parts of the theatre and call out. I have no slaves, but I will myself cry out as much as I can and as loud as all of them together." You ought then to know when you enter the theatre that you enter as a rule and example to the rest how they ought to look at the acting. Why then did they blame you? Because every man hates that which is a hindrance to him. They wished one person to be crowned; you wished another. They were a hindrance to you, and you were a hindrance to them. You were found to be the stronger; and they did what they could; they blamed that which hindered them. What, then, would you have? That you should do what you please, and they should not even say what they please? And what is the wonder? Do not the husbandmen abuse Zeus when they are hindered by him? do not the sailors abuse him? do they ever cease abusing Caesar? What then does not Zeus know? is not what is said reported to Caesar? What, then, does he do? he knows that, if he punished all who abuse him, he would have nobody to rule over. What then? when you enter the theatre, you ought to say not, "Let Sophron be crowned", but you ought to say this, "Come let me maintain my will in this matter so that it shall be conformable to nature: no man is dearer to me than myself. It would be ridiculous, then, for me to be hurt (injured) in order that another who is an actor may be crowned." Whom then do I wish to gain the prize? Why the actor who does gain the prize; and so he will always gain the prize whom I wish to gain it. "But I wish Sophron to be crowned." Celebrate as many games as you choose in your own house, Nemean, Pythian, Isthmian, Olympian, and proclaim him victor.
D: Refashioned:
A Roman governor was humiliated after cheering wildly for an actor at the theatre. He came to Epictetus complaining that people criticized him for it. Epictetus's response: What did you expect? You are the governor — Caesar's representative. When you behave like a fan in the cheap seats, the crowd follows your lead. Then, when they act exactly as you did, you are offended?

If cheering that way is wrong, do not do it yourself. If it is right, why are you angry that others imitated you? You are their example whether you like it or not. The farmer blames Zeus for the weather. The sailor blames him for the wind. Caesar himself is constantly criticized. Does he execute everyone who complains? He would have no one left to govern.

When you enter a public space, enter with this thought: 'Let me keep my will aligned with nature. Whoever wins, let me be content — because my peace does not depend on the outcome.'
D: Elegant: You are the governor. When you cheer like a fan in the cheap seats, the crowd imitates you. Then you are offended that they act exactly as you did. Leaders set the standard whether they intend to or not. Enter every public space with this thought: my peace does not depend on the outcome.
D: Distilled: When leaders behave like the mob, they lose the right to complain when the mob follows their example.
Notes: The governor of Epirus — likely a Roman provincial administrator (corrector or hegemon). The theatre incident reflects Roman social dynamics: officials were expected to model dignitas (dignified restraint). Caesar's tolerance of criticism reflects Stoic political realism. The Nemean, Pythian, Isthmian, and Olympic games — the four great Panhellenic festivals. Epictetus's solution is not to suppress emotion but to redirect it: celebrate whatever happens, because your investment should be in your own composure, not in the outcome.

D: Original: But in public do not claim more than your due, nor attempt to appropriate to yourself what belongs to all. If you do not consent to this, bear being abused: for when you do the same as the many, you put yourself on the same level with them.
D: Refashioned: Do not claim more than your share in public, and do not try to appropriate what belongs to everyone. If you insist on acting like the crowd, accept being treated like the crowd. You cannot have the authority of a leader and the behavior of a spectator at the same time.
D: Elegant: Act like the crowd and you will be treated like the crowd. You cannot claim a leader's authority while indulging a spectator's behavior.
D: Distilled: You cannot claim the respect of your position while acting beneath it.
Notes: A terse closing that restates the chapter's principle. The tension between role and behavior — officials who want deference while abandoning dignity — maps to the framework's concept of earned authority: legitimate leadership requires living at the standard you set for others.

D: Original: Chapter 5: Against those who on account of sickness go away home

"I am sick here," said one of the pupils, "and I wish to return home." At home, I suppose, you free from sickness. Do you not consider whether you are doing, anything here which may be useful to the exercise of your will, that it may be corrected? For if you are doing nothing toward this end, it was to no purpose that you came. Go away. Look after your affairs at home. For if your ruling power cannot be maintained in a state conformable to nature, it is possible that your land can, that you will he able to increase your money, you will take care of your father in his old age, frequent the public place, hold magisterial office: being bad you will do badly anything else that you have to do.
D: Refashioned:
A student wanted to go home because he was sick. Epictetus asked: were you free from sickness at home? And more importantly — are you making any progress here on the thing that matters, the condition of your will?

If you are not working on your will, then you came for nothing. Go home. But understand: if you cannot keep your ruling faculty in order at school, you will not manage your land, your money, your elderly father, or your civic duties any better. A disordered will does everything badly, regardless of location.
D: Elegant: If you are not working on your will here, go home. But know this: a disordered will does everything badly, regardless of location. You will not manage your land, your money, or your family any better with the same broken instrument.
D: Distilled: A disordered will does everything badly — changing your location does not change your instrument.
Notes: The student's desire to go home sick is a pretext that Epictetus sees through immediately. The real issue is whether the student is doing the interior work philosophy demands. This connects to the framework's emphasis on the will (prohairesis) as the root variable: external circumstances are downstream of the internal condition.

D: Original: But if you understand yourself, and know that you are casting away certain bad opinions and adopting others in their place, and if you have changed your state of life from things which are not within your will to things which are within your will, and if you ever say, "Alas!" you are not saying what you say on account of your father, or your brother, but on account of yourself, do you still allege your sickness? Do you not know that both disease and death must surprise us while we are doing something? the husbandman while he is tilling the ground, the sailor while he is on his voyage? what would you be doing when death surprises you, for you must be surprised when you are doing something? If you can be doing anything better than this when you are surprised, do it.
D: Refashioned:
But if you have been making progress — if you are replacing bad opinions with sound ones, transferring your concerns from externals to the will — then sickness is no reason to leave. Death and disease find everyone in the middle of something. The farmer is plowing, the sailor is at sea. What will you be doing when death finds you?

If there is something better you could be doing at that moment than working on your will, do it. Otherwise, stay where you are and keep working.
D: Elegant: Death finds the farmer plowing and the sailor at sea. What will it find you doing? If there is something better than working on your will, do that. Otherwise, stay and keep working.
D: Distilled: Death will find you doing something — make sure it is the best thing you could be doing.
Notes: The farmer and sailor examples echo a common Stoic memento mori motif. The practical test — 'if you can be doing anything better when death surprises you, do it' — is a powerful heuristic for prioritization. This connects to the framework's concept of philosophical practice as the most important activity, not because it is pleasant but because it determines the quality of everything else.

D: Original: For I wish to be surprised by disease or death when I am looking after nothing else than my that may be free from perturbation, own will that I may be free from hindrance, free from compulsion, and in a state of liberty. I wish to be found practicing these things that I may be able to say to God, "Have I in any respect transgressed thy commands? have I in any respect wrongly used the powers which Thou gavest me? have I misused my perceptions or my preconceptions? have I ever blamed Thee? have I ever found fault with Thy administration? I have been sick, because it was Thy will, and so have others, but I was content to be sick. I have been poor because it was Thy will, but I was content also. I have not filled a magisterial office, because it was not Thy pleasure that I should: I have never desired it. Hast Thou ever seen me for this reason discontented? have I not always approached Thee with a cheerful countenance, ready to do Thy commands and to obey Thy signals? Is it now Thy will that I should depart from the assemblage of men? I depart. I give Thee all thanks that Thou hast allowed me to join in this Thy assemblage of men and to see Thy works, and to comprehend this Thy administration." May death surprise me while I am thinking of these things, while I am thus writing and reading.
D: Refashioned:
I want to be found, when death or illness comes, working on the only thing that matters: keeping my will free from perturbation, hindrance, and compulsion.

I want to be able to say to God: 'I did not violate your commands. I did not misuse the faculties you gave me. I did not blame you or find fault with your administration. I was sick because you willed it — and I was content. I was poor because you willed it — and I was content. I never held office — and I never wanted it more than you wished. Have you ever seen me discontented? I approached everything with a willing spirit. Now you want me to leave the gathering? I leave. Thank you for allowing me to attend, to witness your works, to understand your order.'

Let death find me thinking these thoughts, writing these words, reading this page.
