D: Elegant: Let death find me working on the only thing that matters. Let me say to God: I did not misuse what you gave me. I was sick and content, poor and content, overlooked and content. Now you call me to leave. I go — grateful for the invitation.
D: Distilled: The ultimate test of a life well-lived: can you thank God for the invitation as you leave the gathering?
Notes: This passage is one of the most moving in the Discourses — a prayer-like summation of the Stoic ideal. The address to God (theos) reflects Epictetus's deeply religious Stoicism: the universe is rationally administered, and the proper human response is gratitude and compliance. 'May death surprise me while I am thinking of these things, while I am thus writing and reading' — one of Epictetus's most personal statements. This connects to the framework's concept of wisdom as lived orientation: the philosopher is not someone who has achieved perfection but someone who is working on the right things when the end comes.

D: Original: "But my mother will not hold my head when I am sick." Go to your mother then; for you are a fit person to have your head held when you are sick. "But at home I used to lie down on a delicious bed." Go away to your bed: indeed you are fit to lie on such a bed even when you are in health: do not, then, lose what you can do there.
D: Refashioned: 'But my mother will hold my head when I am sick.' Then go home to your mother. If you need someone to cradle you when you have a fever, philosophy is not what you came here for. 'But at home I had a comfortable bed.' Then go lie in your comfortable bed. You are perfectly suited for comfort — even when you are healthy. Do not lose what you can do there.
D: Elegant: If you need your mother to hold your head when you have a fever, go home. You did not come here for philosophy — you came here for comfort. And comfort is available at home even when you are well.
D: Distilled: If comfort is what you need, go home — but do not pretend you came here for philosophy.
Notes: The sarcasm here is sharp but purposeful. Epictetus is distinguishing between genuine illness (which is no excuse to stop working on the will) and using illness as a pretext to escape difficulty. The 'delicious bed' (kline trypheran) is luxury that the student treats as a right rather than a preference.

D: Original: But what does Socrates say? "As one man," he says, "is pleased with improving his land, another with improving his horse, so I am daily pleased in observing that I am growing better." "Better in what? in using nice little words?" Man, do not say that. "In little matters of speculation?" What are you saying? "And indeed I do not see what else there is on which philosophers employ their time." Does it seem nothing to you to have never found fault with any person, neither with God nor man? to have blamed nobody? to carry the same face always in going out and coming in? This is what Socrates knew, and yet he never said that he knew anything or taught anything. But if any man asked for nice little words or little speculations, he would carry him to Protagoras or to Hippias; and if any man came to ask for pot-herbs, he would carry him to the gardener. Who then among you has this purpose? for if indeed you had it, you would both be content in sickness, and in hunger, and in death. If any among you has been in love with a charming girl, he knows that I say what is true.
D: Refashioned:
What did Socrates say? 'What pleases me is that I grow better daily.' Not in clever language. Not in technical argumentation. In character.

He never claimed to know or teach anything. If someone wanted eloquence, he sent them to the sophists. If someone wanted vegetables, he sent them to the gardener. His only concern was this: Have I blamed anyone today? Have I found fault with the gods or with another person? Have I faced what came with the same composure going out as coming in?

Who among you has this aim? If you did, you would bear sickness, hunger, and death with equanimity. Anyone who has ever been deeply in love knows this is possible — you endure extraordinary discomfort without noticing, because your attention is elsewhere. Direct that same intensity toward what actually matters.
D: Elegant: Socrates grew better daily — not at speaking but at being. He never blamed anyone, never found fault with the gods, never lost composure between entering and leaving a room. If you have ever been deeply in love, you know you can endure anything without noticing. Direct that intensity toward what matters.
D: Distilled: Socrates measured progress not in what he learned but in whether he was growing better as a human being each day.
Notes: Protagoras (c. 490-420 BCE) and Hippias of Elis (c. 460-399 BCE) — sophists whom Socrates would send students to for rhetorical training (see Plato's Protagoras and Hippias Major). The claim about love (eros) redirecting attention is psychologically acute: Epictetus uses romantic experience as evidence that humans are capable of extraordinary endurance when properly motivated. This connects to the framework's concept of orientation: the capacity for endurance already exists — the question is what it is directed toward.

D: Original: Chapter 6: Miscellaneous

When some person asked him how it happened that since reason has been more cultivated by the men of the present age, the progress made in former times was greater. In what respect, he answered, has it been more cultivated now, and in what respect was the progress greater then? For in that in which it has now been more cultivated, in that also the progress will now be found. At present it has been cultivated for the purpose of resolving syllogisms, and progress is made. But in former times it was cultivated for the purpose of maintaining the governing faculty in a condition conformable to nature, and progress was made. Do not, then, mix things which are different and do not expect, when you are laboring at one thing, to make progress in another. But see if any man among us when he is intent see I upon this, the keeping himself in a state conformable to nature and living so always, does not make progress. For you will not find such a man.
D: Refashioned:
Someone asked: if reasoning has improved since the old days, why was moral progress greater in earlier times? Because the improvement has been in the wrong department. People now cultivate logic — and they make progress in logic. The ancients cultivated the alignment of their ruling faculty with nature — and they made progress in character.

You get what you practice. If you practice syllogisms, you get better at syllogisms. If you practice keeping your will in order, you get better at keeping your will in order. Do not train in one area and expect improvement in another.
D: Elegant: The ancients cultivated character and made progress in character. We cultivate logic and make progress in logic. You get what you practice. Do not train in one area and expect improvement in another.
D: Distilled: You make progress only in what you actually practice — and we have been practicing the wrong things.
Notes: The question reflects a nostalgia common in Greco-Roman culture: the belief that earlier generations were morally superior. Epictetus's answer is structural, not nostalgic — progress follows from practice, and the direction of practice determines the direction of progress. This maps directly to the framework's concept of paideia (education): civilizations advance or decline based on what they train their citizens to value.

D: Original: The good man is invincible, for he does not enter the contest where he is not stronger. If you want to have his land and all that is on it, take the land; take his slaves, take his magisterial office, take his poor body. But you will not make his desire fail in that which it seeks, nor his aversion fall into that which he would avoid. The only contest into which he enters is that about things which are within the power of his will; how then will he not be invincible?
D: Refashioned:
The good person is invincible — not because they cannot be harmed, but because they never enter a contest they can lose. Take their land, their slaves, their office, their body. You have not touched their desire or their aversion. They compete only in the arena of the will, where no external force has jurisdiction.

How can someone who only fights where they are strongest ever be defeated?
D: Elegant: The good person is invincible — not because they cannot be harmed but because they never enter a contest they can lose. They compete only where no external force has jurisdiction: the arena of the will.
D: Distilled: You are invincible when you only compete in the arena where no one else has power — the arena of the will.
Notes: The 'invincible' (aniketos) person is a Stoic ideal — not physical invincibility but strategic invincibility through correct scope. The athletic metaphor is characteristic of Epictetus. This connects to the framework's concept of Right: moral strength is not the ability to withstand everything but the wisdom to know which contests are yours to fight.

D: Original: Some person having asked him what is Common sense, Epictetus replied: As that may be called a certain Common hearing which only distinguishes vocal sounds, and that which distinguishes musical sounds is not Common, but artificial; so there are certain things which men, who are not altogether perverted, see by the common notions which all possess. Such a constitution of the mind is named Common sense.
D: Refashioned:
Common sense is like common hearing — the basic ability to distinguish sounds. Everyone has it. But musical hearing — the trained ability to distinguish harmony from discord — is not common. It is cultivated.

Similarly, every person who is not completely corrupted can see basic moral truths. But refined moral perception requires training. Common sense is the floor, not the ceiling.
D: Elegant: Everyone can hear sounds. Not everyone can hear music. Common sense is the basic moral hearing every uncorrupted person possesses — the floor, not the ceiling.
D: Distilled: Common sense is the basic moral perception everyone has — but refined judgment requires cultivation.
Notes: The distinction between common (koine) and artificial (technike) hearing maps to the Stoic concept of koinai ennoiai (common conceptions) — the innate moral intuitions all humans share before corruption. This connects to the framework's discussion of natural vs. cultivated wisdom: everyone starts with basic moral perception, but philosophical education refines it into something more reliable.

D: Original: It is not easy to exhort weak young men; for neither is it easy to hold cheese with a hook. But those who have a good natural disposition, even if you try to turn them aside, cling still more to reason. Wherefore Rufus generally attempted to discourage, and he used this method as a test of those who had a good natural disposition and those who had not. "For," it was his habit to say, "as a stone, if you cast it upward, will be brought down to the earth by its own nature, so the man whose mind is naturally good, the more you repel him, the more he turns toward that to which he is naturally inclined."
D: Refashioned:
Weak students are like trying to pick up cheese with a hook — impossible to get a grip. But those with good natural dispositions are different: the harder you push them away, the more they cling to reason.

This is why Musonius Rufus deliberately discouraged his students. It was a test. He would say: 'A stone thrown upward falls back to earth by its own nature. The person naturally inclined toward the good, the more you repel them, the more they turn toward what is right.'
D: Elegant: Rufus deliberately discouraged students as a test. The stone thrown upward returns to earth by its nature. The naturally good person, the more you repel them, the more they turn toward reason.
D: Distilled: The naturally good person clings to reason the way a stone falls to earth — the harder you push them away, the faster they return.
Notes: Musonius Rufus (c. 30-101 CE) — Epictetus's teacher, a Stoic philosopher exiled by Nero. The pedagogical method — discouragement as a filter for genuine students — is characteristic of both Rufus and the Cynic-Stoic tradition. The stone metaphor anticipates the concept of natural inclination: virtue is not imposed from outside but is the natural direction of a well-constituted mind. This connects to the framework's discussion of natural vs. corrupted orientation.

D: Original: Chapter 7: To the administrator of the free cities who was an Epicurean

When the administrator came to visit him, and the man was an Epicurean, Epictetus said: It is proper for us who are not philosophers to inquire of you who are philosophers, as those who come to a strange city inquire of the citizens and those who are acquainted with it, what is the best thing in the world, in order that we also, after inquiry, may go in quest of that which is best and look at it, as strangers do with the things in cities. For that there are three things which relate to man, soul, body, and things external, scarcely any man denies. It remains for you philosophers to answer what is the best. What shall we say to men? Is the flesh the best? and was it for this that Maximus sailed as far as Cassiope in winter with his son, and accompanied him that he might be gratified in the flesh? Then the man said that it was not, and added, "Far be that from him." Is it not fit then, Epictetus said, to be actively employed about the best? "It is certainly of all things the most fit." What, then, do we possess which is better than the flesh? "The soul," he replied. And the good things of the best, are they better, or the good things of the worse? "The good things of the best." And are the good things of the best within the power of the will or not within the power of the will? "They are within the power of the will." Is, then, the pleasure of the soul a thing within the power of the will? "It is," he replied. And on what shall this pleasure depend? On itself? But that cannot be conceived: for there must first exist a certain substance or nature of good, by obtaining which we shall have pleasure in the soul. He assented to this also. On what, then, shall we depend for this pleasure of the soul? for if it shall depend on things of the soul, the substance of the good is discovered; for good cannot be one thing, and that at which we are rationally delighted another thing; nor if that which precedes is not good, can that which comes after be good, for in order that the thing which comes after may be good, that which precedes must be good.
D: Refashioned:
An Epicurean who governed free cities came to visit. Epictetus played the naive inquirer: since you are the philosopher, tell us — what is the best thing in the world? Three things relate to a human being: soul, body, and external possessions. Which matters most? The Epicurean agreed: the soul. And are the goods of the soul within the power of the will? He agreed again.

Then what produces pleasure in the soul? If the soul's pleasure depends on itself, you have found the good in the soul. But that cannot be right — pleasure needs an object. If the soul's pleasure depends on the body's pleasure, then the body is the real source of good, and the soul is just along for the ride.

The Epicurean is trapped: either the good is internal to the will (the Stoic position) or it traces back to bodily pleasure (which is what Epicurus actually taught).
D: Elegant: Three things relate to a human: soul, body, externals. The Epicurean agrees the soul matters most. But if the soul's pleasure depends on the body's pleasure, the body is the real master — and the soul is just along for the ride.
D: Distilled: If the soul's pleasure depends on the body, the body is the real master — and everything the Epicurean says about the soul is decoration.
Notes: Epicurus (341-270 BCE) — founder of the Epicurean school, which identified the good with pleasure (hedone), specifically the absence of pain (ataraxia). The 'free cities' (civitates liberae) were Roman provincial cities that retained nominal self-governance. Maximus — likely a contemporary who traveled to Cassiope (a port on Corfu) with his son. Epictetus's Socratic method here mirrors Plato's dialogues: the interlocutor is led to contradict himself. This maps to the framework's critique of materialist philosophy: any system that locates value in the body cannot consistently value the mind.

D: Original: But you would not affirm this, if you are in your right mind, for you would then say what is inconsistent both with Epicurus and the rest of your doctrines. It remains, then, that the pleasure of the soul is in the pleasure from things of the body: and again that those bodily things must be the things which precede and the substance of the good.
D: Refashioned: The Epicurean cannot escape the trap. If soul-pleasure is the good, that contradicts Epicurus, who grounds pleasure in sensation. If body-pleasure is the good, then everything the Epicurean says about the soul's superiority is empty rhetoric. Either way, the doctrine collapses into: the body is what matters most.
D: Elegant: The Epicurean is caught. If pleasure is in the soul, he contradicts Epicurus. If in the body, everything he says about the soul is decoration. Either way, the body wins — and the philosophy is exposed.
D: Distilled: A philosophy that claims to honor the soul while grounding value in the body will always collapse into materialism.
Notes: Epictetus presses the logical consequence: Epicurean philosophy, despite its subtlety, reduces to bodily hedonism when its premises are followed to their conclusion. This connects to the framework's emphasis on logical consistency: a philosophy must be tested not by its most attractive claims but by where its premises actually lead.

D: Original: For this reason Maximus acted foolishly if he made the voyage for any other reason than for the sake of the flesh, that is, for the sake of the best. And also a man acts foolishly if he abstains from that which belongs to others, when he is a judge and able to take it. But, if you please, let us consider this only, how this thing may be done secretly, and safely, and so that no man will know it. For not even does Epicurus himself declare stealing to be bad, but he admits that detection is; and because it is impossible to have security against detection, for this reason he says, "Do not steal." But I say to you that if stealing is done cleverly and cautiously, we shall not be detected: further also we have powerful friends in Rome both men and women, and the Hellenes are weak, and no man will venture to go up to Rome for the purpose. Why do you refrain from your own good? This is senseless, foolish. But even if you tell me that you do refrain, I will not believe you. For as it is impossible to assent to that which appears false, and to turn away from that which is true, so it is impossible to abstain from that which appears good. But wealth is a good thing, and certainly most efficient in producing pleasure. Why will you not acquire wealth? And why should we not corrupt our neighbor's wife, if we can do it without detection? and if the husband foolishly prates about the matter, why not pitch him out of the house? If you would be a philosopher such as you ought to be, if a perfect philosopher, if consistent with your own doctrines. If you would not, you will not differ at all from us who are called Stoics; for we also say one thing, but we do another: we talk of the things which are beautiful, but we do what is base. But you will be perverse in the contrary way, teaching what is bad, practicing what is good.
D: Refashioned:
If bodily pleasure is the highest good, then what stops you from stealing when you can get away with it? Epicurus says stealing is bad — but only because you might get caught. Remove the risk of detection and you remove the reason not to steal. So I ask: with powerful friends in Rome and weak opponents in Greece, why not take what you want? Why not seduce your neighbor's wife? Why not toss the husband out if he objects?

This is not a straw man — it is the honest consequence of the doctrine. If pleasure is the good and pain the evil, then morality reduces to risk management. The only Epicureans who behave well are the ones who are inconsistent: they teach something bad and practice something good.
D: Elegant: If pleasure is the good, then morality is risk management. Remove the risk of getting caught and you remove the reason not to steal. The only honest Epicureans would be thieves. The decent ones are simply inconsistent — teaching something bad and practicing something good.
D: Distilled: If pleasure is the highest good, then morality is just risk management — and the only honest hedonist would be a clever thief.
Notes: Epictetus's reductio ad absurdum of Epicurean ethics: if detection is the only reason not to steal (as he claims Epicurus taught), then morality is contingent on surveillance. The charge that Epicureans 'teach what is bad, practice what is good' is a backhanded compliment — their character is better than their philosophy. This connects to the framework's insistence that philosophical systems be tested by their logical consequences, not by the behavior of their best adherents.

D: Original: In the name of God, are you thinking of a city of Epicureans? "I do not marry." "Nor I, for a man ought not to marry; nor ought we to beget children, nor engage in public matters." What then will happen? whence will the citizens come? who will bring them up? who will be governor of the youth, who preside wi over gymnastic exercises? and in what also will the teacher instruct them? will he teach them what the Lacedaemonians were taught, or what the Athenians were taught? Come take a young man, bring him up according to your doctrines. The doctrines are bad, subversive of a state, pernicious to families, and not becoming to women. Dismiss them, man. You live in a chief city: it is your duty to be a magistrate, to judge justly, to abstain from that which belongs to others; no woman ought to seem beautiful to you except your own wife, and no youth, no vessel of silver, no vessel of gold. Seek for doctrines which are consistent with what I say, and, by making them your guide, you will with pleasure abstain from things which have such persuasive power to lead us and overpower us.
D: Refashioned:
Imagine a city of consistent Epicureans. No one marries — why accept the burden? No one has children — why bother? No one serves in government — there is no pleasure in it. Who educates the young? Who governs the gymnasiums? And what would they teach — the Spartan virtues? The Athenian love of wisdom? Those require exactly the kind of sacrifice Epicurean doctrine discourages.

You are an administrator of a great city. Your duty is to judge justly, respect others' property, and be faithful to your wife. These duties require principles that Epicureanism cannot supply. Find principles that can — principles consistent with the human capacities for nobility, modesty, and self-restraint.
D: Elegant: Imagine a city of consistent Epicureans. No one marries, no one serves, no one teaches the young. The city would collapse in a generation. You govern a city. Find principles that can sustain one.
D: Distilled: A philosophy that cannot sustain a city has no business governing one.
Notes: The 'city of Epicureans' thought experiment is Epictetus's most devastating critique: test a philosophy by imagining a whole community living by it. Lacedaemonians (Spartans) and Athenians represent the two great models of Greek education (paideia). Epictetus's challenge — 'bring up a child according to your doctrines' — maps to the framework's test of any value system: can it reproduce itself across generations?

D: Original: But if to the persuasive power of these things, we also devise such a philosophy as this which helps to push us on toward them and strengthens us to this end, what will be the consequence? In a piece of toreutic art which is the best part? the silver or the workmanship? The substance of the hand is the flesh; but the work of the hand is the principal part. The duties then are also three; those which are directed toward the existence of a thing; those which are directed toward its existence in a particular kind; and third, the chief or leading things themselves. So also in man we ought not to value the material, the poor flesh, but the principal. What are these? Engaging in public business, marrying, begetting children, venerating God, taking care of parents, and, generally, having desires, aversions, pursuits of things and avoidances, in the way in which we ought to do these things, and according to our nature. And how are we constituted by nature? Free, noble, modest: for what other animal blushes? what other is capable of receiving the appearance of shame? and we are so constituted by nature as to subject pleasure to these things, as a minister, a servant, in order that it may call forth our activity, in order that it may keep us constant in acts which are conformable to nature.
D: Refashioned:
A philosophy that pushes you toward temptation when you are already struggling is worse than useless. In a piece of craftsmanship, the silver matters less than the skill. In a hand, the flesh matters less than what the hand can do. In a human being, the body matters less than the capacities it serves.

Those capacities are: civic engagement, fidelity, parenting, reverence, care for others — and behind all of them, the disciplined management of desire and aversion. Nature made us free, noble, and capable of shame. No other animal blushes. Pleasure exists to serve these higher capacities — as a servant, not as a master. It motivates action and sustains effort. But when pleasure becomes the goal rather than the fuel, the hierarchy inverts and the person collapses.
D: Elegant: No other animal blushes. Pleasure exists to serve our higher capacities — freedom, nobility, shame. When pleasure becomes the master instead of the servant, the hierarchy inverts and the person collapses.
D: Distilled: Pleasure is the servant of virtue — the moment it becomes the master, both the person and the philosophy collapse.
Notes: The blushing observation — 'what other animal is capable of receiving the appearance of shame?' — is a striking argument for the distinctiveness of human moral capacity. The hierarchy of substance and function (silver/workmanship, flesh/hand) leads to the central claim: human nature is constituted for freedom (eleutheria), nobility (eugenes), and modesty (aidos). Pleasure (hedone) serves these as a 'minister' (hyperetes). This maps to the framework's architecture: pleasure is a signal, not a goal.

D: Original: "But I am rich and I want nothing." Why, then, do you pretend to be a philosopher? Your golden and your silver vessels are enough for you. What need have you of principles? "But I am also a judge of the Greeks." Do you know how to judge? Who taught you to know? "Caesar wrote to me a codicil." Let him write and give you a commission to judge of music; and what will be the use of it to you? Still how did you become a judge? whose hand did you kiss? the hand of Symphorus or Numenius? Before whose bedchamber have you slept? To whom have you sent gifts? Then do you not see that to be a judge is just of the same value as Numenius is? "But I can throw into prison any man whom I please." So you can do with a stone. "But I can beat with sticks whom I please." So you may an ass. This is not a governing of men. Govern us as rational animals: show us what is profitable to us, and we will follow it: show us what is unprofitable, and we will turn away from it. Make us imitators of yourself, as Socrates made men imitators of himself. For he was like a governor of men, who made them subject to him their desires, their aversion, their movements toward an object and their turning away from it. "Do this: do not do this: if you do not obey, I will throw you into prison." This is not governing men like rational animals. But I: As Zeus has ordained, so act: if you do not act so, you will feel the penalty, you will be punished. What will be the punishment? Nothing else than not having done your duty: you will lose the character of fidelity, modesty, propriety. Do not look for greater penalties than these.
D: Refashioned:
The Epicurean protests: 'I am rich and want nothing.' Then why pretend to be a philosopher? Your gold is enough for you. 'But I am also a judge.' Who taught you to judge? Caesar gave you a title — but did Caesar give you the capacity to govern? You kissed the right hands and slept outside the right doors. That is how you got your position. And now you think you can govern.

'I can throw anyone in prison.' So can you throw a stone. 'I can have anyone beaten.' So can you beat a donkey. This is not governance of rational beings.

Socrates governed people by making them want to follow him — by demonstrating a life so well-ordered that his desires, his aversions, his impulses became the standard others measured themselves against. That is governance. Everything else is just force decorated with titles.

The real penalty for living badly is not punishment from outside. It is what you become: unfaithful, immodest, undignified. Do not look for worse penalties than those.
D: Elegant: You can throw anyone in prison — so can you throw a stone. You can have anyone beaten — so can you beat a donkey. This is not governance. Socrates governed by making people want to follow him. The penalty for living badly is not external punishment — it is what you become.
D: Distilled: Throwing someone in prison is not governance any more than throwing a stone is — real authority comes from making people want to follow your example.
Notes: Symphorus and Numenius — likely influential freedmen or courtiers at the imperial court whose patronage was required for administrative appointments. The 'codicil' (codicillus) was an imperial letter of appointment. Epictetus's attack on the patronage system reflects his experience as a former slave who understood power structures from the inside. Socrates as the model governor — not through force but through the quality of his character — maps to the framework's concept of the ideational domain of power: the highest form of governance operates through ideas and example, not through coercion.

D: Original: Chapter 8: How we must exercise ourselves against appearances

As we exercise ourselves against sophistical questions, so we ought to exercise ourselves daily against appearances; for these appearances also propose questions to us. "A certain person son is dead." Answer: the thing is not within the power of the will: it is not an evil. "A father has disinherited a certain son. What do you think of it?" It is a thing beyond the power of the will, not an evil. "Caesar has condemned a person." It is a thing beyond the power of the will, not an evil. "The man is afflicted at this." Affliction is a thing which depends on the will: it is an evil. He has borne the condemnation bravely." That is a thing within the power of the will: it is a good. If we train ourselves in this manner, we shall make progress; for we shall never assent to anything of which there is not an appearance capable of being comprehended. Your son is dead. What has happened? Your son is dead. Nothing more? Nothing. Your ship is lost. What has happened? Your ship is lost. A man has been led to prison. What has happened? He has been led to prison. But that herein he has fared badly, every man adds from his own opinion. "But Zeus," you say, "does not do right in these matters." Why? because he has made you capable of endurance? because he has made you magnanimous? because he has taken from that which befalls you the power of being evil? because it is in your power to be happy while you are suffering what you suffer; because he has opened the door to you, when things do not please you? Man, go out and do not complain.
D: Refashioned:
Practice against appearances the way athletes practice against opponents. Drill the responses daily.

Someone's child has died. What happened? Something outside the will occurred. Not an evil. A father disinherited his son. Outside the will. Not an evil. Caesar condemned someone. Outside the will. Not an evil. The person bore it bravely. Inside the will. A good.

Train this way and you will stop adding your own opinions to events. Your son is dead — what happened? Your son is dead. Nothing more. Your ship was lost — what happened? Your ship was lost. That is all. Everything else — the catastrophe, the injustice, the unfairness — you are adding that yourself.

'But Zeus is unjust!' Why? Because he made you capable of endurance? Because he made suffering unable to touch the thing that matters? Because he left you the door open if you truly cannot bear it? Stop complaining and either bear it or leave.
D: Elegant: Your son is dead. What happened? Your son is dead — nothing more. Your ship was lost. What happened? Your ship was lost — nothing more. Everything else you are adding yourself. Zeus made you capable of endurance and left the door open if you cannot bear it. Stop complaining.
D: Distilled: Events are what they are — everything unbearable about them is what you add from your own opinions.
Notes: The rapid-fire catechism — 'What happened? X happened. Nothing more.' — is Epictetus's most direct training exercise. The 'open door' (thyra anoikta) metaphor recurs throughout the Discourses: death is always available as an exit, which transforms continued life into a voluntary commitment. The defense of Zeus/Providence is characteristic: the universe is not unjust for containing suffering; it is generous for equipping you to handle it.

D: Original: Hear how the Romans feel toward philosophers, if you would like to know. Italicus, who was the most in repute of the philosophers, once when I was present being, vexed with his own friends and as if he was suffering something intolerable said, "I cannot bear it, you are killing me: you will make me such as that man is"; pointing to me.
D: Refashioned:
Want to know how Romans regard philosophers? Italicus — one of the most respected philosophers in Rome — once lost his temper with friends and blurted out: 'I cannot bear it! You are killing me! You will make me like that man!' — pointing at Epictetus.

Even the famous philosopher could not hold his composure. And he considered becoming like Epictetus — a poor, crippled Stoic — the worst fate imaginable.
D: Elegant: The most respected philosopher in Rome, losing his temper, pointed at Epictetus and cried: 'You will make me like that man!' Even the famous philosopher considered the Stoic life the worst fate imaginable.
D: Distilled: The fact that a famous philosopher considered the Stoic life a punishment tells you everything about what passes for philosophy.
Notes: Italicus — identity uncertain, possibly a Stoic or Platonist philosopher prominent in Rome. The anecdote is self-deprecating humor: Epictetus, crippled and poor, is held up as the cautionary example of what philosophy might reduce you to. The joke works on two levels — it mocks both Italicus's pretensions and the Roman elite's conception of philosophy as something that should enhance your status rather than strip it away.

D: Original: Chapter 9: To a certain rhetorician who was going up to Rome on a suit

When a certain person came to him, who was going up to Rome on account of a suit which had regard to his rank, Epictetus inquired the reason of his going to Rome, and the man then asked what he thought about the matter. Epictetus replied: If you ask me what you will do in Rome, whether you will succeed or fall, I have no rule about this. But if you ask me how you will fare, I can tell you: if you have right opinions, you will fare well; if they are false, you will fare ill. For to every man the cause of his acting is opinion.
D: Refashioned: A rhetorician heading to Rome for a lawsuit asked Epictetus for advice. Epictetus replied: I cannot predict whether you will win or lose — I have no rule for that. But I can predict how you will fare. If your opinions are sound, you will fare well. If they are false, you will fare badly. Because every action traces back to opinion, and opinion is the only cause that matters.
D: Elegant: I cannot predict whether you will win or lose — I have no rule for that. But I can predict how you will fare: if your opinions are sound, well; if false, badly. Because opinion is the only cause that matters.
D: Distilled: I cannot predict whether you will win your case, but I can predict how you will fare — that depends entirely on the quality of your opinions.
Notes: The rhetorician's case involved his 'rank' (axioma) — likely a dispute over social status or civic honors, common in Roman provincial life. Epictetus's distinction between outcome (unpredictable) and internal experience (predictable based on principles) is the practical application of the dichotomy of control. The Cnossians — citizens of Cnossos (Knossos), the major city of Crete.

D: Original: For what is the reason why you desired to be elected governor of the Cnossians? Your opinion. What is the reason that you are now going up to Rome? Your opinion. And going in winter, and with danger and expense. "I must go." What tells you this? Your opinion. Then if opinions are the causes of all actions, and a man has bad opinions, such as the cause may be, such also is the effect. Have we then all sound opinions, both you and your adversary? And how do you differ? But have you sounder opinions than your adversary? Why? You think so.
D: Refashioned:
Why did you want to be governor of Cnossos? Your opinion told you it was worth wanting. Why are you traveling to Rome in winter, at expense and personal risk? Your opinion says you must. Everything you do traces back to what you believe is worth doing.

If opinion is the cause of every action, then bad opinions produce bad outcomes — not in the external sense, but in the sense that matters: what they do to you. You think your opinions are better than your opponent's. But so does your opponent. So do the delusional. 'I think I am right' is not evidence of anything.
D: Elegant: Why travel to Rome in winter at great risk? Because your opinion says you must. But your opponent's opinion says the same thing. So does every deluded person's. 'I think I am right' is not evidence of anything.
D: Distilled: Every action traces back to opinion — and 'I think I am right' is the one thing shared by the wise and the deluded alike.
Notes: Cnossos (Knossos) — principal city of Roman Crete. The observation that conviction of correctness is shared by the wise, the foolish, and the insane is a powerful epistemological point. This connects to the framework's Reason pillar: confidence in a belief is not evidence of its truth, and the feeling of certainty is the most unreliable guide to actual correctness.

D: Original: And so does he think that his opinions are better; and so do madmen. This is a bad criterion. But show to me that you have made some inquiry into your opinions and have taken some pains about them. And as now you are sailing to Rome in order to become governor of the Cnossians, and you are not content to stay at home with the honors which you had, but you desire something greater and more conspicuous, so when did you ever make a voyage for the purpose of examining your own opinions, and casting them out, if you have any that are bad? Whom have you approached for this purpose? What time have you fixed for it? What age? Go over the times of your life by yourself, if you are ashamed of me. When you were a boy, did you examine your own opinions? and did you not then, as you do all things now, do as you did do? and when you were become a youth and attended the rhetoricians, and yourself practiced rhetoric, what did you imagine that you were deficient in? And when you were a young man and engaged in public matters, and pleaded causes yourself, and were gaining reputation, who then seemed your equal? And when would you have submitted to any man examining and show that your opinions are bad? What, then, do you wish me to say to you? "Help me in this matter." I have no theorem (rule) for this.
D: Refashioned:
Madmen also think their opinions are superior. Conviction is not a criterion for truth. So show me the evidence that you have actually examined your opinions. When did you ever make a voyage not for business but for self-examination? When did you seek out someone who could test your beliefs?

As a boy, you absorbed opinions uncritically. As a young man studying rhetoric, you assumed you knew what mattered. As an adult practicing law, you gained reputation and stopped questioning entirely. At no point did you submit your operating assumptions to scrutiny. And now you want me to help you win a lawsuit.
D: Elegant: You have traveled for business, for politics, for reputation. When did you ever travel for the purpose of examining your own opinions? As a boy, you absorbed them uncritically. As an adult, you stopped questioning. Now you want help winning — but you have never examined the instrument you are using.
D: Distilled: You have traveled the world for wealth and status but never once made a journey to examine your own opinions.
Notes: The biographical sweep — boy, youth, young man, adult — shows how opinions calcify with age and success. Each stage provides apparent validation (grades, reputation, authority) that substitutes for genuine examination. This connects to the framework's concept of the erosion pattern: success and status can entrench bad opinions by removing the incentive to question them.

D: Original: Nor have you, if you came to me for this purpose, come to me as a philosopher, but as to a seller of vegetables or a shoemaker. "For what purpose then have philosophers theorems?" For this purpose, that whatever may happen, our ruling faculty may be and continue to be conformable to nature. Does this seem to you a small thing? "No; but the greatest." What then? does it need only a short time? and is it possible to seize it as you pass by? If you can, seize it.
D: Refashioned: You did not come to me as a philosopher — you came as you would go to a vegetable seller or a cobbler, looking for a quick transaction. But philosophy is not a product to purchase. It is the work of keeping your ruling faculty aligned with nature. Does that sound like a small thing? It is the greatest thing. And you think you can pick it up between appointments?
D: Elegant: You came to me the way you visit a cobbler — looking for a quick transaction. But philosophy is the work of aligning your ruling faculty with nature. It is the greatest thing there is. And you think you can seize it between appointments.
D: Distilled: Philosophy is not a product you purchase on the way to somewhere else — it is the greatest work there is.
Notes: The vegetable-seller (lachanopoles) and cobbler (skutotomos) analogies reduce the rhetorician's visit to its essence: he wants a commodity, not a transformation. Epictetus's frustration here is pedagogical: students who want philosophical wisdom but not philosophical discipline. This connects to the framework's observation about the difference between consuming ideas and being transformed by them.

D: Original: Then you will say, "I met with Epictetus as I should meet with a stone or a statue": for you saw me, and nothing more. But he meets with a man as a man, who learns his opinions, and in his turn shows his own. Learn my opinions: show me yours; and then say that you have visited me. Let us examine one another: if I have any bad opinion, take it away; if you have any, show it. This is the meaning of meeting with a philosopher. "Not so, but this is only a passing visit, and while we are hiring the vessel, we can also see Epictetus. Let us see what he says." Then you go away and say: "Epictetus was nothing: he used solecisms and spoke in a barbarous way." For of what else do you come as judges? "Well, but a man may say to me, "If I attend to such matters, I shall have no land, as you have none; I shall have no silver cups as you have none, nor fine beasts as you have none." In answer to this it is perhaps sufficient to say: I have no need of such things: but if you possess many things you have need of others: whether you choose or not, you are poorer than I am. "What then have I need of?" Of that which you have not: of firmness, of a mind which is conformable to nature, of being free from perturbation. Whether I have a patron or not, what is that to me? but it is something to you. I am richer than you: I am not anxious what Caesar will think of me: for this reason, I flatter no man. This is what I possess instead of vessels of silver and gold. You have utensils of gold; but your discourse, your opinions, your assents, your movements, your desires are of earthen ware.
D: Refashioned:
You will leave saying 'Epictetus was nothing — he spoke in a barbarous way and used bad grammar.' Because you came as a tourist, not as a student. You saw me the way you see a statue — something to glance at, not someone to engage with.

A genuine meeting between two people means this: show me your opinions and let me show you mine. Test mine and let me test yours. That is what it means to visit a philosopher.

You say: 'But if I pursue philosophy, I will have no land, no silver cups, no fine animals — just like you.' Perhaps. But I have no need of those things. You have many possessions and still need more. I am richer than you because I do not care what Caesar thinks of me. I flatter no one. You have vessels of gold, but your opinions, your judgments, your desires — those are made of clay.
D: Elegant: You saw me the way you see a statue — something to glance at. But a real meeting means: show me your opinions and I will show you mine. You have gold vessels. I have no need to flatter Caesar. You are wealthy in things. I am wealthy in not needing them. Your cups are gold, but your opinions are clay.
D: Distilled: You have vessels of gold but opinions of clay — and no amount of wealth can compensate for poverty of judgment.
Notes: The 'barbarous way' (barbarismos) and 'solecisms' (soloikismos) — grammatical errors. Epictetus spoke with a Phrygian accent and did not write polished Greek, which Roman elites would have noticed. His counter-argument — 'I am richer than you' — inverts the social hierarchy: true wealth is freedom from the need for anyone's approval. The 'vessels of gold/opinions of clay' contrast is one of Epictetus's most quotable lines.

D: Original: But when I have these things conformable to nature, why should I not employ my studies also upon reason? for I have leisure: my mind is not distracted. What shall I do, since I have no distraction? What more suitable to a man have I than this? When you have nothing to do, you are disturbed, you go to the theatre or you wander about without a purpose. Why should not the philosopher labour to improve his reason? You employ yourself about crystal vessels: I employ myself about the syllogism named "The Living": you about myrrhine vessels; I employ myself about the syllogism named "The Denying." To you everything appears small that you possess: to me all that I have appears great. Your desire is insatiable: mine is satisfied. To (children) who put their hand into a narrow necked earthen vessel and bring out figs and nuts, this happens; if they fill the hand, they cannot take it out, and then they cry. Drop a few of them and you will draw things out. And do you part with your desires: do not desire many things and you will have what you want.
D: Refashioned:
When my opinions are aligned with nature, I have leisure to study logic and reasoning — not because I need to prove anything, but because my mind is free. You occupy yourself with crystal goblets and fancy pottery. I occupy myself with 'The Liar' and 'The Denier.' Everything I have seems great to me. Everything you have seems small to you. Your desire is insatiable; mine is satisfied.

Children reach into a narrow-necked jar for figs and nuts. They grab as many as they can and then cannot pull their hand out. The solution is obvious: let go of a few. The same applies to desire. Release some of your wants and you will have what you actually need.
D: Elegant: Children reach into a narrow jar and grab as many figs as they can. Then the hand will not come out. The solution: let go of a few. You have crystal vessels and insatiable desire. I have plain walls and satisfaction. Release some of your wants and you will have what you need.
D: Distilled: The child who grabs too many figs cannot pull their hand from the jar — and neither can the person who refuses to let go of their wants.
Notes: The 'Liar' (Pseudomenos) and 'Denier' (Apoarnomenos) — famous logical paradoxes studied in Stoic logic. The narrow-necked jar metaphor is found in several ancient sources and remains one of the most effective images for the trap of excessive desire. This connects to the framework's concept of wisdom as knowing what to release: freedom comes not from acquisition but from the discipline of wanting less.

D: Original: Chapter 10: In what manner we ought to bear sickness

When the need of each opinion comes, we ought to have it in readiness: on the occasion of breakfast, such as relate to breakfast; in the bath, those that concern the bath; in bed, those that concern bed.

Let sleep not come upon thy languid eyes Before each daily action thou hast scann'd; What's done amiss, what done, what left undone; From first to last examine all, and then Blame what is wrong in what is right rejoice.
D: Refashioned:
For every situation, have the relevant principles ready. At meals, have your opinions about eating prepared. In the bath, about bathing. In bed, about rest. The Pythagorean evening verse applies:

Do not let sleep close your weary eyes until you have examined every action of the day. What did I do wrong? What did I do right? What did I leave undone? Start from the first and go to the last. Blame what deserves blame. Rejoice in what deserves it.
D: Elegant: For every situation, have the right principle ready. And each night before sleep: What did I do wrong? What right? What left undone? Blame what deserves blame. Rejoice in what deserves it.
D: Distilled: Have the right principle ready for every situation, and examine every action before sleep.
Notes: The Pythagorean 'Golden Verses' (Chrysa Epe) — a set of moral precepts attributed to Pythagoras, widely used in ancient philosophical education. The evening examination of conscience was practiced by Pythagoreans, Stoics (Seneca describes his own practice in De Ira 3.36), and later adopted by Christian monastic traditions. This connects to the framework's emphasis on daily practice as the mechanism of moral improvement.

D: Original: And we ought to retain these verses in such way that we may use them, not that we may utter them aloud, as when we exclaim "Paean Apollo." Again in fever we should have ready such opinions as concern a fever; and we ought not, as soon as the fever begins, to lose and forget all. (A man who has a fever) may "If I philosophize any longer, may I be hanged: wherever I go, I must take care of the poor body, that a fever may not come." But what is philosophizing? Is it not a preparation against events which may happen? Do you not understand that you are saying something of this kind? "If I shall still prepare myself to bear with patience what happens, may I be hanged." But this is just as if a man after receiving blows should give up the Pancratium. In the Pancratium it is in our power to desist and not to receive blows. But in the other matter, we give up philosophy, what shall we gain I gain? What then should a man say on the occasion of each painful thing? "It was for this that I exercised myself, for this I disciplined myself." God says to you, "Give me a proof that you have duly practiced athletics, that you have eaten what you ought, that you have been exercised, that you have obeyed the aliptes." Then do you show yourself weak when the time for action comes? Now is the time for the fever. Let it be borne well. Now is the time for thirst, well; now is the time for hunger, bear it well. Is it not in your power? who shall hinder you? The physician will hinder you from drinking; but he cannot prevent you from bearing thirst well: and he will hinder you from eating; but he cannot prevent you from bearing hunger well.
D: Refashioned:
Keep these principles not as things to recite but as things to use — not like shouting 'Paean Apollo!' at a festival, but like having a tool ready when the job demands it.

When fever comes, have your opinions about fever ready. Do not abandon philosophy the moment discomfort arrives. 'If I keep philosophizing, I will die!' But what do you think philosophy is? It is preparation for exactly this. Quitting philosophy when hardship comes is like quitting the pancratium after being hit. The whole point of the training was to prepare you for the blows.

God says: 'Show me proof that you trained properly — that you ate right, exercised, obeyed your trainer.' Now is the time for fever. Bear it well. Now is the time for thirst. Bear it well. The physician can forbid you from drinking, but he cannot prevent you from bearing thirst with dignity.
D: Elegant: Quitting philosophy when hardship comes is like quitting the pancratium after being hit. The whole point was to prepare you for blows. The physician can forbid you from drinking. He cannot prevent you from bearing thirst with dignity.
D: Distilled: The physician can prevent you from drinking, but no one can prevent you from bearing thirst with dignity.
Notes: Paean Apollo — a ritual exclamation at festivals honoring Apollo. The pancratium (pankration) metaphor — quitting after being struck — is central to Epictetus's athletic model of philosophy: training exists precisely for the moment of testing. The aliptes (athletic trainer) appears frequently in the Discourses as an analogy for the philosophical teacher. This connects to the framework's concept of tested wisdom: principles that collapse under pressure were never truly held.

D: Original: "But I cannot attend to my philosophical studies." And for what purpose do you follow them? Slave, is it not that you may be happy, that you may be constant, is it not that you may be in a state conformable to nature and live so? What hinders you when you have a fever from having your ruling faculty conformable to nature? Here is the proof of the thing, here is the test of the philosopher. For this also is a part of life, like walking, like sailing, like journeying by land, so also is fever. Do you read when you are walking? No.
D: Refashioned:
'But I cannot study when I have a fever!' And what is the purpose of study? To be happy, steady, and aligned with nature. Does a fever prevent you from maintaining your ruling faculty? Fever is part of life, like walking or sailing. You do not read while walking. You do not study while sailing. But you can walk well and sail well without a book in your hand.

The same applies to being sick. You cannot read during a fever, but you can be sick well. That is the test.
D: Elegant: You do not read while walking. You do not study while sailing. But you can walk well and sail well. Fever is the same — you cannot study during it, but you can bear it well. That is the test.
D: Distilled: You cannot study during a fever, but you can bear a fever well — and bearing it well is the real study.
Notes: The analogy between fever and walking/sailing reframes illness as just another mode of living, each with its own proper conduct. The question 'What hinders you when you have a fever from having your ruling faculty conformable to nature?' goes to the heart of Stoic practice: the ruling faculty operates independently of bodily condition.

D: Original: Nor do you when you have a fever. if you walk about well, you have all that belongs to a man who walks. If you bear fever well, you have all that belongs to a man in a fever. What is it to bear a fever well? Not to blame God or man; not to be afflicted it that which happens, to expect death well and nobly, to do what must be done: when the physician comes in, not to be frightened at what he says; nor if he says, "You are doing well," to be overjoyed. For what good has he told you? and when you were in health, what good was that to you? And even if he says, "You are in a bad way," do not despond.
D: Refashioned:
What does it mean to bear fever well? Not to blame God or other people. Not to be crushed by what is happening. To face death calmly if it comes. To do what must be done without drama.

When the physician says 'You are doing well,' do not celebrate — what real good has he told you? When he says 'You are in bad shape,' do not despair. What is 'bad shape'? Approaching the separation of soul and body. And if not now, then eventually. The news changes nothing about what is under your control.
D: Elegant: Bear fever well: do not blame God, do not blame others, do not despair. When the physician says you are well, what good has he told you? When he says you are dying, what has changed? The separation was always coming.
D: Distilled: The physician's report changes nothing about the only thing that matters — how you bear what comes.
Notes: The physician as a figure of limited authority — he can treat the body but cannot touch the will — recurs throughout the Discourses. The observation that 'you are doing well' from a doctor adds nothing of real value mirrors the Stoic position that health is a 'preferred indifferent' (proegmenon adiaphoron): desirable but not constitutive of the good.

D: Original: For what is it to be ill? is it that you are near the severance of the soul and the body? what harm is there in this? If you are not near now, will you not afterward be near? Is the world going to be turned upside down when you are dead? Why then do you flatter the physician? Why do you say, "If you please, master, I shall be well"? Why do you give him an opportunity of raising his eyebrows? Do you not value a physician, as you do a shoemaker when he is measuring your foot, or a carpenter when he is building your house, and so treat the physician as to the body which is not yours, but by nature dead? He who has a fever has an opportunity of doing this: if he does these things, he has what belongs to him.
D: Refashioned:
Being ill means approaching the separation of soul and body. What harm is there in that? If not now, then later. The world will not collapse when you die.

So why do you flatter the physician? Why do you say 'Please, master, I will get well'? Treat the physician the way you treat a cobbler measuring your foot — as a craftsman working on material that is not really yours. The body is by nature mortal. It was never permanently yours.

The person who is sick and remembers this has everything they need. The person who forgets it has nothing, no matter how good the medical care.
D: Elegant: Treat the physician the way you treat a cobbler — as a craftsman working on material that was never permanently yours. The body is by nature mortal. Remember this while sick and you have everything. Forget it and you have nothing.
D: Distilled: The body was never permanently yours — treat the physician as a craftsman working on borrowed material.
Notes: The cobbler and carpenter analogies reduce the physician's role to its proper scope: technical expertise on the body, which is a tool, not the self. 'By nature dead' (physei nekron) — the Stoic position that the body is already, in principle, a corpse; life is the temporary animation of matter that will return to its elements. This connects to the framework's concept of mortality as a feature, not a bug: the finitude of life is what gives choices their weight.

D: Original:
For it is not the business of a philosopher to look after these externals, neither his wine nor his oil nor his poor body, but his own ruling power. But as to externals how must he act? so far as not to be careless about them. Where then is there reason for fear? where is there, then, still reason for anger, and of fear about what belongs to others, about things which are of no value? For we ought to have these two principles in readiness: that except the will nothing is good nor bad; and that we ought not to lead events, but to follow them. "My brother ought not to have behaved thus to me." No; but he will see to that: and, however he may behave, I will conduct myself toward him as I ought.

For this is my own business: that belongs to another; no man can prevent this, the other thing can be hindered.
D: Refashioned:
A philosopher does not obsess over wine, oil, or the body. A philosopher tends the ruling faculty. As for externals — handle them without being careless, but do not let them become the focus.

Two principles should always be ready: first, nothing is good or bad except the will. Second, do not try to lead events — follow them. 'My brother should not have treated me this way.' Perhaps not. But that is his business. How I respond is mine — and no one can prevent that.

Follow events rather than trying to lead them. What is yours to control, no one can hinder. What is not yours, let it go.
D: Elegant: Two principles, always ready. First: nothing is good or bad except the will. Second: follow events, do not lead them. Your brother should not have treated you that way — perhaps. But his behavior is his business. Your response is yours. And no one can hinder that.
D: Distilled: Follow events rather than trying to lead them — and remember that your response is the only thing no one can hinder.
Notes: The chapter's closing formulation — 'follow events, do not lead them' (hepesthai tois ginomenois, me proageisthai) — is a core Stoic maxim. The brother example demonstrates the practical application: you cannot control another person's behavior, but you can control your orientation toward it. This maps to the framework's Right pillar: right action is responsive, not reactive.

D: Original: Chapter 11: Certain miscellaneous matters

There are certain penalties fixed as by law for those who disobey the divine administration. Whoever thinks any other thing to be good except those things which depend on the will, let him envy, let him desire, let him flatter, let him be perturbed: whoever considers anything else to be evil, let him grieve, let him lament, let him weep, let him be unhappy. And yet, though so severely punished, we cannot desist.
D: Refashioned:
Nature has fixed certain penalties for disobeying its laws — and they are built into the disobedience itself. If you treat externals as good, you will envy, desire, flatter, and be perpetually agitated. If you treat externals as evil, you will grieve, lament, and be miserable.

These are not punishments imposed from outside. They are the automatic consequences of false belief. And yet, even suffering these penalties constantly, we cannot seem to stop.
D: Elegant: The penalties for false belief are built into the believing. Treat externals as good and you will envy, flatter, and be agitated. Treat them as evil and you will grieve and lament. These are not imposed from outside — they are the automatic cost of the error.
D: Distilled: The punishment for valuing the wrong things is not imposed from outside — it is the misery that comes automatically from the valuing itself.
Notes: The concept of natural penalties (zemia) built into disobedience — rather than externally imposed — is a distinctly Stoic contribution to moral philosophy. Vice is its own punishment because it produces the very suffering it tries to avoid. This connects to the framework's observation about the erosion pattern: civilizations do not collapse from external attack alone but from the internal consequences of their own misaligned values.

D: Original:
Remember what the poet says about the stranger:

Stranger, I must not, e'en if a worse man come. This, then, may be applied even to a father: "I must not, even if a worse man than you should come, treat a father unworthily-, for all are from paternal Zeus." And of a brother, "For all are from the Zeus who presides over kindred." And so in the other relations of life we shall find Zeus to be an inspector.
D: Refashioned:
Homer says: 'Stranger, I must not treat you unworthily, even if a worse man than you should come.' Apply this to every relationship. Your father — treat him with dignity, even if he does not deserve it, because all fathers come from Zeus. Your brother — the same. Your kinsman — the same.

Dignity in how you treat others is not conditioned on their merit. It is conditioned on who you are and on the divine origin of every human being.
D: Elegant: Treat your father with dignity even if he does not deserve it — because all fathers come from Zeus. Dignity toward others is not conditioned on their merit. It is conditioned on yours.
D: Distilled: How you treat others reflects who you are, not who they are — and every person comes from the same source.
Notes: Homer, Odyssey 14.56-58 — Eumaeus the swineherd speaking to the disguised Odysseus, invoking Zeus Xenios (protector of strangers and guests). 'Paternal Zeus' (Zeus Pater) and 'Zeus who presides over kindred' (homognios Zeus) — different epithets reflecting Zeus's role as guardian of familial and social bonds. Epictetus universalizes the Homeric ethic: every relationship is sacred because every person shares divine origin. This connects to the framework's concept of Right: treating others well is an expression of your own values, not a reward for theirs.

D: Original: Chapter 12: About exercise

We ought not to make our exercises consist in means contrary to nature and adapted to cause admiration, for, if we do so, we, who call ourselves philosophers, shall not differ at all from jugglers. For it is difficult even to walk on a rope; and not only difficult, but it is also dangerous. Ought we for this reason to practice walking on a rope, or setting up a palm tree, or embracing statues? By no means. Everything, which is difficult and dangerous is not suitable for practice; but that is suitable which conduces to the working out of that which is proposed to us as a thing to be worked out. To live with desire and aversion, free from restraint. And what is this? Neither to be disappointed in that which you desire, nor to fall into anything which you would avoid. Toward this object, then, exercise ought to tend. For, since it is not possible to have your desire not disappointed and your aversion free from falling into that which you would avoid, great and constant practice you must know that if you allow your desire and aversion to turn to things which are not within the power of the will, you will neither have your desire capable of attaining your object, nor your aversion free from the power of avoiding that which you would avoid. And since strong habit leads, and we are accustomed to employ desire and aversion only to things which are not within the power of our will, we ought to oppose to this habit a contrary habit, and where there is great slipperiness in the appearances, there to oppose the habit of exercise.
D: Refashioned:
Do not train with spectacular stunts designed to impress. Walking a tightrope is difficult and dangerous — that does not make it philosophical exercise. The right kind of training is whatever helps you master desire and aversion so that you pursue what you should and avoid what you should without fail.

This requires enormous practice, because your habits run the wrong direction. You are accustomed to wanting what is outside your control and fearing what is outside your control. These habits must be countered with deliberate, sustained effort — especially where the pull of appearances is strongest.
D: Elegant: Walking a tightrope is difficult and dangerous. That does not make it philosophical exercise. The right training is whatever helps you master desire and aversion. And because your habits run in the wrong direction, the effort must be deliberate, sustained, and focused where the pull is strongest.
D: Distilled: Difficult and impressive is not the same as useful — train against what actually tempts you, not against what looks heroic.
Notes: The tightrope (schoinion), palm tree climbing, and statue-embracing were apparently stunts performed by ascetics or showmen. Epictetus's distinction between spectacular asceticism and genuine moral training is a consistent theme. This connects to the framework's concept of practice: the point of discipline is transformation, not display.

D: Original: I am rather inclined to pleasure: I will incline to the contrary side above measure for the sake of exercise. I am averse to pain: I will rub and exercise against this the appearances which are presented to me for the purpose of withdrawing my aversion from every such thing. For who is a practitioner in exercise? He who practices not using his desire, and applies his aversion only to things which are within the power of his will, and practices most in the things which are difficult to conquer. For this reason one man must practice himself more against one thing and another against another thing. What, then, is it to the purpose to set up a palm tree, or to carry about a tent of skins, or a mortar and a pestle? Practice, man, if you are irritable, to endure if you are abused, not to be vexed if you are treated with dishonour. Then you will make so much progress that, even if a man strikes you, you will say to yourself, "Imagine that you have embraced a statue": then also exercise yourself to use wine properly so as not to drink much, for in this also there are men who foolishly practice themselves; but first of all you should abstain from it, and abstain from a young girl and dainty cakes. Then at last, if occasion presents itself, for the purpose of trying yourself at a proper time, you will descend into the arena to know if appearances overpower you as they did formerly. But at first fly far from that which is stronger than yourself: the contest is unequal between a charming young girl and a beginner in philosophy. "The earthen pitcher," as the saying is, "and the rock do not agree."
D: Refashioned:
If you are drawn to pleasure, lean the opposite direction — overcorrect deliberately, as training. If you shrink from pain, expose yourself to it in controlled doses. The true practitioner is not someone who performs impressive feats but someone who does not use desire recklessly and applies aversion only to what is within the will.

If you are irritable, practice enduring insult without reacting. If struck, tell yourself: 'Imagine I embraced a statue.' Practice moderation with wine — not by making a show of abstinence, but by learning to drink without losing yourself.

But start with what you can handle. The earthen pot and the rock do not agree — do not test yourself against temptations that are stronger than your current capacity. A beginner in philosophy should not put themselves in the path of a powerful attraction. That contest is unequal. Build strength first, then test it.
D: Elegant: If pleasure pulls you, lean the opposite direction. If insult burns, practice absorbing it like a statue absorbs a blow. But start with what you can handle. The earthen pot and the rock do not agree. Do not test yourself against what is stronger than your current capacity.
D: Distilled: The earthen pot and the rock do not agree — do not test your beginning virtue against temptations stronger than your current strength.
Notes: The 'earthen pot and the rock' (chytra ostrakine kai petra) is a Greek proverb (cf. Aesop's fable of the earthen and brass pots). Apollonius of Tyana (c. 15-100 CE) — Neo-Pythagorean philosopher and ascetic. The advice to 'spit out the water and tell nobody' (at 3.55) is attributed to him. The overcorrection technique anticipates modern cognitive-behavioral approaches: deliberately opposing habitual tendencies to recalibrate the default.

D: Original: After the desire and the aversion comes the second topic of the movements toward action and the withdrawals from it; that you may be obedient to reason, that you do nothing out of season or place, or contrary to any propriety of the kind. The third topic concerns the assents, which is related to the things which are persuasive and attractive. For as Socrates said, "we ought not to live a life without examination," so we ought not to accept an appearance without examination, but we should say, "Wait, let me see what you are and whence you come"; like the watch at night, "Show me the pass." "Have you the signal from nature which the appearance that may be accepted ought to have?" And finally whatever means are applied to the body by those who exercise it, if they tend in any way toward desire and it, aversion, they also may be fit means of exercise; but if they are for display, they are the indications of one who has turned himself toward something external, and who is hunting for something else, and who looks for spectators who will say, "Oh the great man." For this reason, Apollonius said well, "When you intend to exercise yourself for your own advantage, and you are thirsty from heat, take in a mouthful of cold water, and spit it out, and tell nobody."
D: Refashioned:
After mastering desire and aversion comes the second discipline: right action. Act in season, in place, with propriety — obedient to reason, not to impulse. Third comes assent: do not accept any appearance without examination. As Socrates said, 'The unexamined life is not worth living.' Apply the same to every impression: 'Wait — let me see what you are and where you come from. Show me your credentials.'

As for physical training — if it serves desire and aversion, fine. If it is for display, it belongs to someone who has turned outward and hunts for spectators. Apollonius said it well: 'When you are thirsty from training and tempted by cold water, take a mouthful, spit it out, and tell nobody.'
D: Elegant: Do not accept any appearance without examination. 'Wait — let me see what you are and where you come from.' Physical training that serves character is philosophy. Physical training for display is spectacle. Apollonius: take the water in your mouth, spit it out, and tell nobody.
D: Distilled: Examine every impression before accepting it, and practice every discipline for transformation, not for applause.
Notes: Socrates's 'unexamined life' — from Plato's Apology 38a. The 'night watch' metaphor (nukterinos phylax) — checking credentials at the gate — transforms Socratic questioning into a daily practice. Apollonius of Tyana's advice to spit out the water quietly captures the Stoic attitude toward asceticism: the discipline is for you, not for your audience. This connects to the framework's emphasis on verified practice: genuine transformation is silent; performance is noise.

D: Original: Chapter 13: What solitude is, and what kind of person a solitary man is

Solitude is a certain condition of a helpless man. For because a man is alone, he is not for that reason also solitary; just as though a man is among numbers, he is not therefore not solitary. When then we have lost either a brother, or a son, or a friend on whom we were accustomed to repose, we say that we are left solitary, though we are often in Rome, though such a crowd meet us, though so many live in the same place, and sometimes we have a great number of slaves. For the man who is solitary, as it is conceived, is considered to be a helpless person and exposed to those who wish to harm him.
D: Refashioned:
Being alone is not the same as being solitary. You can be alone in a room and perfectly at peace. You can be in a crowd and desperately lonely. Solitude in the negative sense — helplessness, exposure, vulnerability — comes not from the absence of people but from the absence of reliable connection.

We say we are 'alone' when we lose someone we depended on, even if we are in a city of millions. What we mean is: we feel exposed. We feel that no one is watching out for us.
D: Elegant: Being alone is not being solitary. You can be alone in a room and at peace. You can be in a crowd and desperately lonely. Solitude is not the absence of people — it is the absence of reliable connection.
D: Distilled: Solitude is not the absence of people but the feeling that no one is watching out for you.
Notes: Epictetus distinguishes between eremia (physical aloneness) and monosis (desolate solitude). The distinction maps to modern psychology's finding that loneliness correlates more with perceived social support than with actual social contact. This connects to the framework's concept of community: what matters is not proximity but the quality and reliability of connection.

D: Original: For this reason when we travel, then especially do we say that we are lonely when we fall among robbers, for it is not the sight of a human creature which removes us from solitude, but the sight of one who is faithful and modest and helpful to us. For if being alone is enough to make solitude, you may say that even Zeus is solitary in the conflagration and bewails himself saying, "Unhappy that I am who have neither Hera, nor Athena, nor Apollo, nor brother, nor son, nor descendant nor kinsman." This is what some say that he does when he is alone at the conflagration.
D: Refashioned:
If physical aloneness is solitude, then Zeus himself would be solitary during the periodic conflagration when the cosmos dissolves back into fire and he is the only being left. But Zeus is not solitary — he dwells with himself, converses with himself, contemplates his own nature, and is at peace.

The capacity for self-sufficiency is not coldness. It is the ability to be your own companion when companions are absent — to think about the nature of things, to examine your own progress, to consider what still gives you pain and how to address it.
D: Elegant: If aloneness is solitude, Zeus himself would be lonely during the cosmic conflagration. But Zeus dwells with himself and is at peace. Self-sufficiency is not coldness — it is the capacity to be your own companion when companions are absent.
D: Distilled: Zeus is alone during the cosmic fire and not solitary — because he can dwell with himself in peace.
Notes: The Stoic ekpyrosis (conflagration) — the periodic destruction and regeneration of the cosmos, when everything returns to pure fire (Zeus/Logos). During this period, Zeus exists alone but is not desolate because he is self-sufficient (autarkes). Hera, Athena, Apollo — deities who exist as aspects of Zeus during the conflagration, not as independent beings. This connects to the framework's concept of intellectual self-sufficiency: the person who can think productively alone has a form of freedom that social creatures lack.

D: Original: For they do not understand how a man passes his life when he is alone, because they set out from a certain natural principle, from the natural desire of community and mutual love and from the pleasure of conversation among men. But none the less a man ought to be prepared in a manner for this also, to be able to be sufficient for himself and to be his own companion.
