For as Zeus dwells with himself, and is tranquil by himself, and thinks of his own administration and of its nature, and is employed in thoughts suitable to himself; so ought we also to be able to talk with ourselves, not to feel the want of others also, not to be unprovided with the means of passing our time; to observe the divine administration and the relation of ourselves to everything else; to consider how we formerly were affected toward things that happen and how at present; what are still the things which give us pain; how these also can be cured and how removed; if any things require improvement, to improve them according to reason.
D: Refashioned: People cannot imagine being content alone because they assume community and conversation are needs rather than preferences. These are natural and good — but a person should also be capable of self-sufficiency. Learn to be your own companion: examine your own administration of life, review how you respond to events, identify what still disturbs you, and work on removing it. This is what philosophy is for.
D: Elegant: Community is natural and good. But learn also to be your own companion — to examine your life, review your responses, identify what still disturbs you. That is what philosophy is for.
D: Distilled: The person who can be their own companion has a freedom that those who require constant company will never know.
Notes: The practical program Epictetus describes — self-examination, review of responses, identification of remaining disturbances — is essentially a meditation practice. The Stoic sage is not antisocial but is capable of functioning without social input when necessary. This connects to the framework's emphasis on reflective practice as the foundation of philosophical growth.

D: Original: For you see that Caesar appears to furnish us with great peace, that there are no longer enemies nor battles nor great associations of robbers nor of pirates, but we can travel at every hour and sail from east to west. But can Caesar give us security from fever also, can he from shipwreck, from fire, from earthquake or from lightning? well, I will say, can he give us security against love? He cannot. From sorrow? He cannot. From envy? He cannot. In a word then he cannot protect us from any of these things. But the doctrine of philosophers promises to give us security even against these things. And what does it say? "Men, if you will attend to me, wherever you are, whatever you are doing, you will not feel sorrow, nor anger, nor compulsion, nor hindrance, but you will pass your time without perturbations and free from everything." When a man has this peace, not proclaimed by Caesar (for how should he be able to proclaim it?), but by God through reason, is he not content when he is alone? when he sees and reflects, "Now no evil can happen to me; for me there is no robber, no earthquake, everything is full of peace, full of tranquillity: every way, every city, every meeting, neighbor, companion is harmless. One person whose business it is, supplies me with food; another with raiment; another with perceptions, and preconceptions. And if he does not supply what is necessary, He gives the signal for retreat, opens the door, and says to you, 'Go.' Go whither? To nothing terrible, but to the place from which you came, to your friends and kinsmen, to the elements: what there was in you of fire goes to fire; of earth, to earth; of air, to air; of water to water: no Hades, nor Acheron, nor Cocytus, nor Pyriphlegethon, but all is full of Gods and Demons." When a man has such things to think on, and sees the sun, the moon and stars, and enjoys earth and sea, he is not solitary nor even helpless. "Well then, if some man should come upon me when I am alone and murder me?" Fool, not murder you, but your poor body.
D: Refashioned:
Caesar provides peace from external enemies — no more pirates, no more bandits on the roads, you can travel freely from east to west. But can Caesar protect you from fever? From shipwreck? From grief, envy, or heartbreak? He cannot. Against these, he has no jurisdiction.

Philosophy promises security against exactly these — not by preventing them, but by making them unable to disturb you. The philosopher who has this says: 'No evil can happen to me. There is no robber of the will, no earthquake of the spirit. Everything is full of peace — not because nothing bad happens, but because nothing outside my will can constitute a real harm. And if life becomes truly unbearable, the door is open. I return to the elements — fire to fire, earth to earth, water to water. No Hades, no punishment, just dissolution into what I came from.'

A person who can think this way sees the sun, the moon, and the sea — and is neither solitary nor helpless. 'But what if someone murders me?' They murder my body, which was never me.
D: Elegant: Caesar secures the roads but not the spirit. Philosophy secures the spirit. No evil can touch my will. No earthquake can shake my judgment. If life becomes unbearable, the door is open — I return fire to fire, earth to earth. No Hades, no punishment. And if someone murders me, they murder my body — which was never me.
D: Distilled: Caesar can secure the roads, but only philosophy can secure the spirit — and the spirit is the only thing that was ever truly yours.
Notes: The Pax Romana — the period of relative peace across the Roman Empire, maintained by military power. The rivers of Hades — Acheron (woe), Cocytus (lamentation), Pyriphlegethon (fire) — are dismissed as mythology. The return to elements reflects Stoic physics: the soul is pneuma (breath/fire) that reunites with the cosmic pneuma at death. 'The door is open' (thyra anoikta) — the Stoic teaching that death is always an exit, transforming life into a voluntary commitment. This connects to the framework's concept of ultimate security: the only security worth having is internal, because external security is always contingent.

D: Original: What kind of solitude then remains? what want? why do we make ourselves worse than children? and what do children do when they are left alone? They take up shells and ashes, and they build something, then pull it down, and build something else, and so they never want the means of passing the time. Shall I, then, if you sail away, sit down and weep, because I have been left alone and solitary? Shall I then have no shells, no ashes? But children do what they do through want of thought, and we through knowledge are unhappy.
D: Refashioned:
What kind of solitude really threatens us? Children left alone pick up shells and pebbles, build things, knock them down, and build again. They never run out of occupation because they never run out of imagination.

Shall I weep because you have sailed away and left me alone? Do I not have my own shells and pebbles — my own thoughts, my own reflections, my own principles to build with? Children play out of ignorance and are never bored. We have knowledge — and manage to be miserable anyway.
D: Elegant: Children left alone pick up shells, build castles, knock them down, and build again — never bored, never solitary. We have knowledge and philosophy and manage to be more miserable than children with pebbles.
D: Distilled: Children with shells are never bored because they never run out of imagination — and neither should the philosopher.
Notes: The children-and-shells image is one of Epictetus's most poignant: the contrast between childlike self-sufficiency (which comes from ignorance) and adult dependency (which comes, paradoxically, from knowledge). The implication is that philosophical maturity should restore what childhood had naturally: the ability to be occupied and content with what is available.

D: Original: Every great power is dangerous to beginners. You must then bear such things as you are able, but conformably to nature: but not... Practice sometimes a way of living like a man in health. Abstain from food, drink water, abstain sometimes altogether from desire, in order that you may some time desire consistently with reason; and if consistently with reason, when you have anything good in you, you will desire well. "Not so; but we wish to live like wise men immediately and to be useful to men." Useful how? what are you doing? have you been useful to yourself? "But, I suppose, you wish to exhort them." You exhort them! You wish to be useful to them. Show to them in your own example what kind of men philosophy makes, and don't trifle. When you are eating, do good to those who eat with you; when you are drinking, to those who are drinking with you; by yielding to all, giving way, bearing with them, thus do them good, and do not spit on them your phlegm.
D: Refashioned:
Great power is dangerous for beginners — bear only what you can, and do it in accordance with nature. Practice living like a healthy person even when you are not: fast sometimes, drink only water sometimes, abstain from desire entirely for a period, so that when you do desire, you desire well.

But do not rush to teach others. Be useful first by being an example. When you eat with people, be good company. When you drink with them, be generous. Do not vomit your philosophy onto people who did not ask for it. Show them what philosophy produces — not by lecturing, but by living.
D: Elegant: Do not vomit your philosophy onto people who did not ask for it. Be useful by being an example. When you eat with others, be good company. When you drink, be generous. Show what philosophy produces — not by lecturing but by living.
D: Distilled: Show what philosophy produces by how you live, not by what you preach — and never spit your wisdom on people who did not ask for it.
Notes: The metaphor of 'spitting phlegm' (phlegma) on others captures Epictetus's contempt for unsolicited philosophical lecturing. The practical advice — fast, drink water, practice abstention — resembles monastic discipline. The key insight: your philosophical practice is demonstrated through the quality of your social presence, not through your arguments. This connects to the framework's concept of embodied wisdom: the most powerful teaching is a life well-lived.

D: Original: Chapter 14: Certain miscellaneous matters

As bad tragic actors cannot sing alone, but in company with many: so some persons cannot walk about alone. Man, if you are anything, both walk alone and talk to yourself, and do not hide yourself in the chorus. Examine a little at last, look around, stir yourself up, that you may know who you are.

When a man drinks water, or does anything for the sake of practice, whenever there is an opportunity he tells it to all: "I drink water." Is it for this that you drink water, for the purpose of drinking water? Man, if it is good for you to drink, drink; but if not, you are acting ridiculously. But if it is good for you and you do drink, say nothing about it to those who are displeased with water-drinkers. What then, do you wish to please these very men?
D: Refashioned:
Bad tragic actors cannot sing alone — they need the chorus to cover them. Some people are the same: they cannot walk alone, think alone, or be alone for a moment. If you are worth anything, walk alone sometimes. Examine yourself. Stir yourself up. Find out who you are.

And when you practice austerity — drinking only water, fasting, enduring cold — do not announce it to everyone. If drinking water is good for you, drink it. If it is not, you are being ridiculous. But if it is good and you do drink it, keep quiet about it. The people who are displeased by your discipline are not your audience.
D: Elegant: Bad actors need a chorus. Some people are the same — they cannot walk alone for a moment. Walk alone. Think alone. Find out who you are. And when you practice austerity, do it for yourself. The people displeased by your discipline are not your audience.
D: Distilled: Walk alone, think alone, and never announce your austerities — the discipline is for you, not for applause.
Notes: The bad actor (kakos tragōdos) who needs the chorus is a vivid image for the person who cannot function without social support. The water-drinking passage addresses a common vice among ancient philosophical students: making a performance of asceticism. This connects to the framework's distinction between genuine practice (which is private and transformative) and performative virtue (which is public and hollow).

D: Original:
Of things that are done some are done with a final purpose, some according to occasion, others with a certain reference to circumstances, others for the purpose of complying with others. and some according to a fixed scheme of life.

You must root out of men these two things, arrogance and distrust. Arrogance, then, is the opinion that you want nothing: but distrust is the opinion that you cannot be happy when so many circumstances surround you. Arrogance is removed by confutation; and Socrates was the first who practiced this. And, that the thing is not impossible, inquire and seek. This search will do you no harm; and in a manner this is philosophizing, to seek how it is possible to employ desire and aversion without impediment.
D: Refashioned:
Two diseases must be rooted out: arrogance and distrust. Arrogance is the belief that you already have everything you need — that there is nothing left to learn. Distrust is the belief that happiness is impossible given your circumstances — that the obstacles are too great.

Socrates was the first to treat arrogance: he showed people they did not know what they thought they knew. As for distrust — the search itself is the cure. To seek how desire and aversion can be used without impediment is already to philosophize.
D: Elegant: Two diseases: arrogance (the belief you need nothing) and distrust (the belief that happiness is impossible). Socrates cured the first by showing people their ignorance. The second cures itself — because the search for unimpeded desire is already philosophy.
D: Distilled: Arrogance says you need nothing more; distrust says happiness is impossible — philosophy begins by curing both.
Notes: The pairing of arrogance (oiesis) and distrust (apistia) captures two opposite obstacles to philosophical progress. Socrates's method of elenchus (refutation) addresses the first by demonstrating ignorance. The claim that seeking how to use desire without impediment is itself philosophizing echoes Socrates's identification of philosophy with the examined life. This connects to the framework's concept of philosophical humility: the starting point is acknowledging both what you lack and that improvement is possible.

D: Original: "I am superior to you, for my father is a man of consular rank." Another says, "I have been a tribune, but you have not." If we were horses, would you say, "My father was swifter?" "I have much barley and fodder, or elegant neck ornaments." If, then, while you were saying this, I said, "Be it so: let us run then." Well, is there nothing in a man such as running in a horse, by which it will he known which is superior and inferior? Is there not modesty, fidelity, justice? Show yourself superior in these, that you may be superior as a man. If you tell me that you can kick violently, I also will say to you that you are proud of that which is the act of an ass.
D: Refashioned:
'My father was a consul.' Good for him. Another says: 'I have been a tribune.' Good for you. If we were horses, would you boast that your father was faster? Among horses, the measure is speed. Among humans, the measure should be character.

Is there nothing in a human being like running in a horse — some capacity by which we can judge who is superior? There is: modesty, fidelity, justice. Show yourself superior in these, and you are superior as a human being. If your chief accomplishment is that you can kick hard, congratulations — you are proud of something an ass can do.
D: Elegant: If we were horses, would you boast that your father was faster? Among horses, speed decides. Among humans, modesty, fidelity, and justice decide. If your proudest achievement is a powerful kick, you are celebrating the talent of an ass.
D: Distilled: Among horses, speed decides superiority; among humans, it is modesty, fidelity, and justice — not whose father held higher office.
Notes: The consul (hypatos) was the highest regularly elected office in Rome. The tribune (chiliarchos) was a military officer. The horse-racing analogy strips away social pretension by applying the standard appropriate to the species. The ass (onos) comparison is characteristically Epictetan: blunt, funny, and devastating. This connects to the framework's emphasis on character over credentials.

D: Original: Chapter 15: That we ought to proceed with circumspection to everything

In every act consider what precedes and what follows, and then proceed to the act. If you do not consider, you will at first begin with spirit, since you have not thought at all of the things which follow; but afterward, when some consequences have shown themselves, you will basely desist. "I wish to conquer at the Olympic games." "And I too, by the gods: for it is a fine thing." But consider here what precedes and what follows; and then, if it is for your good, undertake the thing. You must act according to rules, follow strict diet, abstain from delicacies, exercise yourself by compulsion at fixed times, in heat, in cold; drink no cold water, nor wine, when there is opportunity of drinking it. In a word you must surrender yourself to the trainer as you do to a physician. Next in the contest, you must be covered with sand, sometimes dislocate a hand, sprain an ankle, swallow a quantity of dust, be scourged with the whip; and after undergoing all this, you must sometimes be conquered. After reckoning all these things, if you have still an inclination, go to the athletic practice. If you do not reckon them, observe you behave like children who at one time you wi play as wrestlers, then as gladiators, then blow a trumpet, then act a tragedy, when they have seen and admired such things. So you also do: you are at one time a wrestler, then a gladiator, then a philosopher, then a rhetorician; but with your whole soul you are nothing: like the ape, you imitate all that you see; and always one thing after another pleases you, but that which becomes familiar displeases you. For you have never undertaken anything after consideration, nor after having explored the whole matter and put it to a strict examination; but you have undertaken it at hazard and with a cold desire. Thus some persons having seen a philosopher and having heard one speak like Euphrates- yet who can speak like him?- wish to be philosophers themselves.
D: Refashioned:
Before you commit to anything, count the full cost. You want to win at the Olympics? Beautiful ambition. But first consider what comes before and after the victory. Strict diet. Compulsory exercise in heat and cold. No wine, no indulgence. Complete obedience to your trainer. In competition: covered in sand, joints wrenched, possible defeat after all of it. If you still want it after reckoning all this, go.

But if you do not count the cost, you will behave like children who play wrestlers one hour, gladiators the next, trumpeters after that, actors by evening. You imitate whatever impresses you most recently, like an ape. Each new fascination displaces the last, because you never committed with full knowledge of the price.

Some people hear a philosopher speak like Euphrates and immediately want to be philosophers. But have you considered what philosophy actually demands?
D: Elegant: Before committing, count the full cost. Olympic training means strict diet, compulsory exercise, no wine, possible defeat. If you still want it after reckoning all of this, go. Otherwise you are a child playing wrestler one hour, gladiator the next, philosopher by evening — an ape imitating whatever impressed you last.
D: Distilled: Count the full cost before committing — otherwise you are an ape imitating whatever impressed you most recently.
Notes: Euphrates of Tyre (c. 35-118 CE) — Stoic philosopher praised by Pliny the Younger for his eloquence. The Olympic training regimen described here reflects actual ancient athletic practice. The 'ape' (pithekos) — imitating without understanding — is Epictetus's harshest image for the uncommitted student. This connects to the framework's emphasis on commitment: genuine transformation requires full knowledge of the cost and full willingness to pay it.

D: Original:
Man, consider first what the matter is, then your own nature also, what it is able to bear. If you are a wrestler, look at your shoulders, your thighs, your loins: for different men are naturally formed for different things. Do you think that, if you do, you can be a philosopher? Do you think that you can eat as you do now, drink as you do now, and in the same way be angry and out of humour? You must watch, labour, conquer certain desires, you must depart from your kinsmen, be despised by your slave, laughed at by those who meet you, in everything you must be in an inferior condition, as to magisterial office, in honours, in courts of justice. When you have considered all these things completely, then, if you think proper, approach to philosophy, if you would gain in exchange for these things freedom from perturbations, liberty, tranquillity. If you have not considered these things, do not approach philosophy: do not act like children, at one time a philosopher, then a tax collector, then a rhetorician, then a procurator of Caesar These things are not consistent. You must be one man either good or bad: you must either labour at your own ruling faculty or at external things: you must either labour at things within or at external things: that is, you must either occupy the place of a philosopher or that of one of the vulgar.

A person said to Rufus when Galba was murdered, "Is the world now governed by Providence?" But Rufus replied, "Did I ever incidentally form an argument from Galba that the world is governed by Providence?"
D: Refashioned:
Consider what the matter actually requires, and then consider whether your nature can bear it. A wrestler examines his shoulders and thighs. A philosopher must examine something different: can you give up comfort? Endure contempt? Be laughed at by people you once impressed? Accept being inferior in wealth, status, and social standing — in exchange for freedom from perturbation?

If you have not considered this fully, do not approach philosophy. Do not be like children — a philosopher today, a tax collector tomorrow, a rhetorician next week, then a Caesar's agent. These are not consistent. You must be one person: either good or bad. Either work on your ruling faculty or work on externals. Either be a philosopher or be one of the crowd. You cannot occupy both positions.

When Galba was murdered, someone asked Rufus: 'Is the world still governed by Providence?' Rufus replied: 'Did I ever build my argument for Providence on the life of Galba?'
D: Elegant: Can you endure contempt, poverty, and ridicule in exchange for freedom from perturbation? If not, do not approach. You must be one person — good or bad. A philosopher or one of the crowd. When Galba was murdered, someone asked Rufus about Providence. He replied: 'Did I ever build my argument on the life of Galba?'
D: Distilled: You must be one person — either a philosopher who works on the ruling faculty or someone who works on externals, but not both.
Notes: Galba (Servius Sulpicius Galba, 3 BCE-69 CE) — Roman emperor for seven months, murdered by the Praetorian Guard in January 69 CE during the Year of the Four Emperors. Musonius Rufus's response is a masterclass in Stoic argumentation: Providence is not demonstrated by any individual's fate but by the rational structure of the cosmos as a whole. The tax collector (telones), rhetorician, and procurator of Caesar represent typical career paths for Roman elites — each incompatible with philosophical commitment. This connects to the framework's concept of integrity: you cannot serve two masters.

D: Original: Chapter 16: That we ought with caution to enter, into familiar intercourse with men

If a man has frequent intercourse with others, either for talk, or drinking together, or generally for social purposes, he must either become like them, or change them to his own fashion. For if a man places a piece of quenched charcoal close to a piece that is burning, either the quenched charcoal will quench the other, or the burning charcoal will light that which is quenched. Since, then, the danger is so great, we must cautiously enter into such intimacies with those of the common sort, and remember that it is impossible that a man can keep company with one who is covered with soot without being partaker of the soot himself. For what will you do if a man speaks about gladiators, about horses, about athletes, or, what is worse, about men? "Such a person is bad," "Such a person is good": "This was well done," "This was done badly." Further, if he scoff, or ridicule, or show an ill-natured disposition? Is any man among us prepared like a lute-player when he takes a lute, so that as soon as he has touched the strings, he discovers which are discordant, and tunes the instrument? such a power as Socrates had who in all his social intercourse could lead his companions to his own purpose? How should you have this power? It is therefore a necessary consequence that you are carried about by the common kind of people.
D: Refashioned:
When you spend time with people, one of two things happens: either you change them or they change you. Place a dead coal next to a burning one — either the burning coal lights the dead one, or the dead one extinguishes the burning one.

The danger is real, so be cautious about casual social intimacy. You cannot keep company with someone covered in soot without getting soot on yourself. What will you do when the conversation turns to gossip, to spectacles, to petty judgments about people? 'He is bad.' 'She was lucky.' 'That was done badly.' Are you prepared — like Socrates with a lute — to tune the conversation the moment a string goes flat? You are not. So you will be carried along by the current.

Their idle words carry the force of genuine conviction. Your philosophical words carry only the force of recent reading. That is why they win.
D: Elegant: Place a dead coal next to a burning one. One will light the other — or extinguish it. You cannot keep company with soot without becoming sooty. Their idle words carry genuine conviction. Your philosophy carries only recent reading. That is why they win.
D: Distilled: You cannot keep company with soot without becoming sooty — and casual conviction is stronger than recent philosophy.
Notes: The charcoal metaphor (anthrax) is one of the most vivid in the Discourses. The lute-player (kitharistes) represents Socrates's ability to 'tune' any conversation — a capacity Epictetus says his students do not yet have. The observation that the vulgar speak from conviction while students speak from the lips anticipates the modern concept of 'internalized' vs. 'professed' values. This connects to the framework's emphasis on the gap between knowing and being: untested knowledge loses every contest against deeply held habit.

D: Original: Why, then, are they more powerful than you? Because they utter these useless words from their real opinions: but you utter your elegant words only from your lips; for this reason they are without strength and dead, and it is nauseous to listen to your exhortations and your miserable virtue, which is talked of everywhere. In this way the vulgar have the advantage over you: for every opinion is strong and invincible. Until, then, the good sentiments are fixed in you, and you shall have acquired a certain power for your security, I advise you to be careful in your association with like wax in the sun there will be melted away whatever you inscribe on your minds in the school. Withdraw, then, yourselves far from the sun so long as you have these waxen sentiments. For this reason also philosophers advise men to leave their native country, because ancient habits distract them and do not allow a beginning to be made of a different habit; nor can we tolerate those who meet us and say: "See such a one is now a philosopher, who was once so-and-so." Thus also physicians send those who have lingering diseases to a different country and a different air; and they do right, Do you also introduce other habits than those which you have: fix your opinions and exercise yourselves in them. But you do not so: you go hence to a spectacle, to a show of gladiators, to a place of exercise, to a circus; then you come back hither, and again from this place you go to those places, and still the same persons. And there is no pleasing habit, nor attention, nor care about self and observation of this kind, "How shall I use the appearances presented to me? according to nature, or contrary to nature? how do I answer to them? as I ought, or as I ought not? Do I say to those things which are independent of the will, that they do not concern me?" For if you are not yet in this state, fly from your former habits, fly from the common sort, if you intend ever to begin to be something.
D: Refashioned:
The crowd has power over you because they speak from the gut and you speak from the textbook. Their opinions are alive; yours are dead on your lips. Your virtue is something you talk about, not something you are. That is why listening to your exhortations is unbearable.

Until your principles are hardened — until they are as much a part of you as the crowd's opinions are a part of them — guard yourself carefully. Your convictions are like wax in the sun: stay near the heat and they will melt. Philosophers have always advised students to leave home, break old habits, go somewhere that the old patterns cannot reach you. Physicians do the same for patients with chronic illness — change the climate.

You need new habits. You need to fix your opinions, exercise yourself in them, and attend to yourself with the discipline of a night watchman checking every impression: 'Who goes there? Show me your credentials. Are you aligned with nature or against it?' If you are not yet at this level of vigilance, leave. Leave the old environment, leave the spectacles and the circuses, leave the former companions — if you ever intend to become something.
D: Elegant: The crowd speaks from the gut. You speak from the textbook. Until your principles are hardened, your convictions are wax in the sun — stay near the heat and they melt. Physicians send chronic patients to a new climate. Philosophers send students away from home. If you ever intend to become something, leave.
D: Distilled: Your convictions are wax in the sun — until they harden, stay away from the heat that will melt them.
Notes: The wax metaphor captures the vulnerability of newly formed convictions to social pressure. The medical analogy — physicians prescribing a change of climate for chronic illness — was literal practice in ancient medicine (Galen and Hippocrates both recommended it). The philosopher's recommendation to leave home (apodemia) reflects the ancient practice of philosophical exile or relocation as a tool for transformation. This connects to the framework's concept of environment as a variable: the context in which you develop either supports or undermines your growth.

D: Original: Chapter 17: On providence

When you make any charge against Providence, consider, and you will learn that the thing has happened according to reason. "Yes, but the unjust man has the advantage." In what? "In money." Yes, for he is superior to you in this, that he flatters, is free from shame, and is watchful. What is the wonder? But see if he has the advantage over you in being faithful, in being modest: for you will not find it to be so; but wherein you are superior, there you will find that you have the advantage. And I once said to a man who was vexed because Philostorgus was fortunate: "Would you choose to lie with Sura?" "May it never happen," he replied, "that this day should come?" "Why then are you vexed, if he receives something in return for that which he sells; or how can you consider him happy who acquires those things by such means as you abominate; or what wrong does Providence, if he gives the better things to the better men? Is it not better to be modest than to be rich?" He admitted this. Why are you vexed then, man, when you possess the better thing? Remember, then, always, and have in readiness, the truth that this is a law of nature, that the superior has an advantage over the inferior in that in which he is superior; and you will never be vexed.
D: Refashioned:
When you feel Providence has been unjust, look again. The unjust person has more money — true. But that is because he flatters, lies, and feels no shame. These are the purchase price. Would you trade your modesty for his bank account? Would you sleep with his patron to get his position? No? Then stop complaining that he has something you would not pay for.

Nature operates by a simple law: the superior has an advantage over the inferior in the domain of its superiority. The unjust man is superior in acquiring money because he has cultivated the skills money requires — shamelessness, flattery, watchfulness. You are superior in integrity. Each of you has what your cultivation produces. If you want his results, adopt his methods. If his methods repulse you, accept that you have chosen the better thing and stop envying the worse.
D: Elegant: The unjust man has more money because he flatters, lies, and feels no shame. Would you trade your modesty for his bank account? No? Then stop envying what you would not pay for. Each person gets what their cultivation produces.
D: Distilled: Stop envying what you would not pay for — the unjust person's wealth costs exactly what you refuse to spend.
Notes: Philostorgus — a contemporary known for acquiring wealth through servile means (the reference to 'lying with Sura' implies sexual favors for a patron). The natural law Epictetus articulates — each person excels in what they have cultivated — is a restatement of the principle from 3.27: you get what you practice. This connects to the framework's concept of trade-offs: every form of excellence has a price, and the price of material success through corruption is the loss of the very qualities that make success meaningful.

D: Original: "But my wife treats me badly." Well, if any man asks you what this is, say, "My wife treats me badly." "Is there, then, nothing more?" Nothing. "My father gives me nothing." But to say that this is an evil is something which must be added to it externally, and falsely added. For this reason we must not get rid of poverty, but of the opinion about poverty, and then we shall be happy.
D: Refashioned:
'My wife treats me badly.' That is a fact. 'My father gives me nothing.' That is a fact. But to say these are evils — that is an addition you are making from outside, and it is false.

We must eliminate not poverty but the opinion about poverty. Not the difficult spouse but the opinion about the difficult spouse. Strip away what you are adding, and what remains is bearable — often trivially so. The catastrophe is never in the event. It is in the editorial you write about it.
D: Elegant: Your wife treats you badly — that is a fact. That it is an evil — that is your addition. Eliminate not poverty but the opinion about poverty. The catastrophe is never in the event. It is in the editorial you write about it.
D: Distilled: The catastrophe is never in the event — it is in the editorial you write about it.
Notes: This passage distills one of the most important Stoic principles into its simplest form: events are morally neutral; it is our judgments (prosthesis) that make them good or evil. 'We must not get rid of poverty but of the opinion about poverty' echoes Seneca (Letters 78.13) and Marcus Aurelius (Meditations 4.3). This connects to the framework's Reason pillar: the quality of our experience is determined not by what happens but by the framework through which we interpret it.

D: Original: Chapter 18: That we ought not to be disturbed by any news
When anything shall be reported to you which is of a nature to disturb, have this principle in readiness, that the news is about nothing which is within the power
D: Refashioned: When disturbing news reaches you, remember: the news concerns things outside your will. No report, however alarming, can touch the one thing that matters.
D: Elegant: When disturbing news arrives, remember: it is about something outside your will. It cannot touch what matters.
D: Distilled: No news, however alarming, concerns the one thing that is actually in your power.
Notes: This final fragment of Book 3 — apparently incomplete in the manuscript tradition — nevertheless captures the book's central teaching in miniature: the dichotomy of control applied to the most common trigger of anxiety (unexpected news). The text breaks off mid-sentence in the original, suggesting a lost continuation.

D: Original: Chapter 1: About freedom

He is free who lives as he wishes to live; who is neither subject to compulsion nor to hindrance, nor to force; whose movements to action are not impeded, whose desires attain their purpose, and who does not fall into that which he would avoid. Who, then, chooses to live in error? No man. Who chooses to live deceived, liable to mistake, unjust, unrestrained, discontented, mean? No man. Not one then of the bad lives as he wishes; nor is he, then, free. And who chooses to live in sorrow, fear, envy, pity, desiring and failing in his desires, attempting to avoid something and falling into it? Not one. Do we then find any of the bad free from sorrow, free from fear, who does not fall into that which he would avoid, and does not obtain that which he wishes? Not one; nor then do we find any bad man free.
D: Refashioned:
Freedom is not living as you please. It is living without compulsion, without hindrance, without failed desires. No one chooses to live deceived, unjust, out of control, or discontented. No bad person, then, lives as they wish — and no one who does not live as they wish is free.

Test it against the people you think are free. The envious, the fearful, the perpetually disappointed — do any of them escape sorrow? Do any of them avoid what they dread? None. Then none of them are free.
D: Elegant: Freedom is not doing what you want. It is wanting only what you can control. The deceived, the fearful, the perpetually disappointed — none of them live as they wish. And no one who does not live as they wish is free.
D: Distilled: Freedom is not living as you please but living without failed desires — and no one whose desires depend on externals achieves that.
Notes: Epictetus opens Book 4 — the final book — with a redefinition of freedom (eleutheria). In Roman culture, freedom was a legal and political status. Epictetus, a former slave, redefines it as a psychological and philosophical achievement. This connects to the framework's emphasis on the ideational domain of power: true sovereignty is over one's own judgments, not over external circumstances.

D: Original: If, then, a man who has been twice consul should hear this, if you add, "But you are a wise man; this is nothing to you": he will pardon you. But if you tell him the truth, and say, "You differ not at all from those who have been thrice sold as to being yourself not a slave," what else ought you to expect than blows? For he says, "What, I a slave, I whose father was free, whose mother was free, I whom no man can purchase: I am also of senatorial rank, and a friend of Caesar, and I have been a consul, and I own many slaves." In the first place, most excellent senatorial man, perhaps your father also was a slave in the same kind of servitude, and your mother, and your grandfather and all your ancestors in an ascending series. But even if they were as free as it is possible, what is this to you? What if they were of a noble nature, and you of a mean nature; if they were fearless, and you a coward; if they had the power of self-restraint, and you are not able to exercise it.
D: Refashioned: Tell a twice-elected consul that he is a slave, and expect to be struck. 'I, a slave? My parents were free, I am of senatorial rank, a friend of Caesar, I own many slaves.' But perhaps your father was enslaved in the same way — not legally, but through cowardice, through dependence, through inability to restrain himself. Your ancestors' freedom does not make you free. What matters is not your lineage but your character: whether you are fearless or a coward, self-restrained or helpless.
D: Elegant: The consul will hit you for calling him a slave. His parents were free, he says, his rank is senatorial, he is Caesar's friend. But freedom is not inherited. If your ancestors were brave and you are a coward, their freedom is theirs. Yours is still to be earned.
D: Distilled: Freedom is not inherited — your ancestors' courage does not make you brave.
Notes: The twice-elected consul (bis consul) represents the Roman elite who confused legal status with genuine freedom. The 'twelve fasces' (fasces) were bundles of rods carried before Roman magistrates as symbols of authority. Epictetus's point — that inner slavery persists regardless of external rank — echoes his own biography as a former slave who became freer than his masters.

D: Original: "And what," you may say, "has this to do with being a slave?" Does it seem to you to be nothing to do a thing unwillingly, with compulsion, with groans, has this nothing to do with being a slave? "It is something," you say: "but who is able to compel me, except the lord of all, Caesar?" Then even you yourself have admitted that you have one master. But that he is the common master of all, as you say, let not this console you at all: but know that you are a slave in a great family. So also the people of Nicopolis are used to exclaim, "By the fortune of Caesar, are free."
D: Refashioned: 'What does this have to do with slavery?' Everything. Doing things unwillingly, under compulsion, with groaning — this is slavery. 'But who compels me except Caesar?' Then you have admitted you have one master. That he is everyone's master does not improve your position. You are a slave in a large household. The people of Nicopolis say, 'By Caesar's fortune, we are free.' That is not freedom. That is permission.
D: Elegant: You do things unwillingly, under compulsion, with groaning — and ask what this has to do with slavery? You admit Caesar is your master, then console yourself that he is everyone's master. A slave in a large household is still a slave.
D: Distilled: That your master rules everyone does not make you less a slave — it makes the household larger.
Notes: Nicopolis — the city in northwestern Greece where Epictetus ran his school after being expelled from Rome by Domitian in 93 CE. The phrase 'By the fortune of Caesar, we are free' captures the paradox Epictetus attacks: 'freedom' granted by an authority is merely conditional permission, not genuine autonomy. This connects to the framework's distinction between the social and ideational domains of power.

D: Original: However, if you please, let us not speak of Caesar at present. But tell me this: did you never love any person, a young girl, or slave, or free? What then is this with respect to being a slave or free? Were you never commanded by the person beloved to do something which you did not wish to do? have you never flattered your little slave? have you never kissed her feet? And yet if any man compelled you to kiss Caesar's feet, you would think it an insult and excessive tyranny. What else, then, is slavery? Did you never go out by night to some place whither you did not wish to go, did you not expend what you did not wish to expend, did you not utter words with sighs and groans, did you not submit to abuse and to be excluded? But if you are ashamed to confess your own acts, see what Thrasonides says and does, who having seen so much military service as perhaps not even you have, first of all went out by night, when Geta does not venture out, but if he were compelled by his master, would have cried out much and would have gone out lamenting his bitter slavery. Next, what does Thrasonides say? "A worthless girl has enslaved me, me whom no enemy, ever did." Unhappy man, who are the slave even of a girl, and a worthless girl. Why then do you still call yourself free? and why do you talk of your service in the army? Then he calls for a sword and is angry with him who out of kindness refuses it; and he sends presents to her who hates him, and entreats and weeps, and on the other hand, having had a little success, he is elated. But even then how? was he free enough neither to desire nor to fear?
D: Refashioned:
Forget Caesar for a moment. Have you ever been in love? Have you ever done what the person you loved commanded — gone where you didn't want to go, spent what you didn't want to spend, said things with groans, submitted to being shut out? If someone forced you to kiss Caesar's feet, you'd call it tyranny. But you kissed the feet of a lover willingly.

Thrasonides, a military veteran, cried: 'A worthless girl has enslaved me — me, whom no enemy ever conquered.' He called for a sword, begged, wept, sent gifts to someone who despised him, and when things went briefly well, felt briefly elated — but never free. Because even in his success, he still desired and feared.
D: Elegant: You call it tyranny when Caesar demands that you kneel. But you knelt willingly for a lover — went where you did not wish, spent what you did not choose, groaned, begged, submitted. Thrasonides, whom no enemy ever conquered, wept that a worthless girl had enslaved him. The chains were invisible but no less real.
D: Distilled: The chains of desire are no less binding for being chosen — a lover's slave is still a slave.
Notes: Thrasonides is a character from Menander's comedy Misoumenos (The Hated Man), a soldier enslaved by passion for a captive girl. Epictetus regularly draws from New Comedy to illustrate philosophical points — the dramatic examples make the slavery of desire viscerally concrete. The passage anticipates the modern understanding of attachment as a form of dependency.

D: Original: Now consider in the case of animals, how we employ the notion of liberty. Men keep tame lions shut up, and feed them, and some take them about; and who will say that this lion is free? Is it not the fact that the more he lives at his ease, so much the more he is in a slavish condition? and who if he had perception and reason would wish to be one of these lions? Well, these birds when they are caught and are kept shut up, how much do they suffer in their attempts to escape? and some of them die of hunger rather than submit to such a kind of life. And as many of them as live, hardly live and with suffering pine away; and if they ever find any opening, they make their escape. So much do they desire their natural liberty, and to be independent and free from hindrance. And what harm is there to you in this? "What do you say? I am formed by nature to fly where I choose, to live in the open air, to sing when I choose: you deprive me of all this, and say, 'What harm is it to you?' For this reason we shall say that those animals only are free which cannot endure capture, but, as soon as they are caught, escape from captivity by death. So Diogenes says that there is one way to freedom, and that is to die content: and he writes to the Persian king, "You cannot enslave the Athenian state any more than you can enslave fishes." "How is that? cannot I catch them?" "If you catch them," says Diogenes, "they will immediately leave you, as fishes do; for if you catch a fish, it dies; and if these men that are caught shall die, of what use to you is the preparation for war?" These are the words of a free man who had carefully examined the thing and, as was natural, had discovered it. But if you look for it in a different place from where it is, what wonder if you never find it?
D: Refashioned:
Consider how we understand freedom in animals. We cage a lion, feed it well, parade it around — and no one calls it free. The more comfortable its captivity, the deeper its slavery. Caged birds starve themselves trying to escape, and die rather than submit. Their nature demands open air, flight, freedom from constraint.

Diogenes said there was one sure path to freedom: to die content. He wrote to the Persian king: 'You cannot enslave the Athenians any more than you can enslave fish. Catch a fish and it dies. Catch these men and they will do the same. What use, then, is your military preparation?' These are the words of someone who examined what freedom actually is and found it in the only place it exists — inside the will.
D: Elegant: A caged lion is not free because it is well-fed. Caged birds die rather than submit. Diogenes told the Persian king: you cannot enslave fish — catch them and they die. Catch free men and they do the same. Freedom lives only in the will. Look for it anywhere else and you will never find it.
D: Distilled: A well-fed cage is still a cage — freedom exists only in the will, not in the comfort of the circumstances.
Notes: Diogenes of Sinope (c. 412-323 BCE) — the Cynic philosopher who lived in voluntary poverty, regarded by Epictetus as the supreme model of freedom. The letter to the Persian king (likely Artaxerxes or Darius) may be apocryphal but captures the Cynic-Stoic ideal: freedom defined not by external conditions but by internal independence. The fish metaphor connects to the framework's observation that genuine autonomy cannot be granted by power — it can only be cultivated from within.

D: Original: The slave wishes to be set free immediately. Why? Do you think that he wishes to pay money to the collectors of twentieths? No; but because he imagines that hitherto through not having obtained this, he is hindered and unfortunate. "If I shall be set free, immediately it is all happiness, I care for no man, I speak to all as an equal and, like to them, I go where I choose, I come from any place I choose, and go where I choose." Then he is set free; and forthwith having no place where he can eat, he looks for some man to flatter, some one with whom he shall sup: then he either works with his body and endures the most dreadful things; and if he can obtain a manger, he falls into a slavery much worse than his former slavery; or even if he is become rich, being a man without any knowledge of what is good, he loves some little girl, and in his happiness laments and desires to be a slave again. He says, "what evil did I suffer in my state of slavery? Another clothed me, another supplied me with shoes, another fed me, another looked after me in sickness; and I did only a few services for him.
D: Refashioned: The freed slave imagines liberation will solve everything: 'Once I am free, I answer to no one, I go where I please.' Then he is freed. He has nowhere to eat. He flatters strangers, endures worse conditions than before, and falls into a slavery far deeper than what he escaped. Or if he becomes rich, he falls in love with some girl and laments and wishes himself back in servitude. 'What evil did I suffer as a slave? Someone clothed me, fed me, cared for me when I was sick. I did only a few services in return.'
D: Elegant: The slave dreams of freedom. Freed, he has nowhere to eat. He flatters strangers, endures worse than before, and falls into deeper servitude. Or he grows rich, falls in love, and wishes himself back in chains. 'What did I suffer as a slave? I was clothed, fed, cared for.' External freedom without internal freedom is just a change of masters.
D: Distilled: The freed slave who has not freed his mind merely trades one set of masters for another.
Notes: The 'tax of the twentieths' (vicesima libertatis) — a 5% tax on manumission that freed slaves had to pay, a practical detail grounding the example in Roman economic reality. Epictetus's portrait of the freed slave who discovers that legal freedom does not produce psychological freedom is autobiographical in resonance — he was himself a freed slave. The passage illustrates the framework's principle that liberation requires internal transformation, not merely a change in external conditions.

D: Original: But now a wretched man, what things I suffer, being a slave of many instead of to one. But however," he says, "if I shall acquire rings, then I shall live most prosperously and happily." First, in order to acquire these rings, he submits to that which he is worthy of; then, when he has acquired them, it is again all the same. Then he says, "if I shall be engaged in military service, I am free from all evils." He obtains military service. He suffers as much as a flogged slave, and nevertheless he asks for a second service and a third. After this, when he has put the finishing stroke to his career and is become a senator, then he becomes a slave by entering into the assembly, then he serves the finer and most splendid slavery- not to be a fool, but to learn what Socrates taught, what is the nature of each thing that exists, and that a man should not rashly adapt preconceptions to the several things which are.
D: Refashioned: Now the freed slave chases rings, military service, political office — each one supposed to be the final liberation. He acquires senatorial rank and becomes a slave to the assembly. Each step up the ladder is a new form of servitude. The only escape from this sequence is not one more promotion but a fundamental reorientation: learning what Socrates taught — the nature of each thing that exists, and how to match your judgments to reality rather than to your ambitions.
D: Elegant: Rings, military rank, the Senate — each step up the ladder is a new form of servitude. The escape is not one more promotion but a fundamental reorientation: learning, as Socrates taught, to match your judgments to reality rather than your ambitions.
D: Distilled: Every promotion is a new form of servitude until you stop looking for freedom in external advancement.
Notes: The Roman cursus honorum — the sequential ladder of public offices — structures the metaphor. Gold rings (annuli aurei) marked equestrian rank. Epictetus traces the full arc: slave to freedman to equestrian to soldier to senator, each stage merely relocating dependency. The reference to Socrates's teaching about 'adapting preconceptions to things' (prolepsis) introduces the epistemological dimension: freedom requires correct judgment, not just correct circumstances.

D: Original: For this is the cause to men of all their evils, the not being able to adapt the general preconceptions to the several things. But we have different opinions. One man thinks that he is sick: not so however, but the fact is that he does not adapt his preconceptions right. Another thinks that he is poor; another that he has a severe father or mother; and another, again, that Caesar is not favourable to him. But all this is one and only one thing, the not knowing how to adapt the preconceptions. For who has not a preconception of that which is bad, that it is hurtful, that it ought to be avoided, that it ought in every way to be guarded against? One preconception is not repugnant to another, only where it comes to the matter of adaptation.
D: Refashioned:
The root of all human misery is a single failure: the inability to apply general principles to specific cases. Everyone has a concept of what is harmful and should be avoided. The concepts are not in conflict with each other. The problem arises only in application.

One person decides that their real problem is illness. Another blames poverty. Another blames a difficult parent. Another blames Caesar's disfavor. These are all the same mistake: misidentifying where the harm actually lies.
D: Elegant: Everyone agrees that harm should be avoided. The concepts never conflict. Only the application does. One person blames illness, another poverty, another Caesar's disfavor. These are all the same mistake: misidentifying where the real harm lies.
D: Distilled: We all agree that harm should be avoided — we just disagree, catastrophically, about what actually harms us.
Notes: Prolepsis (preconception) — a key Stoic technical term for innate, pre-theoretical concepts that all humans share (e.g., 'the good is beneficial'). The Stoic claim is that we never disagree about the concepts themselves, only about their application to particulars. This maps to the framework's epistemological emphasis: the crisis is not in values but in the failure to reason correctly from values to judgments.

D: Original: What then is this evil, which is both hurtful, and a thing to be avoided? He answers, "Not to be Caesar's friend." He is gone far from the mark, he has missed the adaptation, he is embarrassed, he seeks the things which are not at all pertinent to the matter; for when he has succeeded in being Caesar's friend, nevertheless he has failed in finding what he sought. For what is that which every man seeks? To live secure, to be happy, to do everything as he wishes, not to be hindered, nor compelled. When then he is become the friend of Caesar, is he free from hindrance? free from compulsion, is he tranquil, is he happy? Of whom shall we inquire? What more trustworthy witness have we than this very man who is, become Caesar's friend? Come forward and tell us when did you sleep more quietly, now or before you became Caesar's friend? Immediately you hear the answer, "Stop, I entreat you, and do not mock me: you know not what miseries I suffer, and sleep does not come to me; but one comes and says, 'Caesar is already awake, he is now going forth': then come troubles and cares." Well, when did you sup with more pleasure, now or before? Hear what he says about this also. He says that if he is not invited, he is pained: and if he is invited, he sups like a slave with his master, all the while being anxious that he does not say or do anything foolish. And what do you suppose that he is afraid of; lest he should be lashed like a slave? How can he expect anything so good? No, but as befits so great a man, Caesar's friend, he is afraid that he may lose his head. And when did you bathe more free from trouble, and take your gymnastic exercise more quietly? In fine, which kind of life did you prefer? your present or your former life? I can swear that no man is so stupid or so ignorant of truth as not to bewail his own misfortunes the nearer he is in friendship to Caesar.
D: Refashioned:
A man decides that his greatest good is being Caesar's friend. He has missed the mark entirely — he has misapplied his general concept of good to the wrong object. For what does everyone actually seek? Security, happiness, freedom to act without hindrance. Once he becomes Caesar's friend, does he have these things?

Ask him. 'When did you sleep better — before or after becoming Caesar's friend?' He begs you not to mock him. 'When did you eat with more pleasure?' He dines in terror, afraid of saying something foolish. He fears not a whipping — that would be too dignified — but losing his head. The nearer a man gets to power, the more he suffers.
D: Elegant: He decided that being Caesar's friend was the greatest good. Ask him now: when did you sleep better, before or after? He begs you not to mock him. He dines in terror, afraid of a wrong word. He fears not a whipping but a beheading. The nearer you get to power, the more you suffer.
D: Distilled: No one sleeps worse than the man who finally got what he thought would make him secure.
Notes: The dramatic dialogue with Caesar's friend is one of Epictetus's most vivid passages. The detail about dining anxiety — afraid to say or do anything foolish — reflects the real terror of Nero's and Domitian's courts, where dinner guests were executed for minor offenses. This connects to the framework's analysis of the social domain of power: proximity to concentrated power is not security but vulnerability.

D: Original: Since, then, neither those who are called kings live as they choose, nor the friends of kings, who finally are those who are free? Seek, and you will find; for you have aids from nature for the discovery of truth. But if you are not able yourself by going along these ways only to discover that which follows, listen to those who have made the inquiry. What do they say? Does freedom seem to you a good thing? "The greatest good." Is it possible, then, that he who obtains the greatest good can be unhappy or fare badly? "No." Whomsoever, then, you shall see unhappy, unfortunate, lamenting, confidently declare that they are not free. "I do declare it." We have now, then, got away from buying and selling and from such arrangements about matters of property; for if you have rightly assented to these matters, if the Great King is unhappy, he cannot be free, nor can a little king, nor a man of consular rank, nor one who has been twice consul. "Be it so."
D: Refashioned: If neither kings nor friends of kings live as they wish, then who is free? Follow the logic. Freedom is the greatest good. Can the possessor of the greatest good be unhappy? No. Therefore anyone who is unhappy is not free — regardless of rank. If the Great King is miserable, he is not free. If the consul is miserable, he is not free. We have now moved past legal and political definitions entirely. Freedom is a condition of the soul, not of the state.
D: Elegant: If freedom is the greatest good, then no unhappy person possesses it — regardless of rank. If the Great King is miserable, he is a slave. If the consul is miserable, he is a slave. Freedom is a condition of the soul, not of the state.
D: Distilled: If the possessor of the greatest good cannot be unhappy, then every unhappy person — king or consul — is a slave.
Notes: The 'Great King' (ho megas basileus) — the standard Greek term for the Persian king, symbol of absolute worldly power. The Socratic method structures this passage: each admission leads inexorably to the conclusion. Epictetus's redefinition of freedom as eudaimonia (human flourishing) rather than as legal status (eleutheria in the political sense) is the chapter's central philosophical move.

D: Original: Further, then, answer me this question also: Does freedom seem to you to be something great and noble and valuable? "How should it not seem so?" Is it possible, then, when a man obtains anything, so great and valuable and noble to be mean? "It is not possible." When, then, you see any man subject to another, or flattering him contrary to his own opinion, confidently affirm that this man also is not free; and not only if he do this for a bit of supper, but also if he does it for a government or a consulship: and call these men "little slaves" who for the sake of little matters do these things, and those who do so for the sake of great things call "great slaves," as they deserve to be. "This is admitted also." Do you think that freedom is a thing independent and self-governing? "Certainly." Whomsoever, then, it is in the power of another to hinder and compel, declare that he is not free. And do not look, I entreat you, after his grandfathers and great-grandfathers, or inquire about his being bought or sold; but if you hear him saying from his heart and with feeling, "Master," even if the twelve fasces precede him, call him a slave. And if you hear him say, "Wretch that I am, how much I suffer," call him a slave. If, finally, you see him lamenting, complaining, unhappy, call him a slave though he wears a praetexta. If, then, he is doing nothing of this kind, do not yet say that he is free, but learn his opinions, whether they are subject to compulsion, or may produce hindrance, or to bad fortune; and if you find him such, call him a slave who has a holiday in the Saturnalia: say that his master is from home: he will return soon, and you will know what he suffers. "Who will return?" Whoever has in himself the power over anything which is desired by the man, either to give it to him or to take it away? "Thus, then, have we many masters?" We have: for we have circumstances as masters prior to our present masters; and these circumstances are many.
D: Refashioned:
Freedom is something great, noble, and valuable. Can someone who possesses something great and noble be petty? No. Then whoever flatters, whoever says what they don't believe in order to gain a dinner or a consulship, is not free. Call the man who flatters for a dinner a 'small slave,' and the man who flatters for a consulship a 'great slave' — the difference is only in scale.

Do not look at a person's ancestry. If you hear someone say 'Master' from the heart — even if twelve lictors walk before him — call him a slave. If you see him lamenting, complaining, unhappy — call him a slave in a purple-bordered toga. And if none of these apply, do not yet call him free. Examine his opinions. If they can be compelled or hindered, he is a slave on holiday — his master is simply away. The master will return.
D: Elegant: If he flatters for a dinner, call him a small slave. If he flatters for a consulship, call him a great slave. The difference is only in scale. Don't check his ancestry — listen for the word 'Master' spoken from the heart. If you hear it, he is a slave, though twelve lictors walk before him. And if you hear nothing yet, check his opinions. If they can be compelled, he is a slave on holiday. The master will return.
D: Distilled: The man who flatters for a consulship is a greater slave than the man who flatters for a dinner — the difference is only in the price.
Notes: The twelve fasces — bundles of rods and axes carried by lictors before Roman magistrates. The toga praetexta — the purple-bordered toga worn by senior magistrates. The Saturnalia — the December festival when slaves were temporarily given freedoms, including being served by their masters. Epictetus uses the Saturnalia as metaphor: apparent freedom without genuine inner freedom is just a holiday from visible servitude.

D: Original: Therefore it must of necessity be that those who have the power over any of these circumstances must be our masters. For no man fears Caesar himself, but he fears death, banishment, deprivation of his property, prison, and disgrace. Nor does any man love Caesar, unless Caesar is a person of great merit, but he loves wealth, the office of tribune, praetor or consul. When we love, and hate, and fear these things, it must be that those who have the power over them must be our masters. Therefore we adore them even as gods; for we think that what possesses the power of conferring the greatest advantage on us is divine. Then we wrongly assume that a certain person has the power of conferring the greatest advantages; therefore he is something divine.
D: Refashioned:
No one actually fears Caesar himself. They fear death, banishment, confiscation, prison. No one actually loves Caesar. They love wealth, office, status. When we love and fear these things, whoever controls them becomes our master. We worship such people as gods — not because they are divine, but because we have decided that the power to grant external advantages is divine power.

The error is in the premise. Once you assume a person controls your greatest good, the conclusion — that they deserve worship — follows logically. The conclusion is false because the premise is false.
D: Elegant: No one fears Caesar. They fear death, banishment, ruin. No one loves Caesar. They love wealth, rank, power. Whoever controls what you love and fear becomes your god. The worship is logical — it follows from a false premise about where your good actually lies.
D: Distilled: You do not fear the powerful — you fear what they can take. Correct that error and they lose their power over you.
Notes: The analysis of political worship as a logical error — not a moral failing but a false premise about the location of the good — is one of Epictetus's most sophisticated arguments. It anticipates the framework's ideational domain analysis: power over others rests ultimately on their beliefs about what constitutes their own good. Change the beliefs and the power structure dissolves.

D: Original:
For if we wrongly assume that a certain person has the power of conferring the greatest advantages, it is a necessary consequence that the conclusion from these premises must be false.

What, then, is that which makes a man free from hindrance and makes him his own master? For wealth does not do it, nor consulship, nor provincial government, nor royal power; but something else must be discovered. What then is that which, when we write, makes us free from hindrance and unimpeded? "The knowledge of the art of writing." What, then, is it in playing the lute? "The science of playing the lute." Therefore in life also it is the science of life. You have, then, heard in a general way: but examine the thing also in the several parts. Is it possible that he who desires any of the things which depend on others can be free from hindrance? "No." Is it possible for him to be unimpeded? "No." Therefore he cannot be free. Consider then: whether we have nothing which is in our own power only, or whether we have all things, or whether some things are in our own power, and others in the power of others. "What do you mean?" When you wish the body to be entire, is it in your power or not? "It is not in my power." When you wish it to be healthy? "Neither is this in my power." When you wish it to be handsome? "Nor is this." Life or death? "Neither is this in my power." Your body, then, is another's, subject to every man who is stronger than yourself? "It is." But your estate, is it in your power to have it when you please, and as long as you please, and such as you please? "No." And your slaves? "No." And your clothes? "No." And your house? "No." And your horses? "Not one of these things." And if you wish by all means your children to live, or your wife, or your brother, or your friends, is it in your power? "This also is not in my power."
D: Refashioned:
If the premise is false — that another person controls your greatest good — then every conclusion built on it collapses.

What, then, makes a person genuinely free? Not wealth, not office, not power. In every domain, freedom comes from knowledge: the writer is free in writing because he understands grammar; the musician is free in music because he understands harmony. Therefore freedom in life comes from understanding life.

Now examine: is anything fully in your power? Your body? No. Your health? No. Your property, your reputation, your family? No. Can anyone force you to assent to what is false? No. Can anyone force you to desire what you do not choose? No. Then freedom lives in the will and nowhere else. Desire anything that depends on others and you have surrendered it.
D: Elegant: Freedom in writing comes from mastering grammar. Freedom in music from mastering harmony. Freedom in life from mastering the will. Your body can be seized, your property taken, your family threatened. But no one can force you to assent to what is false or desire what you do not choose. Freedom lives there and nowhere else.
D: Distilled: In every craft, freedom comes from knowledge of the craft — in life, it comes from knowing what is and is not within your power.
Notes: The analogy from craft knowledge (techne) to life knowledge (episteme tou biou) is a classic Socratic move — virtue as a kind of expertise. The rapid-fire Socratic dialogue ('Is your body in your power? No. Your health? No.') strips away every external candidate until only the will (prohairesis) remains. This is the structural center of the chapter: the argument that freedom and prohairesis are identical.

D: Original: Whether, then, have you nothing which is in your own power, which depends on yourself only and cannot be taken from you, or have you anything of the kind? "I know not." Look at the thing, then, thus, examine it. Is any man able to make you assent to that which is false? "No man." In the matter of assent, then, you are free from hindrance and obstruction. "Granted." Well; and can a man force you to desire to move toward that to which you do not choose? "He can, for when he threatens me with death or bonds, he compels me to desire to move toward it." If, then, you despise death and bonds, do you still pay any regard to him? "No." Is, then, the despising of death an act of your own, or is it not yours? "It is my act." It is your own act, then, also to desire to move toward a thing: or is it not so? "It is my own act." But to desire to move away from a thing, whose act is that? This also is your act. "What, then, if I have attempted to walk, suppose another should hinder me." What part of you does he hinder? does he hinder the faculty of assent? "No: but my poor body." Yes, as he would do with a stone. "Granted; but I no longer walk." And who told you that walking is your act free from hindrance? for I said that this only was free from hindrance, to desire to move: but where there is need of body and its co-operation, you have heard long ago that nothing is your own. "Granted also." And who can compel you to desire what you do not wish? "No man." And to propose, or intend, or in short to make use of the appearances which present themselves, can any man compel you? "He cannot do this: but he will hinder me when I desire from obtaining what I desire." If you desire anything which is your own, and one of the things which cannot be hindered, how will he hinder you? "He cannot in any way." Who, then, tells you that he who desires the things that belong to another is free from hindrance?
D: Refashioned:
Can anyone overpower your will? No — not if you truly despise death and chains. 'But if I despise death, can someone still hinder me?' They can hinder your body. They can stop you from walking. But no one told you that walking was your act free from hindrance. Only the faculty of desire, of assent, of using appearances as they present themselves — these are yours. The body needs cooperation from the physical world, and the physical world is not yours to command.

Who then tells you that the person who desires what belongs to another is free from hindrance?
D: Elegant: They can hinder your body but not your will. They can stop you from walking but not from choosing. Walking requires the body's cooperation, and the body is not fully yours. Only desire, assent, and the use of appearances belong to you. Desire what belongs to another and you are hindered by definition.
D: Distilled: Your body can be stopped, but your will cannot — the only person who hinders you is the one who desires what belongs to another.
Notes: This passage completes the Socratic dialogue begun in 4.13, making the critical distinction between bodily freedom (which can be hindered) and volitional freedom (which cannot). The key Stoic technical term is prohairesis — the faculty of choice/will that is the seat of genuine freedom. Epictetus's argument is that confusing bodily freedom with real freedom is the fundamental category error that produces slavery.

D: Original: "Must I, then, not desire health?" By no means, nor anything else that belongs to another: for what is not in your power to acquire or to keep when you please, this belongs to another. Keep, then, far from it not only your hands but, more than that, even your desires. If you do not, you have surrendered yourself as a slave; you have subjected your neck, if you admire anything not your own, to everything that is dependent on the power of others and perishable, to which you have conceived a liking. "Is not my hand my own?" It is a part of your own body; but it is by nature earth, subject to hindrance, compulsion, and the slave of everything which is stronger. And why do I say your hand? You ought to possess your whole body as a poor ass loaded, as long as it is possible, as long as you are allowed. But if there be a press, and a soldier should lay hold of it, let it go, do not resist, nor murmur; if you do, you will receive blows, and nevertheless you will also lose the ass. But when you ought to feel thus with respect to the body, consider what remains to be done about all the rest, which is provided for the sake of the body. When the body is an ass, all the other things are bits belonging to the ass, pack-saddles, shoes, barley, fodder. Let these also go: get rid of them quicker and more readily than of the ass.
D: Refashioned:
'Then I should not even desire health?' Not as something that belongs to you. Whatever you cannot acquire or keep at will belongs to another. Keep not just your hands but your desires away from it. The moment you admire anything that is not your own, you have submitted your neck to whatever controls it.

Your hand is yours? It is earth — vulnerable, subject to anyone stronger. Treat your entire body as a loaded donkey: useful while you have it, but if a soldier seizes it, let it go. Do not resist and do not complain. If the body is a donkey, then possessions are the saddle and shoes. Let those go even faster.
D: Elegant: Whatever you cannot keep at will belongs to another. Desire it and you submit your neck. Treat your body as a loaded donkey — useful while you have it, but if seized, let it go without complaint. If the body is a donkey, possessions are the saddle. Let them go faster.
D: Distilled: Whatever you cannot keep at will belongs to another — desire it and you have already surrendered yourself.
Notes: The donkey metaphor (onos phortion) is characteristic of Epictetus's earthy, non-academic style. The soldier seizing the donkey reflects a real Roman practice: soldiers could requisition civilian property and labor (angareia), the same practice referenced in Matthew 5:41 ('if anyone forces you to go one mile, go with them two miles'). The loaded donkey represents the body — useful but not essential, to be surrendered without resistance when circumstances demand it.

D: Original: When you have made this preparation, and have practiced this discipline, to distinguish that which belongs to another from that which is your own, the things which are subject to hindrance from those which are not, to consider the things free from hindrance to concern yourself, and those which are not free not to concern yourself, to keep your desire steadily fixed to the things which do concern yourself, and turned from the things which do not concern yourself; do you still fear any man? "No one." For about what will you be afraid? about the things which are your own, in which consists the nature of good and evil? and who has power over these things? who can take them away? who can impede them? No man can, no more than he can impede God. But will you be afraid about your body and your possessions, about things which are not yours, about things which in no way concern you? and what else have you been studying from the beginning than to distinguish between your own and not your own, the things which are in your power and not in your power, the things subject to hindrance and not subject? and why have you come to the philosophers? was it that you may nevertheless be unfortunate and unhappy? You will then in this way, as I have supposed you to have done, be without fear and disturbance. And what is grief to you? for fear comes from what you expect, but grief from that which is present. But what further will you desire?
