For of the things which are within the power of the will, as being good and present, you have a proper and regulated desire: but of the things which are not in the power of the will you do not desire any one, and so you do not allow any place to that which is irrational, and impatient, and above measure hasty.
D: Refashioned:
Once you have trained yourself to distinguish what is yours from what is not — what can be hindered from what cannot — you will fear no one. What would you fear about? The things that are yours — your will, your judgments — no one can touch. The things that are not yours — body, possessions, reputation — you have already released.

This is what philosophy was supposed to teach you. If you came to the school and still leave unfortunate and disturbed, you studied the wrong subject. With correct desire, grief dissolves. Fear comes from expecting bad things in the future — but if you desire only what is within your will, there is nothing bad to expect. This is not resignation. It is the only position from which genuine confidence is possible.
D: Elegant: Once you know what is yours and what is not, fear ends. Your will cannot be touched. Everything else you have already released. Fear comes from expecting loss — but if you desire only what cannot be taken, there is nothing left to lose. This is not resignation. It is the only position from which confidence is possible.
D: Distilled: When you desire only what cannot be taken from you, fear has no remaining foothold.
Notes: This passage synthesizes the three Stoic disciplines: desire/aversion (orexis/ekklisis), impulse (horme), and assent (synkatathesis). The elimination of grief and fear through correct desire is the Stoic path to apatheia — not emotional numbness but freedom from irrational disturbance. This connects to the framework's Reality pillar: accurate perception of what is and isn't within your control is the prerequisite for both wisdom and tranquility.

D: Original: When, then, you are thus affected toward things, what man can any longer be formidable to you? For what has a man which is formidable to another, either when you see him or speak to him or, finally, are conversant with him? Not more than one horse has with respect to another, or one dog to another, or one bee to another bee. Things, indeed, are formidable to every man; and when any man is able to confer these things on another or to take them away, then he too becomes formidable. How then is an acropolis demolished? Not by the sword, not by fire, but by opinion.
D: Refashioned:
When your opinions are truly settled, no person is formidable. One person has no more power over another than one horse has over another horse, or one dog over another dog. Things are formidable, not people. And when someone has the power to grant or remove those things, they become formidable by proxy.

How is a citadel demolished? Not by sword or fire — by opinion. Demolish the citadel within, and every external fortress loses its power over you.
D: Elegant: No person is formidable. One horse does not fear another horse. Things are formidable — and whoever controls them borrows their power. How is a citadel demolished? Not by sword or fire. By opinion.
D: Distilled: Citadels are demolished not by swords but by opinions — demolish the one inside you and no external fortress can hold you.
Notes: The acropolis metaphor has multiple layers: the literal Athenian Acropolis, the Roman political fortress, and the internal 'citadel' of false beliefs. The claim that the acropolis is demolished 'by opinion' (dogmati) is Epictetus's most compressed statement of Stoic psychology: all power over you operates through your own judgments. Change the judgment and the power evaporates. This maps directly to the framework's ideational domain: the most fundamental form of power is the power over beliefs.

D: Original: For if we abolish the acropolis which is in the city, can we abolish also that of fever, and that of beautiful women? Can we, in a word, abolish the acropolis which is in us and cast out the tyrants within us, whom we have dally over us, sometimes the same tyrants, at other times different tyrants? But with this we must begin, and with this we must demolish the acropolis and eject the tyrants, by giving up the body, the parts of it, the faculties of it, the possessions, the reputation, magisterial offices, honours, children, brothers, friends, by considering all these things as belonging to others. And if tyrants have been ejected from us, why do I still shut in the acropolis by a wall of circumvallation, at least on my account; for if it still stands, what does it do to me? why do I still eject guards? For where do I perceive them? against others they have their fasces, and their spears, and their swords.
D: Refashioned:
Even if you demolish the political citadel, can you demolish the citadel of fever? Of desire? Of fear? The external fortress is not the real problem. The tyrants within are. Some days one tyrannizes you, some days another.

Begin there. Give up the body, possessions, reputation, office, honors, even family — recognize all of them as belonging to others. Once the inner tyrants are ejected, the external ones become irrelevant. Their swords and spears point at other people's treasures, not at yours.
D: Elegant: You demolished the political fortress. But the citadel of desire still stands, and the garrison of fear. Begin with the tyrants within. Recognize body, possessions, reputation, and family as belonging to others. Once the inner tyrants are ejected, the external ones lose their weapons — their swords point at treasures you no longer claim.
D: Distilled: The real fortress to demolish is not the one in the city but the one inside you — the citadel of desire, fear, and attachment.
Notes: The 'wall of circumvallation' (periteikhisma) — a siege technique where attackers build their own wall around a city to prevent escape. Epictetus inverts the military metaphor: once you have expelled the inner tyrants, there is nothing left to besiege. The fasces and spears of external power become irrelevant because they threaten things you have already surrendered. This connects to the framework's concept of the four domains: retreat from the social and physical domains into the ideational domain is not weakness but strategic reorientation.

D: Original: But I have never been hindered in my will, nor compelled when I did not will. And how is this possible? I have placed my movements toward action in obedience to God. Is it His will that I shall have fever? It is my will also. Is it His will that I should move toward anything? It is my will also. Is it His will that I should obtain anything? It is my wish also. Does He not will? I do not wish. Is it His will that I be put to the rack? It is my will then to die; it is my will then to be put to the rack. Who, then, is still able to hinder me contrary to my own judgement, or to compel me? No more than he can hinder or compel Zeus.
D: Refashioned: I have aligned my will with God's. If He wills that I have fever, I will it too. If He wills that I act, I act. If He wills that I die, I accept it. If He does not will something, I do not wish for it. Who can hinder or compel someone whose will moves in perfect agreement with the order of things? No one — no more than they can hinder Zeus himself.
D: Elegant: My will moves with God's. Fever? I will it. Death? I accept it. Who can hinder someone whose will is aligned with the order of things? No more than they can hinder Zeus.
D: Distilled: When your will moves in perfect agreement with the order of things, nothing that happens to you is against your will.
Notes: This passage expresses the Stoic concept of living 'according to nature' (kata physin) at its most radical: willing what the universe wills. The identification of personal will with divine will is not passive submission but active assent — choosing to want what is, rather than merely enduring it. The comparison to Zeus echoes 1.6: 'my will not even Zeus can overpower.' Here Epictetus completes the circle: your will cannot be overpowered because it has aligned itself with the will that governs everything.

D: Original: Thus the more cautious of travelers also act. A traveler has heard that the road is infested by robbers; he does not venture to enter on it alone, but he waits for the companionship on the road either of an ambassador, or of a quaestor, or of a proconsul, and when he has attached himself to such persons he goes along the road safely. So in the world the wise man acts. There are many companies of robbers, tyrants, storms, difficulties, losses of that which is dearest. "Where is there any place of refuge? how shall he pass along without being attacked by robbers? what company shall he wait for that he may pass along in safety? to whom shall he attach himself? To what person generally? to the rich man, to the man of consular rank? and what is the use of that to me? Such a man is stripped himself, groans and laments. But what if the fellow-companion himself turns against me and becomes my robber, what shall I do? I will be 'a friend of Caesar': when I am Caesar's companion no man will wrong me. In the first place, that I may become illustrious, what things must I endure and suffer? how often and by how many must I he robbed? Then, if I become Caesar's friend, he also is mortal. And if Caesar from any circumstance becomes my enemy, where is it best for me to retire? Into a desert? Well, does fever not come there? What shall be done then? Is it not possible to find a safe fellow traveler, a faithful one, strong, secure against all surprises?" Thus he considers and perceives that if he attaches himself to God, he will make his journey in safety.
D: Refashioned:
The cautious traveler does not journey alone on a dangerous road. He waits for an ambassador or proconsul and travels in their company. The wise person in life does the same — but seeks a different kind of escort.

Who provides real safety? Not the rich man, who is himself stripped and groaning. Not Caesar's friend, who lies awake in terror. The wise person considers every possible companion and finds each one mortal, fallible, and potentially treacherous. Only one companion is unfailing: align yourself with God — with the order of things — and you travel safely regardless of the road.
D: Elegant: The cautious traveler seeks an armed escort. The wise person seeks the only escort that cannot be ambushed: alignment with the order of things. The rich man is stripped and groaning. Caesar's friend cannot sleep. Only the person who has attached themselves to what cannot be taken travels safely.
D: Distilled: The only escort that cannot be ambushed is alignment with the order of things — every other companion is as vulnerable as you are.
Notes: The travel metaphor draws from real Roman conditions: roads were genuinely dangerous, and travelers routinely attached themselves to official convoys for protection. The ambassador (legatus), quaestor, and proconsul represent increasing levels of worldly power — all of which prove inadequate. The conclusion — attachment to God (prosthesis theo) — is not religious mysticism but Stoic physics: aligning your will with the logos that governs the cosmos.

D: Original: "How do you understand 'attaching yourself to God'?" In this sense, that whatever God wills, a man also shall will; and what God does not will, a man shall not will. How, then, shall this he done? In what other way than by examining the movements of God and his administration What has He given to me as my own and in my own power? what has He reserved to Himself? He has given to me the things which are in the power of the will: He has put them in my power free from impediment and hindrance. How was He able to make the earthly body free from hindrance? And accordingly He has subjected to the revolution of the whole, possessions, household things, house, children, wife. Why, then, do I fight against God? why do I will what does not depend on the will? why do I will to have absolutely what is not granted to ma? But how ought I to will to have things? In the way in which they are given and as long as they are given. But He who has given takes away. Why then do I resist? I do not say that I shall be fool if I use force to one who is stronger, but I shall first be unjust. For whence had I things when I came into the world? My father gave them to me. And who gave them to him? and who made the sun? and who made the fruits of the earth? and who the seasons? and who made the connection of men with one another and their fellowship?
D: Refashioned:
What does it mean to 'attach yourself to God'? It means willing what God wills, and not willing what God does not will. How do you do this? By examining what God has given you as your own and what He has reserved for Himself.

He gave you the will — free, unimpeded. He could not make the body free from hindrance because it exists in a physical world subject to physical laws. So He subjected everything else — possessions, house, children, wife — to the revolution of the whole. Why fight this? Why insist on having things as though they were permanently yours? Where did you get them in the first place? Who gave them to your father? Who made the sun, the seasons, the connections between human beings?
D: Elegant: Attaching yourself to God means willing what He wills. He gave you the will — free and unimpeded. Everything else He subjected to the world's turning. Your possessions, your family, even your body — none of these were ever permanently yours. Who gave them to your father? Who made the sun and the seasons? Why fight the one who lent you everything?
D: Distilled: To attach yourself to God is simply to will what He wills — and to stop demanding permanent ownership of things that were always on loan.
Notes: The passage moves from Stoic psychology to Stoic cosmology: the will is free because it participates in the divine logos, while the body is subject to heimarmene (fate/destiny) because it is physical matter. The rhetorical questions ('Who gave them to your father?') echo Job 1:21: 'The Lord gave, and the Lord has taken away.' The Stoic version is less personal but structurally identical: everything external is borrowed, and the lender has the right to reclaim it.

D: Original: Then after receiving everything from another and even yourself, are you angry and do you blame the Giver if he takes anything from you? Who are you, and for what purpose did you come into the world? Did not He introduce you here, did He not show you the light, did he not give you fellow-workers, and perception, and reason? and as whom did He introduce you here? did He not introduce you as a subject to death, and as one to live on the earth with a little flesh, and to observe His administration, and to join with Him in the spectacle and the festival for a short time? Will you not, then, as long as you have been permitted, after seeing the spectacle and the solemnity, when he leads you out, go with adoration of Him and thanks for what you have seen, and heard? "No; but I would, still enjoy the feast." The initiated, too, would wish to be longer in the initiation: and perhaps also those, at Olympia to see other athletes; but the solemnity is ended: go away like a grateful and modest man; make room for others: others also must be born, as you were, and being born they must have a place, and houses and necessary things. And if the first do not retire, what remains? Why ire you insatiable? Why are you not content? why do you contract the world? "Yes, but I would have my little children with me and my wife." What, are they yours? do they not belong to the Giver, and to Him who made you? then will you not give up what belongs to others? will you not give way to Him who is superior? "Why, then, did He introduce me into the world on these conditions," And if the conditions do not suit you depart. He has no need of a spectator who is not satisfied. He wants those who join in the festival, those who take part in the chorus, that they may rather applaud, admire, and celebrate with hymns the solemnity.
D: Refashioned:
You received everything from another — including yourself — and you dare complain when something is taken back? You were introduced into the world as a mortal guest, shown the light, given companions and perception and reason. You were invited to observe the spectacle, to join the festival — for a limited time.

'But I want the feast to continue.' So does everyone at Olympia. But the event ends. Leave with gratitude and make room for others. 'But I want my children with me.' Are they yours? They belong to the one who gave them. 'Then why was I brought here on these conditions?' If the conditions don't suit you, leave. God does not need a discontented spectator. He wants participants who celebrate the spectacle, not guests who complain that it must end.
D: Elegant: You were invited to observe the spectacle, to join the festival — for a limited time. Now you demand an extension. Everyone at Olympia wants one more event, but the games end. Leave with gratitude and make room for others. God does not need discontented spectators. He wants participants who celebrate what they were given.
D: Distilled: Life is a festival you were invited to attend — leave with gratitude when it ends rather than complaining that it was not longer.
Notes: The 'festival' and 'spectacle' metaphors (panegyris, thea) are among Epictetus's most characteristic images — life as an event to be attended with gratitude. The Olympia reference grounds it: the ancient Olympic Games lasted five days, and no one complained that they weren't ten. The Eleusinian mysteries ('the initiated would wish to be longer in the initiation') add a religious dimension. The demand for continuation — for children to live, for the feast to go on — is the fundamental human error: treating a temporary gift as a permanent possession.

D: Original: But those who can bear no trouble, and the cowardly He will not willingly see absent from the great assembly; for they did not when they were present behave as they ought to do at a festival nor fill up their place properly, but they lamented, found fault with the deity, fortune, their companions; not seeing both what they had. and their own powers, which they received for contrary purposes, the powers of magnanimity, of a generous mind, manly spirit, and what we are now inquiring about, freedom. "For what purpose, then, have I received these things? To use them. "How long;" So long as He who his lent them chooses. "What if they are necessary to me?" Do not attach yourself to them and they will not be necessary: do not say to yourself that they are necessary, and then they are not necessary.
D: Refashioned:
The cowardly and the ungrateful are not missed from the assembly. When they were present, they did not behave as guests should — they lamented, blamed fortune, blamed their companions. They had powers designed for exactly these circumstances — magnanimity, generosity, courage, freedom — and used none of them.

'How long may I use these things?' As long as they are lent to you. 'But they are necessary to me.' Then do not tell yourself they are necessary. The necessity is created by the telling.
D: Elegant: They attended the festival and complained the whole time — blamed the gods, blamed their companions, used none of the powers they were given. 'But these things are necessary.' Do not tell yourself they are necessary, and they will not be. The necessity is in the telling.
D: Distilled: Nothing becomes necessary until you tell yourself it is — the attachment creates the dependency.
Notes: The powers 'received for contrary purposes' — magnanimity (megalopsychia), generosity, courage — are the Stoic virtues. Epictetus's point is that these capacities exist precisely to handle loss and difficulty, yet we refuse to deploy them when they are most needed. The final move — 'do not say to yourself that they are necessary, and then they are not necessary' — is pure Stoic cognitive therapy: the judgment creates the disturbance, not the event.

D: Original: This study you ought to practice from morning to evening, beginning, with the smallest things and those most liable to damage, with an earthen pot, with a cup. Then proceed in this way to a tunic to a little dog, to a horse, to a small estate in land: then to yourself, to your body, to the parts of your body, to your brothers. Look all round and throw these things from you. Purge your opinions so that nothing cleave to you of the things which are not your own, that nothing grow to you, that nothing give you pain when it is torn from you; and say, while you are daily exercising yourself as you do there, not that you are philosophizing, for this is an arrogant expression, but that you are presenting an asserter of freedom: for this is really freedom. To this freedom Diogenes was called by Antisthenes, and he said that he could no longer be enslaved by any man.
D: Refashioned:
Practice this daily, starting with the smallest things. A broken pot, a spilled cup — say, 'This is not mine.' Work up to larger things: a coat, a pet, a plot of land. Then to yourself: your body, your limbs, the people you love. Look at each one and release your grip. Purify your judgments until nothing clings to you, nothing grows into you, nothing tears you when it is removed.

Do not call this philosophizing — that is pretentious. Call it asserting your freedom. This is the freedom Diogenes found when Antisthenes showed him that no one could enslave a person who had nothing left to seize.
D: Elegant: Start with a broken cup. Then a coat, a pet, a plot of land. Then your body, your limbs, the people you love. Release each one until nothing clings, nothing tears when removed. Do not call this philosophizing. Call it asserting your freedom.
D: Distilled: Practice freedom daily: release your grip on small things first, then larger ones, until nothing can tear you when it is removed.
Notes: Antisthenes of Athens (c. 446-366 BCE) — Socrates's student and philosophical forefather of the Cynics, who reportedly introduced Diogenes to the path of radical freedom. The graduated practice (from pot to body to loved ones) is Epictetus's practical program for achieving freedom — not as a single dramatic renunciation but as daily, incremental training. This connects to the framework's emphasis on paideia: wisdom is built through disciplined practice, not through a moment of insight.

D: Original: For this reason when he was taken prisoner, how did he behave to the pirates? Did he call any of them master? and I do not speak of the name, for I am not afraid of the word, but of the state of mind by which the word is produced. How did he reprove them for feeding badly their captives? How was he sold? Did he seek a master? no; but a slave, And, when he was sold, how did he behave to his master? Immediately he disputed with him and said to his master that he ought not to be dressed as he was, nor shaved in such a manner; and about the children he told them how he ought to bring them up. And what was strange in this? for if his master had bought an exercise master, would he have employed him in the exercises of the palaestra as a servant or as a master? and so if he had bought a physician or an architect. And so, in every matter, it is absolutely necessary that he who has skill must be the superior of him who has not. Whoever, then, generally possesses the science of life, what else must he be than master? For who is master of a ship? "The man who governs the helm." Why? Because he who will not obey him suffers for it. "But a master can give me stripes." Can he do it, then, without suffering for it?' "So I also used to think." But because he can not do it without suffering for it, for this reason it is not in his power: and no man can do what is unjust without suffering for it. "And what is the penalty for him who puts his own slave in chains, what do you think that is?" The fact of putting the slave in chains: and you also will admit this, if you choose to maintain the truth, that man is not a wild beast, but a tame animal.
D: Refashioned:
When Diogenes was captured by pirates, did he call any of them 'Master'? Not the word — the word is nothing — but the mental state that produces the word. He criticized them for how they fed their captives. When sold, he did not look for a master — he looked for a slave. He told his owner how to dress, how to raise his children, how to run his household.

Why shouldn't he? If you buy a trainer, the trainer gives the orders. If you buy a physician, the physician directs the treatment. Whoever possesses the science of living must be the master, regardless of legal ownership. Can a master beat the wise man? He can — but not without paying for it. The punishment for chaining a person is the act of chaining them. Because a human being is not a wild beast — we are cooperative by nature, and whoever acts against that nature harms himself.
D: Elegant: Diogenes, captured by pirates, told his buyers how to dress and how to raise their children. If you buy a physician, the physician gives the orders. Whoever possesses the science of living is the true master. Can the legal owner beat him? He can — but the punishment for chaining a person is the act of chaining. To act against human nature is to harm yourself.
D: Distilled: The person who possesses the science of living is the true master — legal ownership merely determines who pays the price of injustice.
Notes: Diogenes's capture and sale — reportedly to a Corinthian named Xeniades — is one of the most famous anecdotes in ancient philosophy. When asked at the slave market what he could do, Diogenes allegedly replied: 'Govern men.' The inversion of master and slave through superior knowledge maps to the framework's ideational domain: genuine authority flows from understanding, not from legal or physical control.

D: Original: For when is a a vine doing badly? When it is in a condition contrary to its nature. When is a cock? Just the same. Therefore a man also is so. What then is a man's nature? To bite, to kick, and to throw into prison and to behead? No; but to do good, to co-operate with others, to wish them well. At that time, then, he is in a bad condition, whether you choose to admit it or not, when he is acting foolishly.
D: Refashioned: When is a vine doing badly? When it acts against its nature. When is a cock? The same. A human being's nature is not to bite, kick, imprison, or behead. It is to do good, to cooperate, to wish others well. When a person acts cruelly, they are in a bad condition — whether they admit it or not.
D: Elegant: A vine fails when it acts against its nature. So does a human being — and our nature is not to bite and imprison but to cooperate and wish others well. The cruel person is in a bad condition, whether they know it or not.
D: Distilled: A human being's nature is to cooperate and wish others well — anyone acting otherwise is malfunctioning, regardless of their power.
Notes: The vine analogy establishes Epictetus's natural law ethics: each thing has a nature (physis), and flourishing means acting in accordance with it. The claim that human nature is fundamentally cooperative (koinonikon) — not aggressive — is shared across Stoic thought (cf. Marcus Aurelius, Meditations 2.1, 6.39). This connects to the framework's Right pillar: ethical action is not arbitrary but grounded in an accurate understanding of human nature.

D: Original: "Socrates, then, did not fare badly?" No; but his judges aid his accusers did. "Nor did Helvidius at Rome fare badly?" No; but his murderer did. "How do you mean?" The same as you do when you say that a cock has not fared badly when he has gained the victory and been severely wounded; but that the cock has fared badly when he has been defeated and is unhurt: nor do you call a dog fortunate who neither pursues game nor labors, but when you see him sweating, when you see him in pain and panting violently after running. What paradox do we utter if we say that the evil in everything's that which is contrary to the nature of the thing? Is that a paradox? for do you not say this in the case of all other things? Why then in the case of man only do you think differently, But because we say that the nature of man is tame and social and faithful, you will not say that this is a paradox? "It is not." What then is it a paradox to say that a man is not hurt when he is whipped, or put in chains, or beheaded? does he not, if he suffers nobly, come off even with increased advantage and profit? But is he not hurt, who suffers in a most pitiful and disgraceful way, who in place of a man becomes a wolf, or viper or wasp?
D: Refashioned:
Did Socrates fare badly? No — his accusers did. Did Helvidius fare badly in Rome? No — his murderer did. A fighting cock that wins but is badly wounded has not fared badly. The one that loses without a scratch has fared worse. A dog that sweats and strains in the chase is not wretched — the one that lies idle is.

It is no paradox: the evil in anything is what contradicts its nature. A person who suffers nobly gains something. A person who avoids suffering by becoming a wolf or a viper has lost everything worth having.
D: Elegant: Socrates did not fare badly — his accusers did. The wounded fighting cock who wins has not suffered. The unwounded one who loses has. The evil in anything is what contradicts its nature. Suffer nobly and you gain. Avoid suffering by becoming a wolf and you lose everything.
D: Distilled: The person who suffers nobly gains; the person who avoids suffering by abandoning their nature loses everything worth having.
Notes: Helvidius Priscus (d. c. 75 CE) — the Stoic senator executed by Vespasian, referenced earlier in 1.13. The fighting cock metaphor turns conventional thinking inside out: the visible wound is not the real harm; the invisible corruption of character is. This passage contains Epictetus's most direct statement of a counterintuitive Stoic thesis: that injustice harms the perpetrator more than the victim.

D: Original: Well then let us recapitulate the things which have been agreed on. The man who is not under restraint is free, to whom things are exactly in that state in which he wishes them to be; but he who can be restrained or compelled or hindered, or thrown into any circumstances against his will, is a slave. But who is free from restraint? He who desires nothing that belongs to others. And what are the things which belong to others? Those which are not in our power either to have or not to have, or to have of a certain kind or in a certain manner. Therefore the body belongs to another, the parts of the body belong to another, possession belongs to another. If, then, you are attached to any of these things as your own, you will pay the penalty which it is proper for him to pay who desires what belongs to another. This road leads to freedom, that is the only way of escaping from slavery, to be able to say at last with all your soul
D: Refashioned:
To summarize: the free person desires nothing that belongs to another. What belongs to another? Everything not in our power to have or not have, to have in a particular way or not. The body belongs to another. Possessions belong to another. Attach yourself to any of these as your own and you pay the penalty anyone pays who desires what is not theirs.

This is the only road to freedom, the only way to escape slavery — to say at last, with your whole soul:
D: Elegant: The body belongs to another. Possessions belong to another. Attach yourself to any of these and you pay the penalty of desiring what is not yours. There is only one road to freedom: to say at last, with your whole soul —
D: Distilled: Freedom has exactly one road: releasing every claim on what is not within the power of your will.
Notes: This passage serves as the chapter's first summary and transition to the Cleanthes hymn. The phrase 'to say at last with all your soul' (hol' te psyche) signals that the intellectual argument must become a lived commitment. The road metaphor (hodos) echoes Socrates's language in the Crito and Phaedo: philosophy as a path, not a position.

D: Original: Lead me, O Zeus, and thou O destiny, The way that I am bid by you to go. But what do you say, philosopher? The tyrant summons you to say something which does not become you. Do you say it or do you not? Answer me. "Let me consider." Will you consider now? But when you were in the school, what was it which you used to consider? Did you not study what are the things that are good and what are bad, and what things are neither one nor the other? "I did." What then was our opinion? "That just and honourable acts were good; and that unjust and disgraceful acts were bad." Is life a good thing? "No." Is death a bad thing? "No." Is prison? "No." But what did we think about mean and faithless words and betrayal of a friend and flattery of a tyrant? "That they are bad." Well then, you are not considering, nor have you considered nor deliberated.
D: Refashioned:
'Lead me, O Zeus, and thou O Destiny, the way that I am bid by you to go.'

Now — the tyrant summons you to say something dishonorable. Do you say it? 'Let me think about it.' Think about it now? What were you studying in the schools? You agreed that just acts are good and unjust acts are bad. That life is not a good and death is not an evil. That betrayal and flattery are the worst things. You already deliberated. You already decided. There is nothing left to consider.
D: Elegant: 'Lead me, O Zeus, and thou O Destiny.' The tyrant summons you to say something dishonorable. 'Let me think about it.' You already thought about it — in the schools, where you agreed that betrayal is evil and death is not. There is nothing left to consider.
D: Distilled: The person who must deliberate when the tyrant demands dishonor never truly learned what they claimed to believe.
Notes: The Hymn to Zeus by Cleanthes (c. 330-230 BCE), third head of the Stoic school, is the Stoic creed: voluntary submission to fate. Epictetus quotes it repeatedly throughout the Discourses as the practical expression of Stoic freedom. The dramatic scenario — the tyrant's demand — tests whether school learning has become genuine conviction. This connects to the framework's distinction between knowledge and wisdom: knowing the right answer in a classroom is not the same as living it under pressure.

D: Original: For what is the matter for consideration: is it whether it is becoming for me, when I have it in my power, to secure for myself the greatest of good things, and not to secure for myself the greatest evils? A fine inquiry indeed, and necessary, and one that demands much deliberation. Man, why do you mock us? Such an inquiry is never made. If you really imagined that base things were bad and honourable things were good, and that all other things were neither good nor bad, you would not even have approached this inquiry, nor have come near it; but immediately you would have been able to distinguish them by the understanding as you would do by the vision.
D: Refashioned: Is this really a matter for deliberation — whether to secure the greatest good and avoid the greatest evil? What a fine inquiry! If you truly believed that dishonorable acts are bad and that death and prison are indifferent, you would not even approach this question. You would distinguish them instantly, the way you distinguish by sight what is black and what is white.
D: Elegant: Is choosing between honor and survival really a question? If you truly believed your principles, the answer would be as obvious as distinguishing black from white. Your hesitation is the proof that you never believed them.
D: Distilled: If your principles were genuine convictions, acting on them would be as automatic as distinguishing black from white.
Notes: The sensory analogy (vision distinguishing black from white) makes the epistemological point concrete: genuine moral knowledge produces immediate action, just as genuine perceptual knowledge does. The Stoic term is phantasia kataleptike — a 'grasping impression' so clear it compels assent. If your moral principles do not function this way, they are not truly held beliefs but verbal habits. This connects to the framework's emphasis on the gap between stated values and lived behavior.

D: Original: For when do you inquire if black things are white, if heavy things are light, and do not comprehend the manifest evidence of the senses? How, then, do you now say that you are considering whether things which are neither good nor bad ought to be avoided more than things which are bad? But you do not possess these opinions; and neither do these things seem to you to he neither good nor bad, but you think that they are the greatest evils; nor do you think those other things to be evils, but matters which do not concern us at all.
D: Refashioned: You do not actually hold these opinions. If you did, you would never need to deliberate. You think that external losses — prison, death, banishment — are the greatest evils, and that dishonorable compromise is merely unfortunate. Your stated principles and your real beliefs are in conflict. The stated principles lose every time.
D: Elegant: You think prison is the worst evil and dishonor merely unfortunate. Your stated principles and your real beliefs are in conflict — and in every crisis, the real beliefs win.
D: Distilled: In a crisis, your real beliefs always defeat your stated principles — and that is how you discover which is which.
Notes: Epictetus diagnoses the gap between professed and operative values. The Stoic technical framework: prolepsis (preconception) is correct, but its application (epharmoge) to specific cases has been corrupted by habit and social conditioning. This maps to the framework's erosion pattern: the danger is not that people reject good values but that their actual operating values have silently diverged from their professed ones.

D: Original: For thus from the beginning you have accustomed yourself. "Where am I? In the schools: and are any listening to me? I am discoursing among philosophers. But I have gone out of the school. Away with this talk of scholars and fools." Thus a friend is overpowered by the testimony of a philosopher: thus a philosopher becomes a parasite; thus he lets himself for hire for money: thus in the senate a man does not say what he thinks; in private he proclaims his opinions. You are a cold and miserable little opinion, suspended from idle words as from a hair.
D: Refashioned:
This has been your pattern from the beginning: philosophy in the classroom, compromise everywhere else. 'In the schools I am a philosopher. Outside, away with that talk of scholars and fools.' And so a philosopher becomes a parasite, a friend is overpowered by a bribe, and in the Senate a person says what is expedient rather than what is true — while at home they proclaim their real opinions to anyone who will listen.

Your convictions are cold, miserable things, suspended from idle words as if by a hair.
D: Elegant: In the classroom, a philosopher. Outside, a parasite. In the Senate, an opportunist. At home, a truth-teller — where it costs nothing. Your convictions are cold, miserable things, hanging from idle words as if by a hair.
D: Distilled: Principles that exist only in the classroom and evaporate under pressure are not principles — they are performances.
Notes: The contrast between school rhetoric and real-world behavior is Epictetus's constant target. The phrase 'suspended from idle words as from a hair' (ek logon argon hos apo trichos) captures the fragility of merely verbal commitment. This connects to the framework's critique of modern educational institutions: credentialed knowledge that does not change behavior is worse than useless — it creates the illusion of competence.

D: Original: But keep yourself strong and fit for the uses of life and initiated by being exercised in action. How do you hear? I do not say that your child is dead- for how could you bear that?- but that your oil is spilled, your wine drunk up. Do you act in such a way that one standing by you while you are making a great noise, may say this only, "Philosopher, you say something different in the school. Why do you deceive us? Why, when you are only a worm, do you say that you are a man?" I should like to be present when one of the philosophers is lying with a woman, that I might see how he is exerting himself, and what words he is uttering, and whether he remembers his title of philosopher, and the words which he hears or says or reads.
D: Refashioned:
Keep yourself strong and fit for actual life, not for school exercises. When your oil is spilled, when your wine is drunk, when small things go wrong — do you fall apart? Then what will you do when the real tests come?

I want to see the philosopher not in the lecture hall but in the moment of crisis. When someone says, 'You claim to be wise but you are just a worm,' the test is not whether you can refute them but whether they are right.
D: Elegant: Your oil is spilled and you fall apart. What will you do when the real tests come? I want to see the philosopher not in the lecture hall but in the crisis. When they call you a worm, the question is not whether you can refute them — but whether they are right.
D: Distilled: The test of philosophy is not the lecture hall but the moment of crisis — and the question is not whether you can argue but whether you can endure.
Notes: The oil and wine examples are deliberately trivial — Epictetus's point is that most people cannot handle even minor disruptions without losing composure. The 'worm' (skolex) insult is his harshest judgment: the person whose philosophical training does not survive contact with reality has not been trained at all. This connects to the framework's emphasis on stress-testing: wisdom that has not been tested under real conditions is untested, and untested wisdom is no wisdom.

D: Original: "And what is this to liberty?" Nothing else than this, whether you who are rich choose or not. "And who is your evidence for this?" who else than yourselves? who have a powerful master, and who live in obedience to his nod and motion, and who faint if he only looks at you with a scowling countenance; you who court old women and old men, and say, "I cannot do this: it is not in my power." Why is it not in your power? Did you not lately contend with me and say that you are free "But Aprulla has hindered me." Tell the truth, then, slave, and do not run away from your masters, nor deny, nor venture to produce any one to assert your freedom, when you have so many evidences of your slavery. And indeed when a man is compelled by love to do something contrary to his opinion, and at the same time sees the better but has not the strength to follow it, one might consider him still more worthy of excuse as being held by a certain violent and, in a manner, a divine power. But who could endure you who are in love with old women and old men, and wipe the old women's noses, and wash them and give them presents, and also wait on them like a slave when they are sick, and at the same time wish them dead, and question the physicians whether they are sick unto death? And again, when in order to obtain these great and much admired magistracies and honours, you kiss the hands of these slaves of others, and so you are not the slave even of free men. Then you walk about before me in stately fashion, praetor or a consul. Do I not know how you became a praetor, by what means you got your consulship, who gave it to you? I would not even choose to live, if I must live by help of Felicion and endure his arrogance and servile insolence: for I know what a slave is, who is fortunate, as he thinks, and puffed up by pride.
D: Refashioned:
What does all this have to do with freedom? Everything. You claim to be free, but you live at the nod of a powerful person, faint when they frown, court elderly patrons, flatter their servants, wash their faces and nurse them — all while secretly wishing they would die and asking the physicians how close to death they are.

And for what? To obtain offices and honors by kissing the hands of other people's slaves. Then you parade before me as a praetor or consul. I know how you got there. I know who gave it to you. I would rather not live at all than live at the mercy of someone like Felicion — a slave, puffed up and insolent because fortune smiled on him.
D: Elegant: You fawn over elderly patrons, nurse them while wishing them dead, and kiss the hands of other people's slaves — all for a consulship. Then you parade before me in purple. I know how you got there. I would rather not live at all than live at the mercy of a puffed-up slave of fortune.
D: Distilled: A consulship purchased through flattery and servility is not an achievement — it is a receipt for your self-respect.
Notes: Aprulla — an otherwise unknown patron, possibly a wealthy woman whose favor was needed for political advancement. Felicion — similarly unknown, but the name (from felix, 'fortunate') suggests a freedman who rose to power through imperial favor. The portrait of the client-patron relationship is scathing: the patron-courter is a slave not even to a free person but to another slave. This connects to the framework's social domain analysis: hierarchies of dependency create chains where everyone serves someone above them.

D: Original: "You then," a man may say, "are you free?" I wish, by the Gods, and pray to be free; but I am not yet able to face my masters, I still value my poor body, I value greatly the preservation of it entire, though I do not possess it entire. But I can point out to you a free man, that you may no longer seek an example. Diogenes was free. How was he free?- not because he was born of free parents, but because he was himself free, because he had cast off all the handles of slavery, and it was not possible for any man to approach him, nor had any man the means of laying hold of him to enslave him. He had everything easily loosed, everything only hanging to him. If you laid hold of his property, he would rather have let it go and be yours than he would have followed you for it: if you had laid hold of his leg, he would have let go his leg; if of all his body, all his poor body; his intimates, friends, country, just the same.
D: Refashioned:
'Are you free, then?' Epictetus admits: not yet. He still values his body, still wants to keep it whole. But he can point to a free man. Diogenes was free — not because he was born free but because he had cut every handle by which a person could be seized. His property? Take it. His leg? Take it. His body, his friends, his country? The same.

Nothing was fastened tight. Everything hung loosely from him. Grab any piece and he would let it go rather than be dragged after it.
D: Elegant: Epictetus admits he is not yet free — he still values his body. But Diogenes was free. Not by birth but by practice. Nothing was fastened tight. Everything hung loosely. Grab any piece and he would let it go rather than be dragged.
D: Distilled: Diogenes was free because nothing was fastened to him — grab any piece and he would release it rather than be dragged.
Notes: Epictetus's admission that he himself is not fully free ('I still value my poor body') is remarkable honesty from a teacher who demands radical detachment from his students. The 'handles of slavery' metaphor (labai tes douleias) — attachment points by which a person can be seized — is one of Epictetus's most practical images. Diogenes's freedom is not the absence of possessions but the absence of attachment to them.

D: Original: For he knew from whence he had them, and from whom, and on what conditions. His true parents indeed, the Gods, and his real country he would never have deserted, nor would he have yielded to any man in obedience to them or to their orders, nor would any man have died for his country more readily. For he was not used to inquire when he should be considered to have done anything on behalf of the whole of things, but he remembered that everything which is done comes from thence and is done on behalf of that country and is commanded by him who administers it.
D: Refashioned: Diogenes knew where everything came from, on what conditions it was given, and to whom it ultimately belonged. His true parents were the gods; his true country was the cosmos. He would never have deserted either, and would have died for his country more readily than any patriot — because his country was the whole of things, and everything done anywhere was done on its behalf.
D: Elegant: His true parents were the gods, his true country the cosmos. He would have died for it more readily than any patriot — because his country was the whole of things, and he never forgot it.
D: Distilled: Diogenes's patriotism was to the cosmos — and no one would have died for their country more readily.
Notes: Stoic cosmopolitanism (kosmopolites — 'citizen of the cosmos') redefines patriotic loyalty: the sage's true country is the rational order of the universe, not any particular city-state. Diogenes reportedly coined the term. This passage shows the political implications of Stoic physics: if logos pervades everything, then serving the whole is a higher duty than serving any part. This connects to the framework's concept of universal wisdom: the pursuit of understanding is not parochial but cosmopolitan.

D: Original: Therefore see what Diogenes himself says and writes: "For this reason," he says, "Diogenes, it is in your power to speak both with the King of the Persians and with Archidamus the king of the Lacedaemonians, as you please." Was it because he was born of free parents? I suppose all the Athenians and all the Lacedaemonians, because they were born of slaves, could not talk with them as they wished, but feared and paid court to them. Why then does he say that it is in his power? "Because I do not consider the poor body to be my own, because I want nothing, because law is everything to me, and nothing else is." These were the things which permitted him to be free.
D: Refashioned: Diogenes could speak with the King of Persia and the King of Sparta as freely as with anyone else. Not because he was born of free parents — if birth were enough, all Athenians and Spartans would have been equally bold. He was free because: 'I do not consider my body to be my own. I want nothing. Law is everything to me, and nothing else.' These three things permitted him to be free.
D: Elegant: 'I do not consider my body my own. I want nothing. Law is everything to me.' Diogenes could speak to kings as equals — not by birth, but by these three commitments. They are all that freedom requires.
D: Distilled: Three commitments made Diogenes free: he did not claim his body, he wanted nothing, and he followed only the law of reason.
Notes: Archidamus — likely Archidamus III of Sparta (r. 360-338 BCE), a contemporary of Diogenes. The 'King of the Persians' is the Achaemenid ruler, symbol of absolute Eastern power. Diogenes's three declarations constitute a minimal Stoic creed: non-attachment to the body, absence of desire for externals, and obedience to rational law (nomos). This connects to the framework's emphasis on Reason as the organizing principle: freedom comes from recognizing and submitting to the rational order.

D: Original: And that you may not think that I show you the example of a man who is a solitary person, who has neither wife nor children, nor country, nor friends nor kinsmen, by whom he could be bent and drawn in various directions, take Socrates and observe that he had a wife and children, but he did not consider them as his own; that he had a country, so long as it was fit to have one, and in such a manner as was fit; friends and kinsmen also, but he held all in subjection to law and to the obedience due to it.
D: Refashioned: But Diogenes was a solitary man — no wife, no children, no obligations to pull him in different directions. So consider Socrates, who had all of these. He had a wife and children but did not regard them as his own. He had a country, and served it — but only so long and in such a manner as was fitting. He held friends and family, but subordinated everything to law and to what was right.
D: Elegant: Diogenes had no attachments to test him. Socrates had them all — wife, children, country, friends — and held every one subordinate to law and to what was right. His freedom was proven, not merely claimed.
D: Distilled: Socrates proved that freedom does not require solitude — it requires holding every attachment subordinate to what is right.
Notes: The pairing of Diogenes (radical solitude) and Socrates (full engagement with social life) is deliberate: Epictetus answers the objection that Stoic freedom requires withdrawal from the world. Socrates's wife Xanthippe was proverbially difficult; his son Lamprocles appears briefly in Xenophon's Memorabilia. The point is that attachment, not relationship, is the problem — you can have a family without being enslaved by it.

D: Original: For this reason he was the first to go out as a soldier, when it was necessary; and in war he exposed himself to danger most unsparingly, and when he was sent by the tyrants to seize Leon, he did not even deliberate about the matter, because he thought that it was a base action, and he knew that he must die, if it so happened. And what difference did that make to him? for he intended to preserve something else, not his poor flesh, but his fidelity, his honourable character. These are things which could not be assailed nor brought into subjection. Then, when he was obliged to speak in defense of his life, did he behave like a man who had children, who had a wife? No, but he behaved like a man who has neither. And what did he do when he was to drink the poison, and when he had the power of escaping from prison, and when Crito said to him, "Escape for the sake of your children," what did Socrates say? Did he consider the power of escape as an unexpected gain? By no means: he considered what was fit and proper; but the rest he did not even look at or take into the reckoning.
D: Refashioned:
Socrates was the first to march when the city needed soldiers and exposed himself to danger without calculation. When the Thirty Tyrants ordered him to arrest Leon of Salamis, he refused without deliberation — it was a base act, and he knew he might die for the refusal. He intended to preserve not his flesh but his fidelity and character.

When he stood trial for his life, did he plead as a man with children, as a man with a wife? No. When Crito told him to escape prison for his children's sake, did he treat the offer as a lucky break? He considered only what was fitting. Everything else — survival, family, safety — he did not even include in the calculation.
D: Elegant: Ordered to arrest Leon, Socrates refused — because it was base, and that was all he needed to know. At trial he did not plead as a father. In prison he did not escape as a husband. He considered only what was fitting. Everything else he left out of the calculation entirely.
D: Distilled: Socrates did not include survival in his moral calculations — he considered only what was fitting, and let everything else follow.
Notes: Leon of Salamis — an Athenian citizen whose arrest was ordered by the Thirty Tyrants (404-403 BCE). Socrates refused to participate, as recorded in Plato's Apology (32c-d). The Thirty Tyrants were the oligarchic regime installed by Sparta after Athens's defeat. Crito's plea for escape is dramatized in Plato's Crito. The passage illustrates role ethics: Socrates consistently chose the role of 'just person' over 'father,' 'husband,' or 'survivor.'

D: Original: For he did not choose, he said, to save his poor body, but to save that which is increased and saved by doing what is just, and is impaired and destroyed by doing what is unjust. Socrates will not save his life by a base act; he who would not put the Athenians to the vote when they clamoured that he should do so, he who refused to obey the tyrants, he who discoursed in such a manner about virtue and right behavior. It is not possible to save such a man's life by base acts, but he is saved by dying, not by running away.
D: Refashioned: Socrates chose to save not his body but the thing that is strengthened by just action and destroyed by unjust action. He would not save his life through a base act — this same man who refused to put an illegal vote before the Athenian assembly, who defied the tyrants, who spent his life teaching virtue. You cannot save such a person through cowardice. He is saved by dying well, not by running away.
D: Elegant: Socrates would not save his life by a base act. He who defied the assembly, refused the tyrants, and taught virtue his whole life could not be saved by cowardice. He was saved by dying, not by running away.
D: Distilled: A person who has lived for principle cannot be saved by abandoning it — their death preserves what their escape would destroy.
Notes: The illegal vote refers to the trial of the generals after the Battle of Arginusae (406 BCE), when Socrates, as the presiding officer (epistates), refused to allow an unconstitutional collective verdict. The refusal to obey the Thirty Tyrants and the refusal to escape from prison complete a triptych: Socrates consistently chose principle over survival under democratic, oligarchic, and judicial pressure alike.

D: Original: For the good actor also preserves his character by stopping when he ought to stop, better than when he goes on acting beyond the proper time. What then shall the children of Socrates do? "If," said Socrates, "I had gone off to Thessaly, would you have taken care of them; and if I depart to the world below, will there be no man to take care of them?" See how he gives to death a gentle name and mocks it. But if you and I had been in his place, we should have immediately answered as philosophers that those who act unjustly must be repaid in the same way, and we should have added, "I shall be useful to many, if my life is saved, and if I die, I shall be useful to no man." For, if it had been necessary, we should have made our escape by slipping through a small hole. And how in that case should we have been useful to any man? for where would they have been then staying? or if we were useful to men while we were alive, should we not have been much more useful to them by dying when we ought to die, and as we ought? And now, Socrates being dead, no less useful to men, and even more useful, is the remembrance of that which he did or said when he was alive.
D: Refashioned:
A good actor preserves their character by knowing when to exit — not by dragging the performance beyond its proper time. Socrates said: 'If I had fled to Thessaly, would you have taken care of my children? And if I depart to the world below, will no one take care of them?'

If you and I had been in his position, we would have rationalized escape: 'I will be useful to many if I survive; dead, I am useful to no one.' But Socrates dead has been more useful than any of us alive. The memory of what he said and did has outlasted every argument for his survival.
D: Elegant: A good actor knows when to exit. Socrates said: if I fled, would you care for my children? And if I die, will no one? We would have rationalized escape. But Socrates dead has been more useful than any of us alive.
D: Distilled: The memory of Socrates's principled death has been more useful to humanity than his continued survival could ever have been.
Notes: Thessaly — a region in northern Greece, the proposed escape destination in Plato's Crito. The dramatic irony is intentional: Socrates's choice to die rather than escape did in fact prove more useful — it produced the most powerful moral example in Western philosophy. Epictetus's self-deprecating admission ('if you and I had been there, we would have rationalized escape') grounds the lesson: the gap between what we profess and what we would actually do under pressure is the real subject of the chapter.

D: Original: Think of these things, these opinions, these words: look to these examples, if you would be free, if you desire the thing according to its worth. And what is the wonder if you buy so great a thing at the price of things so many and so great? For the sake of this which is called "liberty," some hang themselves, others throw themselves down precipices, and sometimes even whole cities have perished: and will you not for the sake of the true and unassailable and secure liberty give back to God when He demands them the things which He has given? Will you not, as Plato says, study not to die only, but also to endure torture, and exile, and scourging, and, in a word, to give up all which is not your own? If you will not, you will be a slave among slaves, even you be ten thousand times a consul; and if you make your way up to the Palace, you will no less be a slave; and you will feel, that perhaps philosophers utter words which are contrary to common opinion, as Cleanthes also said, but not words contrary to reason. For you will know by experience that the words are true, and that there is no profit from the things which are valued and eagerly sought to those who have obtained them; and to those who have not yet obtained them there is an imagination that when these things are come, all that is good will come with them; then, when they are come, the feverish feeling is the same, the tossing to and fro is the same, the satiety, the desire of things which are not present; for freedom is acquired not by the full possession of the things which are desired, but by removing the desire. And that you may know that this is true, as you have laboured for those things, so transfer your labour to these; be vigilant for the purpose of acquiring an opinion which will make you free; pay court to a philosopher instead of to a rich old man: be seen about a philosopher's doors: you will not disgrace yourself by being seen; you will not go away empty nor without profit, if you go to the philosopher as you ought, and if not, try at least: the trial is not disgraceful.
D: Refashioned:
Study these examples if you want to be free, and consider the price. People have hanged themselves, thrown themselves off cliffs, even destroyed whole cities for the sake of what they called liberty. Will you not, for the sake of true and unassailable liberty, return to God what He has given?

As Plato said: study not only how to die but how to endure torture, exile, and loss. If you refuse, you will be a slave — even if you become consul ten thousand times, even if you enter the imperial palace itself.

The philosophers' words sound contrary to common opinion, as Cleanthes observed, but they are not contrary to reason. You will discover this through experience: the things the world chases bring no peace to those who obtain them. The fever and restlessness remain the same, before and after. Freedom is acquired not by possessing what you desire but by eliminating the desire.

Transfer the energy you spend courting the powerful to studying with a philosopher. The trial, at least, is not disgraceful.
D: Elegant: People have destroyed whole cities for what they called freedom. Will you not return to God what He gave, for the sake of the real thing? The philosopher's words sound strange but are not contrary to reason. Everything the world chases brings the same fever before and after. Freedom comes not from possessing what you desire but from removing the desire. Transfer the energy you spend courting the powerful to studying wisdom. The trial, at least, is not disgraceful.
D: Distilled: Freedom is acquired not by possessing what you desire but by eliminating the desire — everything else is just a change of chains.
Notes: Cleanthes of Assos (c. 330-230 BCE) — second head of the Stoic school, whose Hymn to Zeus was quoted in 4.29. His observation that Stoic teachings are 'contrary to opinion but not to reason' (para doxan, ou para logon) is the Stoic defense against the charge of paradox. Plato's reference is to Phaedo 67e: philosophy as 'practicing for death.' This passage closes the great chapter on freedom with a practical invitation: stop courting the powerful and start courting wisdom. The image of the philosopher's door replacing the patron's door inverts the Roman client-patron relationship.

D: Original: Chapter 2: On familiar intimacy

To This matter before all you must attend: that you be never so closely connected with any of your former intimates or friends as to come down to the same acts as he does. If you do not observe this rule, you will ruin yourself. But if the thought arises in your mind. "I shall seem disobliging to him, and he will not have the same feeling toward me," remember that nothing is done without cost, nor is it possible for a man if he does not do the same to be the same man that he was. Choose, then, which of the two you will have, to be equally loved by those by whom you were formerly loved, being the same with your former self; or, being superior, not to obtain from your friends the same that you did before.
D: Refashioned:
Guard this above all: never become so close to a former friend or companion that you descend to their level. If you do, you will ruin yourself. If the thought arises — 'He will think I am cold, he will not feel the same way toward me' — remember: nothing comes without cost. You cannot remain unchanged and simultaneously become a better person.

Choose: be loved as you were by remaining as you were, or be better and accept that some people will not love the new version.
D: Elegant: Nothing comes without cost. You cannot become better and remain exactly the same to everyone. Choose: be loved as you were by staying as you were, or grow and accept that some will not love who you become.
D: Distilled: Growth always costs something — the people who loved the old you may not love the new one, and that is the price of becoming better.
Notes: Chapter 4.2 shifts from the grand freedom argument to the practical challenge of social relationships. The core tension — that moral improvement inevitably changes your social circle — is one Epictetus's students would have faced directly. Roman social life depended on reciprocal obligations (amicitia); breaking those patterns carried real social cost.

D: Original: For if this is better, turn away to it, and let not other considerations draw you in a different direction. For no man is able to make progress, when he is wavering between opposite things, but if you have preferred this to all things, if you choose to attend to this only, to work out this only, give up everything else. But if you will not do this, your wavering will produce both these results: you will neither improve as you ought, nor will you obtain what you formerly obtained. For before, by plainly desiring the things which were worth nothing, you pleased your associates.
D: Refashioned: If the better path is clear, commit to it fully and let nothing pull you in the other direction. Wavering between the old life and the new produces the worst of both: you neither improve nor preserve what you had. Before, you pleased your companions by sharing their trivial desires. That option is gone the moment you choose something higher.
D: Elegant: Wavering between the old life and the new produces the worst of both. Before, you pleased your companions by sharing their desires. That option disappears the moment you choose something higher. Commit fully or not at all.
D: Distilled: Half-measures in moral improvement produce the worst of both worlds — you lose your old friends without gaining the new self.
Notes: The 'wavering' (amphoterizein — literally 'going both ways') captures the psychological cost of indecision. Epictetus's advice is characteristically absolute: partial commitment is worse than none because it generates internal conflict without progress. This connects to the framework's emphasis on settled orientation: the person who has decided what they stand for does not need to redecide in each situation.

D: Original: But you cannot excel in both kinds, and it is necessary that so far as you share in the one, you must fall short in the other. You cannot, when you do not drink with those with whom you used to drink, he agreeable to them as you were before. Choose, then, whether you will be a hard drinker and pleasant to your former associates or a sober man and disagreeable to them. You cannot, when you do not sing with those with whom you used to sing, be equally loved by them. Choose, then, in this matter also which of the two you will have.
D: Refashioned: You cannot excel in both directions. If you stop drinking with people you used to drink with, you will not be as pleasant to them. Choose: be a heavy drinker and agreeable to your old companions, or be sober and disagreeable to them. The same goes for every former habit. You cannot sing with the crowd and cultivate your character simultaneously.
D: Elegant: Stop drinking with your old friends and they will find you less pleasant. Stop singing with the crowd and they will find you less fun. You cannot cultivate your character and keep every old companion. Choose which you want more.
D: Distilled: You cannot improve yourself and remain equally agreeable to everyone you used to be — something must be given up.
Notes: The drinking and singing examples are deliberately social — the point is not that these activities are inherently bad but that participation in them is the currency of belonging. Refusing to participate is refusing to pay, and the social cost is immediate and real.

D: Original: For if it is better to be modest and orderly than for a man to say, "He is a jolly fellow," give up the rest, renounce it, turn away from it, have nothing to do with such men. But if this behavior shall not please you, turn altogether to the opposite: become a catamite, an adulterer, and act accordingly, and you will get what you wish. And jump up in the theatre and bawl out in praise of the dancer. But characters so different cannot be mingled: you cannot act both Thersites and Agamemnon. If you intend to be Thersites, you must be humpbacked and bald: if Agamemnon, you must be tall and handsome, and love those who are placed in obedience to you.
D: Refashioned:
If being modest and disciplined is worth more than being called a good time, then give up the rest. Turn away from it completely. But if that does not appeal to you, go the other direction entirely — become dissolute, cheer for dancers in the theater, and get what that life offers.

You cannot play both Thersites and Agamemnon. If you choose to be Thersites, accept the full costume. If you choose Agamemnon, accept the responsibilities. The roles cannot be mixed.
D: Elegant: Be modest and lose the crowd's affection, or be dissolute and keep it. You cannot play both Thersites and Agamemnon. Choose your role and accept the full costume.
D: Distilled: You cannot play both Thersites and Agamemnon — choose your character and accept everything that comes with it.
Notes: Thersites — the ugly, cowardly, insubordinate soldier in Homer's Iliad (2.211-277), beaten by Odysseus for mocking the commanders. Agamemnon — king of Mycenae, commander of the Greek forces at Troy. The contrast is between the buffoon who gains attention through vulgarity and the leader who earns respect through dignity. The theatrical metaphor (prosopon — 'mask/role') reflects Epictetus's role ethics: life assigns characters, and integrity means playing yours consistently.

D: Original: Chapter 3: What things we should exchange for other things

Keep this thought in readiness, when you lose anything external, what you acquire in place of it; and if it be worth more, never say, "I have had a loss"; neither if you have got a horse in place of an ass, or an ox in place of a sheep, nor a good action in place of a bit of money, nor in place of idle talk such tranquillity as befits a man, nor in place of lewd talk if you have acquired modesty. If you remember this, you will always maintain your character such as it ought to be.
D: Refashioned: When you lose anything external, notice what you gain in its place. If the gain is greater, never say you have suffered a loss. Trading money for a good action, idle talk for tranquility, lewdness for modesty — these are not losses. Remember this and you will maintain your character.
D: Elegant: When you lose something external, ask what you gained in its place. Money for integrity, gossip for peace, indulgence for modesty — never call these losses. Remember this and your character will hold.
D: Distilled: Never call it a loss when you have traded something external for something that strengthens your character.
Notes: Chapter 4.3 — 'What things we should exchange for other things' — establishes the economics of character. The examples move from trivial (horse for an ass) to profound (money for virtue). The Stoic framework treats moral development as a series of exchanges, and the key skill is accurate valuation: knowing which trades are gains, even when they feel like losses.

D: Original: But if you do not, consider that the times of opportunity are perishing, and that whatever pains you take about yourself, you are going to waste them all and overturn them. And it needs only a few things for the loss and overturning of all, namely a small deviation from reason. For the steerer of a ship to upset it, he has no need of the same means as he has need of for saving it: but if he turns it a little to the wind, it is lost; and if he does not do this purposely, but has been neglecting his duty a little, the ship is lost. Something of the kind happens in this case also: if you only fall to nodding a little, all that you have up to this time collected is gone. Attend therefore to the appearances of things, and watch over them; for that which you have to preserve is no small matter, but it is modesty and fidelity and constancy, freedom from the affects, a state of mind undisturbed, freedom from fear, tranquillity, in a word, "liberty." For what will you sell these things? See what is the value of the things which you will obtain in exchange for these. "But shall I not obtain any such thing for it?" See, and if you do in return get that, see what you receive in place of it. "I possess decency, he possesses a tribuneship: be possesses a praetorship, I possess modesty.
D: Refashioned:
But if you fail to keep this accounting, your time is wasted and everything you have built can be overthrown. It takes very little to capsize a ship — just a small turn toward the wind. The same is true of character: a small deviation from reason and everything you have accumulated is gone.

Watch over your judgments. What you are preserving is not small — it is modesty, fidelity, constancy, tranquility, freedom from fear. In a word: liberty. What would you sell these things for? Look at the price being offered. 'He has a tribuneship; I have decency. He has a praetorship; I have self-respect.' Consider which exchange serves you better.
D: Elegant: It takes very little to capsize a ship — just a small turn toward the wind. Character works the same way. What you are preserving is not small: modesty, fidelity, tranquility, freedom. What would you sell these for? 'He has a praetorship; I have self-respect.' Consider which exchange is actually favorable.
D: Distilled: A ship capsizes from a small turn toward the wind — character is lost the same way, one small compromise at a time.
Notes: The helmsman metaphor (kubernetes) appears throughout Stoic literature — the image of a slight deviation causing catastrophe captures the non-linear nature of moral failure. The tribuneship and praetorship represent Roman political offices obtained through compromised integrity. The exchange framework forces the student to make the valuation explicit: what, precisely, did the office cost?

D: Original: But I do not make acclamations where it is not becoming: I will not stand up where I ought not; for I am free, and a friend of God, and so I obey Him willingly. But I must not claim anything else, neither body nor possession, nor magistracy, nor good report, nor in fact anything. For He does not allow me to claim them: for if He had chosen, He would have made them good for me; but He has not done so, and for this reason I cannot transgress his commands." Preserve that which is your own good in everything; and as to every other thing, as it is permitted, and so far as to behave consistently with reason in respect to them, content with this only. If you do not, you will be unfortunate, you will fall in all things, you will be hindered, you will be impeded. These are the laws which have been sent from thence; these are the orders. Of these laws a man ought to be an expositor, to these he ought to submit, not to those of Masurius and Cassius.
D: Refashioned:
I will not applaud where it is not fitting. I will not stand where I should not. I am free, a friend of God, and I obey Him willingly. I claim nothing else — not body, not property, not office, not reputation. He did not make those things good for me, and I will not transgress His commands.

Preserve your own good in everything. Handle the rest reasonably, without attachment, and be content. If you do not, you will be impeded and unfortunate in all things. These are the laws sent from above. Study these, not the legal opinions of Masurius and Cassius.
D: Elegant: I will not applaud where it is not fitting. I am free, a friend of God, and I claim nothing He did not make mine. Preserve your own good and handle the rest without attachment. These are the laws from above. Study these, not the opinions of jurists.
D: Distilled: The only laws worth studying are the laws of what is truly yours — everything else is commentary.
Notes: Masurius Sabinus and Cassius Longinus — prominent Roman jurists of the 1st century CE who founded the Sabinian school of law. Epictetus contrasts human legal codes with the 'laws from above' (nomoi ekeithen) — the Stoic natural law that governs what is genuinely in our power. The claim 'I am a friend of God' (theou philos) elevates the philosophical life above the Roman patronage system: instead of being a 'friend of Caesar' (amicus Caesaris), the Stoic is a friend of the rational order.

D: Original: Chapter 4: To those who are desirous of passing life in tranquility

Remember that not only the desire of power and of riches makes us mean and subject to others, but even the desire of tranquillity, and of leisure. and of traveling abroad, and of learning. For, to speak plainly, whatever the external thing may be, the value which we set upon it places us in subjection to others. What, then, is the difference between desiring, to be a senator or not desiring to be one; what is the difference between desiring power or being content with a private station; what is the difference between saying, "I am unhappy, I have nothing, to do, but I am bound to my books as a corpse"; or saying, "I am unhappy, I have no leisure for reading"? For as salutations and power are things external and independent of the will, so is a book. For what purpose do you choose to read? Tell me. For if you only direct your purpose to being amused or learning something, you are a silly fellow and incapable of enduring labour.
