But if you refer reading to the proper end, what else is this than a tranquil and happy life? But if reading does not secure for you a happy and tranquil life, what is the use of it? But it does secure this," the man replies, "and for this reason I am vexed that I am deprived of it." And what is this tranquil and happy life, which any man can impede; I do not say Caesar or Caesar's friend, but a crow, a piper, a fever, and thirty thousand other things? But a tranquil and happy life contains nothing so sure is continuity and freedom from obstacle. Now I am called to do something: I will go, then, with the purpose of observing the measures which I must keep, of acting with modesty, steadiness, without desire and aversion to things external; and then that I may attend to men, what they say, how they are moved; and this not with any bad disposition, or that I may have something to blame or to ridicule; but I turn to myself, and ask if I also commit the same faults. "How then shall I cease to commit them?" Formerly I also acted wrong, but now I do not: thanks to God.
D: Refashioned:
Not only the desire for power and wealth enslaves us — so does the desire for tranquility, leisure, travel, even learning. Whatever external thing you overvalue, it places you in subjection to others. There is no difference between 'I am miserable because I have no political power' and 'I am miserable because I have no time to read.' Both are attachments to things outside your control.

If reading does not produce tranquility, what use is it? But real tranquility cannot be disrupted by anyone — not by Caesar, not by a crow, not by a fever. When called away from your books, go — not resentfully but observantly. Watch people, study how they are moved, and ask whether you commit the same errors. That practice is philosophy. The reading was only preparation.
D: Elegant: Even the desire for leisure enslaves. 'I am miserable without my books' is the same attachment as 'I am miserable without power.' If reading does not produce unshakable tranquility, it serves no purpose. When called away from your books, go observantly. Study the living. That is the philosophy. The reading was only preparation.
D: Distilled: The desire for tranquility can enslave you just as thoroughly as the desire for power — any external attachment is a chain.
Notes: Chapter 4.4 opens with a deliberate provocation: even the desire for philosophical study can become a form of bondage. This is Epictetus's most direct critique of the 'scholar-monk' type — the person who confuses studying wisdom with possessing it. The crow and piper as disruptors of tranquility are deliberately trivial: if your peace can be broken by minor annoyances, it was never genuine peace.

D: Original: Come, when you have done these things and have attended to them, have you done a worse act than when you have read a thousand verses or written as many? For when you eat, are you grieved because you are not reading? are you not satisfied with eating according to what you have learned by reading, and so with bathing and with exercise? Why, then, do you not act consistently in all things, both when you approach Caesar and when you approach any person? If you maintain yourself free from perturbation, free from alarm, and steady; if you look rather at the things which are done and happen than are looked at yourself; if you do not envy those who are preferred before you; if surrounding circumstances do not strike you with fear or admiration, what do you want? Books? How or for what purpose? for is not this a preparation for life? and is not life itself made up of certain other things than this? This is just as if an athlete should weep when he enters the stadium, because he is not being exercised outside of it. It was for this purpose that you used to practice exercise; for this purpose were used the halteres, the dust, the young men as antagonists; and do you seek for those things now when it is the time of action? This is just as if in the topic of assent when appearances present themselves, some of which can he comprehended, and some cannot be comprehended, we should not choose to distinguish them but should choose to read what has been written about comprehension.
D: Refashioned:
When you have practiced this — observing people, managing your responses, maintaining composure in action — have you accomplished less than reading a thousand lines? An athlete who weeps upon entering the stadium because he is no longer in the training gym has missed the point entirely. The gym was for this. The weights and drills and sparring partners were for this moment of action.

Books are training equipment. Life is the contest. Do not confuse the two.
D: Elegant: An athlete who weeps upon entering the stadium because he misses the gym has missed the point. The training was for the contest. Books are training equipment. Life is the event. Do not confuse the two.
D: Distilled: Books are training equipment — life is the contest. The athlete who prefers the gym to the stadium has missed the point.
Notes: The halteres — jumping weights used in Greek athletic training. The gymnasium (from gymnos, 'naked') was both a physical training facility and an intellectual gathering place. The metaphor is precisely chosen: the athlete does not read about jumping in the stadium — they jump. Similarly, the philosopher does not read about virtue in a crisis — they act virtuously. This connects to the framework's emphasis on applied wisdom over theoretical knowledge.

D: Original: What then is the reason of this? The reason is that we have never read for this purpose, we have never written for this purpose, so that we may in our actions use in a way conformable to nature the appearances presented to us; but we terminate in this, in learning what is said, and in being able to expound it to another, in resolving a syllogism, and in handling the hypothetical syllogism. For this reason where our study is, there alone is the impediment. Would you have by all means the things which are not in your power? Be prevented then, be hindered, fail in your purpose. But if we read what is written about action, not that we may see what is said about action, but that we may act well: if we read what is said about desire and aversion, in order that we may neither fall in our desires, nor fall into that which we try to avoid: if we read what is said about duty, in order that, remembering the relations, we may do nothing irrationally nor contrary to these relations; we should not be vexed in being hindered as to our readings, but we should be satisfied with doing, the acts which are conformable, and we should be reckoning not what so far we have been accustomed to reckon; "To-day I have read so many verses, I have written so many"; but, "To-day I have employed my action as it is taught by the philosophers; I have not employed any desire; I have used avoidance only with respect to things which are within the power of my will; I have not been afraid of such a person, I have not been prevailed upon by the entreaties of another; I have exercised my patience, my abstinence my co-operation with others"; and so we should thank God for what we ought to thank Him.
D: Refashioned:
The reason we never progress is that we study for the wrong purpose. We read to master arguments and resolve syllogisms — to demonstrate knowledge, not to change behavior. Where your study is, there alone is the impediment: if you study words, you will be impeded in words; if you study action, you will succeed in action.

Instead of counting pages read, count what you actually did. 'Today I directed my desire properly. I used aversion only toward what is in my power. I was not afraid. I was not flattered. I practiced patience and cooperation.' Thank God for this — not for books consumed.
D: Elegant: We read to master arguments, not to change behavior. If you study words, you are impeded in words. If you study action, you succeed in action. Stop counting pages. Start counting what you did: 'Today I desired properly, feared nothing, flattered no one.' Thank God for that, not for books consumed.
D: Distilled: The measure of philosophical progress is not pages read but responses improved — count what you did, not what you studied.
Notes: The Stoic three topoi (fields of study) — desire/aversion, impulse/duty, and assent — are the practical curriculum Epictetus proposes as replacement for syllogistic training. His critique targets the Academic and Peripatetic schools whose emphasis on logic and rhetoric he viewed as displacing genuine moral education. This connects to the framework's critique of credentialism: institutional learning that does not produce wisdom is not education.

D: Original: But now we do not know that we also in another way are like the many. Another man is afraid that he shall not have power: you are afraid that you will. Do not do so, my man; but as you ridicule him who is afraid that he, shall not have power, so ridicule yourself also. For it makes no difference whether you are thirsty like a man who has a fever, or have a dread of water like a man who is mad. Or how will you still be able to say as Socrates did, "If so it pleases God, so let it be"? Do you think that Socrates, if he had been eager to pass his leisure in the Lyceum or in the Academy and to discourse dally with the young men, would have readily served in military expeditions so often as he did; and would he not have lamented and groaned, "Wretch that I am; I must now be miserable here, when I might be sunning myself in the Lyceum"? Why, was this your business, to sun yourself? And is it not your business to be happy, to be free from hindrance, free from impediment? And could he still have been Socrates, if he had lamented in this way: how would he still have been able to write Paeans in his prison?
D: Refashioned:
You make the same error from the opposite direction. Another person fears not having power; you fear having it. One person dreads thirst like a fever patient; you dread water like a madman. The attachment is the same — only the object differs.

Could Socrates have written hymns in prison if he had spent his life lamenting that he was not sunning himself in the Lyceum? He served in military campaigns because that was what was required. He did not complain about missing the Academy. His business was to be free from hindrance — and that business travels with you everywhere.
D: Elegant: One person dreads thirst; you dread water. The attachment is the same. Could Socrates have written hymns in prison if he had spent his life mourning the Lyceum? His business was to be free — and that business travels with you everywhere.
D: Distilled: The philosopher who clings to the lecture hall is no freer than the politician who clings to office — the attachment, not the object, is the chain.
Notes: The Lyceum — Aristotle's school in Athens. The Academy — Plato's school. Socrates reportedly composed a hymn to Apollo while awaiting execution (Phaedo 60d-61b). Epictetus's point: if your tranquility depends on specific circumstances (a quiet library, the right city, the right companions), it is not tranquility but another form of dependency. The paeans (hymns) in prison demonstrate that genuine philosophical freedom is circumstance-independent.

D: Original: In short, remember this, that what you shall prize which is beyond your will, so far you have destroyed your will. But these things are out of the power of the will, not only power, but also a private condition: not only occupation, but also leisure. "Now, then, must I live in this tumult?" Why do you say "tumult"? "I mean among many men." Well what is the hardship? Suppose that you are at Olympia: imagine it to be a panegyris, where one is calling out one thing, another is doing another thing, and a third is pushing another person: in the baths there is a crowd: and who of us is not pleased with this assembly and leaves it unwillingly, Be not difficult to please nor fastidious about what happens. "Vinegar is disagreeable, for it is sharp; honey is disagreeable, for it disturbs my habit of body. I do not like vegetables." So also, "I do not like leisure; it is a desert: I do not like a crowd; it is confusion." But if circumstances make it necessary for you to live alone or with a few, call it quiet and use the thing as you ought: talk with yourself, exercise the appearances, work up your preconceptions. If you fall into a crowd, call it a celebration of games, a panegyris, a festival: try to enjoy the festival with other men. For what is a more pleasant sight to him who loves mankind than a number of men? We see with pleasure herds of horses or oxen: we are delighted when we see many ships: who is pained when he sees many men? "But they deafen me with their cries." Then your hearing is impeded. What, then, is this to you? Is, then, the power of making use of appearances hindered? And who prevents you from using, according to nature, inclination to a thing and aversion from it; and movement toward a thing and movement from it? What tumult is able to do this?
D: Refashioned:
In short: whatever you prize beyond your will, you have destroyed your will to that extent. This applies to everything external — not just power and ambition but also leisure, solitude, and the absence of noise.

'I must live in this tumult.' Why call it tumult? Call it a festival. You are at Olympia — people shout, push, crowd the baths. Who leaves the Olympics unwillingly? Do not be fastidious about conditions. If you are alone, call it quiet and use it. If you are in a crowd, call it a celebration and use it. What is more pleasant to a person who loves humanity than the sight of other human beings? We delight in herds of horses, flocks of ships. Why not in gatherings of people?
D: Elegant: Whatever you prize beyond your will, you have destroyed your will to that extent. If alone, call it quiet and use it. If in a crowd, call it a festival and use it. You are at Olympia — who leaves unwillingly? What is more pleasant to a lover of humanity than the sight of other human beings?
D: Distilled: Whatever you prize beyond your will, you destroy your will to that extent — even prizing solitude can become a chain.
Notes: The panegyris (festival gathering) metaphor reframes the problem: what seems like an obstacle is actually the event itself. The Olympic Games reference is characteristic — Epictetus repeatedly uses athletic and festival metaphors to reframe unwanted circumstances as opportunities for practice. The phrase 'lover of humanity' (philanthropos) connects to Stoic cosmopolitanism: if human beings are by nature social, then a crowd is not an annoyance but an expression of our nature.

D: Original: Do you only bear in mind the general rules: "What is mine, what is not mine; what is given to me; what does God will that I should do now? what does He not will?" A little before he willed you to be at leisure, to talk with yourself, to write about these things, to read, to hear, to prepare yourself. You had sufficient time for this. Now He says to you: "Come now to the contest; show us what you have learned, how you have practiced the athletic art. How long will you be exercised alone? Now is the opportunity for you to learn whether you are an athlete worthy of victory, or one of those who go about the world and are defeated." Why, then, are; you vexed? No contest is without confusion. There be many who exercise themselves for the contests, many who call out to those who exercise themselves, many masters, many spectators. "But my wish is to live quietly." Lament, then, and groan as you deserve to do. For what other is a greater punishment than this to the untaught man and to him who disobeys the divine commands: to be grieved, to lament, to envy, in a word, to be disappointed and to he unhappy? Would you not release yourself from these things? "And how shall I release myself?" Have you not often heard that you ought to remove entirely desire, apply aversion to those things only which are within your power, that you ought to give up everything, body, property, fame, books, tumult, power, private station? for whatever way you turn, you are a slave, you are subjected, you are hindered, you are compelled, you are entirely in the power of others. But keep the words of Cleanthes in readiness,
D: Refashioned:
Keep only these questions ready: What is mine? What is not mine? What does God will that I do now? A little while ago, God willed your leisure — you had time to read, to reflect, to prepare. Now He says: 'Enter the contest. Show what you have learned.' How long will you practice alone? This is the event.

No contest is without confusion. 'But I want to live quietly.' Then lament, because that wish is your punishment. Release desire entirely, direct aversion only toward what is in your power, and surrender everything else — body, property, books, reputation, crowds, solitude. Keep the words of Cleanthes ready.
D: Elegant: God gave you leisure to prepare. Now He says: enter the contest. How long will you practice alone? No contest is without confusion. If you want quiet, that want is your punishment. Surrender everything — body, books, reputation, crowds, solitude. Keep Cleanthes ready.
D: Distilled: The training was the leisure; the contest is the life. If you still wish for the training when the contest has begun, the wish itself is your defeat.
Notes: Cleanthes's Hymn to Zeus — quoted repeatedly throughout Book 4 — serves as the Stoic mantra of acceptance. The athletic contest metaphor reaches its climax: the student who prefers the gymnasium to the stadium has failed, not because the training was bad but because they confused preparation for the thing itself. This connects to the framework's emphasis on wisdom as lived practice rather than accumulated knowledge.

D: Original:
Lead me, O Zeus, and thou necessity.

Is it your will that I should go to Rome? I will go to Rome. To Gyara? I will go to Gyara. I will go to Athens? I will go to Athens. To prison? I will go to prison. If you should once say, "When shall a man go to Athens?" you are undone. It is a necessary consequence that this desire, if it is not accomplished, must make you unhappy; and if it is accomplished, it must make you vain, since you are elated at things at which you ought not to be elated; and on the other hand, if you are impeded, it must make you wretched because you fall into that which you would not fall into. Give up then all these things. "Athens is a good place." But happiness is much better; and to be free from passions, free from disturbance, for your affairs not to depend on any man. "There is tumult at Rome and visits of salutation." But happiness is an equivalent for all troublesome things. If, then, the time comes for these things, why do you not take away the wish to avoid them? what necessity is there to carry to avoid a burden like an ass, and to be beaten with a stick? But if you do not so, consider that you must always be a slave to him who has it in his power to effect your release, and also to impede you, and you must serve him as an evil genius.
D: Refashioned:
'Lead me, O Zeus, and thou Necessity.'

To Rome? I will go. To exile on Gyara? I will go. To prison? I will go. The moment you say, 'I wish I could go to Athens,' you are undone. If the desire is fulfilled, you become vain; if it is frustrated, you become miserable. Either way, your peace depends on something outside your control.

'Athens is a fine place.' Happiness is finer — and happiness that depends on being in Athens is not happiness at all. If troublesome circumstances arrive, do not resist them like a donkey being beaten along the road. Accept them, or accept that you are a slave to whoever has the power to change your circumstances.
D: Elegant: 'Lead me, O Zeus.' To Rome? I go. To exile? I go. To prison? I go. The moment you say 'I wish I could go to Athens,' you are undone — because your peace now depends on Athens. Happiness that requires a particular place is not happiness. It is a leash.
D: Distilled: Happiness that requires a particular place or circumstance is not happiness — it is a leash held by whoever controls that circumstance.
Notes: Gyara (modern Gyaros) — a barren Aegean island used by the Romans as a place of exile, proverbially desolate. The Hymn to Zeus reappears as the practical formula: wherever fate sends you, go willingly. The donkey beaten along the road (onos deiromenon) echoes the donkey metaphor from 4.15 — the body as beast of burden. Athens represents not just a city but the ideal of the philosophical life; Epictetus's point is that even clinging to the ideal conditions for philosophy is a form of bondage.

D: Original: There is only one way to happiness, and let this rule be ready both in the morning and during the day and by night; the rule is not to look toward things which are out of the power of our will, to think that nothing is our own, to give up all things to the Divinity, to Fortune; to make them the superintendents of these things, whom Zeus also has made so; for a man to observe that only which is his own, that which cannot be hindered; and when we read, to refer our reading to this only, and our writing and our listening.
D: Refashioned: There is only one way to happiness, and it should be ready in the morning, during the day, and at night: do not look toward what is outside the power of your will. Think that nothing external is your own. Hand everything over to fortune and to God, and observe only what is genuinely yours — what cannot be hindered. Let every act of reading, writing, and listening point back to this single principle.
D: Elegant: One rule, ready morning, noon, and night: nothing outside the will is yours. Hand everything else to God and fortune. Observe only what cannot be hindered. Let every act of reading and writing point to this alone.
D: Distilled: The one rule for happiness: nothing outside your will is yours — everything else belongs to God and fortune.
Notes: This passage is the most compressed statement of the Stoic way of life in the Discourses. The daily practice framework (morning, day, night) anticipates Marcus Aurelius's morning and evening meditations in the Meditations. The insistence that reading and writing serve this single purpose connects to the chapter's opening critique: even intellectual pursuits become bondage when they are not directed toward genuine freedom.

D: Original: For this reason, I cannot call the man industrious, if I hear this only, that he reads and writes; and even if a man adds that he reads all night, I cannot say so, if he knows not to what he should refer his reading. For neither do you say that a man is industrious if he keeps awake for a girl; nor do I. But if he does it for reputation, I say that he is a lover of reputation. And if he does it for money, I say that he is a lover of money, not a lover of labour; and if he does it through love of learning, I say that he is a lover of learning.
D: Refashioned: I cannot call a person industrious just because they read and write all night. What are they reading for? If for a lover, they are a lover of pleasure. If for reputation, they are a lover of glory. If for money, they are a lover of money. Only if they direct their labor toward keeping their rational faculty in a state conformable to nature are they genuinely industrious.
D: Elegant: Reading all night does not make you industrious. Reading for a lover makes you amorous. For reputation, vain. For money, greedy. Only reading directed toward keeping your rational faculty aligned with nature makes you wise.
D: Distilled: Industry is defined not by the hours you spend but by what you direct those hours toward — effort without right aim is just a different form of waste.
Notes: The distinction between 'lovers of' various things (philoponos vs. philodoxos vs. philargyros vs. philomathes) is Stoic vocabulary for misdirected desire. The only legitimate love of labor (philoponos in the true sense) is labor directed toward the hegemonikon — the ruling faculty. This connects to the framework's emphasis on directed wisdom: effort without correct orientation is energy dissipated.

D: Original: But if he refers his labour to his own ruling power, that he may keep it in a state conformable to nature and pass his life in that state, then only do I say that he is industrious. For never commend a man on account of these things which are common to all, but on account of his opinions; for these are the things which belong to each man, which make his actions bad or good. Remembering these rules, rejoice in that which is present, and be content with the things which come in season. If you see anything which you have learned and inquired about occurring, to you in your course of life, be delighted at it. If you have laid aside or have lessened bad disposition and a habit of reviling; if you have done so with rash temper, obscene words, hastiness, sluggishness; if you are not moved by what you formerly were, and not in the same way as you once were, you can celebrate a festival daily, to-day because you have behaved well in one act, and to-morrow because you have behaved well in another. How much greater is this a reason for making sacrifices than a consulship or the government of a province? These things come to you from yourself and from the gods. Remember this, Who gives these things and to whom, and for what purpose. If you cherish yourself in these thoughts, do you still think that it makes any difference where yon shall be happy, where you shall please God? Are not the gods equally distant from all places? Do they not see from all places alike that which is going on?
D: Refashioned:
Never praise a person for skills that are common to everyone, but for their opinions — these are what make actions good or bad. If you have laid aside the habit of reviling, of speaking rashly, of obscene words, of sluggishness — celebrate. Not with a parade but with genuine satisfaction. Today you behaved well in one respect; tomorrow, in another. This is more worth celebrating than any consulship.

These gifts come from yourself and from the gods. If you hold this in mind, does it matter where you are? The gods are equally distant from all places and see equally well from everywhere.
D: Elegant: Praise people for their opinions, not their skills — opinions are what make actions good or bad. If today you laid aside one bad habit, celebrate that. It is worth more than a consulship. And if you hold this in mind, what does it matter where you are? The gods see equally well from everywhere.
D: Distilled: Laying aside one bad habit is worth more than any promotion — celebrate the improvement, not the advancement.
Notes: The passage inverts Roman values: the consulship (the highest regular magistracy) is worth less than a day of genuine moral improvement. The claim that the gods are 'equally distant from all places' (ex hapanton topon isos apechousin) expresses Stoic pantheism: logos pervades everything uniformly, so no location is privileged for philosophical practice. This closes the chapter on tranquility with its central message: true peace is location-independent because it depends on nothing external.

D: Original: Chapter 5: Against the quarrelsome and ferocious

The wise and good man neither himself fights with any person, nor does he allow another, so far as he can prevent it. And an example of this as well as of all other things is proposed to us in the life of Socrates, who not only himself on all occasions avoided fights, but would not allow even others to quarrel. See in Xenophon's Symposium how many quarrels he settled; how further he endured Thrasymachus and Polus and Callicles; how he tolerated his wife, and how he tolerated his son who attempted to confute him aid to cavil with him. For he remembered well that no man has in his power another man's ruling principle. He wished, therefore nothing else than that which was his own. And what is this? Not that this or that man may act according to nature; for that is a thing which belongs to another; but that while others are doing their own acts, as they choose, he may never the less be in a condition conformable to nature and live in it, only doing what is his own to the end that others also may be in a state conformable to nature. For this is the object always set before him by the wise and good man. Is it to be commander of an army? No: but if it is permitted him, his object is in this matter to maintain his own ruling principle. Is it to marry? No; but if marriage is allowed to him, in this matter his object is to maintain himself in a condition conformable to nature. But if he would have his son not to do wrong, or his wife, he would have what belongs to another not to belong to another; and to he instructed is this: to learn what things are a man's own and what belongs to another.
D: Refashioned:
The wise and good person does not fight with anyone, and prevents others from fighting when possible. Socrates is the model: he settled quarrels, tolerated Thrasymachus, Polus, and Callicles in argument, endured his difficult wife and obstinate son. He remembered that no one has power over another person's ruling principle.

The wise person desires only what is their own — to maintain their rational faculty in conformity with nature while others do as they choose. A commander? Maintain your character while commanding. A spouse? Maintain your character while married. But if you want your son not to make mistakes or your wife not to err, you are wanting what belongs to someone else. The only education that matters is learning which things are yours and which are not.
D: Elegant: Socrates tolerated Thrasymachus, Callicles, his wife, and his son — because he remembered that no one controls another's ruling principle. He wanted only what was his own: to maintain his character regardless of circumstances. Wanting your child not to err is wanting what belongs to another. Learn which things are yours. That is the whole education.
D: Distilled: Wanting your child not to make mistakes is wanting what belongs to another — the only thing you control is your own ruling principle.
Notes: Chapter 4.5 opens with Socrates as the exemplar of non-quarrelsomeness. Thrasymachus, Polus, and Callicles — aggressive interlocutors in Plato's Republic and Gorgias who challenged Socrates with confrontational rhetoric. Xanthippe (Socrates's wife) and Lamprocles (his son) represent the domestic sphere where Stoic equanimity is most tested. The teaching that 'another's ruling principle is not yours' (to hegemonikon allotrios) is the foundation of Stoic social ethics.

D: Original: How, then, is there left any place for fighting, to a man who has this opinion? Is he surprised at anything which happens, and does it appear new to him? Does he not expect that which comes from the bad to be worse and more grievous than what actually befalls him? And does he not reckon as pure gain whatever they may do which falls short of extreme wickedness? "Such a person has reviled you." Great thanks to him for not having, struck you. "But he has struck me also." Great thanks that he did not wound you "But he wounded me also." Great thanks that he did not kill you.
D: Refashioned:
Where is there room for fighting in a person who holds these views? Is anything that happens surprising? Does anything from bad people turn out worse than expected? The person who understands human nature expects worse and counts anything less than the worst as pure gain.

'He insulted me.' Be grateful he didn't hit you. 'He hit me.' Be grateful he didn't wound you. 'He wounded me.' Be grateful he didn't kill you.
D: Elegant: 'He insulted me.' Be grateful he did not hit you. 'He hit me.' Be grateful he did not wound you. 'He wounded me.' Be grateful he did not kill you. Expect the worst and everything less is a gift.
D: Distilled: The person who expects the worst from bad people counts everything less as pure gain — and finds no reason to fight.
Notes: The escalating insult/blow/wound/death sequence uses the Stoic technique of premeditation of evils (praemeditatio malorum): by imagining the worst, you make the actual outcome seem mild. This is not pessimism but calibration — adjusting your expectations to reality. The approach echoes Marcus Aurelius's morning meditation (Meditations 2.1): 'Begin the morning by telling yourself: I shall meet the busybody, the ungrateful, the violent.'

D: Original: For when did he learn or in what school that man is a tame animal, that men love one another, that an act of injustice is a great harm to him who does it. Since then he has not to him who does it. Since then he has not learned this and is not convinced of it, why shall he not follow that which seems to be for his own "Your neighbour has thrown stones." Have you then done anything wrong? "But the things in the house have been broken." Are you then a utensil? No; but a free power of will. What, then, is given to you in answer to this? If you are like a wolf, you must bite in return, and throw more stones.
D: Refashioned:
When did this person learn that human beings are cooperative by nature, and that injustice harms the one who commits it? If they never learned this, why should they act otherwise?

Your neighbor throws stones. Have you done anything wrong? 'But my household possessions are broken.' You are not a pot. You are a free power of will. If you respond like a wolf — biting back, throwing more stones — you have answered the question of what you are. But if you examine what faculties you actually came into the world with, you will find none that are designed for revenge.
D: Elegant: Your neighbor throws stones. 'My possessions are broken!' You are not a pot. You are a free power of will. If you bite back like a wolf, you have answered the question of what you are. Examine the faculties you came with: none of them were designed for revenge.
D: Distilled: You are not a pot that shatters when struck — you are a will that chooses how to respond, and none of your faculties were designed for revenge.
Notes: The distinction between being a 'utensil' (skeuos) and a 'free power of will' (prohairesis eleuthera) is the chapter's practical core: the quarrelsome person has confused themselves with their possessions. If your neighbor breaks your things, the things are damaged but you are not — unless you choose to damage yourself through retaliation. This connects to the framework's Right pillar: the correct response to injustice is not counter-injustice but a return to principle.

D: Original: But if you consider what is proper for a man, examine your store-house, see with at faculties you came into the world. Have you the disposition of a wild beast, Have you the disposition of revenge for an injury? When is a horse wretched? When he is deprived of his natural faculties; not when he cannot crow like a cock, but when he cannot run. When is a dog wretched? Not when he cannot fly, but when he cannot track his game. Is, then, a man also unhappy in this way, not because he cannot strangle lions or embrace statues, for he did not come into the world in the possession of certain powers from nature for this purpose, but because he has lost his probity and his fidelity? People ought to meet and lament such a man for the misfortunes into which he has fallen; not indeed to lament because a man his been born or has died, but because it has happened to him in his lifetime to have lost the things which are his own, not that which he received from his father, not his land and house, and his inn, and his slaves; for not one of these things is a man's own, but all belong to others, are servile and subject to account, at different times given to different persons by those who have them in their power: but I mean the things which belong to him as a man, the marks in his mind with which he came into the world, such as we seek also on coins, and if we find them, we approve of the coins, and if we do not find the marks, we reject them. What is the stamp on this Sestertius? "The stamp of Trajan." Present it. "It is the stamp of Nero." Throw it away: it cannot be accepted, it is counterfeit. So also in this case. What is the stamp of his opinions? "It is gentleness, a sociable disposition, a tolerant temper, a disposition to mutual affection." Produce these qualities. I accept them: I consider this man a citizen, I accept him as a neighbour, a companion in my voyages. Only see that he has not Nero's stamp. Is he passionate, is he full of resentment, is he faultfinding? If the whim seizes him, does he break the heads of those who come in his way? Why, then did you say that he is a man? Is everything judged by the bare form? If that is so, say that the form in wax is all apple and has the smell and the taste of an apple.
D: Refashioned:
Ask what is proper for a human being. When is a horse wretched? Not when it cannot crow, but when it cannot run. When is a dog? Not when it cannot fly, but when it cannot track. A human being is wretched not when unable to strangle lions but when they have lost their integrity and faithfulness.

We should mourn the person who has lost these qualities — not the one who has lost property, land, or an inheritance. None of those are a person's own. What is yours is the stamp on your character. Check the coin: does it bear the mark of Trajan — gentleness, sociability, tolerance, affection? Accept it. Does it bear the mark of Nero — rage, resentment, cruelty? Throw it away. It is counterfeit.
D: Elegant: A horse is wretched when it cannot run, not when it cannot crow. A person is wretched when they have lost their integrity, not their property. Check the coin: does it bear Trajan's stamp — gentleness, tolerance, affection? Accept it. Nero's stamp — rage, cruelty, resentment? Throw it away. It is counterfeit.
D: Distilled: A person's worth is stamped on their character like a coin — check for integrity and faithfulness, not wealth and status.
Notes: Trajan (r. 98-117 CE) — widely regarded as one of Rome's best emperors, known for fairness and competence. Nero (r. 54-68 CE) — symbol of tyranny and cruelty. The sestertius (Roman coin) metaphor is brilliantly concrete: just as a coin's value depends on its stamp, a person's value depends on their character. The contrast between Trajan and Nero provides an immediate visual test. This connects to the framework's concept of Right: character is not what you claim but what you consistently demonstrate.

D: Original: But the external figure is not enough: neither then is the nose enough and the eyes to make the man, but he must have the opinions of a man. Here is a man who does not listen to reason, who does not know when he is refuted: he is an ass: in another man the sense of shame is become dead: he is good for nothing, he is anything rather than a man. This man seeks whom he may meet and kick or bite, so that he is not even a sheep or an ass, but a kind of wild beast.
D: Refashioned:
The external form is not enough. A nose and eyes do not make a person — the opinions of a person make one. Someone who does not listen to reason is an ass. Someone whose sense of shame has died is worthless. Someone who seeks people to kick and bite is not even a sheep but a wild beast.

Do not judge by appearance. A wax apple has the shape but not the smell or taste. A person can have the form of a human being without any of the qualities that make one.
D: Elegant: A nose and eyes do not make a person. Without reason, you are an ass. Without shame, you are worthless. Without restraint, you are a beast. A wax apple has the shape but not the taste. Do not judge by the form.
D: Distilled: The human form without human character is a wax apple — it has the shape but not the substance.
Notes: The wax apple (kerinos melos) metaphor is Epictetus's most vivid image for the gap between appearance and reality. The taxonomy of dehumanization — ass (stubbornness), wild beast (aggression), wax fruit (empty form) — provides a framework for diagnosing character failures. This connects to the framework's Reality pillar: accurate perception requires looking past surface to substance.

D: Original: "What then would you have me to be despised?" By whom? by those who know you? and how and how shall those who know you despise a man who is gentle and modest? Perhaps you mean by those who do not know you? What is that to you? For no other artisan cares for the opinion of those who know not his art. "But they will be more hostile to me for this reason." Why do you say "me"? Can any man injure your will, or prevent you from using in a natural way the appearances which are presented to you, "In no way can he." Why, then, are still disturbed and why do you choose to show yourself afraid? And why do you not come forth and proclaim that you are at peace with all men whatever they may do, and laugh at those chiefly who think that they can harm you? "These slaves," you can say, "know not either who I am nor where lies my good or my evil, because they have no access to the things which are mine."
D: Refashioned:
'Won't people despise me if I am gentle and modest?' Those who know you will not. Those who do not know you — what is that to you? No craftsman cares about the opinions of those who do not understand their craft.

'But they will be more hostile.' To what? Can anyone injure your will or prevent you from responding naturally? Then why be disturbed? Come forward and declare that you are at peace with everyone, whatever they do. Laugh at those who think they can harm you. Say: 'These people do not know who I am or where my good lies, because they have no access to anything that is actually mine.'
D: Elegant: Those who know you will not despise your gentleness. Those who do not know you — what is that to you? No craftsman cares for the opinion of the ignorant. Can anyone injure your will? Then declare peace with everyone and laugh at those who think they can harm you. They have no access to anything that is truly yours.
D: Distilled: No one can harm what is truly yours — declare peace with everyone and laugh at those who think otherwise.
Notes: The craftsman analogy (technitēs) reinforces the Stoic position that the wise person's authority, like any expert's, is independent of popular opinion. The declaration of peace — being at peace with everyone regardless of what they do — is the practical expression of Stoic apatheia: not emotional numbness but immunity to genuine harm because nothing external can touch the will.

D: Original: In this way, also, those who occupy a strong city mock the besiegers; "What trouble these men are now taking for nothing: our wall is secure, we have food for a very long time, and all other resources." These are the things which make a city strong and impregnable: but nothing else than his opinions makes a man's soul impregnable. For what wall is so strong, or what body is so hard, or what possession is so safe, or what honour so free from assault? All things everywhere are perishable, easily taken by assault, and, if any man in any way is attached to them, he must be disturbed, expect what is bad, he must fear, lament, find his desires disappointed, and fall into things which he would avoid. Then do we not choose to make secure the only means of safety which are offered to us, and do we not choose to withdraw ourselves from that which is perishable and servile and to labour at the things, which are imperishable and by nature free; and do we not remember that no man either hurts another or does good to another, but that a man's opinion about each thing is that which hurts him, is that which overturns him; this is fighting, this is civil discord, this is war? That which made Eteocles and Polynices enemies was nothing else than this opinion which they had about royal power, their opinion about exile, that the one is the extreme of evils, the other the greatest good.
D: Refashioned:
A strong city mocks its besiegers: our walls are secure, our supplies are abundant. What makes a soul impregnable is the same thing — not walls but opinions. Everything external is perishable, easily taken, and whoever attaches themselves to it must be disturbed, afraid, and lamenting.

Will we not secure the only means of safety available? Will we not withdraw from what is perishable and work on what is imperishable? No person harms or benefits another — a person's opinion about each thing is what harms them. This is what causes every conflict. Eteocles and Polynices destroyed each other not over royal power but over their opinions about royal power and exile.
D: Elegant: A strong city laughs at its besiegers. What makes a soul impregnable is the same — not walls but opinions. Nothing external is secure. Everything can be taken. Will you not work on the one thing that cannot? Eteocles and Polynices destroyed each other not over a kingdom but over their opinions about a kingdom.
D: Distilled: What makes a soul impregnable is not walls but opinions — and every war, from cities to brothers, is fought over opinions, not things.
Notes: Eteocles and Polynices — sons of Oedipus who killed each other fighting over the throne of Thebes, dramatized in Aeschylus's Seven Against Thebes and Euripides's Phoenician Women. The fratricidal war illustrates the Stoic claim that all conflict originates in false judgments about externals: the brothers agreed that kingship was the supreme good and exile the supreme evil, and this opinion — not the throne itself — caused the war. This connects to the framework's analysis of conflict in the ideational domain: wars are fought over beliefs about what constitutes the good, not over material resources alone.

D: Original: Now this is the nature of every man to seek the good, to avoid the bad; to consider him who deprives us of the one and involves us in the other an enemy and treacherous, even if he be a brother, or a son or a father. For nothing is more akin to us than the good: therefore if these things are good and evil, neither is a father a friend to sons, nor a brother to a brother, but all the world is everywhere full of enemies, treacherous men, and sycophants. But if the will, being what it ought to be, is the only good; and if the will, being such as it ought not to be, is the only evil, where is there any strife, where is there reviling? about what? about the things which do not concern us? and strife with whom? with the ignorant, the unhappy, with those who are deceived about the chief things?
D: Refashioned:
Human nature seeks the good and avoids the bad. When we think someone has deprived us of the good or involved us in the bad, we treat them as an enemy — even a brother, a son, a father. If externals are the good, then everyone is a potential enemy and the world is full of treachery and conflict.

But if the will properly oriented is the only good, and the will improperly oriented the only evil, then there is no basis for strife. What would you fight about? Things that do not concern you? With whom? The ignorant, the unhappy, the deceived? Pity them instead.
D: Elegant: If externals are the good, everyone is a potential enemy — even your own father. But if the only good is a rightly oriented will, what remains to fight about? Things that do not concern you? With whom? The deceived? Pity them.
D: Distilled: If the only good is a rightly oriented will, there is nothing left to fight over and no one left to fight — only people to pity.
Notes: The logic is airtight: if good and evil reside in externals, then anyone who has what you want or threatens what you have is your enemy, and universal conflict is inevitable. If good and evil reside only in the will, the entire basis for interpersonal conflict dissolves. The move from hostility to pity marks the transition from conventional morality to Stoic ethics: the unjust person is not your enemy but a fellow human being who has made a catastrophic error about where their good lies.

D: Original: Remembering this Socrates managed his own house and endured a very ill-tempered wife and a foolish son. For in what did she show her bad temper? In pouring water on his head as much as she liked, and in trampling on the cake. And what is this to me, if I think that these things are nothing to me? But this is my business; and neither tyrant shall check my will nor a master; nor shall the many check me who am only one, nor shall the stronger check me who am the weaker; for this power of being free from check is given by God to every man. For these opinions make love in a house, concord in a state, among nations peace, and gratitude to God; they make a man in all things cheerful in externals as about things which belong to others, as about things which are of no value. We indeed are able to write and to read these things, and to praise them when they are read, but we do not even come near to being convinced of them. Therefore what is said of the Lacedaemonians, "Lions at home, but in Ephesus foxes," will fit in our case also, "Lions in the school, but out of it foxes."
D: Refashioned:
Socrates managed his household on this principle and endured a difficult wife and a foolish son. Xanthippe poured water on his head and trampled his dinner cake. What is that to me, if I decide these things are nothing to me?

This is my business: no tyrant will overpower my will, no master will control it, no crowd will pressure it, no stronger person will subdue it. This power is given by God to every person. These opinions produce love in a household, peace in a state, gratitude toward God. They make a person cheerful about externals — because externals belong to others and have no real value.

We can write these words and praise them when we read them. But we do not come close to being convinced of them. What was said of the Spartans applies to us: 'Lions in the school, foxes outside it.'
D: Elegant: Xanthippe poured water on Socrates's head. What is that, if you have decided it is nothing? No tyrant controls your will, no crowd pressures it, no stronger person subdues it. God gave this power to everyone. But we praise these words in the classroom and abandon them at the door. Lions in the school, foxes outside it.
D: Distilled: We are lions in the classroom and foxes in the world — praising principles we do not live by the moment we leave the school.
Notes: Xanthippe's water-pouring and cake-trampling are famous anecdotes about Socrates's domestic life. The Spartan proverb — 'lions at home, foxes in Ephesus' (originally about Spartan courage failing in foreign territory) — Epictetus inverts: 'lions in the school, foxes outside.' This is his final diagnosis of the gap between philosophical knowledge and philosophical practice, and it closes the chapter on quarrelsomeness. The framework parallel is direct: institutional learning that does not survive contact with reality is the definition of the erosion pattern.

D: Original: Chapter 6: Against those who lament over being pitied

"I am grieved," a man says, "at being pitied." Whether, then, is the fact of your being pitied a thing which concerns you or those who pity you? Well, is it in your power to stop this pity? "It is in my power, if I show them that I do not require pity." And whether, then, are you in the condition of not deserving pity, or are you not in that condition? "I think I am not: but these persons do not pity me
D: Refashioned: 'I am grieved at being pitied.' Is being pitied something that concerns you, or the person doing the pitying? Is it in your power to stop them? If it is — by showing them you do not require pity — then show them. Are you in a condition that does not deserve pity, or are you not? If you are not, then the problem is not their pity but your condition. Address that first.
D: Elegant: You are grieved at being pitied. Is their pity your concern or theirs? If you can stop it by showing you need none, show them. If you cannot stop it, the problem is not their pity but your condition.
D: Distilled: If someone's pity disturbs you, the problem is not their judgment but the condition in yourself that you have not yet addressed.
Notes: Chapter 4.6 — the final chapter of the Discourses — is characteristically brief. The teaching is simple: being disturbed by another person's pity is itself a form of dependency on their opinion. The only legitimate response is either to demonstrate that pity is unwarranted (by genuinely not needing it) or to acknowledge that the pity is warranted and work on the underlying condition. Either way, the disturbance comes from within, not from the person who pities you. This is a fitting coda to Book 4: freedom from external judgment, the last citadel to be secured.
